topics.title: Programs for Screening
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT862 - AI FOR MEDICAL IMAGE ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Programs for Screening

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Screening Program** in medical imaging is a *systematic, population-level public health intervention* that uses one or more diagnostic tests (imaging, molecular, or AI-assisted) to **asymptomatically identify individuals with early-stage disease or high-risk conditions** so they can be referred for confirmatory diagnosis and timely treatment.

In the context of **AI for Medical Image Analysis**, *Programs for Screening* refers to the integration of **computer-aided detection (CADe)**, **computer-aided diagnosis (CADx)**, and **deep learning triage systems** into established screening workflows (mammography, low-dose CT, chest X-ray, retinal fundus, cervical cytology, etc.) to:

1. **Reduce radiologist/clinician workload** via automated triaging.
2. **Improve early-detection rates** of diseases such as cancer, tuberculosis, and diabetic retinopathy.
3. **Standardize interpretation** across geographic and expertise boundaries.
4. **Lower the cost per case detected** by prioritizing high-risk cases.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Approved):**
> *"An AI-enabled screening program is a population-scale deployment of a validated imaging AI model that operates on asymptomatic subjects within a defined demographic cohort, producing a binary or risk-stratified output that determines downstream referral, while satisfying pre-defined performance, equity, and regulatory benchmarks."*

## 1.2 The Wilson & Jungner Criteria (WHO Gold Standard for Screening)

The **World Health Organization (WHO)** endorses ten principles (originally Wilson & Jungner, 1968; revised by WHO in 2008 and updated in 2020) that any screening program — AI-augmented or otherwise — must satisfy. KTU expects students to know these because they appear directly in Part A and Part B questions.

| # | Criterion | Why It Matters for AI |
|---|-----------|----------------------|
| 1 | The condition should be an important health problem. | Justifies data-collection investment. |
| 2 | There should be a recognizable latent or early symptomatic stage. | Defines the AI's *detection window*. |
| 3 | There should be a suitable test or examination. | The AI model itself is the "test." |
| 4 | The test should be acceptable to the population. | UI/UX of screening kiosks matters. |
| 5 | There should be an agreed-upon policy on whom to treat. | Defines positive-class decision boundary. |
| 6 | There should be an accepted treatment for patients with recognized disease. | AI must link to a clinical pathway. |
| 7 | Facilities for diagnosis and treatment should be available. | AI output must be tied to referral. |
| 8 | The cost of case-finding should be economically balanced. | Cost-effectiveness drives AI deployment. |
| 9 | Case-finding should be a continuing process, not a "once and for-all" project. | Necessitates **model monitoring & recalibration**. |
| 10 | The overall cost of the program should be economically balanced in relation to medical expenditure as a whole. | Health-technology assessment (HTA) gate. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Airport Security Sieve" Intuition

Imagine an **airport security checkpoint**:

- **Passengers** = the asymptomatic population entering a screening program.
- **Security scanners** = the AI imaging model (chest X-ray, mammogram, fundus camera).
- **High-risk travelers** (those carrying prohibited items) = patients with subtle, subclinical disease.
- **Human security officers** = the radiologist/physician who acts as a *second reader* or *confirmer*.
- **A flagged passenger goes to a deeper inspection bay** = a positive screen → confirmatory diagnostic test → treatment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** AI does **not** replace the radiologist in screening — it acts as a **triage sieve** that either (a) auto-reports clearly normal cases, (b) prioritizes suspicious cases for urgent human review, or (c) operates as an independent *second reader* alongside the human.

## 1.4 Why "Screening" Demands Different AI Metrics Than Diagnostic AI

This is a *favourite KTU 2-mark question*. The performance bar for screening AI is **asymmetric**:

- **Sensitivity (Recall)** must be **extremely high** (often ≥ **95%**) because *missing* a cancer in an asymptomatic person is catastrophic.
- **Specificity** can be moderately lower (~**85–90%**) because false positives are caught by confirmatory testing.
- **Positive Predictive Value (PPV)** depends heavily on **disease prevalence** in the screened cohort — low prevalence (e.g., breast cancer ~0.5% in 40–50 year olds) makes PPV the *limiting metric* for cost-effectiveness.

$$
\text{PPV} \;=\; \frac{\text{Sensitivity} \times \text{Prevalence}}{\text{Sensitivity} \times \text{Prevalence} \;+\; (1 - \text{Specificity}) \times (1 - \text{Prevalence})}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often claim "higher sensitivity = better screening model." The *correct* exam answer is: *In screening, sensitivity is prioritized, but the model's operating point must be chosen jointly with specificity to keep PPV and downstream referral cost sustainable.*

## 1.5 Taxonomy of Screening Programs (KTU Module 1 Sub-Classification)

```
Screening Programs
├── By Population Strategy
│     ├── Mass (Population-wide) Screening        e.g., National Breast Screening
│     ├── Selective (High-risk cohort) Screening  e.g., Lung cancer in smokers
│     └── Opportunistic Screening                  e.g., incidental findings
├── By Modality
│     ├── Mammography (2D / 3D tomosynthesis)
│     ├── Low-Dose CT (LDCT)
│     ├── Chest X-ray (CXR)
│     ├── Retinal Fundus Photography
│     ├── Cervical Cytology / HPV testing
│     └── Whole-slide Histopathology
└── By AI Role
      ├── Triage (prioritize worklist)
      ├── Second Reader (parallel to human)
      ├── Replacement (autonomous, e.g., IDx-DR)
      └── Population Risk Stratification
```
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The AI-Screening Operational Pipeline (Five-Stage Workflow)

Every population-scale AI screening program, irrespective of disease, funnels through these **five canonical stages**. KTU examiners expect students to enumerate, label, and explain each stage.

### Stage 1 — **Image Acquisition**
- Standardized acquisition protocols (e.g., Hologic/Siemens mammography, Canon retinal cameras).
- Critical for AI: **device heterogeneity** (vendor, dose, FOV) causes **dataset shift**.
- DICOM metadata must capture: kVp, mAs, exposure time, pixel spacing, laterality, view position.
- *AI implication:* the model must either be trained on multi-vendor data or have a **domain-adaptation** module.

### Stage 2 — **Pre-processing & Quality Control**
- Intensity normalization (e.g., Z-score per image, percentile clipping).
- Resolution standardization (resampling to a common voxel/pixel grid, e.g., 512×512 or 224×224).
- Anatomical alignment: breast orientation in mammograms, lung segmentation in CXR.
- *Quality gates:* blur detection, exposure adequacy, presence/absence of anatomy — *rejected images are NOT passed downstream*.

### Stage 3 — **AI Inference (Detection / Classification / Segmentation)**
The model — usually a **Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)** or **Vision Transformer (ViT)** — produces:

- A **bounding box + class probability** (object detection, e.g., YOLOv5, RetinaNet, nnDetection).
- A **segmentation mask** (e.g., U-Net, nnU-Net for lung nodules, breast masses).
- A **scalar risk score** (e.g., probability of malignancy, presence of TB).
- A **per-image embedding** for downstream retrieval (e.g., self-supervised foundation models like RadImageNet, MedSAM).

### Stage 4 — **Decision / Triage Logic**
- The scalar score is mapped to one of three buckets:
  - **Negative** (auto-report or low-priority queue).
  - **Indeterminate** (standard human reading).
  - **Suspicious / Positive** (priority human review).
- Threshold chosen to satisfy the **program's sensitivity target** (e.g., ≥ 95%).

### Stage 5 — **Human Review + Confirmatory Diagnosis + Outcome Capture**
- Radiologist reads priority cases first.
- Positive screens undergo **gold-standard confirmatory test** (biopsy, follow-up imaging, microbiology).
- The result is fed back as a *label* to retrain / monitor the AI — this closes the **continuous-learning loop** (Criterion 9 of Wilson & Jungner).

## 2.2 Theoretical Foundations — Statistical & Epidemiological

### 2.2.1 Sensitivity, Specificity, PPV, NPV at the Population Level

For a screening cohort of size $N$ with disease prevalence $p$:

$$
\text{Sensitivity (TPR)} \;=\; \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

$$
\text{Specificity (TNR)} \;=\; \frac{TN}{TN + FP}
$$

$$
\text{PPV} \;=\; \frac{TP}{TP + FP}
$$

$$
\text{NPV} \;=\; \frac{TN}{TN + FN}
$$

$$
\text{F1-score} \;=\; \frac{2 \cdot \text{PPV} \cdot \text{Sensitivity}}{\text{PPV} + \text{Sensitivity}}
$$

### 2.2.2 Number Needed to Screen (NNS) and Number Needed to Treat (NNT)

KTU has previously asked derivations of these in 14-mark questions.

$$
\text{NNS} \;=\; \frac{1}{\text{absolute risk reduction}}
$$

$$
\text{NNT} \;=\; \frac{1}{\text{ARR}}
$$

For AI-augmented screening, **NNS drops** because the AI prioritizes high-yield cases, but the *absolute detection rate* per 1,000 screens remains the epidemiologically meaningful statistic.

### 2.2.3 Free-Response ROC (FROC) — The Screening Metric

Unlike a standard ROC, **FROC** plots sensitivity vs. average number of false positives per image. It is the *de facto* metric for **lesion-detection** AI in screening (lung nodules, mammographic masses, TB cavities).

$$
\text{FROC point} \;=\; \left( \overline{FP}_{\text{image}},\; \text{Sensitivity} \right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FROC vs. ROC** — Students must state in the exam: *ROC is for classification (one image = one label), FROC is for detection (one image = N lesions). Screening AI is overwhelmingly detection, so FROC is the board-preferred metric.*

### 2.2.4 Equity Metric — Subgroup Sensitivity Disparity

A *newer* (and increasingly examined) requirement is **algorithmic fairness**:

$$
\Delta_{\text{fair}} \;=\; \max_{g \in \mathcal{G}} \text{Sensitivity}(g) \;-\; \min_{g \in \mathcal{G}} \text{Sensitivity}(g)
$$

where $\mathcal{G}$ is the set of demographic subgroups (sex, age band, ethnicity, skin tone, device). KTU 2024 scheme includes **Ethics & Bias** under its CO mapping, so this formula is high-yield.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat-Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Exam Use |
|---|-------------------|-----------|----------|
| 1 | Sensitivity | $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Mandatory in any screening question. |
| 2 | Specificity | $\frac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | Mandatory. |
| 3 | PPV (Bayes' form) | $\frac{\text{Se}\cdot p}{\text{Se}\cdot p + (1-\text{Sp})(1-p)}$ | Use when prevalence $p$ is given. |
| 4 | NPV | $\frac{\text{Sp}(1-p)}{\text{Sp}(1-p) + (1-\text{Se})p}$ | Use in ruling-out screening. |
| 5 | Youden's J Index | $J = \text{Se} + \text{Sp} - 1$ | Threshold selection. |
| 6 | FROC coordinates | $(\overline{FP}_{\text{img}},\;\text{Se})$ | Lesion-detection tasks. |
| 7 | Cohen's Kappa (reader–AI agreement) | $\kappa = \frac{p_o - p_e}{1 - p_e}$ | Human–AI concordance. |
| 8 | Subgroup Disparity | $\Delta_{\text{fair}} = \max \text{Se}(g) - \min \text{Se}(g)$ | Fairness/ethics questions. |
| 9 | NNS / NNT | $\frac{1}{\text{ARR}}$ | Population-impact questions. |
| 10 | Wilson & Jungner | 10 criteria (1968/2008/2020) | 2-mark direct question. |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Public Health Engineering:** National health ministries (NHS-UK, AIIMS-India, NHSN-Kerala) commission AI screening to compensate for radiologist shortages.
- **Industrial Deployment:** Companies like **Qure.ai** (chest X-ray TB screening), **IDx / Digital Diagnostic** (diabetic retinopathy — first FDA-autonomous AI), **Viz.ai** (stroke triage on CT-angiography), **Lunit** (mammography) have shipped >20 M AI-assisted screening reads in 2023–2025.
- **Edge Deployment:** Modern screening AI runs on-device inside portable X-ray units (e.g., Delft Imaging's CAD4TB) — *no internet, no cloud, sub-3-second inference*, enabling rural/field deployment.
- **Cost-Effectiveness:** A peer-reviewed NHS study (2023) showed AI-triage in CXR reduced radiologist reporting time by **~30%** while maintaining sensitivity.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION_3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Why Prevalence Governs PPV (Bayesian Derivation)

This is the **most-tested KTU derivation** under the screening module. We derive PPV from sensitivity, specificity, and prevalence using Bayes' theorem.

### Step 1 — Define the Joint Probability Table

Let the random variable $D \in \{1, 0\}$ denote *disease present / absent*, and $S \in \{+, -\}$ denote *screening test positive / negative*. Define:

$$
P(S=+ \mid D=1) \;=\; \text{Se} \quad\quad P(S=- \mid D=0) \;=\; \text{Sp}
$$

Complement rules give:

$$
P(S=- \mid D=1) \;=\; 1 - \text{Se}
$$

$$
P(S=+ \mid D=0) \;=\; 1 - \text{Sp}
$$

> *These four statements encode the operating point of the screening model.*

### Step 2 — Express Joint Probabilities

The probability that a randomly selected screened individual is *both diseased AND positive* is:

$$
P(D=1,\; S=+) \;=\; P(S=+ \mid D=1) \cdot P(D=1) \;=\; \text{Se} \cdot p
$$

where $p = P(D=1)$ is the prevalence.

Similarly, the probability of being *disease-free AND positive*:

$$
P(D=0,\; S=+) \;=\; P(S=+ \mid D=0) \cdot P(D=0) \;=\; (1 - \text{Sp}) \cdot (1 - p)
$$

### Step 3 — Total Probability of a Positive Screen

$$
P(S=+) \;=\; P(D=1,\; S=+) + P(D=0,\; S=+) \;=\; \text{Se} \cdot p + (1 - \text{Sp})(1 - p)
$$

### Step 4 — Apply Bayes' Theorem for PPV

By definition, $\text{PPV} = P(D=1 \mid S=+)$, so:

$$
\text{PPV} \;=\; \frac{P(S=+ \mid D=1)\,P(D=1)}{P(S=+)} \;=\; \frac{\text{Se}\cdot p}{\text{Se}\cdot p + (1-\text{Sp})(1-p)}
$$

**Result derived.** This is the canonical PPV formula and is the cornerstone of every screening-related KTU numerical problem.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** As $p \to 0$ (rare disease — typical in population screening), $\text{PPV} \to 0$ *regardless* of model sensitivity. This is the **rare-disease paradox** of screening and is a guaranteed KTU Part-A question.

### Step 3a — Worked Numerical Example (Past-Year Style)

A mammography-AI is deployed in a population where:
- Prevalence of breast cancer $p = 0.005$ (5 per 1,000).
- Sensitivity $\text{Se} = 0.95$.
- Specificity $\text{Sp} = 0.90$.

Compute the PPV.

$$
\text{PPV} \;=\; \frac{0.95 \times 0.005}{0.95 \times 0.005 + (1 - 0.90)(1 - 0.005)}
$$

$$
= \frac{0.00475}{0.00475 + 0.10 \times 0.995}
$$

$$
= \frac{0.00475}{0.00475 + 0.0995}
$$

$$
= \frac{0.00475}{0.10425}
$$

$$
\approx 0.0456 \;\approx\; 4.56\%
$$

**Conclusion:** Even with 95% sensitivity, only ~4.6% of AI-flagged women will actually have cancer. This is why screening programs use **two-reader** or **AI + radiologist** workflows to re-filter false positives.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The FROC Operating Curve from First Principles

A detector outputs a confidence score $c \in [0, 1]$ for *each predicted lesion* per image. Define:

- $TP(\tau)$ = number of predicted lesions that fall on a true lesion at threshold $\tau$.
- $FP(\tau)$ = number of predicted lesions that fall outside any true lesion at threshold $\tau$.
- $N_{\text{img}}$ = number of images.

Then:

$$
\text{Sensitivity}(\tau) \;=\; \frac{TP(\tau)}{N_{\text{lesions, total}}}
$$

$$
\overline{FP}_{\text{image}}(\tau) \;=\; \frac{FP(\tau)}{N_{\text{img}}}
$$

Varying $\tau$ from $0$ (most permissive) to $1$ (strictest) traces the **FROC curve**:

$$
\text{FROC}(\tau) \;=\; \left(\; \frac{FP(\tau)}{N_{\text{img}}},\;\; \frac{TP(\tau)}{N_{\text{lesions}}}\;\right)
$$

> *Lowering $\tau$* allows more detections (higher sensitivity) but more false alarms (higher FP/image). A *better* screening AI shifts the FROC curve **up and to the left** — same FP budget, more true lesions caught.

**Common KTR (Key Take-away for the Reader):** A radiologist working with 0.5 FP/image budget expects a sensitivity of, say, 80%. If AI delivers 92% at the same FP budget, the marginal value of AI is +12 percentage-points in sensitivity — *this is the KTU-standard way to argue AI utility in screening.*

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Three Canonical AI Screening Programs

The matrix below is **directly examinable** as a 14-mark Part-B sub-part (a). KTU examiners love such comparison tables.

| Dimension | **Breast Cancer (Mammography AI)** | **Lung Cancer (LDCT AI)** | **Diabetic Retinopathy (Fundus AI)** |
|-----------|------------------------------------|----------------------------|---------------------------------------|
| **Modality** | 2D D mammogram, 3D tomosynthesis | Low-dose thoracic CT | Color fundus photograph |
| **Target Lesion** | Microcalcifications, masses, architectural distortion | Solid / sub-solid pulmonary nodules | Microaneurysms, exudates, neovascularization |
| **Screening Age / Cohort** | Women 40–74 (varies by country) | Smokers 55–74, ≥30 pack-years | Diabetics, all ages, annual |
| **Prevalence in Cohort** | ~0.3–0.5% | ~1–2% | ~20–35% (in diabetics) |
| **Required Sensitivity** | ≥ 95% | ≥ 90% | ≥ 85% (FDA threshold: 85%) |
| **Specificity Target** | ≥ 85% | ≥ 85% | ≥ 82.5% |
| **AI Role** | Second reader / co-reader (Lunit INSIGHT MMG, Google LYNA successor) | Triage + nodule CADe (Aidoc, Riverain ClearRead) | Autonomous (IDx-DR — first FDA-cleared autonomous AI, 2018) |
| **Reference Standard** | Biopsy + 1-year imaging follow-up | CT follow-up, PET, biopsy | Dilated ophthalmoscopy by specialist |
| **Key Regulatory Body** | FDA, CE-MDR, ICMR (India) | FDA, NCCN guidelines | FDA De Novo (DEN180001) |
| **Equity Concern** | Lower sensitivity in dense breasts & non-white cohorts | Higher FP in regions with endemic granulomatous disease (TB) | Underperformance in darker retinal pigmentation (documented in 2020 studies) |
| **Evidence Level** | Multiple RCTs (MASAI trial 2023: AI + single reader ≈ two-reader) | NLST-derived retrospective validations | Real-world deployments in India, Thailand, EU |

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Sample-Size Justification for a Screening Validation Study

KTU may ask: *"If your AI must achieve sensitivity ≥ 90% with a 95% CI width of ±5%, how many disease-positive cases must you enroll?"*

### Step 1 — Wilson Score Interval for a Proportion

For an observed proportion $\hat{p}$, the Wilson 95% confidence interval half-width is approximately:

$$
\text{CI half-width} \;\approx\; 1.96 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n_{\text{pos}}}}
$$

### Step 2 — Solve for $n_{\text{pos}}$

We want this half-width ≤ 0.05 at $\hat{p} = 0.90$:

$$
1.96 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{0.90 \times 0.10}{n_{\text{pos}}}} \;\le\; 0.05
$$

$$
\frac{0.3528}{\sqrt{n_{\text{pos}}}} \;\le\; 0.05
$$

$$
\sqrt{n_{\text{pos}}} \;\ge\; \frac{0.3528}{0.05} \;=\; 7.056
$$

$$
n_{\text{pos}} \;\ge\; 49.79 \;\approx\; 50 \text{ disease-positive cases}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to Total Cohort via Prevalence

If prevalence in the screening cohort is 0.005, the total sample needed is:

$$
N_{\text{total}} \;=\; \frac{n_{\text{pos}}}{p} \;=\; \frac{50}{0.005} \;=\; 10{,}000 \text{ screened individuals}
$$

> **Implication for AI screening validation:** A new mammography-AI typically needs **~10,000–50,000** screening mammograms (50–250 cancers) to demonstrate its performance claim with regulator-acceptable precision. KTU students should remember the rule of thumb: **~50 positives per claimed sensitivity level** for a Wilson 95% CI of ±5%.

## 3.5 Decision-Theoretic Framework — When Does AI Add Net Value?

Define the *net benefit* of an AI-assisted screening strategy over a human-only baseline:

$$
\text{NB}_{\text{AI}} \;=\; \big(\text{Se}_{\text{AI+human}} - \text{Se}_{\text{human}}\big) \cdot w_{\text{TP}} \;-\; \big(\text{FPR}_{\text{AI+human}} - \text{FPR}_{\text{human}}\big) \cdot w_{\text{FP}}
$$

where $w_{\text{TP}}$ and $w_{\text{FP}}$ are the *clinical weights* of a true positive and a false positive. For cancer screening:

$$
w_{\text{TP}} \;\gg\; w_{\text{FP}}
$$

which is why even modest sensitivity gains dominate over modest FP losses. This is the **decision-curve analytic** justification for AI in screening.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** A 14-mark answer should always end with a *cost-benefit* sentence: *"AI is justified in screening when its incremental sensitivity, weighted by the long-term survival benefit of early detection, exceeds the incremental cost of additional confirmatory imaging and patient anxiety from false positives."*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> *KTU board examiners award marks for a *labelled, multi-stage* architecture diagram in any AI-screening question. The two Mermaid schematics below are the standard responses students must reproduce.*

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — End-to-End AI Screening Architecture (Population-Scale)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Population Cohort Definition] --> B[Image Acquisition Modality]
    B --> C[Quality Control Gate]
    C --> D{Quality OK?}
    D -- No --> E[Reject / Re-acquire]
    D -- Yes --> F[Pre-processing Pipeline]
    F --> G[AI Inference Engine]
    G --> H[Detection / Segmentation / Score]
    H --> I[Triage Decision Module]
    I --> J[Negative Bucket Auto Queue]
    I --> K[Indeterminate Human Read]
    I --> L[Positive Priority Read]
    K --> M[Confirmatory Diagnostic Test]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Outcome Labelled Archive]
    N --> O[Continuous Learning Model Update]
    O --> G
    J --> P[Screening Report Issued]
    M --> P
    P --> Q[Health System Registry]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Box **G** (AI Inference Engine) is the only *learned* component.
- The loop **G → O → G** is the **closed feedback cycle** that satisfies Wilson & Jungner Criterion 9 ("continuing process").
- Box **D** is the *first safeguard* — bad images never reach the model.

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — AI Triage vs. Second-Reader vs. Autonomous Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Tri[Triage Workflow]
        T1[Image] --> T2[AI Scores]
        T2 --> T3{Priority Bucket}
        T3 -->|Low| T4[Delayed Human Read]
        T3 -->|High| T5[Immediate Human Read]
    end
    subgraph Sec[Second-Reader Workflow]
        S1[Image] --> S2[Human Read]
        S2 --> S3[AI Read]
        S3 --> S4{Concorde?}
        S4 -- Yes --> S5[Single Report Issued]
        S4 -- No --> S6[Arbitration by Senior Reader]
    end
    subgraph Aut[Autonomous Workflow]
        A1[Image] --> A2[AI Read]
        A2 --> A3{Confidence Above Threshold}
        A3 -- Yes --> A4[Auto Report Issued]
        A3 -- No --> A5[Refer to Clinician]
    end
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Triage** *prioritises* but does not *decide* — most common in chest X-ray and CT programs.
- **Second-reader** *parallels* the human — common in mammography (Lunit INSIGHT, Volpara).
- **Autonomous** *decides* without human on positive cases above a confidence threshold — only legally permitted for narrow indications (e.g., **IDx-DR** for diabetic retinopathy).

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Wilson & Jungner Coverage Map for an AI Screening Program

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Required[Wilson Jungner Ten Criteria]
    R1[Important Health Problem]
    R2[Recognisable Latent Stage]
    R3[Suitable Test]
    R4[Acceptable to Population]
    R5[Agreed Treatment Policy]
    R6[Accepted Treatment Exists]
    R7[Diagnosis and Treatment Facilities]
    R8[Cost Effective Case Finding]
    R9[Continuing Process]
    R10[Overall Economic Balance]
    end
    subgraph AIAugmentation[How AI Augments Each Criterion]
    A1[Quantifies disease burden from imaging archives]
    A2[Detects subclinical signatures below human threshold]
    A3[The AI model IS the test]
    A4[Faster turnaround improves acceptability]
    A5[Standardises decision boundary across sites]
    A6[Earlier detection makes treatment viable]
    A7[Cloud and edge AI extend reach]
    A8[Lowers per case cost via triage]
    A9[Continuous learning loop]
    A10[Population scale cost amortisation]
    end
    R1 -.- A1
    R2 -.- A2
    R3 -.- A3
    R4 -.- A4
    R5 -.- A5
    R6 -.- A6
    R7 -.- A7
    R8 -.- A8
    R9 -.- A9
    R10 -.- A10
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — FROC Operating Curve — Screening Performance Visualised

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[False Positives per Image] --> P1[Threshold 0.9 Strict]
    X --> P2[Threshold 0.7 Moderate]
    X --> P3[Threshold 0.5 Permissive]
    P1 --> S1[Sensitivity 60 percent]
    P2 --> S2[Sensitivity 80 percent]
    P3 --> S3[Sensitivity 92 percent]
    S3 -.- C1[Better AI shifts curve up left]
    S2 -.- C1
    S1 -.- C1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the FROC diagram:** As the model's detection threshold decreases, sensitivity rises (more true lesions caught) *and* false positives per image rise. The **operating point** is selected where the marginal benefit of catching one more cancer is balanced against the marginal cost of one more false positive.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
