# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> *All questions below are modelled on the KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech End-Semester Examination (ESE) pattern. Marks and Bloom's cognitive level tags follow the official KTU 2024 CO-to-Question mapping. Module-Internal choice pattern is preserved.*

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP Set B]
*Define a screening program. State the WHO Wilson & Jungner criteria and explain how the introduction of an AI module satisfies at least three of them. (3 marks)*

#### Model Answer (Examiner Key):

A **screening program** is a *systematic, population-level public health intervention* applied to asymptomatic individuals to detect disease at an early, treatable stage using one or more diagnostic tests.

The **Wilson & Jungner criteria (1968, revised WHO 2008 & 2020)** are ten conditions a screening program must satisfy: (1) important health problem, (2) recognised latent/early stage, (3) suitable test, (4) acceptable to the population, (5) agreed-upon treatment policy, (6) accepted treatment, (7) facilities for diagnosis and treatment, (8) cost-effective case-finding, (9) continuing process, (10) overall economic balance.

**How AI satisfies three of them:**
- *Criterion 3 (Suitable test)* — The AI model itself is the test, processing medical images at scale.
- *Criterion 8 (Cost-effective case-finding)* — AI triage reduces radiologist workload per case detected.
- *Criterion 9 (Continuing process)* — Closed-loop outcome feedback enables continuous learning, so the test improves over time.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for criteria list, 1 for AI-augmentation justification.)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]
*Differentiate between screening and diagnosis in the context of medical imaging AI. Why is the operating point of a screening AI typically chosen at a higher sensitivity than that of a diagnostic AI? (3 marks)*

#### Model Answer (Examiner Key):

| Aspect | Screening | Diagnosis |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| Population | Asymptomatic, large cohort | Symptomatic, selected |
| Goal | Triage / detect early disease | Confirm or rule out a specific disease |
| Test certainty | Imperfect; needs confirmatory test | High pre-test probability; used for final decision |
| Cost per case | Must be low | Can be higher |
| AI role | Triage, second-reader, autonomous | Decision support, second opinion |

The screening operating point is set at **higher sensitivity** because the cost of *missing* a subclinical cancer (delayed treatment, mortality) is far higher than the cost of a *false positive* (which will be caught by confirmatory diagnostic testing). Diagnostic AI tolerates a more balanced sensitivity–specificity trade-off because the patient is already symptomatic and the confirmatory pathway is in motion.

*(3 marks: 2 for table-style differentiation, 1 for operating-point justification.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Instructions for Students (KTU 2024 ESE Standard Wording):**
> *"Answer either A OR B from the following Module 1 question. Each sub-part carries 7 marks. Total: 14 marks."*

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

#### **A(a).** [7 Marks]
*Describe the end-to-end pipeline of an AI-enabled population screening program for breast cancer using mammography. Your answer must include: (i) the four operational stages, (ii) the role of the AI at each stage, and (iii) the specific imaging AI tasks (detection, segmentation, classification) performed. (7 marks)*

#### Model Answer (Examiner Key):

**Stage 1 — Image Acquisition.** 2D full-field digital mammography (FFDM) or 3D digital breast tomosynthesis (DBT) is acquired from Hologic / Siemens / GE / Fujifilm units. DICOM metadata (laterality, view, kVp, mAs) is stored. The AI must handle multi-vendor data, which is achieved either by training on a multi-vendor cohort or by vendor-specific fine-tuning.

**Stage 2 — Pre-processing & QC.** Images are intensity-normalised (Z-score / percentile clipping), resampled to a common resolution (e.g., 1024×1024 or 224×224 patches), pectoral-muscle and skin-line are segmented to restrict analysis to the breast region. A *quality-control CNN* rejects images that are too blurry, underexposed, or have insufficient breast tissue visible.

**Stage 3 — AI Inference (three sub-tasks).**
- *Detection* (CADe): A region-proposal CNN (RetinaNet, YOLOv8, or transformer-based DETR) localises suspicious lesions — microcalcifications, masses, architectural distortions — with bounding boxes and confidence scores.
- *Segmentation* (CADx prep): A U-Net / nnU-Net produces pixel-level masks of the lesion, which are then fed to a classifier.
- *Classification* (CADx): A second CNN (ResNet, EfficientNet, or ViT) classifies the lesion as BI-RADS 2, 3, 4, 5 (and outputs a malignancy probability).

**Stage 4 — Triage / Decision.** The malignancy probability is thresholded to three buckets: low-risk (auto / delayed report), intermediate (standard radiologist read), high-risk (priority read + same-day recall). The system feeds the radiologist a *heat-map* overlay of suspicious regions, supporting interpretability.

**Closing the loop (Stage 5).** Outcomes (biopsy results, follow-up imaging, 1-year cancer registry matches) are captured and periodically used to retrain / fine-tune the model — satisfying Wilson & Jungner Criterion 9.

*(7 marks: 1 each for the four stages named, 1 for AI task mapping, 1 for clinical integration, 1 for outcome feedback.)*

---

#### **A(b).** [7 Marks]
*A national screening program plans to deploy a mammography AI with claimed sensitivity 95% and specificity 90%. The prevalence of breast cancer in the target cohort is 0.4%. Calculate the PPV. If the cohort size is 100,000 women, calculate the number of false positives the program will generate. Comment on the cost-effectiveness implications. (7 marks)*

#### Model Answer (Examiner Key):

**Step 1 — Compute PPV using the Bayes-form formula:**

$$
\text{PPV} \;=\; \frac{\text{Se} \cdot p}{\text{Se} \cdot p + (1 - \text{Sp})(1 - p)}
$$

$$
= \frac{0.95 \times 0.004}{0.95 \times 0.004 + (1 - 0.90) \times (1 - 0.004)}
$$

$$
= \frac{0.0038}{0.0038 + 0.10 \times 0.996}
$$

$$
= \frac{0.0038}{0.0038 + 0.0996}
$$

$$
= \frac{0.0038}{0.1034} \;\approx\; 0.0368 \;\approx\; 3.68\%
$$

*(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for final value.)*

**Step 2 — Total positives flagged and false positives in 100,000 women:**

- Total positives flagged $= \text{Se} \cdot p \cdot N + (1 - \text{Sp}) \cdot (1 - p) \cdot N$

$$
= 0.0038 \times 100{,}000 + 0.0996 \times 100{,}000
$$

$$
= 380 + 9{,}960
$$

$$
= 10{,}340 \text{ women flagged positive}
$$

- True positives $\approx 380$.
- **False positives $\approx 9{,}960$ women.**

*(1 mark each for the two values.)*

**Step 3 — Cost-effectiveness comment (3 marks):**

The PPV is only ~3.7%, meaning for every 100 women flagged by the AI as "positive," only 4 will have cancer; the remaining 96 will undergo unnecessary downstream workup (diagnostic mammography, ultrasound, biopsy, anxiety, lost work-days). At a national scale (1 million women, 10× this scenario), the program would generate ~100,000 false positives. Therefore, the program *must*:
1. Use the AI as a *second reader* or *triage* tool — not as an autonomous diagnostic — to leverage human adjudicators.
2. Run a confirmatory *diagnostic* workflow (multi-modality) for AI-flagged positives before biopsy.
3. Recalibrate the AI's operating point if false-positive burden becomes unsustainable.

*(3 marks for the three-point commentary, with full 7 marks awarded only if all three cost-mitigation steps are explicitly listed.)*

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **B(a).** [7 Marks]
*Explain, with a suitable block diagram, the role of AI in screening for **diabetic retinopathy (DR)** using retinal fundus photography. Compare the *autonomous*, *triage*, and *second-reader* deployment modes and identify which mode is currently permitted by the US FDA for DR. (7 marks)*

#### Model Answer (Examiner Key):

**Diabetic retinopathy** is a microvascular complication of diabetes mellitus and the leading cause of preventable blindness in working-age adults. Population-level DR screening uses **colour fundus photographs** captured by trained technicians or even non-specialist operators in primary-care settings.

**AI pipeline for DR screening:**
1. *Image acquisition:* 45° or 50° macula-centred fundus photo, captured by tabletop (Topcon, Canon CR-2) or handheld (iNview, Pictor) cameras.
2. *QC:* blur detection, vessel visibility, macula-in-frame check.
3. *Pre-processing:* colour-space normalisation (often green-channel extraction), optic-disc and macula localisation, image resizing to 512×512 or 224×224.
4. *AI inference:* a CNN (e.g., Inception-v3, EfficientNet, or a custom architecture) classifies the image into one of five ICDR severity levels: no DR, mild NPDR, moderate NPDR, severe NPDR, proliferative DR. Heat-maps (Grad-CAM) highlight microaneurysms, exudates, and neovascularisation.
5. *Decision:* output is mapped to a *refer* vs. *no-refer* binary action.

**Three deployment modes (with reference to Section 4.2 diagram):**

| Mode | Description | When Used |
|------|-------------|-----------|
| **Autonomous** | AI issues the report with no human in the loop. | Only when regulator has approved it. |
| **Triage** | AI prioritises flagged images; human reads all. | Most common in NHS, public health systems. |
| **Second-reader** | Human reads first; AI flags disagreements. | Conservative deployments. |

**FDA position:** The US FDA granted **De Novo authorisation (DEN180001, April 2018)** to **IDx-DR (Digital Diagnostics)** as the *first fully autonomous AI diagnostic system* authorised for any medical condition. It is permitted to issue a referral decision for DR *without* a clinician's interpretation, in primary-care settings. Subsequently, **EyeArt (Eyenuk)** received FDA clearance in 2020 for autonomous detection of *more-than-mild* DR.

*(7 marks: 2 for pipeline, 2 for the three-mode table, 1 for FDA reference, 1 for the autonomous mode identification, 1 for the system name.)*

---

#### **B(b).** [7 Marks]
*A team is validating a new chest X-ray (CXR) AI for **tuberculosis (TB) screening** in a field deployment. The CXR-AI has sensitivity 92% and specificity 88% on a held-out test set. The active-TB prevalence in the screened community is estimated at 1.5%. Compute the PPV, NPV, and the number of false positives per 1,000 screened individuals. Critically discuss why the **negative predictive value (NPV)** is the more clinically important metric for ruling out TB in a community-screening setting. (7 marks)*

#### Model Answer (Examiner Key):

**Step 1 — Compute PPV:**

$$
\text{PPV} \;=\; \frac{0.92 \times 0.015}{0.92 \times 0.015 + (1 - 0.88) \times (1 - 0.015)}
$$

$$
= \frac{0.0138}{0.0138 + 0.12 \times 0.985}
$$

$$
= \frac{0.0138}{0.0138 + 0.1182}
$$

$$
= \frac{0.0138}{0.1320} \;\approx\; 0.1045 \;\approx\; 10.5\%
$$

*(1 mark.)*

**Step 2 — Compute NPV:**

$$
\text{NPV} \;=\; \frac{\text{Sp} \cdot (1-p)}{\text{Sp} \cdot (1-p) + (1 - \text{Se}) \cdot p}
$$

$$
= \frac{0.88 \times 0.985}{0.88 \times 0.985 + (1 - 0.92) \times 0.015}
$$

$$
= \frac{0.8668}{0.8668 + 0.0012}
$$

$$
= \frac{0.8668}{0.8680} \;\approx\; 0.9986 \;\approx\; 99.86\%
$$

*(1 mark.)*

**Step 3 — False positives per 1,000:**

- $FP = (1 - \text{Sp}) \times (1 - p) \times 1000 = 0.12 \times 0.985 \times 1000 \approx 118$ false positives.
- For comparison, true positives $= \text{Se} \times p \times 1000 = 0.92 \times 0.015 \times 1000 \approx 14$ true positives.

*(1 mark.)*

**Step 4 — Clinical discussion (4 marks):**

In community-screening settings, the **negative predictive value (NPV) ≈ 99.86%** is the more clinically important metric for *ruling out* TB because:

1. **High NPV permits safe discharge from screening** — a CXR-AI-negative individual can, with high confidence, be considered not to have active TB at that moment. This dramatically reduces the burden of confirmatory sputum GeneXpert / culture testing, which is expensive and lab-bound in low-resource settings.
2. **PPV is intentionally low (10.5%)** because prevalence in the community is low. The PPV is not a *failure* of the AI; it is a *property* of the low-prevalence screening cohort. The confirmatory sputum test is what filters the false positives.
3. **Operational efficiency** — when a screening tool is used to *triage* (rule out) rather than to *rule in*, NPV is the correct governing metric. This is a textbook example of *rule-out* screening design.
4. **Equity and access** — in high-TB-burden, low-radiologist settings (e.g., rural Kerala, sub-Saharan Africa, South-East Asia), the CXR-AI's high NPV lets community health workers confidently send symptomatic CXR-negative patients home, conserving scarce radiologist and laboratory resources for the AI-positive cohort.

*(1 mark each for the four points. 7 marks total.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on "Programs for Screening" Questions**

1. **Conflating screening and diagnosis.** A screening program is for **asymptomatic** populations; the operating point and metrics differ. Examiners explicitly *deduct 1 mark* when students use diagnostic-AI language in a screening question.
2. **Forgetting Wilson & Jungner.** Any 7-mark question that mentions a screening program *must* cite the ten WHO criteria. Failing to do so costs at least 1–2 marks.
3. **Ignoring prevalence.** A student who computes sensitivity/specificity without relating to prevalence in the PPV derivation loses the *integration* mark. The Bayes-form of PPV **must** explicitly contain $p$.
4. **Not labelling FROC vs. ROC.** Detection ≠ classification. For lesion-localisation AI (lung nodules, mammographic masses, TB cavities), the correct performance curve is **FROC**, not ROC.
5. **Omitting equity / bias.** A 14-mark answer in the 2024 scheme *should* include a sentence on subgroup performance disparities, per KTU's outcome-based CO mapping (Ethics & Equity is now an examinable graduate attribute).
6. **Failing to name the autonomous AI.** A surprising number of students describe autonomous AI for DR without naming **IDx-DR** (or EyeArt). Examiners award 1 mark specifically for the *named system and its FDA De Novo reference.*
7. **Confusing sensitivity and PPV.** Sensitivity is intrinsic to the model; PPV depends on the cohort. Examiners expect students to *state this distinction explicitly*.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Screening** targets **asymptomatic** populations; **diagnosis** targets symptomatic patients.
- The **Wilson & Jungner (WHO)** ten criteria govern every screening program — know all ten.
- The AI-screening pipeline has **five canonical stages**: *Acquisition → QC → Inference → Triage → Outcome Capture*.
- **Sensitivity is prioritised** in screening; **specificity is sacrificed** within tolerable limits to keep the program viable.
- **PPV** $\text{PPV} = \frac{\text{Se}\cdot p}{\text{Se}\cdot p + (1-\text{Sp})(1-p)}$ — the Bayes-form is the *single most-tested formula* in this module.
- **NPV** is the metric of choice for *rule-out* screening designs (e.g., TB CXR-AI).
- **FROC** is the curve-of-record for *lesion-detection* screening AI; **ROC** is for whole-image classification.
- Three AI deployment modes in screening: **Triage / Second-reader / Autonomous** — only one autonomous system is FDA-cleared for DR (**IDx-DR, 2018**).
- A Wilson-score 95% CI of ±5% at 90% sensitivity needs **~50 disease-positive** cases for regulatory-grade validation.
- **Equity** subgroup sensitivity disparity $\Delta_{\text{fair}}$ is a mandatory 2024-scheme graduate-attribute checkpoint.
- Cost-effectiveness is justified when *marginal sensitivity gain × clinical weight of true positive* exceeds *marginal FP cost*.
- Outcome feedback and **continuous learning** satisfy Wilson & Jungner Criterion 9 — the program is never "one-shot."
- **Real-world landmark systems to remember:** IDx-DR (autonomous DR), Lunit INSIGHT MMG (mammography), Qure.ai qXR (CXR-TB), Aidoc (CT triage), Volpara (mammography density), Google LYNA (lymph-node metastasis on pathology — research).
- **KTU Board Tip:** Always end a 14-mark screening answer with a one-line *cost-effectiveness / equity* sentence — it converts a "good" answer into a "distinction" answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Businesses in the Field of Medical Science
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT862 - AI FOR MEDICAL IMAGE ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Businesses in the Field of Medical Science

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Medical Science Business Ecosystem** refers to the integrated network of commercial enterprises, service providers, regulatory bodies, and technology innovators that develop, deliver, and monetize products and services aimed at preserving health, diagnosing disease, treating illness, and monitoring patient outcomes. In the context of **AI for Medical Image Analysis**, this ecosystem specifically encompasses organizations that leverage artificial intelligence, deep learning, and computer vision techniques to interpret radiological, pathological, ophthalmological, and other biomedical imaging data for clinical decision support.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The medical imaging AI market is a sub-segment of the broader **HealthTech / MedTech industry**, characterized by stringent regulatory oversight (FDA, CE, CDSCO), high capital intensity, and long product development cycles.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the medical science business ecosystem as a **multi-layered hospital supply chain with an attached innovation lab**:

- **Layer 1 (Foundation) — The Pharmacies and Device Stores**: These are traditional businesses that sell pills, stethoscopes, and syringes. They are predictable, low-margin, and operate on high volume.
- **Layer 2 (Middle) — The Equipment Manufacturers**: Companies like Siemens Healthineers, GE Healthcare, and Philips that build MRI, CT, and X-ray machines. Their business revolves around hardware sales, service contracts, and consumables.
- **Layer 3 (Top) — The AI Software Companies**: Startups and big-tech entrants (e.g., Aidoc, Zebra Medical, Google Health, Qure.ai) that build software which *reads the images* produced by Layer 2 machines. Their business model is often subscription-based, pay-per-scan, or licensed.
- **Layer 4 (Connective Tissue) — The Hospitals, Insurers, and Regulators**: The buyers, payers, and gatekeepers that determine whether the AI products are purchased, reimbursed, and approved.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** When AI is introduced into a hospital workflow, it is not a single "sale" — it triggers a chain reaction affecting radiologists' workload, insurance reimbursement codes (CPT codes), and hospital P&L (Profit and Loss) statements.

### 1.3 Key Market Metrics (Standard Industry Benchmarks)

The following are universally cited industry metrics used in the medical science business literature:

- **Global Medical Imaging Market Size (2024):** Approximately **USD 45–50 billion**, growing at a **CAGR of ~5.8%**.
- **AI in Medical Imaging Sub-Market:** Approximately **USD 1.5–2.0 billion** in 2024, projected to reach **USD 10–15 billion by 2030** (CAGR ~30–35%).
- **Average Hospital PACS Storage Cost:** **USD 0.05–0.10 per study per year**.
- **FDA-Cleared AI/ML-Enabled Medical Devices (cumulative, as of 2024):** Over **950+ devices**, with the majority in radiology.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Businesses in Medical Science

The medical science industry can be decomposed into the following major business categories:

1. **Pharmaceutical / Biotechnology Companies**
   - Develop drugs, vaccines, and biologics.
   - Business model: Patent-protected blockbuster drugs, generics post-patent cliff.
   - Examples: Pfizer, Sun Pharma, Biocon.

2. **Medical Device Manufacturers**
   - Develop physical instruments (MRI, CT, ultrasound, pacemakers).
   - Business model: Hardware sales + recurring service/maintenance contracts.
   - Examples: Medtronic, Siemens Healthineers, GE HealthCare.

3. **In-Vitro Diagnostics (IVD) & Laboratory Companies**
   - Blood tests, genetic panels, biopsy assays.
   - Examples: Roche Diagnostics, Abbott Labs.

4. **Healthcare Information Technology (HealthIT) / Digital Health**
   - EHR systems, PACS, RIS, telemedicine platforms.
   - Business model: SaaS (Software as a Service), per-seat licensing.
   - Examples: Epic Systems, Cerner (Oracle Health), Practo.

5. **AI / Machine Learning Medical Imaging Companies**
   - Develop deep learning models for image interpretation.
   - Business model: Per-scan pricing, subscription, outcome-based pricing.
   - Examples: Aidoc, Qure.ai, Zebra Medical Vision, Google Health.

6. **Healthcare Service Providers (Hospitals, Clinics, Tele-radiology)**
   - End-users and sometimes in-house AI developers.
   - Examples: Apollo Hospitals, Mayo Clinic, Teleradiology Solutions (India).

7. **Payers and Insurers**
   - Reimburse clinical procedures; increasingly demand AI-augmented evidence.
   - Examples: UnitedHealth, Star Health.

8. **Regulatory & Accreditation Bodies**
   - FDA (US), CE / MDR (EU), CDSCO (India), ICMR (India), NABL.
   - Not strictly "businesses" but define the rules of market entry.

### 2.2 Business Model Frameworks

> [!NOTE]
> **Why study business models in an AI course?** Because the *commercialization strategy* of an AI imaging algorithm directly determines its **regulatory pathway** (Class I/II/III device), **clinical adoption curve**, and **revenue potential**.

#### Framework 1: The Value Chain (Porter, adapted for MedTech)

| Stage | Activity | Value Capture Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Upstream** | R&D, IP filing, data acquisition | Patent licensing, dataset sales |
| **Midstream** | Clinical validation, regulatory clearance | Regulatory exclusivity (e.g., FDA De Novo) |
| **Downstream** | Distribution, sales, post-market surveillance | Recurring revenue, service contracts |
| **End-of-chain** | Outcome measurement, real-world evidence | Performance-based contracts |

#### Framework 2: Revenue Models in AI Medical Imaging

| Revenue Model | Mechanism | Typical Pricing (2024) |
|---|---|---|
| **Per-Scan / Pay-Per-Use** | Hospital pays per AI inference call | **USD 1 – 10 per scan** |
| **Annual Subscription** | Flat fee for unlimited scans | **USD 20,000 – 200,000 per site/year** |
| **Capital Purchase + License** | One-time hardware/software purchase | **USD 50,000 – 500,000 upfront** |
| **Outcome-Based / Risk-Share** | Payment tied to diagnostic accuracy or cost savings | Variable, 5–15% of measured savings |
| **Freemium + Premium API** | Free basic tier, paid advanced algorithms | API call pricing |

#### Framework 3: Stakeholder Power Matrix

| Stakeholder | Primary Interest | Influence on AI Adoption |
|---|---|---|
| **Radiologist** | Workflow efficiency, reduced fatigue | **High** (gatekeeper) |
| **Hospital CFO** | ROI, reimbursement codes | **High** (budget controller) |
| **Patient** | Accurate diagnosis, lower cost | **Medium** (indirect) |
| **Payer / Insurer** | Reduced misdiagnosis, cost containment | **High** (reimbursement policy) |
| **Regulator** | Safety, efficacy, equity | **Very High** (market access) |
| **AI Vendor** | Revenue, market share | Direct seller |

### 2.3 Why This Matters to an AI Engineer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An AI model that achieves **99% accuracy on a benchmark dataset** is commercially worthless unless it satisfies three business conditions: (1) it integrates with hospital **PACS/DICOM** workflows, (2) it receives **regulatory clearance** for the indicated clinical use, and (3) it has a **reimbursement code** so hospitals can bill insurers for its use.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

> [!NOTE]
> Since this topic is **conceptual-business in nature** (rather than purely mathematical), the "derivations" below take the form of **structured analytical walkthroughs** that a KTU examiner expects for full marks: *identification → framework application → quantitative estimation → conclusion*.

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Market Sizing for an AI Lung Nodule Detection Product (KTU-style Analytical Problem)

**Problem Statement (style):**
*A Kerala-based HealthTech startup wants to estimate the addressable market for an AI product that detects lung nodules on chest CT scans in India. Given the following data, estimate the **Total Addressable Market (TAM)**, **Serviceable Addressable Market (SAM)**, and **Serviceable Obtainable Market (SOM)**.*

**Given Data:**
- Indian population: **1,400,000,000**
- Annual CT scan volume in India: **~10,000,000 scans/year**
- Proportion of chest CT scans: **30%**
- Hospitals with digital PACS (eligible for AI integration): **40%**
- Realistic market capture in Year 1: **5%**
- Price per AI inference: **USD 2.00 ≈ ₹165**

**Step 1 — Compute TAM (Total Addressable Market)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TAM}_{\text{scans}} &= \text{Total CT Scans} \times \text{Proportion Chest CT} \\
&= 10{,}000{,}000 \times 0.30 \\
&= 3{,}000{,}000 \text{ scans/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TAM}_{\text{currency}} &= 3{,}000{,}000 \times ₹165 \\
&= ₹495{,}00{,}00{,}000 \\
&= ₹495 \text{ Crore/year (approx. USD 60 million)}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* This is the universe if **every** chest CT in India could theoretically be processed by the AI.

**Step 2 — Compute SAM (Serviceable Addressable Market)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SAM}_{\text{scans}} &= \text{TAM}_{\text{scans}} \times \text{PACS-equipped proportion} \\
&= 3{,}000{,}000 \times 0.40 \\
&= 1{,}200{,}000 \text{ scans/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SAM}_{\text{currency}} &= 1{,}200{,}000 \times ₹165 \\
&= ₹198 \text{ Crore/year (approx. USD 24 million)}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* SAM is restricted to hospitals whose infrastructure (PACS) can actually host the AI inference.

**Step 3 — Compute SOM (Serviceable Obtainable Market)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SOM}_{\text{scans}} &= \text{SAM}_{\text{scans}} \times \text{Realistic Capture} \\
&= 1{,}200{,}000 \times 0.05 \\
&= 60{,}000 \text{ scans/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SOM}_{\text{currency}} &= 60{,}000 \times ₹165 \\
&= ₹99{,}00{,}000 \\
&= ₹0.99 \text{ Crore/year (approx. USD 1.2 million)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The startup's realistic Year-1 revenue opportunity is **~₹1 Crore (~USD 1.2 M)** — this figure justifies a small Series A funding round of USD 2–5 M for product development and sales infrastructure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion used:** 1 USD ≈ ₹83 (approximate 2024 average). For KTU exam purposes, you may use ₹80 or ₹85 as the standard rate; always state the assumed rate.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: ROI Calculation for a Hospital Deploying an AI Stroke Triage Tool

**Problem Statement:**
*A 500-bed tertiary hospital performs **4,000 head CT scans/year** for suspected stroke. The AI tool (cost: **₹15 lakh/year subscription**) reduces the time-to-treatment by **20 minutes**, enabling an additional **2% of patients** to receive thrombolysis within the golden window. Average reimbursement per thrombolysed patient: **₹75,000**. Should the hospital purchase the tool?*

**Step 1 — Calculate additional thrombolysis cases**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Additional Cases} &= 4{,}000 \times 0.02 \\
&= 80 \text{ patients/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate incremental revenue**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Incremental Revenue} &= 80 \times ₹75{,}000 \\
&= ₹60{,}00{,}000 \\
&= ₹60 \text{ Lakh/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate net benefit**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Benefit} &= \text{Incremental Revenue} - \text{AI Cost} \\
&= ₹60{,}00{,}000 - ₹15{,}00{,}000 \\
&= ₹45 \text{ Lakh/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute ROI**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROI} &= \frac{\text{Net Benefit}}{\text{AI Cost}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{45{,}00{,}000}{15{,}00{,}000} \times 100\% \\
&= 300\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The hospital achieves a **300% ROI in Year 1**, which is an overwhelming financial case. *Note: In real settings, additional costs (IT integration, training, downtime) reduce this to ~200–250%, but the business case remains positive.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This type of "**business case ROI calculation**" is the **single most common analytical question** asked in KTU ESE papers for HealthTech and AI-in-Medicine electives. Memorize the four-step structure: *Benefit Quantification → Revenue Translation → Net Benefit → ROI %.*

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Business Model vs. Regulatory Complexity

| Business Model | FDA / CDSCO Class | Validation Burden | Time to Market | Capital Required |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Triage / Worklist Prioritization** | Class II (510(k)) | Retrospective + Prospective | 12–18 months | **Medium** |
| **Diagnostic Assist (CADe/CADx)** | Class II (De Novo or 510(k)) | Multi-reader multi-case (MRMC) | 18–36 months | **High** |
| **Autonomous Diagnosis** | Class III (PMA) | RCT-equivalent evidence | 36–60+ months | **Very High** |
| **Workflow / Non-Diagnostic AI** | Class I (general wellness) | Lightweight | 6–12 months | **Low** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **CADe (Computer-Aided Detection)** with **CADx (Computer-Aided Diagnosis)**. CADe *flags* a region; CADx *characterizes* it (benign vs malignant). CADx faces a *much* higher regulatory bar.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Medical Science Business Ecosystem (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Regulators and Accreditation Bodies] --> B[Medical Device and Imaging Equipment Manufacturers]
    A --> C[AI Software Vendors]
    A --> D[Pharmaceutical and Biotech Companies]
    B --> E[Hospitals and Diagnostic Centers]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Patients and Clinicians]
    G[Payers and Insurers] --> E
    G --> C
    H[Academic and Research Institutions] --> C
    H --> B
    I[Data Providers and Annotators] --> C
    J[Investors and Venture Capital] --> C
    J --> D
    J --> B
    E --> K[Real World Evidence and Outcomes Data]
    K --> C
    K --> B
    K --> G
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- **A → B, C, D**: Regulators gatekeep *all* market entrants.
- **B, C, D → E**: All product categories converge at the **hospital** (the central buyer).
- **G → E and C**: Payers reimburse hospitals and increasingly reimburse AI vendors directly.
- **H → C**: Universities (e.g., AIIMS, MIT, IIT) supply algorithms and talent.
- **I → C**: Annotated data providers (e.g., Seg.AI, Scale Medical) feed the training pipeline.
- **J → C, D, B**: VC funding flows mainly into AI vendors and biotech.
- **K → C, B, G**: The outcomes loop — feedback from the hospital improves products and informs payer policy.

---

### 4.2 The AI Imaging Product Commercialization Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Problem Identification] --> P2[Data Acquisition and Annotation]
    P2 --> P3[Model Development and Validation]
    P3 --> P4[Regulatory Submission]
    P4 --> P5[Reimbursement Strategy]
    P5 --> P6[Hospital Sales and Integration]
    P6 --> P7[Post Market Surveillance]
    P7 --> P3
    P7 --> P4
```

**Key Takeaway:** The pipeline is **non-linear** — post-market data loops back into model retraining (P7 → P3) and sometimes triggers new regulatory submissions (P7 → P4) for expanded indications.

---

### 4.3 Stakeholder Value Flow (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Suppliers
        S1[Data and Annotation Vendors]
        S2[Cloud and Compute Providers]
        S3[Algorithm Research Labs]
    end
    subgraph Producers
        PR1[AI Imaging Startups]
        PR2[Big Tech Health Divisions]
        PR3[OEM Device Manufacturers with Embedded AI]
    end
    subgraph Distributors
        D1[Hospital Chains]
        D2[Teleradiology Networks]
        D3[Government Health Programs]
    end
    subgraph EndUsers
        U1[Radiologists and Clinicians]
        U2[Patients]
        U3[Payers]
    end
    S1 --> PR1
    S1 --> PR2
    S2 --> PR1
    S2 --> PR2
    S2 --> PR3
    S3 --> PR1
    S3 --> PR2
    PR1 --> D1
    PR1 --> D2
    PR1 --> D3
    PR2 --> D1
    PR2 --> D3
    PR3 --> D1
    D1 --> U1
    D1 --> U2
    D2 --> U1
    D3 --> U2
    U1 --> U3
    U2 --> U3
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, PEBRT862]**
*Define the term "Medical Imaging AI Market" and list any three major business models used by AI imaging startups.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Medical Imaging AI Market** refers to the commercial segment of the healthcare industry that develops, validates, and sells artificial intelligence algorithms — primarily based on deep learning — for the automated interpretation of medical images such as X-ray, CT, MRI, ultrasound, and pathology slides. (1 mark)

**Three major business models:** (1.5 marks — 0.5 each)

1. **Per-Scan / Pay-Per-Use Pricing** — Hospital is charged a small fee for every AI inference call (e.g., USD 1–10 per scan). Aligns vendor incentives with hospital volume.

2. **Annual Subscription (SaaS)** — Hospital pays a flat annual fee for unlimited use of the AI tool across all eligible imaging studies. Provides predictable revenue for the vendor.

3. **Capital Purchase + Software License** — One-time purchase of on-premise hardware and perpetual/perpetual-license software, with optional annual maintenance. Common in large public hospital procurements in India.

*Conclusion remark tying back to KTU context (0.5 mark):*
The choice of business model directly influences the **regulatory pathway** (e.g., subscription-based SaaS may require a different FDA submission than on-premise capital equipment) and the **total cost of ownership** for the hospital.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, PEBRT862]**
*Explain the difference between TAM, SAM, and SOM with one example each from the medical imaging domain.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Term | Definition | Example (Lung Nodule Detection AI) |
|---|---|---|
| **TAM — Total Addressable Market** | The total global/domestic revenue opportunity if the product captured **100%** of the relevant imaging volume. | All chest CT scans performed globally in a year. |
| **SAM — Serviceable Addressable Market** | The segment of TAM that the product can realistically reach given **regulatory approval and infrastructure constraints**. | Chest CT scans performed in PACS-equipped hospitals where the AI is approved. |
| **SOM — Serviceable Obtainable Market** | The share of SAM the company can realistically win in a given timeframe, considering competition and sales capacity. | The first-year customer base in India that the startup can onboard. |

*(1 mark per row of the table; 0.5 for the introductory sentence, 0.5 for the concluding insight that SAM ⊂ TAM and SOM ⊂ SAM.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module 1 — Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE follows the pattern: *Part B carries 14 marks, choice between two questions. Each question typically has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.* The model answers below are structured exactly to that pattern.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — PEBRT862 ESE Style Question]**

**(a)** Describe the major categories of businesses operating in the field of medical science. Provide at least two examples for each category. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain the **Porter Value Chain** as applied to a company that develops and sells an AI-based chest X-ray interpretation product. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Answer — Q.A (a)**

The major categories of businesses in medical science are:

**1. Pharmaceutical / Biotechnology Companies** (1 mark)
   - *Examples:* Pfizer (mRNA vaccines), Biocon (biosimilars), Sun Pharma.
   - *Core activity:* Drug discovery, clinical trials, FDA/EMA approvals, mass production.

**2. Medical Device Manufacturers** (1 mark)
   - *Examples:* Siemens Healthineers (MRI, CT), Medtronic (pacemakers), GE HealthCare.
   - *Core activity:* Hardware engineering, regulatory clearance (510(k) or PMA), installation and service.

**3. In-Vitro Diagnostics and Lab Services** (1 mark)
   - *Examples:* Roche Diagnostics, Abbott, Thyrocare.
   - *Core activity:* Reagent kits, lab automation, sample logistics.

**4. HealthIT / Digital Health Companies** (1 mark)
   - *Examples:* Epic Systems (EHR), Oracle Health, Practo, 1mg.
   - *Core activity:* Software platforms for clinical workflows, patient engagement, and data management.

**5. AI in Medical Imaging Companies** (1 mark)
   - *Examples:* Aidoc (Israel), Qure.ai (India), Zebra Medical Vision, Google Health.
   - *Core activity:* Deep learning model development, clinical validation, regulatory submission as **Software as a Medical Device (SaMD)**.

**6. Healthcare Service Providers** (1 mark)
   - *Examples:* Apollo Hospitals, Manipal Hospitals, Amrita Institute, Narayana Health.
   - *Core activity:* Patient care delivery; increasingly also in-house AI development.

**7. Payers and Insurers** (0.5 mark)
   - *Examples:* UnitedHealth, Star Health, ICICI Lombard, government schemes like Ayushman Bharat.
   - *Core activity:* Reimbursement, risk pooling, prior authorization.

**8. Regulators and Accreditation Bodies** (0.5 mark)
   - *Examples:* FDA (US), CE / MDR (EU), CDSCO and ICMR (India), NABL.
   - *Core activity:* Market authorization, post-market surveillance, quality standards.

*(Examiner's note: full 7 marks require a clear table-like structure with category + 2 examples + 1-line activity. Diagrams earn bonus.)*

---

#### **Model Answer — Q.A (b)**

**Porter's Value Chain Applied to an AI Chest X-Ray Product (e.g., Qure.ai's qXR)**

**1. Primary Activities:**

- **Inbound Logistics** (1 mark)
  *Data acquisition:* Curating large, diverse, multi-site chest X-ray datasets (e.g., CheXpert, MIMIC-CXR, NIH ChestX-ray14). Logistics of **DICOM ingestion, de-identification (per HIPAA / DPDP Act), and storage** in a secure cloud (AWS, Azure).

- **Operations** (1 mark)
  *Model training and validation:* Pre-processing (normalization, lung segmentation), CNN/ViT training on GPUs, internal validation, and **multi-reader multi-case (MRMC) clinical studies** to demonstrate non-inferiority vs radiologists.

- **Outbound Logistics** (1 mark)
  *Distribution:* Cloud-hosted inference API, on-premise server deployment for low-bandwidth sites, PACS integration via **DICOM SR/HL7** standards.

- **Marketing and Sales** (1 mark)
  *Customer acquisition:* Direct sales to hospital radiology departments, channel partners in tier-2/3 Indian cities, government tenders, and KOL (Key Opinion Leader) engagement with academic radiologists.

- **Service** (1 mark)
  *Post-market:* 24×7 technical support, performance monitoring dashboards, periodic model retraining using locally collected data (with IRB approval), adverse event reporting to CDSCO.

**2. Support Activities:**

- **Firm Infrastructure** (0.5 mark)
  Cross-functional teams — clinical, regulatory, ML engineering, sales — coordinated by a CEO/COO. ISO 13485 quality management system.

- **Human Resource Management** (0.5 mark)
  Hiring radiologists for annotation, regulatory affairs specialists, and AI engineers. Continuous training on FDA AI/ML Action Plan updates.

- **Technology Development** (0.5 mark)
  R&D investment in self-supervised learning, federated learning for privacy-preserving multi-hospital training, and explainability (Grad-CAM heatmaps for clinician trust).

- **Procurement** (0.5 mark)
  GPU compute contracts (AWS/GCP), annotation vendor contracts (e.g., iMerit, Scale AI), and clinical site contracts for validation studies.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
The value chain reveals that for an AI imaging company, **the most defensible source of competitive advantage is the intersection of proprietary data + clinical validation evidence + regulatory clearance** — not the algorithm alone, which is increasingly commoditized.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — PEBRT862 ESE Style Question]**

**(a)** Explain the key **stakeholders** in the medical imaging AI business and describe their primary interests and influence. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A Kerala-based hospital performs **5,000 head CT scans per year**. The hospital is considering an AI tool for **intracranial hemorrhage detection** that costs **₹10 lakh per year**. The AI tool is expected to reduce the average **door-to-report time by 30 minutes**, which the hospital estimates will enable an additional **3% of stroke patients to be thrombolysed**. The average reimbursement per thrombolysed patient is **₹80,000**. Compute the **Year-1 ROI** of this AI tool and recommend whether the hospital should purchase it. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Answer — Q.B (a)**

**Key Stakeholders in Medical Imaging AI:**

1. **Radiologists and Clinicians** (1.5 marks)
   - *Interest:* Reduced fatigue, faster turnaround, less time on routine cases, more time on complex cases; concern about **AI replacing them** and about **algorithmic bias**.
   - *Influence:* **High** — they are the primary end-users and gatekeepers of clinical adoption. If they reject the AI, the hospital will not renew the contract.

2. **Hospital Administration (CFO / CMIO)** (1.5 marks)
   - *Interest:* Capital efficiency, throughput improvement, competitive differentiation, integration with existing PACS/EHR.
   - *Influence:* **High** — they control the procurement budget and vendor selection.

3. **Patients** (1 mark)
   - *Interest:* Faster, more accurate diagnosis; lower out-of-pocket cost; data privacy.
   - *Influence:* **Medium** — indirectly powerful through patient-choice-driven hospital competition, and directly through consent for data use.

4. **Payers and Insurers (Including Government Schemes)** (1 mark)
   - *Interest:* Reduced misdiagnosis, fewer unnecessary biopsies, better population health outcomes, **new reimbursement CPT codes for AI-assisted reads**.
   - *Influence:* **High** — without reimbursement codes, hospitals cannot recover the cost of AI.

5. **Regulators (FDA, CE, CDSCO, ICMR)** (1 mark)
   - *Interest:* Patient safety, algorithmic fairness, post-market surveillance, transparency in performance claims.
   - *Influence:* **Very High** — they grant or deny market authorization.

6. **AI Vendors and Investors** (1 mark)
   - *Interest:* Revenue, scalability, intellectual property protection, exit (IPO or acquisition).
   - *Influence:* Direct seller, but constrained by regulators and payers.

---

#### **Model Answer — Q.B (b) — Numerical ROI Calculation**

**Given:**
- Annual head CT scans: **5,000**
- AI subscription cost: **₹10,00,000 / year**
- Additional thrombolysed patients: **3% of total stroke suspects**
- Reimbursement per thrombolysed patient: **₹80,000**

**Step 1 — Estimate additional thrombolysis cases (1.5 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Additional Cases} &= 5{,}000 \times 0.03 \\
&= 150 \text{ patients/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute incremental revenue (1.5 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Incremental Revenue} &= 150 \times ₹80{,}000 \\
&= ₹1{,}20{,}00{,}000 \\
&= ₹120 \text{ Lakh/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute net benefit (1.5 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Benefit} &= ₹120{,}00{,}000 - ₹10{,}00{,}000 \\
&= ₹110 \text{ Lakh/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute ROI (1.5 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROI} &= \frac{1{,}10{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} \times 100\% \\
&= 1{,}100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Recommendation (1 mark)**

> A Year-1 ROI of **1,100%** is exceptional. Even after accounting for realistic adjustments — IT integration cost (~₹2 L), staff training (~₹1 L), and a 50% discounting of projected benefits to reflect real-world variability — the adjusted ROI remains above **400%**. The hospital should **definitely purchase** the tool, subject to a positive clinical validation report and CDSCO/FDA clearance verification.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Writing "₹120" instead of "₹1.2 Crore" or "₹120 Lakh" loses the calculation trail.
> 2. **Forgetting the sign convention:** Net Benefit = Revenue − Cost. Students often subtract in the wrong order.
> 3. **Not stating assumptions:** Examiners award a 1-mark bonus for explicitly stating *assumed parameters* (e.g., "Assuming 3% applies to the total CT volume").
> 4. **No conclusion:** Always end with a *decision* (purchase / do not purchase / pilot first).
> 5. **Confusing ROI with Profit Margin:** ROI uses **cost as denominator**; margin uses **revenue**. Do not interchange.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for the KTU Exam — Module 1, "Businesses in the Field of Medical Science"**

- ✅ **Medical science businesses span 8 major categories:** Pharma, Devices, IVD, HealthIT, AI Imaging, Service Providers, Payers, Regulators.
- ✅ **AI imaging sub-market is ~USD 1.5–2 B in 2024**, growing at **~30% CAGR** — the fastest-growing health-tech segment.
- ✅ **Three core business models:** Per-Scan, Annual Subscription, Capital Purchase. Newer models: **outcome-based pricing** and **freemium API**.
- ✅ **The Value Chain** has 5 primary activities and 4 support activities. For AI companies, the defensible moat is *data + clinical evidence + regulatory clearance*, not the algorithm alone.
- ✅ **TAM > SAM > SOM** is a strict subset relationship. Always compute in the same currency. State conversion assumptions.
- ✅ **ROI formula** to memorize: `ROI = (Incremental Revenue − AI Cost) / AI Cost × 100%`.
- ✅ **FDA-cleared AI medical devices** exceed **950+ as of 2024**; majority are in radiology.
- ✅ **Key regulators to remember:** **FDA (US)**, **CE / MDR (EU)**, **CDSCO + ICMR (India)**, **NABL (India accreditation)**, **PMDA (Japan)**, **NMPA (China)**.
- ✅ **Regulatory classes:** Class I (low risk) → Class II (510(k) / De Novo) → Class III (PMA). AI imaging is usually **Class II**.
- ✅ **CADe vs CADx:** Detection (flagging) vs Diagnosis (characterization). Different regulatory burdens.
- ✅ **Standard medical imaging AI pricing benchmarks:** **USD 1–10 per scan** OR **USD 20 K–200 K per site per year**.
- ✅ **Major global players to know:** Siemens Healthineers, GE HealthCare, Philips, Aidoc, Qure.ai, Zebra Medical, Google Health, IBM Watson Health (legacy).
- ✅ **Indian players to know:** Qure.ai, Predible Health, Synapsica, DeepTek, 5C Network (teleradiology), Aindra (pathology AI).
- ✅ **Always end business case answers with a clear recommendation** — *purchase / don't purchase / pilot study recommended* — and a justification line.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip for PEBRT862:** Questions on this topic are almost always framed as *either* a **conceptual map question** (categories, stakeholders, value chain) *or* a **numerical market-sizing/ROI question**. Prepare both types. Diagrams fetch bonus marks in the KTU valuation scheme.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application of AI in Present Day
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT862 - AI FOR MEDICAL IMAGE ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Application of AI in Present Day

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **AI for Medical Image Analysis (PEBRT862)**, the *Application of AI in Present Day* refers to the deployment of machine learning (ML) and deep learning (DL) algorithms in contemporary clinical workflows to acquire, process, interpret, and report findings from medical imaging modalities such as X-ray, Computed Tomography (CT), Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI), Ultrasound, Mammography, and histopathology slides.

According to the KTU 2024 scheme outcome descriptors, present-day AI applications in medical imaging span the following functional categories:

1. **Computer-Aided Detection (CADe)** – Flagging suspicious regions (lesions, nodules, microcalcifications).
2. **Computer-Aided Diagnosis (CADx)** – Characterizing the detected region as benign/malignant.
3. **Image Segmentation** – Delineating anatomical structures or tumor boundaries.
4. **Image Reconstruction & Enhancement** – Reducing radiation dose, denoising MRI, super-resolution.
5. **Workflow Triage & Reporting Automation** – Prioritizing urgent cases in radiology worklists.
6. **Predictive Analytics & Prognosis** – Forecasting disease progression from longitudinal imaging.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** "AI in medical imaging" is officially classified under the broader umbrella of *Clinical Decision Support Systems (CDSS)* regulated by frameworks such as the **U.S. FDA Software-as-a-Medical-Device (SaMD)** and the **EU MDR (Medical Device Regulation)**. Indian CDSCO guidance also follows these principles.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a present-day AI medical imaging system as **a tireless, hyper-specialized digital resident doctor** who:

- Has read **millions of past patient scans** (training data) instead of a few thousand.
- Never gets tired, never gets distracted, and never has a bad day.
- Can spot a **3 mm lung nodule** hidden in 300 slices of a chest CT in under 5 seconds.
- Does *not* replace the radiologist — it acts like a **spell-checker for the radiologist**, drawing their attention to the most suspicious areas first.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Auto-Pilot Analogy):** Just as an aircraft's autopilot assists the pilot by handling routine flight adjustments while the human pilot makes critical take-off and landing decisions, AI in medical imaging handles the *routine screening and quantification* tasks, allowing the radiologist to focus on complex diagnostic decisions and patient communication.

---

## 1.3 Key Present-Day Modalities & AI Pairings

| Medical Modality | Common AI Task | Clinical Impact (2023–2025) |
|---|---|---|
| **Chest X-ray (CXR)** | Pneumonia / TB / COVID-19 detection | Triage in emergency rooms |
| **CT Scan (Chest)** | Lung nodule detection, pulmonary embolism | Lung cancer screening programs |
| **CT (Head)** | Stroke detection (large-vessel occlusion) | Time-critical alerts to neuro-radiology |
| **MRI (Brain)** | Tumor segmentation, MS lesion tracking | Quantitative follow-up |
| **Mammography** | Breast cancer detection | Adjunct to single/double reading |
| **Retinal Fundus** | Diabetic retinopathy grading | Mass screening in primary care |
| **Pathology (WSI)** | Cancer cell detection, mitosis counting | Digital pathology workflows |
| **Dermatology** | Skin lesion classification (melanoma) | Smartphone-based preliminary screening |

---

## 1.4 Foundational Building Blocks (Vocabulary Lock)

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus-Locked Vocabulary (must memorize for KTU):**
> - **CNN (Convolutional Neural Network):** The dominant neural network architecture for image data.
> - **Transfer Learning:** Reusing a model pre-trained on ImageNet and fine-tuning it on medical data.
> - **U-Net:** The gold-standard encoder–decoder architecture for biomedical image segmentation.
> - **GAN (Generative Adversarial Network):** Used for image synthesis, denoising, and modality translation (e.g., MRI → CT).
> - **Transformer / Vision Transformer (ViT):** Newer attention-based architectures increasingly applied to 3D medical volumes.
> - **Federated Learning:** Training across hospitals *without* moving patient data (preserves privacy).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Pipeline of a Present-Day AI Medical Imaging System

A deployed AI system in a hospital follows a **5-stage pipeline**. Understanding each stage is critical for KTU module-end questions.

### Stage 1 — Data Acquisition
- Imaging devices (CT, MRI, X-ray) generate **DICOM (Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine)** files.
- DICOM contains: pixel data + patient metadata (anonymized via tags).
- Anonymization is mandatory under **HIPAA** / **DISHA (India, 2018)** / **DPDP Act 2023**.

### Stage 2 — Pre-processing
- **Normalization:** Scaling pixel intensities to a standard range (e.g., 0 to 1 or −1 to +1).
- **Resampling:** Standardizing voxel spacing across scanners of different brands.
- **Bias-field correction:** Especially for MRI.
- **Augmentation:** Random rotations, flips, elastic deformations — to artificially expand the dataset and prevent overfitting.

### Stage 3 — Model Inference
- The pre-trained deep learning model (e.g., a U-Net) processes the input volume.
- Produces one of:
  - A **class label** (classification).
  - A **bounding box** (detection).
  - A **pixel-wise mask** (segmentation).
  - A **heatmap / saliency map** (explainability).

### Stage 4 — Post-processing
- Thresholding probability maps.
- Connected-component analysis to merge fragmented predictions.
- Computing volumes, diameters, and structured report values.

### Stage 5 — Clinical Integration & Reporting
- Outputs are pushed into **PACS (Picture Archiving and Communication System)**.
- Radiologist sees AI overlays next to original images.
- A **structured report** is auto-generated and edited by the radiologist.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Most KTU 2024 questions on this topic will ask you to *list the stages* and *explain one stage in detail*. Memorize the 5-stage pipeline above.

---

## 2.2 Real-World Deployment Examples (Present Day)

| Product / System | Vendor | FDA Status | Clinical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Aidoc** | Aidoc Medical | Cleared | CT brain bleed triage |
| **Viz.ai LVO** | Viz.ai | Cleared | Stroke large-vessel-occlusion alert |
| **Paige.AI** | Paige | Cleared | Prostate cancer pathology |
| **IDx-DR** | Digital Diagnostics | Cleared (autonomous) | Diabetic retinopathy |
| **Arterys** | Arterys | Cleared | Cardiac MRI segmentation |
| **Qure.ai qXR** | Qure.ai | CE / CDSCO | Chest X-ray triage |
| **Niramai Thermalytix** | Niramai (India) | CE | Breast cancer screening |
| **Google LYNA** | Google Health | Research | Lymph node metastasis in pathology |

> [!NOTE]
> The world's first **fully-autonomous AI diagnostic** (no radiologist required for the result) approved by FDA is **IDx-DR** for diabetic retinopathy — a landmark in 2018 still referenced in KTU case-study questions.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

These are the **must-know metrics** for any AI medical imaging question paper.

| Metric | Formula | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | $\text{Acc} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Overall correctness | Ratio (0 to 1) |
| **Sensitivity (Recall / TPR)** | $\text{Sens} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Ability to find all sick patients | Ratio (0 to 1) |
| **Specificity (TNR)** | $\text{Spec} = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | Ability to avoid false alarms | Ratio (0 to 1) |
| **Precision (PPV)** | $\text{Prec} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Trustworthiness of a positive call | Ratio (0 to 1) |
| **F1-Score** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\text{Prec} \cdot \text{Sens}}{\text{Prec} + \text{Sens}}$ | Harmonic mean of P & R | Ratio (0 to 1) |
| **Dice Coefficient (DSC)** | $\text{DSC} = \dfrac{2 \cdot \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$ | Overlap between prediction and truth | Ratio (0 to 1) |
| **Intersection over Union (IoU / Jaccard)** | $\text{IoU} = \dfrac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | Tighter overlap metric | Ratio (0 to 1) |
| **AUC-ROC** | Area under (TPR vs FPR) curve | Threshold-independent discrimination | Ratio (0 to 1) |

> [!WARNING]
> In markdown tables above, the absolute-value / set-cardinality notation is written using `\vert` and `\mid` (not the literal `|` character) to prevent the markdown table parser from breaking. **Use `\vert` in your exam answers too** if you write LaTeX inside tables.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of These Formulas

- **Sensitivity vs Specificity trade-off:** In cancer screening, missing a cancer is worse than a false alarm → **high sensitivity** is mandatory. In surgical planning, a false positive could lead to unnecessary resection → **high specificity** is preferred.
- **Dice vs IoU:** Dice is more lenient (numerator has a factor of 2). KTU questions often ask: *given Dice = 0.8, find IoU* — this requires the algebraic identity below in Section 3.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Dice Score to IoU (Identity Used in KTU Numericals)

**Statement to prove:**

$$\text{IoU} = \dfrac{\text{DSC}}{2 - \text{DSC}}$$

**Step-by-step derivation:**

Let $A$ = predicted segmentation set, $B$ = ground-truth segmentation set.
Let $x = \vert A \vert$, $y = \vert B \vert$, $z = \vert A \cap B \vert$.

Step 1: Write the definitions explicitly.

$$\text{DSC} = \frac{2z}{x + y}$$

$$\text{IoU} = \frac{z}{x + y - z}$$

Step 2: From the IoU denominator, factor out $(x + y)$.

$$\text{IoU} = \frac{z}{(x + y)\left(1 - \frac{z}{x + y}\right)}$$

Step 3: Rearrange the Dice expression to isolate $\dfrac{z}{x + y}$.

$$\frac{z}{x + y} = \frac{\text{DSC}}{2}$$

Step 4: Substitute Step 3 into Step 2.

$$\text{IoU} = \frac{\frac{\text{DSC}}{2}}{1 - \frac{\text{DSC}}{2}}$$

Step 5: Multiply numerator and denominator by 2.

$$\text{IoU} = \frac{\text{DSC}}{2 - \text{DSC}}$$

**Hence proved.** $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> This identity is a **favourite KTU Part-B sub-question**. Memorize the derivation, not just the formula.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example (Confusion Matrix Computation)

A hospital deploys an AI system on **1000 chest X-rays**. The AI flags cases suspicious for tuberculosis. The ground truth is established later by sputum culture. The results are tabulated as:

|  | TB Positive (Truth) | TB Negative (Truth) |
|---|---|---|
| **AI Positive** | 180 | 30 |
| **AI Negative** | 20 | 770 |

**Step 1: Identify the four cardinalities.**

- $TP = 180$
- $FP = 30$
- $FN = 20$
- $TN = 770$

**Step 2: Compute Accuracy.**

$$\text{Acc} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{180 + 770}{1000} = \frac{950}{1000} = 0.950$$

**Step 3: Compute Sensitivity.**

$$\text{Sens} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{180}{180 + 20} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.900$$

**Step 4: Compute Specificity.**

$$\text{Spec} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{770}{770 + 30} = \frac{770}{800} = 0.9625$$

**Step 5: Compute Precision.**

$$\text{Prec} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{180}{180 + 30} = \frac{180}{210} \approx 0.857$$

**Step 6: Compute F1-Score.**

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot \text{Prec} \cdot \text{Sens}}{\text{Prec} + \text{Sens}} = \frac{2 \times 0.857 \times 0.900}{0.857 + 0.900} = \frac{1.5426}{1.757} \approx 0.878$$

**Step 7: Clinical interpretation.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - Sensitivity of **0.90** means the AI catches 9 out of 10 true TB cases — clinically acceptable for screening.
> - Specificity of **0.9625** means false alarms are rare — important for not overloading the radiologist.
> - Accuracy of **0.95** looks excellent, but is misleading because the dataset is *class-imbalanced* (only 200 positives out of 1000). KTU questions often highlight this pitfall.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Metric

| Clinical Scenario | Preferred Metric | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Cancer screening** (must not miss) | Sensitivity / Recall | Missing a cancer is worse than a false alarm |
| **Confirmatory diagnostic test** | Specificity / PPV | We need to be sure before invasive treatment |
| **Class-imbalanced dataset** (rare disease) | F1-Score / AUC-ROC | Accuracy is misleading |
| **Image segmentation (tumor volume)** | Dice / IoU | Pixel-level overlap matters |
| **Triage in ER** | Time-to-alert + Sensitivity | Speed + sensitivity dominate |

---

## 3.4 Sensitivity–Specificity Trade-off (Mathematical Intuition)

The threshold $\tau$ of the AI's output probability controls this trade-off.

- If we set $\tau = 0.3$ (low threshold) → more cases flagged positive → Sensitivity ↑, Specificity ↓.
- If we set $\tau = 0.8$ (high threshold) → only highly confident cases flagged → Sensitivity ↓, Specificity ↑.

The **ROC curve** plots TPR (Sensitivity) against FPR (1 − Specificity) as $\tau$ varies from 0 to 1. The **AUC** summarizes this curve into a single number between 0.5 (random) and 1.0 (perfect).

$$\text{FPR} = 1 - \text{Specificity} = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: End-to-End AI Medical Imaging Pipeline (Present Day)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hospital Scanner] --> B[DICOM Image Generated]
    B --> C[Anonymization Engine]
    C --> D[Preprocessing: Normalize, Resample, Augment]

    F --> G1[Classification Label]
    F --> G2[Bounding Box]
    F --> G3[Segmentation Mask]
    F --> G4[Heatmap Saliency]
    G1 --> H[Post-processing and Quantification]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    H --> I[PACS Integration]
    I --> J[Radiologist Review and Final Report]
    J --> K[Structured Report to EMR]
    K --> L[Patient Management]
```
