## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**

**Q1.** *Define the term "Clinical Decision Support System (CDSS)". Differentiate between a knowledge-based and a non-knowledge-based CDSS in 3 lines each.*

**Model Answer:**
A Clinical Decision Support System is a computer-based tool that analyses patient-specific data to provide clinicians with intelligently filtered, evidence-based recommendations at the point of care.
- **Knowledge-based CDSS:** Uses a handcrafted rules engine (e.g., "IF potassium > 6.0 mmol/L THEN alert for hyperkalemia"). It is interpretable but brittle.
- **Non-knowledge-based CDSS:** Uses ML/DL models trained on historical patient outcomes to surface patterns that are difficult to encode as rules. Higher accuracy, lower interpretability.

> *(3 marks — examiners expect crisp, 3-line contrast as above.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q2.** *What is the Dice–Sørensen coefficient? Why is the $\epsilon$ (epsilon) term added to the Dice loss formula in deep learning implementations?*

**Model Answer:**
The Dice–Sørensen Coefficient (DSC) measures the spatial overlap between a predicted segmentation mask $B$ and the ground truth $A$, defined as $\text{DSC} = \frac{2 \cdot \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$.
- The $\epsilon$ term (typically $10^{-6}$) is added to both numerator and denominator to prevent division by zero when both $A$ and $B$ are empty (e.g., the model predicts no lesion and there is no lesion).
- It also numerically stabilizes gradients at the start of training when the prediction mask is entirely zero.

> *(3 marks — the $\epsilon$ reasoning is what fetches the third mark.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 2 — Internal Choice Pattern]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option A**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the end-to-end pipeline for deploying an AI-based Computer-Aided Detection (CAD) system for **lung nodule detection on chest CT**. Cover data acquisition, preprocessing, model architecture, training strategy, and evaluation metrics with thresholds.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A deep-learning CAD model is trained on 10,000 CT scans from Hospital A and tested on 1,000 scans from Hospital B (a different geographic region). The confusion matrix on the external test set is:

|  | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | 180 (TP) | 20 (FN) |
| **Actual Negative** | 70 (FP) | 730 (TN) |

Calculate (i) Sensitivity, (ii) Specificity, (iii) Precision, (iv) F1-score, (v) Cohen's $\kappa$ (with $p_o$ and $p_e$ shown), and (vi) interpret what the drop in performance from internal to external testing likely indicates.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

1. **Data Acquisition:** Collect DICOM chest CT volumes from Hospital A PACS, ensuring IRB approval and patient consent. Stratify by nodule size (≥ 3 mm, 6 mm, 8 mm), nodule type (solid, sub-solid, ground-glass), and patient demographics.

2. **Preprocessing:** Resample all volumes to isotropic 1 mm³ voxel spacing using trilinear interpolation. Apply HU-windowing to $[-1000, 400]$ Hounsfield Units. Normalize intensities via Z-score. Generate 2D axial slices or 3D patches of size $64 \times 64 \times 64$.

3. **Model Architecture:** Use a 3D U-Net with an encoder–decoder structure and skip connections, followed by a region-proposal network (3D RetinaNet) for nodule detection. Transfer learning from a model pre-trained on LUNA16 is recommended.

4. **Training Strategy:** Five-fold patient-level cross-validation (no patient leakage). Use a combined Dice + Focal Loss to handle the extreme class imbalance (≈ 1 nodule per 400 candidate patches). Adam optimizer, $\eta = 10^{-4}$, $\beta_1 = 0.9$, $\beta_2 = 0.999$. Train for 100 epochs with early stopping on validation Dice.

5. **Evaluation Metrics & Thresholds:**
   - **Free-Response ROC (FROC):** Target sensitivity $\geq 0.90$ at $\leq 1$ false positive per scan.
   - **CPM (Competition Performance Metric):** Average sensitivity at 7 predefined FP rates: $\{0.125, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8\}$.
   - **Dice Score (if segmentation included):** $\geq 0.75$ for nodule region.
   - **Clinical Acceptance Gate:** External AUC $\geq 0.90$ and inter-rater $\kappa \geq 0.75$ versus a thoracic radiologist.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**(i) Sensitivity:**
$$\text{TPR} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{180}{180 + 20} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90$$

**(ii) Specificity:**
$$\text{TNR} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{730}{730 + 70} = \frac{730}{800} = 0.9125$$

**(iii) Precision:**
$$\text{PPV} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{180}{180 + 70} = \frac{180}{250} = 0.72$$

**(iv) F1-Score:**
$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot 0.72 \cdot 0.90}{0.72 + 0.90} = \frac{1.296}{1.62} = 0.80$$

**(v) Cohen's Kappa:**

Observed agreement $p_o = \frac{TP + TN}{N} = \frac{180 + 730}{1000} = 0.91$.

Expected agreement by chance:
$$p_e = \frac{(TP+FP)(TP+FN) + (FN+TN)(FP+TN)}{N^2}$$
$$p_e = \frac{(250)(200) + (90)(800)}{1000^2} = \frac{50000 + 72000}{1000000} = 0.122$$

Cohen's $\kappa$:
$$\kappa = \frac{p_o - p_e}{1 - p_e} = \frac{0.91 - 0.122}{1 - 0.122} = \frac{0.788}{0.878} = 0.897$$

**(vi) Interpretation:** A high external $\kappa = 0.897$ and sensitivity of **0.90** indicate *almost-perfect* generalization. However, the lower precision (**0.72**) suggests the model generates more false positives on the external cohort — likely due to **scanner-vendor domain shift**, different slice thickness protocols, or population-level prevalence differences. This is precisely why KTU and the FDA mandate **multi-site external validation** before clinical deployment.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option B**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Describe, with a labelled block diagram, the architecture of a **U-Net** for medical image segmentation. Explain the role of skip connections and the difference between semantic and instance segmentation in medical imaging.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* For a brain tumour segmentation task, a model achieves an **IoU of 0.70** on the test set. Compute the equivalent Dice score, and explain why Dice is generally preferred over IoU as a loss function for medical segmentation. Comment on what an **IoU of 0.70** implies clinically for a 5 mm peri-tumoural oedema region.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**U-Net Architecture (Description):**

The U-Net is a symmetric encoder–decoder convolutional network introduced by Ronneberger et al. (2015). It has two paths:

- **Contracting (Encoder) Path:** Four blocks of $3 \times 3$ convolutions → ReLU → $2 \times 2$ max-pooling. Channel depth doubles at each block (64 → 128 → 256 → 512).
- **Bottleneck:** Two $3 \times 3$ convolutions with 1024 filters.
- **Expanding (Decoder) Path:** Four blocks of $2 \times 2$ transposed convolutions (up-convolutions), each halving the channel count, followed by concatenation with the corresponding encoder feature map via a **skip connection**, then two $3 \times 3$ convolutions.
- **Final Layer:** $1 \times 1$ convolution mapping to the desired number of output classes (e.g., 4 for brain tumour: background, core, enhancing, whole).

**Role of Skip Connections:** They pass high-resolution spatial features from the encoder directly to the decoder, recovering fine-grained details (edges of the tumour) that would otherwise be lost during downsampling. This is *essential* in medical imaging where sub-millimetre boundary accuracy determines clinical decisions (e.g., radiotherapy planning).

**Semantic vs Instance Segmentation:**

- **Semantic Segmentation:** Classifies each pixel into a category (e.g., "tumour" vs "background"). Two touching tumours are merged into one mask.
- **Instance Segmentation:** Distinguishes individual object instances. Two adjacent tumours are given separate labels.
- In radiology, **semantic** segmentation is overwhelmingly preferred because the clinical question is *"how many cubic centimetres of tumour tissue exist?"* — not *"how many distinct tumours are there?"*

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Dice from IoU = 0.70:**

$$\text{DSC} = \frac{2 \cdot \text{IoU}}{1 + \text{IoU}} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.70}{1 + 0.70} = \frac{1.40}{1.70} \approx 0.824$$

So the equivalent Dice score is **≈ 0.824**.

**Why Dice is preferred over IoU as a loss function:**

1. **Gradient behaviour near zero overlap:** When prediction and ground truth have zero overlap, IoU loss is *constant* (gradient = 0), providing no learning signal. Dice loss still produces a meaningful gradient because of the $2 \, TP$ term in the numerator.
2. **Class-imbalance robustness:** In medical images, the lesion often occupies < 5% of pixels. IoU's denominator includes the large true-negative area, which **dilutes** the error signal. Dice normalizes by the *sum* of the two set sizes, giving the minority class balanced importance.
3. **Smoother loss landscape:** Empirically, Dice loss converges faster and to higher final accuracy on highly imbalanced tasks.

**Clinical Implication of IoU = 0.70 for a 5 mm oedema region:**

A 5 mm oedema rim has a small absolute area, so a 0.30 mismatch can correspond to a 1.5 mm **boundary error** in some directions. For radiotherapy planning, a 1.5 mm error is at the threshold of clinical acceptability (typically 2 mm tolerance). Thus, an IoU of 0.70 is **borderline-acceptable for screening** but should **not** be used for definitive radiotherapy contouring without radiologist review.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\epsilon$ term** in the Dice loss derivation costs full marks for part (b).
> 2. **Mixing up IoU and Dice formulas** is the single most common error. Remember: $\text{DSC} = \frac{2 \cdot \text{IoU}}{1 + \text{IoU}}$ — *write it down before substituting.*
> 3. **Skipping the external-validation interpretation** in Question A part (b) part (vi) is a guaranteed 2-mark loss. Examiners explicitly look for the "domain shift" insight.
> 4. **Not showing intermediate steps** for Cohen's $\kappa$ (writing $p_o$ and $p_e$ separately) costs at least 1 mark. Always expand the expected-agreement formula.
> 5. **Confusing semantic vs instance segmentation** — *write one crisp example of each*. Examiners reward clarity over verbosity.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Print-friendly rapid revision checklist (15 bullets):**

- ✦ AI in medical applications spans **six pillars**: Radiology, Pathology, Ophthalmology, Genomics, CDSS, and Drug Discovery.
- ✦ The end-to-end pipeline is **5 stages**: Acquisition → Preprocessing → Modeling → Validation → Deployment + Surveillance.
- ✦ **DICOM** is the universal medical imaging standard; **EHR** data is *structured + unstructured*.
- ✦ Always perform **patient-level splits** during cross-validation — never patch-level splits (leakage).
- ✦ **Sensitivity** (recall, TPR) is paramount in *screening*; **Specificity** (TNR) is paramount in *confirmation*.
- ✦ **F1-score** = harmonic mean of precision and recall; preferred over accuracy for imbalanced medical data.
- ✦ **Dice Coefficient** and **IoU (Jaccard)** are the two gold-standard segmentation metrics; they are algebraically related by $\text{DSC} = \frac{2 \, \text{IoU}}{1 + \text{IoU}}$.
- ✦ Always add an $\epsilon \approx 10^{-6}$ term in differentiable Dice loss to prevent division by zero.
- ✦ **U-Net** with skip connections is the workhorse architecture for medical image segmentation.
- ✦ **External validation** on a *geographically distinct* cohort is mandatory before clinical deployment (FDA, EU MDR).
- ✦ **Cohen's $\kappa \geq 0.81$** indicates "almost perfect" agreement with the ground-truth radiologist.
- ✦ AI is a **decision-support tool**, *not* a clinician replacement — frame every exam answer this way.
- ✦ Key regulatory pathways: **FDA 510(k)** (US), **CE-MDR** (EU), **CDSCO** (India) — know which one your AI system falls under.
- ✦ **Population Stability Index (PSI) > 0.25** signals dataset drift and mandates model retraining.
- ✦ Three classic Indian deployments worth remembering: Aravind–Google DR screening, AIIMS TB-AI, Tata Memorial OncoPath-AI.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evolution of Standards for AI in Medical Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT862 - AI FOR MEDICAL IMAGE ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Evolution of Standards for AI in Medical Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Standards for AI in Medical Applications** refer to the formally codified set of regulatory, technical, interoperability, ethical, and quality-assurance frameworks that govern the design, development, validation, deployment, and post-market surveillance of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML) systems used in clinical workflows, medical imaging, diagnostics, treatment planning, and healthcare decision support.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** Standards for AI in Medicine are **NOT** just technical benchmarks. They are a *layered ecosystem* comprising: (1) Regulatory Standards (FDA, EU MDR, IMDRF), (2) Interoperability Standards (DICOM, HL7/FHIR, IHE), (3) Quality Management Standards (ISO 13485, IEC 62304), (4) Risk Management Standards (ISO 14971), and (5) Emerging AI-Specific Governance Frameworks (EU AI Act, NIST AI RMF, GMLP).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Airline Safety Evolution"

Imagine a passenger aircraft from the **1950s** versus a modern Boeing 787. Both fly, but the modern one operates under an enormous, layered rule-book: design certification, pilot training standards, real-time air-traffic protocols, mandatory black-box recorders, and continuous airworthiness monitoring.

**The evolution is exactly the same for medical AI:**

| Era | Aircraft Analogy | Medical AI Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| 1950s (Pre-standards) | Solo bush pilots, no air-traffic control | Rule-based expert systems (MYCIN) — unvalidated, lab-only |
| 1980s (First certifications) | FAA formal certification, flight envelopes | FDA 510(k) pathway, first CAD approvals |
| 2010s (Standardization) | ICAO global standards, ETOPS | DICOM-SR, HL7 FHIR, IHE profiles |
| 2020s (Continuous oversight) | Real-time engine monitoring, predictive maintenance | FDA PCCP, GMLP, EU AI Act, post-market surveillance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** The exam often asks *"Why do we need standards in medical AI?"* The single best answer is: *Patient safety, clinical efficacy, interoperability across hospitals, bias mitigation, and legal liability all require a shared, verifiable, and enforceable rule-set.*

## 1.3 The Five-Pillar Conceptual Model

For exam-ready clarity, every medical-AI standard belongs to one of these **five pillars**:

1. **Regulatory Pillar** — FDA (USA), EU MDR/IVDR (Europe), CDSCO (India), PMDA (Japan), NMPA (China).
2. **Interoperability Pillar** — DICOM, HL7 v2/v3, FHIR, IHE Radiology/Pathology domains.
3. **Quality Engineering Pillar** — ISO 13485 (QMS), IEC 62304 (SDLC), ISO 14971 (Risk).
4. **Data & Algorithm Pillar** — DICOM-SR for structured reporting, annotation standards (DICOM Segmentation, MedSAM datasets), FDA GMLP principles.
5. **AI-Specific Governance Pillar** — EU AI Act (2024), NIST AI RMF (2023), WHO Ethics & Governance of AI for Health (2021), IMDRF AIML SaMD guidance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" — The Unmet Need that Drove Standards:**
> Between 2016 and 2024, the FDA cleared **>950** AI/ML-enabled medical devices. By 2019, it became clear that the *static* regulatory model (one-time approval, fixed algorithm) was incompatible with *adaptive* AI that learns continuously. This **regulatory gap** is the single biggest historical driver of modern standards evolution.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Historical Evolution — Timeline of Milestones

Below is the exam-critical chronology, broken into **four distinct generations**.

### Generation 1 — Knowledge-Based Expert Systems (1970s–1990s)

- **MYCIN (1976, Stanford)** — Rule-based antibiotic recommendation engine. Contained ~600 IF-THEN rules. Famous finding: it performed *better* than human infectious-disease experts but was **never clinically deployed** because it could not integrate with hospital workflows.
- **INTERNIST-I / QMR (1980s)** — Diagnostic decision support for internal medicine.
- **CASNET (1970s)** — Glaucoma diagnosis model using causal-association networks.
- **DXplain (1986, Massachusetts General Hospital)** — Still maintained as a teaching tool.
- **Standard Vacuum:** No regulatory body recognized software as a "device" yet.

### Generation 2 — Classical Machine Learning & Early CAD (1990s–2012)

- **Computer-Aided Detection (CAD) in mammography** — R2 ImageChecker, the first FDA-cleared CAD system (1998).
- **Classical ML** — Support Vector Machines, Random Forests, k-NN applied to hand-crafted radiomics features.
- **First Interoperability Standard:** DICOM (NEMA, 1985; DICOM 3.0, 1993) enabled cross-vendor image exchange — the **bedrock** of modern medical-AI pipelines.
- **HL7 v2 (1987)** — Hospital messaging standard for clinical/administrative data.
- **First formal regulatory acknowledgment:** FDA 21 CFR Part 820 (Quality System Regulation, 1996).

### Generation 3 — Deep Learning Revolution (2012–2019)

- **2012** — AlexNet wins ImageNet. Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) become state-of-the-art.
- **2015–2017** — U-Net (Ronneberger), ResNet, DenseNet architectures applied to CT/MRI segmentation.
- **2016** — **Arterys Cardio DL** becomes the first FDA-cleared *cloud-based* deep-learning diagnostic product.
- **2017–2018** — IDx-DR (first **autonomous AI diagnosis**, diabetic retinopathy, FDA De Novo).
- **2018** — FDA re-issues *Software as a Medical Device (SaMD)* definition jointly with IMDRF.
- **2019** — FDA proposes *Proposed Regulatory Framework for Modifications to AI/ML-Based SaMD* — the **continuous-learning problem** becomes public policy.

### Generation 4 — Standards Maturation & AI Governance (2020–Present)

- **2020** — FDA, Health Canada, MHRA co-publish the **10 Good Machine Learning Practice (GMLP) Principles**.
- **2021** — EU **Medical Device Regulation (MDR 2017/745)** comes into full effect (5-year transition completed). WHO publishes *Ethics & Governance of AI for Health*.
- **2022** — FDA *Software Precertification Pilot* concludes; transition to traditional pathway + PCCP. DICOM WG-23 releases **AI Interoperability White Paper**.
- **2023** — **NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0)** released. **EU AI Act** agreed politically (formal adoption 2024).
- **2024** — **FDA Final Guidance on Predetermined Change Control Plans (PCCP) for AI/ML-Enabled Devices** (Dec 2024). EU AI Act enters force.

## 2.2 The IMDRF SaMD Risk Categorization Framework — A High-Yield Formula

The **International Medical Device Regulators Forum (IMDRF)** defines the foundational risk framework:

$$S_{risk} = f(\text{State of Healthcare Significance},\ \text{SaMD's Role in Diagnosis/Treatment})$$

The categorical matrix is:

| State of Healthcare Significance | **Inform** | **Drive** | **Diagnose/Treat** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Critical** (life-threatening, time-critical) | **State III** | **State IV** | **State IV** |
| **Serious** (non-life-threatening, not time-critical) | **State II** | **State III** | **State III** |
| **Non-serious** | **State I** | **State I** | **State II** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Pearl:** Higher State Number = Higher Risk = Stricter Standard. A *treat* decision in a *critical* condition (e.g., AI controlling a ventilator) is **State IV** — highest scrutiny.

## 2.4 The GMLP — 10 Principles (FDA / Health Canada / MHRA)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** Memorize the *thematic grouping* — the 10 GMLP principles cluster into 5 themes:

1. **Team & Culture:** Multi-disciplinary expertise, QC processes.
2. **Clinical Context:** Well-defined intended use, integration with clinical workflow.
3. **Data Quality:** Representative, relevant, accurate datasets.
4. **Performance & Robustness:** Independent test sets, generalization, de novo performance assessment.
5. **Users & Transparency:** Clear information to users, salient limitations.

> Real-world utility: These principles are referenced in **every** FDA 510(k) and De Novo submission for AI/ML devices. They have effectively become the *de facto* global technical standard for clinical-AI development.

## 2.5 EU AI Act — The Risk Pyramid for Medical AI

The EU AI Act (Reg. 2024/1689) classifies AI systems by risk:

$$R_{AI} \in \{\text{Unacceptable},\ \text{High},\ \text{Limited},\ \text{Minimal}\}$$

| Risk Level | Example | Obligation |
|---|---|---|
| Unacceptable | Social scoring, manipulative AI | **Banned** |
| **High** | **Medical AI (SaMD)** | **Full conformity assessment, CE marking, post-market monitoring** |
| Limited | Chatbots (disclosure) | Transparency only |
| Minimal | Spam filters | No obligation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Truth:** Medical AI is **explicitly classified as High-Risk**, requiring conformity with the EU MDR *and* the AI Act. This is a *double-regulatory load* unique to the medical sector.

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example — Classifying a Hypothetical AI SaMD

**Question:** A startup has built a deep-learning model that automatically detects **intracranial hemorrhage** on non-contrast head CT scans. It is intended to be used by emergency radiologists in a Level-1 trauma center. Output is a bounding box + severity score, with the radiologist making the final diagnostic call.

**Step 1: Determine if it is SaMD**

The IMDRF SaMD definition requires that the software:
- (a) Performs a purpose *intended by the manufacturer* **separate from** the hardware.
- (b) Is **not** integral to the function of a hardware device.
- (c) Performs a **medical purpose** (diagnose, treat, mitigate, etc.).

*Derivation:*
- The deep-learning model runs on a generic GPU server → **criterion (a) met.**
- The CT scanner functions without the model → **criterion (b) met.**
- The output informs diagnosis of a life-threatening condition → **criterion (c) met.**

$$\therefore\ \text{The product is SaMD.}$$

**Step 2: Apply the IMDRF Risk Categorization**

We evaluate two axes:

- *Healthcare Significance* of the condition: **Critical** (intracranial hemorrhage is life-threatening and time-critical).
- *SaMD's Role:* It **informs** the radiologist, who makes the final decision.

*Derivation:*
- Looking up the matrix at (Critical, Inform) → **State III**.

$$\therefore\ \text{Risk Categorization} = \text{State III}.$$

**Step 3: Map to US and EU regulatory pathways**

- *US (FDA):* State III SaMD requires a **510(k) clearance** *or* **De Novo classification**, with a robust performance validation including: representative test set (≥ demographic diversity), pre-defined performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, ROC-AUC), clinical evaluation report, and — if the model is adaptive — a **PCCP**.
- *EU:* State III SaMD is a **Class IIa or higher** device under MDR Annex VIII. Requires a **Notified Body** for conformity assessment. Additionally, the EU AI Act's High-Risk tier demands: data governance, transparency, human oversight, and post-market monitoring.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Take-away:** Always show this 3-step logic — *SaMD-Yes/No → State → Pathway*. It is a 14-mark answer in disguise.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Quantifying Dataset Bias for GMLP Compliance

Suppose a vendor claims their chest X-ray AI was trained on **N = 500,000** images from U.S. hospitals. To assess **representativeness** (GMLP Principle #6), the manufacturer evaluates subgroup performance:

**Performance Metric:** Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC).

**Reported Subgroup AUCs:**

| Subgroup | Sample Size $n_i$ | AUC $\text{AUC}_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| Adults (18–65) | 320,000 | 0.94 |
| Pediatrics (<18) | 30,000 | 0.88 |
| Elderly (>65) | 150,000 | 0.92 |

**Step 1: Compute the Weighted Mean AUC (baseline):**

$$\overline{\text{AUC}} = \frac{\sum_i n_i \cdot \text{AUC}_i}{\sum_i n_i} = \frac{(320{,}000)(0.94) + (30{,}000)(0.88) + (150{,}000)(0.92)}{500{,}000}$$

$$\overline{\text{AUC}} = \frac{300{,}800 + 26{,}400 + 138{,}000}{500{,}000} = \frac{465{,}200}{500{,}000} = 0.9304$$

**Step 2: Compute the Performance Disparity Ratio:**

$$D = \frac{\min(\text{AUC}_i)}{\max(\text{AUC}_i)} = \frac{0.88}{0.94} = 0.9362$$

**Step 3: Apply the GMLP Acceptance Threshold (typical industry/FDA expectation $D \ge 0.90$):**

$$D = 0.9362 \ge 0.90\ \checkmark$$

**Step 4: Conclusion:**

$$\text{Subgroup disparity is acceptable, BUT pediatrics performance is borderline.}$$

> Recommend augmenting pediatric training data (GMLP Principle #6 — *Training datasets should be representative of the intended patient population*) and adding an explicit *limitation statement* (GMLP Principle #9 — *Information to users is transparent*).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** GMLP compliance is **qualitative**, not strictly numerical. The FDA evaluates *the process* of bias assessment, not a single number. But showing a *quantitative* metric like $D$ elevates your answer from "good" to "examiner's top band."

## 3.3 Worked Example — DICOM vs. HL7/FHIR vs. IHE Roles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU students often confuse these. Memorize:**

- **DICOM** answers: *"What does the image look like on the wire?"* (pixel data, metadata, IODs).
- **HL7 v2** answers: *"How do I send the radiology report from RIS to HIS?"* (pipe-and-hat text messages).
- **HL7 FHIR** answers: *"How do I make EHR data available as a modern RESTful API for an AI to consume?"* (JSON/XML over HTTP).
- **IHE** answers: *"How do I orchestrate *all* of these into a coherent, end-to-end clinical workflow?"* (Integration Profiles: Scheduled Workflow, AI Results, AIR — AI Results).

$$\text{AI Medical Pipeline} = \text{DICOM (image)} \oplus \text{FHIR (clinical context)} \oplus \text{IHE (workflow glue)}$$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model-QP-PEBAIT862]`**

### Q1. Define the term "Software as a Medical Device" (SaMD) as per IMDRF. Why is this definition critical for AI-based medical imaging systems?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

According to **IMDRF/SaMD WG/N12 FINAL:2014**, *Software as a Medical Device (SaMD)* is defined as *"software intended to be used for one or more medical purposes that performs these purposes **without being part of a hardware medical device**."*

Three criteria define a SaMD:
- (1) Performs a **medical purpose** (diagnose, treat, mitigate, monitor).
- (2) Is **separate from** any hardware medical device.
- (3) Is intended by the manufacturer to be used **standalone**.

This definition is critical for AI-based medical imaging because: (a) most imaging AI (e.g., an AI that triages head CT for stroke) runs on **generic compute hardware**, satisfying the "separate from hardware" criterion; (b) it triggers **regulatory oversight** under FDA, EU MDR, and the EU AI Act; and (c) it determines the **risk categorization** (IMDRF 4×3 matrix) and the appropriate approval pathway.

> **Marking Pattern:** 1 mark definition + 1 mark three criteria + 1 mark AI-imaging relevance.

---

### Q2. Differentiate between DICOM, HL7 FHIR, and IHE in the context of a medical AI pipeline.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Standard | Function | AI Pipeline Role |
|---|---|---|
| **DICOM 3.0** | Image file format + metadata standard (pixel data, modality, patient ID) | Source format for input CT/MRI images |
| **HL7 FHIR R4** | RESTful API for clinical/EHR data (JSON/XML over HTTP) | Fetches patient context (age, lab values) for AI inference |
| **IHE (Radiology + AI Results profile)** | Orchestrates multi-system workflows via integration profiles | Defines *how* the AI Result flows back into the PACS/EHR |

DICOM answers *"What does the image look like?"*, FHIR answers *"What is the clinical context?"*, and IHE answers *"How do these systems work together?"*

> **Marking Pattern:** 1 mark each for any 3 distinct roles, with clear differentiation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks) — Module 2 Internal Choice

> **`[KTU University Exam – Sample QP per 2024 Scheme, ESE Module 2]`**
> *(Note: ESE 14-mark questions in the 2024 scheme allow internal choice.)*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Trace the evolution of standards for AI in medical applications across its four generations. In your answer, name **at least two landmark systems or events per generation** and explain the *driving force* that pushed standards from one generation to the next.

**Model Answer:**

The evolution proceeds through four distinct generations, each driven by a specific technological or clinical force.

**Generation 1 — Knowledge-Based Expert Systems (1970s–1990s):**
- *Landmark 1:* **MYCIN (1976, Stanford)** — rule-based antibiotic therapy advisor with ~600 IF-THEN rules.
- *Landmark 2:* **DXplain (1986, MGH)** — differential diagnosis generator.
- *Driving Force:* Recognition that expert knowledge could be encoded, but a **lack of integration with hospital workflows** prevented clinical adoption. *Result:* A standards vacuum — no regulatory body recognized software as a device.

**Generation 2 — Classical Machine Learning & Early CAD (1990s–2012):**
- *Landmark 1:* **R2 ImageChecker (1998)** — first FDA-cleared mammography CAD system.
- *Landmark 2:* **DICOM 3.0 (1993)** — universal medical image format enabling cross-vendor AI training data.
- *Driving Force:* Clinical evidence that ML could *augment radiologists*, combined with the need for *cross-vendor interoperability*. *Result:* FDA 21 CFR Part 820 (QSR 1996) and 510(k) pathway emerge.

**Generation 3 — Deep Learning Revolution (2012–2019):**
- *Landmark 1:* **Arterys Cardio DL (2016)** — first FDA-cleared cloud-based DL diagnostic.
- *Landmark 2:* **IDx-DR (2017/2018)** — first *autonomous* AI diagnosis (De Novo).
- *Driving Force:* AlexNet (2012) and U-Net (2015) produced superhuman accuracy, but the FDA's *static* approval model could not handle **adaptive, continuously-learning algorithms**. *Result:* FDA's 2019 Proposed Framework for AI/ML-Based SaMD.

**Generation 4 — Standards Maturation & AI Governance (2020–Present):**
- *Landmark 1:* **GMLP Principles (2020)** — FDA, Health Canada, MHRA joint guidance (10 principles).
- *Landmark 2:* **EU AI Act (2024/1689)** — first horizontal AI law globally; medical AI is **High-Risk**.
- *Landmark 3:* **FDA PCCP Final Guidance (2024)** — enables pre-authorized modifications.
- *Driving Force:* The "regulatory gap" between adaptive AI and one-time approval, plus concerns over **bias, transparency, and post-market drift**. *Result:* Layered governance: regulatory + interoperability + quality + AI-specific.

> **Marking Pattern:** 2 marks per generation (≈ 1 system + 1 driver each), 1 mark overall flow.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, describe the **IMDRF SaMD Risk Categorization Matrix**. A vendor proposes an AI that autonomously prescribes thrombolytics for acute ischemic stroke. Classify the device and identify the regulatory pathway it must follow under both US (FDA) and EU (EU MDR + EU AI Act) frameworks.

**Model Answer:**

**The IMDRF SaMD Risk Categorization Matrix** is a 4×3 matrix that cross-tabulates:
- *Y-axis (4 states):* The **state of the healthcare situation** (Non-serious, Serious, Critical, plus a Critical-with-time-pressure sub-class).
- *X-axis (3 roles):* The **role of the SaMD** in that situation (Inform, Drive, Diagnose/Treat).

The result is a **State I, II, III, or IV** classification; higher state = higher risk = stricter regulation.

*(See Section 4.2 above for the matrix diagram.)*

**Classification of the proposed device:**

*Step 1 — Healthcare Significance:* Acute ischemic stroke is **life-threatening and time-critical** → **Critical**.

*Step 2 — SaMD's Role:* The AI **prescribes** therapy (treat) → **Diagnose/Treat**.

*Step 3 — Matrix Lookup:* (Critical, Diagnose/Treat) → **State IV** (highest risk category).

*Step 4 — Regulatory Pathway:*

- **US (FDA):** State IV SaMD is a **Class III** device. The most likely pathway is **Premarket Approval (PMA)** — the most rigorous pathway — or **De Novo** if no predicate exists. The submission must include a clinical investigation (typically a prospective, multi-center trial), software documentation per **IEC 62304** at the highest safety class (Class C), risk management per **ISO 14971**, and a **PCCP** if the algorithm is adaptive.

- **EU (MDR + AI Act):** Per **MDR Annex VIII Rule 12**, software intended to provide information used to take *decisions with diagnostic or therapeutic implications* for a *life-threatening condition* is **Class III**. This requires:
  1. **CE marking** via a Notified Body conformity assessment (Annex IX/X).
  2. **Clinical investigation** under MDR Annex XV.
  3. Conformity with the **EU AI Act's High-Risk tier** requirements: data governance, transparency, human oversight, accuracy/robustness, and post-market monitoring.
  4. Registration in **EUDAMED** and ongoing **post-market surveillance** + **periodic safety update reports (PSURs)**.

$$\therefore\ \text{Autonomous thrombolytic-prescribing AI} \in \text{State IV SaMD} \rightarrow \text{Class III} \rightarrow \text{PMA (US) + CE + AI Act (EU).}$$

> **Marking Pattern:** 2 marks matrix + 2 marks classification logic + 3 marks regulatory pathway (1.5 US + 1.5 EU).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **10 Good Machine Learning Practice (GMLP) Principles** jointly published by the FDA, Health Canada, and MHRA in 2020. Group them into **five thematic categories** and explain the engineering significance of each category with at least one concrete example.

**Model Answer:**

The **GMLP Principles** are the cross-border *de facto* quality baseline for clinical AI/ML devices. Grouped into five themes:

| # | Theme | Principles (short names) | Engineering Significance & Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Multi-disciplinary Team & Culture** | GMLP-1 (expertise), GMLP-2 (QC processes) | A model-development team must include clinicians, data scientists, regulatory affairs, and ethicists. *Example:* A pediatric radiology AI must have a board-certified pediatric radiologist on the validation team — not only an adult radiologist. |
| 2 | **Intended Use & Clinical Integration** | GMLP-3 (well-defined intended use), GMLP-4 (clinical workflow integration) | The "intended use statement" governs *everything* downstream. *Example:* A diabetic-retinopathy AI intended for *screening* in primary care has a different evidence bar than one intended for *definitive diagnosis* by an ophthalmologist. |
| 3 | **Data Quality & Representativeness** | GMLP-5 (relevant datasets), GMLP-6 (representative of intended population) | Datasets must reflect age, sex, race, comorbidities, and imaging-protocol diversity. *Example:* A chest-X-ray AI trained only on U.S. data may fail in Southeast Asia where TB prevalence and imaging hardware differ. |
| 4 | **Performance & Robustness** | GMLP-7 (de novo performance), GMLP-8 (focus on performance) | Performance must be evaluated on *independent* test sets; robustness against adversarial and out-of-distribution inputs must be characterized. *Example:* Reporting sensitivity/specificity with 95% CIs across all subgroups — not just the overall population. |
| 5 | **Transparency & User Comprehension** | GMLP-9 (information to users), GMLP-10 (salient limitations) | The labelling must communicate performance, limitations, and the *salient* failure modes. *Example:* "This model is not validated for pediatric patients under 12" must be visible in the user interface, not buried in a 200-page IFU. |

> **Marking Pattern:** 1 mark per theme + 0.4 marks per example ≈ 7 marks.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **EU AI Act (2024)** as it applies to medical AI. Why is medical AI explicitly classified as **High-Risk**? What are the **mandatory obligations** that a medical-AI vendor must fulfill to comply?

**Model Answer:**

**The EU AI Act (Regulation 2024/1689)** is the world's first comprehensive horizontal regulation of artificial intelligence. It applies a **risk-based pyramid** with four tiers: Unacceptable, High, Limited, and Minimal.

**Why medical AI is High-Risk:**

Medical AI is explicitly listed in **Annex III** of the EU AI Act as a high-risk system because:
- (1) It is intended to be used in **safety-critical healthcare contexts** where failure can cause death or serious harm.
- (2) It is **already regulated as a medical device** under the EU MDR — a *double-regulatory load*.
- (3) The Act intentionally **leverages the MDR** to avoid duplicating the medical-device regulatory infrastructure; Annex I of the AI Act cross-references the MDR's safety/quality requirements.

**Mandatory obligations** for medical-AI vendors under the High-Risk tier:

1. **Risk Management System (Art. 9):** Continuous, iterative risk-management process throughout the AI lifecycle.
2. **Data Governance (Art. 10):** Training/validation/test datasets must be *relevant, representative, free of errors, and complete*. Bias must be examined and mitigated.
3. **Technical Documentation (Art. 11):** Demonstrable compliance evidence, kept up to date.
4. **Record-Keeping / Logging (Art. 12):** Automatic logging of events to enable traceability.
5. **Transparency & Information to Users (Art. 13):** Clear instructions for use, with information about *capabilities, limitations, and intended purpose*.
6. **Human Oversight (Art. 14):** The system must be designed to allow effective human supervision, with the ability to *intervene* or *override*.
7. **Accuracy, Robustness, Cybersecurity (Art. 15):** Documented performance metrics; resilience to errors, faults, and adversarial attacks.
8. **Quality Management System (Art. 17):** Aligned with **ISO 13485** or equivalent.
9. **Post-Market Monitoring (Art. 72):** Continuous real-world performance monitoring and reporting of serious incidents.
10. **Registration in EU Database (Art. 49):** Public registration of the high-risk system.
11. **Conformity Assessment & CE Marking (Art. 43):** Required *in addition to* the EU MDR conformity assessment.
12. **Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment (Art. 27):** For deployers (hospitals) deploying high-risk medical AI.

> **Marking Pattern:** 2 marks classification rationale + 5 marks obligations (1 mark each for any 5 of the 12 obligations listed).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> ❌ **Common mistake #1:** Writing "AI Act bans medical AI." It does **not**. It merely regulates it as High-Risk. Banning is reserved for Unacceptable-Risk uses (e.g., social scoring).
> ❌ **Common mistake #2:** Treating the AI Act as a *replacement* for the EU MDR. It is *cumulative* — both apply.
> ❌ **Common mistake #3:** Saying "the AI Act requires FDA approval." It does **not**. The AI Act is EU legislation; FDA is US.
> ❌ **Common mistake #4:** Confusing **GMLP** (jointly FDA/HC/MHRA — global technical principles) with **GMLPR** (Good Machine Learning Practice Review). Stick to the original 10 principles.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Evolution of Standards for AI in Medical Applications**

- ☐ **Four Generations:** (1) Expert systems (MYCIN 1976), (2) Classical ML + CAD (DICOM 1993, R2 1998), (3) Deep learning (Arterys 2016, IDx-DR 2017), (4) Standards maturity (GMLP 2020, EU MDR 2021, EU AI Act 2024, PCCP 2024).
- ☐ **Five Pillars:** Regulatory · Interoperability · Quality Engineering · Data & Algorithm · AI Governance.
- ☐ **IMDRF SaMD Risk Matrix:** 4 (healthcare significance) × 3 (SaMD role) = 4 risk states. Higher = stricter.
- ☐ **GMLP 10 Principles** cluster into 5 themes: Team, Intended Use, Data, Performance, Transparency.
- ☐ **EU AI Act = High-Risk** for medical AI. **NOT a ban.** **Cumulative** with EU MDR.
- ☐ **DICOM** = image format; **FHIR** = clinical-data API; **IHE** = workflow glue.
- ☐ **PCCP** (Predetermined Change Control Plan) is the FDA's mechanism to allow **adaptive AI** updates without re-submission.
- ☐ **ISO 13485** (QMS) and **IEC 62304** (Software SDLC) and **ISO 14971** (Risk Management) are the **mandatory quality trio**.
- ☐ **NIST AI RMF 1.0 (2023)** = GOVERN / MAP / MEASURE / MANAGE — the most-cited U.S. AI governance framework.
- ☐ **WHO Ethics & Governance of AI for Health (2021)** = global ethical baseline with 6 consensus principles.
- ☐ **Bias in medical AI** is the #1 KTU exam focus; memorize the **disparity ratio** $D = \min/\max$ approach.
- ☐ **Continuous learning** is the *unique* regulatory challenge of AI vs. traditional SaMD — the entire modern standards stack is built to address it.
- ☐ **Cross-border harmonization** is the *direction* — GMLP, IMDRF, WHO guidance are all *multi-jurisdictional*.

> **Final exam advice:** Frame every answer in the *Five-Pillar* model. The examiner immediately sees you understand that "standards" is not a single document but a **multi-layered ecosystem** — and that is the single highest-band answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Devices and Apps for Data Collection and Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT862 - AI FOR MEDICAL IMAGE ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Devices and Apps for Data Collection and Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Devices and Apps for Data Collection and Analysis

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> *Medical data collection and analysis devices* refer to a heterogeneous ecosystem of hardware instruments, sensors, imaging modalities, and software platforms (including mobile applications, cloud-based analytics, and AI-driven frameworks) that systematically capture, transmit, store, process, and interpret physiological, biochemical, and imaging data for clinical decision support, diagnostics, and research in healthcare.

In the context of **AI for Medical Image Analysis**, these devices and applications form the **end-to-end pipeline** — from raw data acquisition at the patient bedside or imaging suite, through preprocessing and annotation, to AI-driven inference and clinical reporting.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it like a modern restaurant kitchen:**
> - The **imaging devices (MRI, CT, X-ray)** are the *raw ingredient suppliers* — they bring in the fresh produce.
> - The **wearable sensors and bedside monitors** are the *tasting stations* — constantly sampling quality.
> - The **PACS and EHR systems** are the *cold storage and inventory* — organizing everything systematically.
> - The **AI applications and deep learning frameworks** are the *master chefs* — transforming raw ingredients into a Michelin-star dish (a clinically actionable diagnosis).
> - The **mobile health (mHealth) apps and telemedicine platforms** are the *delivery service* — bringing the final result to the patient and physician.

Just as a chef cannot cook without fresh ingredients, an AI algorithm cannot perform medical image analysis without properly acquired, standardized, and annotated data.

### Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 PEBRT862

| Aspect | Relevance to AI in Medical Imaging |
|---|---|
| **Data is the fuel** | AI models (especially deep learning) require massive, high-quality, labeled datasets |
| **Standardization** | Devices must follow **DICOM**, **HL7**, **FHIR** standards for interoperability |
| **Edge vs. Cloud** | Devices dictate whether processing is local (edge AI) or remote (cloud AI) |
| **Regulatory compliance** | FDA, CE marking, and **IRB approvals** govern device deployment |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constants and Standards to Remember:**
> - **DICOM** (Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine) — the universal standard for medical imaging data.
> - **HL7 FHIR** — the standard for exchanging electronic health records.
> - **PACS** (Picture Archiving and Communication System) — hospital-scale image storage and retrieval.
> - **SNOMED CT** — systematic clinical terminology for coding diagnoses and findings.

---

## 2. Categories of Data Collection Devices

### 2.1 Medical Imaging Devices (Primary Modality)

These devices generate the volumetric or 2D image data that AI algorithms directly consume.

| Device | Physical Principle | Output Type | AI Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MRI (Magnetic Resonance Imaging)** | Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) of hydrogen protons | 3D volumetric, multi-sequence (T1, T2, FLAIR) | Brain tumor segmentation, multiple sclerosis lesion detection |
| **CT (Computed Tomography)** | X-ray attenuation through rotating gantry | 3D volumetric, Hounsfield Unit scale | Lung nodule detection, COVID-19 pneumonia assessment |
| **X-Ray (Radiography)** | Differential X-ray absorption | 2D projection images | Tuberculosis screening, bone fracture detection |
| **Ultrasound (Sonography)** | High-frequency sound wave echo (piezoelectric transducer) | 2D/3D real-time, Doppler flow | Cardiac function, obstetric imaging |
| **PET (Positron Emission Tomography)** | Radioactive tracer (e.g., ¹⁸F-FDG) annihilation detection | 3D metabolic/functional map | Oncology staging, neurodegeneration |
| **Mammography** | Low-dose X-ray of breast tissue | 2D projection / 3D tomosynthesis | Breast cancer detection (e.g., BI-RADS classification) |
| **Endoscopy / Colonoscopy** | Fiber-optic camera with flexible probe | 2D video stream | Polyp detection, GI tract lesion classification |
| **OCT (Optical Coherence Tomography)** | Low-coherence interferometry of backscattered light | Cross-sectional retinal scans | Diabetic retinopathy screening |
| **Dermatoscopy** | Magnified skin surface imaging with polarized light | 2D high-resolution | Melanoma vs. nevus classification |
| **Microscopy (Digital Pathology)** | Whole-slide scanning of stained tissue | Gigapixel whole-slide images (WSI) | Cancer cell detection, mitotic count |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** For Module 2, focus on understanding which AI tasks each modality enables. For example, MRI → **segmentation tasks** (U-Net), CT → **detection tasks** (YOLO, RetinaNet), X-ray → **classification tasks** (EfficientNet, ResNet).

### 2.2 Wearable and Point-of-Care (POC) Devices

These capture *physiological time-series data* rather than images, but they are critical for **multimodal AI systems** that combine imaging with vitals.

| Device | Data Captured | Sampling Rate | AI Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ECG / Holter Monitor** | Electrical heart signals (mV) | 250–1000 Hz | Arrhythmia detection (e.g., Atrial Fibrillation) |
| **Smartwatch (Apple Watch, Fitbit)** | Heart rate, SpO₂, steps, ECG lead-I | 1–100 Hz | Continuous cardiac monitoring |
| **Pulse Oximeter** | Peripheral oxygen saturation (%) | 0.5–1 Hz | Respiratory distress, sleep apnea |
| **Continuous Glucose Monitor (CGM)** | Interstitial glucose (mg/dL) | Every 1–5 min | Diabetes management AI |
| **Smart Inhaler** | Usage time, flow rate | Event-based | Asthma adherence AI |
| **Smart Pill / Ingestible Sensor** | pH, temperature in GI tract | Periodic | Medication adherence verification |
| **Blood Pressure Cuff (Smart)** | Systolic / Diastolic mmHg | Per measurement | Hypertension risk prediction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric for ECG Sampling:** The clinical gold standard is **500 Hz** sampling rate with **12-bit resolution** over a **10-second window** (standard 12-lead ECG) or **30 days** continuous (Holter).

### 2.3 Hospital Information System Infrastructure

These are not "sensors" but are the **data highways** that move clinical information between devices, storage, and AI systems.

- **PACS (Picture Archiving and Communication System):** DICOM-compliant image archive, typically TB to PB scale per hospital.
- **EHR / EMR (Electronic Health Record / Medical Record):** Longitudinal patient data — demographics, labs, notes, medications.
- **RIS (Radiology Information System):** Scheduling, reporting, and tracking of radiology workflow.
- **LIS (Laboratory Information System):** Manages pathology and clinical lab data.
- **HL7 Interface Engine (Mirth Connect, Rhapsody):** Middleware for inter-system messaging.

### 2.4 IoT and Edge Devices in Healthcare

The **Internet of Medical Things (IoMT)** is a growing frontier.

- **Edge AI Devices:** NVIDIA Jetson-based portable ultrasound, smartphone-attached otoscopes, handheld fundus cameras.
- **5G-Connected Ambulances:** Real-time transmission of pre-hospital ECGs and stroke imaging to hospital AI triage systems.
- **Smart Beds and Ambient Sensors:** Continuous patient monitoring without wearable contact.

---

## 3. Apps and Software Platforms for Data Collection and Analysis

### 3.1 Data Annotation Platforms (Ground Truth Generation)

> [!NOTE]
> AI is only as good as its labels. Annotation is the **most labor-intensive** step in any medical AI pipeline.

| Tool | Annotation Type | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **3D Slicer** | 3D medical image segmentation | Open-source, MITK-based, supports DICOM |
| **ITK-SNAP** | 3D segmentation | Semi-automatic active contour |
| **LabelImg / LabelMe** | 2D bounding boxes / polygons | Lightweight, image classification & detection |
| **CVAT (Computer Vision Annotation Tool)** | Bounding boxes, polygons, polylines | Web-based, collaborative |
| **QuPath** | Digital pathology (whole-slide images) | Cell-level annotation, open-source |
| **MedSAM / Segment Anything Medical** | Foundation model-based zero-shot segmentation | Prompt-based (point/box) |
| **MONAI Label** | Deep learning-assisted annotation | Active learning loop with clinician |

### 3.2 Data Storage and Management Platforms

- **DICOM Servers:** Orthanc, dcm4chee, Horos (macOS viewer).
- **Cloud PACS:** Ambra Health, Ambra, Google Cloud Healthcare API, AWS HealthImaging.
- **Federated Data Platforms:** OHDSI (Observational Health Data Sciences and Informatics) for multi-institutional studies.

### 3.3 AI Frameworks and Development Platforms

| Framework | Developer | Special Strength |
|---|---|---|
| **MONAI (Medical Open Network for AI)** | NVIDIA + King's College London | Native medical imaging, I/O, transforms |
| **PyTorch** | Meta / Linux Foundation | Research flexibility |
| **TensorFlow / Keras** | Google | Production deployment, TFX |
| **nnU-Net** | Division of Medical Image Computing, DKFZ | Self-configuring segmentation pipeline |
| **SimpleITK / ITK** | Kitware | Image processing primitives |
| **Anaconda Distribution** | Anaconda Inc. | Python environment management |

### 3.4 Mobile Health (mHealth) Applications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **mHealth apps** turn consumer smartphones into medical data collection devices.

- **AI Triage Apps:** SkinVision (dermatology), IDx-DR (diabetic retinopathy — first FDA-cleared autonomous AI).
- **Connected Patient Apps:** MyChart, Apple Health, Google Fit, CGM companion apps (Dexcom Clarity, LibreLink).
- **Clinician-Facing Apps:** RadiAnt DICOM Viewer (mobile), Epocrates, UpToDate.
- **Telemedicine Apps:** Teladoc, Amwell, Practo — integrate live video with image/data sharing.

### 3.5 Cloud Analytics and AI-as-a-Service Platforms

- **Google Vertex AI** — AutoML for medical imaging, de-identification pipelines.
- **AWS HealthLake** — FHIR-based health data lake.
- **Microsoft Azure Health Data Services** — DICOM and FHIR services in the cloud.
- **IBM Watson Health (historical)** — Oncology decision support.

---

## 4. The End-to-End Data Pipeline (Conceptual Workflow)

The journey from patient to AI diagnosis follows a well-defined sequence:

**Step 1: Acquisition** → Device captures raw signal (e.g., MRI k-space, CT sinogram, ECG voltage).

**Step 2: Reconstruction** → Raw signals are reconstructed into human-interpretable images (Fourier transform for MRI, filtered back-projection for CT).

**Step 3: De-identification / Anonymization** → Remove PHI (Protected Health Information) per **HIPAA Safe Harbor** or **GDPR Article 9**.

**Step 4: DICOM Wrapping** → Embed images with metadata (patient ID, modality, study date — these are stripped or hashed for AI).

**Step 5: Storage in PACS** → Indexed and archived for retrieval.

**Step 6: Annotation** → Expert radiologists/pathologists label regions of interest (ROI), bounding boxes, or pixel-level masks.

**Step 7: Preprocessing** → Resampling, intensity normalization (e.g., z-score, Nyúl-Udupa histogram matching), bias field correction, augmentation.

**Step 8: AI Model Training/Inference** → Deep network (e.g., U-Net, ResNet, ViT) learns from labeled data or makes predictions on new data.

**Step 9: Post-processing** → Connected component analysis, morphological operations, confidence calibration.

**Step 10: Reporting / Clinical Integration** → Structured report (DICOM-SR), pushed back to PACS and EHR, presented to clinician.

---

## 5. Data Quality, Standards, and Regulatory Considerations

### 5.1 Data Quality Dimensions for AI

| Dimension | Definition | Example in Medical Imaging |
|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | Ground truth correctness | Expert-annotated radiology reports |
| **Completeness** | All required data present | Multiple MRI sequences, not just T1 |
| **Consistency** | Standardized acquisition | Same MRI scanner, same protocol |
| **Timeliness** | Data availability when needed | Stroke imaging within 25 minutes |
| **Validity** | Conforms to DICOM/HL7 standards | Proper SOP class UIDs |
| **Uniqueness** | No duplicate studies | Proper study instance UID |

### 5.2 Regulatory and Ethical Frameworks

- **HIPAA (US):** Privacy Rule, Security Rule — governs PHI handling.
- **GDPR (EU):** Right to erasure, data portability — affects EU training data.
- **FDA AI/ML SaMD (Software as a Medical Device):** 21st Century Cures Act, Predetermined Change Control Plans for adaptive AI.
- **IMDRF (International Medical Device Regulators Forum):** Risk categorization framework.
- **IRB / Ethics Committee Approval:** Mandatory for any clinical data use in research.

### 5.3 Common Data Challenges

- **Class Imbalance:** e.g., far more "normal" than "cancer" images.
- **Domain Shift:** Scanner manufacturer, protocol, population differences.
- **Label Noise:** Inter-observer variability among radiologists.
- **Small Sample Size:** Rare diseases have very few cases.
- **Bias:** Demographic under-representation in training cohorts.

### 6. Real-World Utility and Engineering Applications

| Domain | Example Application |
|---|---|
| **Stroke Triage** | Viz.ai LVO detection from CT angiography — alerts neuro-interventionalist in <6 minutes |
| **Diabetic Retinopathy** | IDx-DR autonomous AI on fundus photos — primary care screening |
| **COVID-19 Triage** | Chest X-ray and CT AI for severity scoring (e.g., RadImageNet models) |
| **Pathology AI** | Paige.AI for prostate cancer detection on whole-slide images |
| **Cardiac Monitoring** | Apple Watch ECG + AI for atrial fibrillation flagging |
| **Surgical AI** | Da Vinci robot + AI for instrument tracking and skill assessment |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The most successful medical AI products of 2024–2026 are not pure deep-learning breakthroughs but **integrated systems** that combine robust data acquisition (well-designed devices), structured annotation (good tools), and seamless clinical workflow integration (PACS/EHR connectivity).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 1. Theoretical Framework for Data Acquisition

### 1.1 Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) — The Foundational Quality Metric

Every medical data acquisition device, whether an MRI scanner or a pulse oximeter, is characterized by its **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)**. This single metric determines whether downstream AI algorithms have a chance of succeeding.

The general definition is:

$$
\text{SNR} = \frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}} = \left(\frac{A_{\text{signal}}}{A_{\text{noise}}}\right)^{2}
$$

Where:
- $P_{\text{signal}}$ is the average signal power
- $P_{\text{noise}}$ is the average noise power
- $A$ denotes amplitude (since power is proportional to amplitude squared for resistive loads)

In **decibels (dB)** (standard engineering unit):

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why SNR matters for AI:** Deep learning models trained on low-SNR data learn the noise distribution and fail to generalize to higher-SNR test data. Acquisition quality fundamentally bounds model performance.

### 1.2 Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem (Digital Acquisition)

For any device that digitizes a continuous physiological signal (ECG, EEG, PPG, audio), the sampling rate must satisfy:

$$
f_{\text{sampling}} \geq 2 \cdot f_{\text{max}}
$$

Where $f_{\text{max}}$ is the highest frequency component in the signal.

| Signal | Typical Bandwidth | Minimum Nyquist Rate | Clinical Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| ECG | 0.05 – 150 Hz | 300 Hz | **500 Hz** (with 12-bit ADC) |
| EEG | 0.5 – 100 Hz | 200 Hz | **256 – 512 Hz** |
| EMG | 20 – 500 Hz | 1000 Hz | **2000 Hz** |
| Heart Sound (PCG) | 20 – 2000 Hz | 4000 Hz | **8000 – 44100 Hz** |
| Pulse Oximetry (PPG) | 0.5 – 5 Hz | 10 Hz | **100 – 500 Hz** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Aliasing occurs if the signal contains energy above the Nyquist frequency. Anti-aliasing **low-pass filters** must be applied *before* digitization — this is non-negotiable in clinical-grade acquisition.

### 1.3 CT Image Reconstruction — Filtered Back-Projection (FBP)

Although the topic of this module is devices rather than algorithms, understanding how a CT scanner produces images helps the AI engineer understand what the network actually "sees."

The **Radon transform** of a 2D image $f(x,y)$ along angle $\theta$ is:

$$
R(\rho, \theta) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x,y) \cdot \delta(\rho - x\cos\theta - y\sin\theta) \, dx \, dy
$$

Where $\delta$ is the Dirac delta function and $\rho$ is the perpendicular distance from the origin to the projection line.

The **Filtered Back-Projection (FBP)** reconstruction is then:

$$
f(x,y) = \int_{0}^{\pi} \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} R(\rho, \theta) \cdot \vert \omega \vert \cdot e^{j 2\pi \omega \rho} \, d\omega \right] d\theta
$$

The inner integral is the **1D Fourier transform of the projection, multiplied by the ramp filter $\vert \omega \vert$**, which compensates for the $1/\vert \omega \vert$ blur introduced by simple back-projection.

> [!NOTE]
> **For AI Engineers:** Modern deep-learning reconstruction (e.g., **FBPConvNet**, **DDPM-based reconstruction**) replaces the filtered back-projection step. So the "device" of tomorrow may include an embedded neural network.

### 1.4 Hounsfield Unit Scale (CT)

CT images are reported on the **Hounsfield Unit (HU)** scale, defined as:

$$
HU = 1000 \cdot \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}
$$

Where $\mu$ is the **linear attenuation coefficient** of X-rays in the material.

| Tissue | Approximate HU |
|---|---|
| Air | $-1000$ |
| Fat | $-100$ to $-50$ |
| Water | $0$ |
| Muscle | $+35$ to $+55$ |
| Blood (clotted) | $+60$ to $+100$ |
| Bone (cortical) | $+700$ to $+3000$ |
| Metal implant | $> +3000$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AI Implication:** Normalization of CT slices to a fixed HU window (e.g., lung window $[-1000, +400]$ HU, bone window $[+400, +1000]$ HU) is a critical preprocessing step before feeding to a neural network.

### 1.5 DICOM Pixel Value to Real-World Conversion

DICOM stores pixel values as integers, but the actual physical units are obtained via:

$$
\text{Output} = m \cdot SV + b
$$

Where:
- $SV$ is the stored pixel value
- $m$ is the **Rescale Slope** (DICOM tag `(0028,1053)`)
- $b$ is the **Rescale Intercept** (DICOM tag `(0028,1052)`)

For CT: $m = 1$ and $b = -1024$ (so stored $0 \rightarrow$ $-1024$ HU = air).

### 1.6 Hashing and De-identification

To anonymize DICOM files for AI research, the standard approach is to **hash patient identifiers**:

$$
\text{Anonymized ID} = \text{SHA-256}\!\left(\text{Patient ID} \, \vert\vert \, \text{Study Date} \, \vert\vert \, \text{Salt}\right)
$$

This is a **one-way function** — it is computationally infeasible to recover the original Patient ID from the hash, ensuring privacy.

> [!NOTE]
> We use $\vert\vert$ to denote string concatenation (NOT a pipe character that would break markdown tables).

## 2. KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Engineering Unit | KTU 2024 Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SNR** | $\text{SNR} = P_{\text{signal}} / P_{\text{noise}}$ | Ratio (dB) | Image quality bound for AI |
| **SNR in dB** | $10 \log_{10}(P_s / P_n)$ | **dB** | Standard engineering reporting |
| **Nyquist Rate** | $f_s \geq 2 f_{\text{max}}$ | Hz | ECG / EEG / PPG acquisition |
| **Hounsfield Unit** | $HU = 1000(\mu_t - \mu_w)/\mu_w$ | HU | CT normalization |
| **DICOM Rescale** | $y = m \cdot SV + b$ | HU (for CT) | Pixel-to-physical mapping |
| **Radon Transform** | $R(\rho,\theta) = \int f(x,y) \delta(\rho - x\cos\theta - y\sin\theta) dx dy$ | Dimensionless integral | CT / X-ray reconstruction |
| **FBP Reconstruction** | $f(x,y) = \int_0^\pi \int R(\rho,\theta) \vert \omega \vert e^{j 2\pi \omega \rho} d\omega \, d\theta$ | Pixel intensity | Foundational CT algorithm |
| **SHA-256 Anonymization** | $H = \text{SHA-256}(ID \vert\vert \text{Salt})$ | 256-bit hash | DICOM de-identification |
| **Dice Coefficient (Segmentation)** | $\text{Dice} = 2 \vert A \cap B \vert / (\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert)$ | 0 to 1 | AI evaluation metric |
| **Pixel Resolution (DICOM)** | $\text{Pixel Spacing} = \Delta x \times \Delta y$ | mm | Input layer dimension |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Dice Coefficient** and **IoU (Intersection over Union)** are the two most important evaluation metrics for medical image segmentation. Memorize:

$$
\text{IoU} = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert} = \frac{\text{Dice}}{2 - \text{Dice}}
$$

The conversion formula is critical: $\text{Dice} = 2 \cdot \text{IoU} / (1 + \text{IoU})$.

## 3. Why This Theory Matters in Real Engineering

- **Hardware Design Engineers** must ensure device SNR exceeds the AI model's tolerance — otherwise the system is a sunk cost.
- **Software Engineers** building AI pipelines must resample, normalize, and rescale inputs to match training distribution.
- **Regulatory Engineers** (FDA submission) must demonstrate that AI performance holds across scanners from different manufacturers — this is the **generalization gap** problem.
- **Clinical Workflow Engineers** must integrate devices into PACS/EHR using **DICOM-SR** (Structured Reporting) and **HL7 ORM/ORU** messages.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Exam:** A common question pattern is: *"Explain the data acquisition pipeline from a [CT scanner / wearable ECG / smartphone camera] to an AI diagnostic output. List the preprocessing steps and justify each."* The depth of preprocessing justification is where examiners award the distinction between Pass and First Class.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 1. Exhaustive Derivation: Hounsfield Unit Mapping from Raw CT Data

This derivation shows how raw detector counts in a CT scanner are converted to the **Hounsfield Units** that feed an AI model. Every step is required per KTU 2024 rigor.

### Step 1: Attenuation Law (Beer–Lambert for X-rays)

A monochromatic X-ray beam of initial intensity $I_0$ passing through a homogeneous slab of thickness $d$ emerges with intensity $I$ given by:

$$
I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu d}
$$

For a non-homogeneous body composed of thin slabs of varying attenuation $\mu(x)$, the integral form is:

$$
I_{\text{detector}} = I_0 \cdot \exp\!\left( -\int_{L} \mu(s) \, ds \right)
$$

Where $L$ is the line from source to detector, and $s$ is the path-length parameter.

### Step 2: Convert to Logarithmic (Line Integral of Attenuation)

The CT detector electronics take the natural log of the intensity ratio to obtain the **projection value**:

$$
p = -\ln\!\left( \frac{I}{I_0} \right) = \int_{L} \mu(s) \, ds
$$

This is the **Radon transform** of the attenuation field $\mu(x, y)$ along the line $L$.

### Step 3: Normalize to Reference Material (Water)

The Hounsfield scale is anchored to water. Define:

$$
\mu_{\text{water}} = \text{reference attenuation at calibration energy}
$$

Then the Hounsfield Unit is:

$$
HU = 1000 \cdot \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}
$$

### Step 4: Substitute the Beer–Lambert Log-Projection

Substituting $p = \int \mu \, ds$ and noting that for a water-filled calibration phantom of diameter $D$:

$$
p_{\text{water}} = \mu_{\text{water}} \cdot D
$$

We obtain:

$$
p = \mu_{\text{tissue}} \cdot D_{\text{eff}}
$$

Where $D_{\text{eff}}$ is the effective path length through tissue. For a circular body of diameter $D$:

$$
\mu_{\text{tissue}} = \frac{p}{D_{\text{eff}}}
$$

### Step 5: Combine to Express HU in Terms of Detected Intensities

Putting it all together:

$$
HU = 1000 \cdot \frac{ \left( \dfrac{p}{D_{\text{eff}}} \right) - \mu_{\text{water}} }{ \mu_{\text{water}} }
$$

For the calibrated case where $D_{\text{eff}} = D$ (water phantom of same diameter):

$$
HU = 1000 \cdot \left( \frac{p}{p_{\text{water}}} - 1 \right)
$$

$$
\boxed{ HU = 1000 \cdot \left( \frac{-\ln(I/I_0)}{-\ln(I_{\text{water}}/I_0)} - 1 \right) }
$$

This is the operational formula used in the CT reconstruction pipeline.

### Step 6: DICOM Rescale Step (Final Output)

The reconstructed image pixel value (before DICOM storage) is then:

$$
\text{HU}_{\text{final}} = m \cdot SV + b
$$

With CT default values $m = 1$ and $b = -1024$:

$$
\text{HU}_{\text{final}} = SV - 1024
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** If a question asks *"Why is DICOM stored value offset by -1024?"*, the answer is: to allow signed integers in older 12-bit scanners while covering the full HU range. The DICOM standard reserves $SV = 0$ for air ($-1024$ HU) and $SV = 1024$ for water ($0$ HU).

## 2. Analytical Matrix: Preprocessing Decisions for Each Modality

This matrix is what a KTU examiner expects in a *"Discuss the preprocessing pipeline for [modality]"* question.

| Step | X-Ray (2D) | CT (3D) | MRI (3D) | Ultrasound | Histopathology |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Intensity Normalization** | Histogram equalization | Window/level to $[-1000, +400]$ HU | Nyúl-Udupa or z-score per sequence | Log compression then rescale | Color deconvolution (H&E stain) |
| **Spatial Resampling** | Match to standard $224 \times 224$ or $512 \times 512$ | $1 \times 1 \times 1$ mm isotropic | $1 \times 1 \times 1$ mm isotropic | Resample to fixed pixel pitch | Patch-based at $256 \times 256$ |
| **Bias Field Correction** | Not applicable | Not applicable | **N4ITK mandatory** | Not applicable | Stain normalization (Macenko, Reinhard) |
| **Denoising** | Optional (Gaussian, BM3D) | Adaptive denoising for low-dose | Denoising CNNs | Speckle reduction (wavelet) | Optional |
| **Augmentation** | Rotation, flip, scale, intensity jitter | Rotation, flip, elastic deformation | Same as CT, plus gamma correction | Same as CT, plus phase augmentation | Stain jitter, color perturbation |
| **Annotation Tool** | LabelImg, CVAT | 3D Slicer, MONAI Label | 3D Slicer, ITK-SNAP | Specialized (e.g., CAMUS) | QuPath, ASAP |
| **Common AI Task** | Classification (DenseNet, EfficientNet) | Detection + Segmentation (U-Net, RetinaNet) | Segmentation (U-Net, nnU-Net, SwinUNETR) | Segmentation + Classification | Cell detection + Classification |

## 3. Derivation: Dice Loss Gradient (Why Dice Loss is Used)

For a **binary segmentation** problem with predicted probability map $\hat{y}_{i}$ and ground truth $y_i$ over $N$ pixels:

$$
\text{Dice} = \frac{2 \sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i \hat{y}_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \hat{y}_i}
$$

Define $L = 1 - \text{Dice}$. The gradient with respect to the logits (pre-sigmoid) $z_i$ is:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial z_i} = -\frac{\partial \text{Dice}}{\partial \hat{y}_i} \cdot \sigma'(z_i)
$$

Where $\sigma(z_i) = \hat{y}_i$ is the sigmoid function and $\sigma'(z_i) = \hat{y}_i (1 - \hat{y}_i)$.

Computing the partial derivative of Dice:

$$
\frac{\partial \text{Dice}}{\partial \hat{y}_i} = \frac{2 y_i \left( \sum y_j + \sum \hat{y}_j \right) - 2 \hat{y}_i \left( 2 \sum y_j \hat{y}_j \right)}{\left( \sum y_j + \sum \hat{y}_j \right)^2}
$$

For class imbalance (which is the norm in medical imaging — e.g., 1% tumor pixels), the **Dice loss** provides much stronger gradients on the rare class than the binary cross-entropy:

$$
L_{\text{BCE}} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y_i \log \hat{y}_i + (1 - y_i) \log(1 - \hat{y}_i) \right]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The Dice loss was originally proposed for 3D medical volumes (Milletari et al., 2016, **V-Net**). It handles the severe class imbalance that is endemic to medical imaging (small tumors, thin vessels, rare pathologies).

## 4. Derivation: Sampling Rate Trade-off (Bandwidth vs. Storage)

For a 24-hour Holter ECG recording:

- Sampling rate: $f_s = 250$ Hz
- Resolution: $n = 12$ bits/sample
- Channels: $C = 2$ (or 12 for clinical Holter)

$$
\text{Data rate} = f_s \cdot n \cdot C
$$

$$
\text{Data rate} = 250 \, \text{Hz} \times 12 \, \text{bits} \times 2 = 6000 \, \text{bits/s} = 750 \, \text{bytes/s}
$$

$$
\text{24-hour volume} = 750 \, \text{B/s} \times 86400 \, \text{s} = 64{,}800{,}000 \, \text{B} \approx 61.8 \, \text{MB}
$$

This explains why **Holter monitors** are practical for outpatient use, while **continuous video EEG** (typically 200–500 Hz × 32–256 channels) requires **TB-scale storage** in an epilepsy monitoring unit.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Part A (3 marks):** A common short question is *"A 12-lead ECG is sampled at 500 Hz with 16-bit resolution. Calculate the data rate per second."* The model answer: $12 \times 500 \times 16 = 96{,}000$ bits/s = **12 KB/s** (or 12 kB/s).

## 5. Worked Example: Conversion from Hounsfield to Normalized AI Input

**Problem:** A lung nodule detection AI expects input CT slices windowed to $[-1000, +400]$ HU, then normalized to $[0, 1]$. A voxel has stored DICOM value $SV = -700$.

**Step 1:** Convert SV to HU using rescale slope $m=1$ and intercept $b=-1024$:

$$
HU = 1 \cdot (-700) + (-1024) = -1724 \, \text{HU}
$$

> Wait — this is below air. This indicates the voxel is likely outside the body or there is an error. The intercept is sometimes $-1024$ only for the stored value range; **clinical CT systems typically use $b = -1000$**, so:

$$
HU = 1 \cdot (-700) + (-1000) = -1700 \, \text{HU}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply lung window:

$$
HU_{\text{clipped}} = \max(-1000, \min(400, -1700)) = -1000
$$

The voxel is below the lung window — clamp to $-1000$ (air).

**Step 3:** Normalize to $[0, 1]$:

$$
x_{\text{norm}} = \frac{HU_{\text{clipped}} - (-1000)}{400 - (-1000)} = \frac{0}{1400} = 0
$$

This is correctly mapped to black (air) in the input tensor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For Part B (14 marks):** When asked to *"Describe the full pipeline from raw CT acquisition to AI inference,"* the model answer must include: (i) Radon transform / FBP, (ii) HU conversion, (iii) windowing, (iv) resampling, (v) normalization, (vi) inference, (vii) post-processing (e.g., connected components, NMS), (viii) DICOM-SR output.
