| $\mathcal{T}_T = \{\mathcal{Y}_T, f_T(\cdot)\}$ | Target task definition | Label space $\mathcal{Y}_T$ |
| $\theta_{final} = \theta_{pretrained} + \Delta\theta$ | Fine-tuning weight update rule | Vectors in $\mathbb{R}^d$ |
| $\theta_{final} = \theta_{pretrained}$ | Feature extraction (no weight update) | Frozen |
| $\eta_{FT} \ll \eta_{scratch}$ | Fine-tuning learning rate (very small) | $\eta_{FT} \approx 1 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| $\eta_{scratch}$ | From-scratch learning rate (large) | $\eta_{scratch} \approx 1 \times 10^{-2}$ |
| $\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_c \log(\hat{y}_c)$ | Cross-Entropy loss for classification | Unitless, $\mathcal{L}_{CE} \geq 0$ |
| $\theta^* = \arg\min_\theta \mathcal{L}_{CE}(f_\theta(x), y)$ | Optimization objective | $\theta^* \in \mathbb{R}^d$ |
| $\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \theta}$ | Gradient used to update $\Delta\theta$ | Vector in $\mathbb{R}^d$ |

> **Note on $\Delta\theta$:** In fine-tuning, we initialize $\theta$ at $\theta_{pretrained}$ and then iteratively update using **Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD)** or **Adam**:
> $$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}_T(\theta_t)$$

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Impact in Medical AI:**
> - **Faster Convergence:** Fine-tuned models reach 90%+ accuracy in **10–20 epochs** vs. **100+ epochs** from scratch.
> - **Lower Compute Cost:** Enables training of sophisticated CNNs on **single consumer GPUs** (e.g., NVIDIA RTX 3060 with **12 GB VRAM**) instead of data-center clusters.
> - **Democratization of AI:** Hospitals in low-resource settings can build diagnostic tools using **public pre-trained weights** rather than collecting millions of local samples.
> - **Regulatory Acceptance:** FDA-cleared AI radiology tools (e.g., **IDx-DR** for diabetic retinopathy) rely heavily on transfer-learned backbones.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From-Scratch Training vs. Transfer Learning Loss

**Step 1 — Define the source-domain empirical risk.**

The standard supervised learning objective on the source domain $\mathcal{D}_S$ is to minimize the empirical risk over $N_S$ samples:

$$
\mathcal{L}_S(\theta) = \frac{1}{N_S} \sum_{i=1}^{N_S} \ell\left(f_\theta(x_i^S), y_i^S\right)
$$

Here, $\ell(\cdot,\cdot)$ is a per-sample loss function, $f_\theta$ is the model parameterized by weights $\theta$, and the goal is to find:

$$
\theta_S^* = \arg\min_\theta \mathcal{L}_S(\theta)
$$

**Step 2 — Recognize the data scarcity challenge in the target domain.**

The medical (target) dataset contains only $N_T$ samples, where typically $N_T \ll N_S$. A from-scratch model would optimize:

$$
\theta_T^{(scratch)*} = \arg\min_\theta \mathcal{L}_T(\theta) = \arg\min_\theta \frac{1}{N_T} \sum_{i=1}^{N_T} \ell\left(f_\theta(x_i^T), y_i^T\right)
$$

**Textual Explanation:** With $N_T$ being small (e.g., 500 MRI slices), the model has insufficient data to learn robust hierarchical features, leading to severe **overfitting** and poor **generalization**.

**Step 3 — Introduce the transfer learning initialization.**

Instead of initializing $\theta$ randomly (e.g., He initialization or Xavier initialization), we initialize using the source-trained weights:

$$
\theta^{(0)} = \theta_S^*
$$

This initialization captures universal low-level patterns: edge detectors, Gabor-like filters, color blob detectors, and texture operators.

**Step 4 — Formulate the fine-tuning objective.**

The transfer learning optimization is now:

$$
\theta_T^{(TL)*} = \arg\min_\theta \mathcal{L}_T(\theta) \quad \text{subject to} \quad \theta^{(0)} = \theta_S^*
$$

**Textual Explanation:** We are searching for target weights that minimize target loss, but starting from a much better point in the loss landscape.

**Step 5 — Express the update rule (Adam / SGD).**

Using gradient descent with a small learning rate $\eta$:

$$
\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}_T(\theta_t)
$$

The update magnitude $\Delta\theta = \eta \cdot \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}_T$ is intentionally kept small so that we do not catastrophically forget the pre-learned features.

**Step 6 — Frozen (feature extraction) case.**

If we freeze the backbone parameters, then the gradient with respect to the backbone is set to zero, leaving only the classifier head trainable:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_T}{\partial \theta_{backbone}} = 0, \quad \theta_{backbone} = \theta_S^*
$$

Only the new head parameters $\theta_{head}$ are updated.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Layer-by-Layer Behavior

> [!NOTE]
> **What each layer learns, and how it transfers:**

| Layer Group | Layer Type | Features Learned | Transfer Strategy | Rationale |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Layer Block 1** (Early) | Conv1 + Pool1 | Edges, color blobs, gradients | **Always Frozen** | Universal low-level features; identical across natural and medical images |
| **Layer Block 2** (Mid-Early) | Conv2 + Pool2 | Textures, simple shapes (circles, lines) | **Usually Frozen** | Task-agnostic; still highly transferable |
| **Layer Block 3** (Middle) | Conv3 + Pool3 | Object parts (lobes, vessels) | **Optional freeze** | Domain-specific; sometimes unfrozen with small $\eta$ |
| **Layer Block 4** (Mid-Late) | Conv4 + Pool4 | Object-level semantics (organ shapes) | **Fine-tuned with low $\eta$** | Needs adaptation to medical context |
| **Layer Block 5** (Late) | Conv5 + Pool5 | High-level disease patterns (nodules, opacities) | **Always trainable** | Highly task-specific to medical classification |
| **Classifier Head** | Fully-Connected + Softmax | Class probability output | **Replaced and re-initialized** | Output dimension must match medical classes (e.g., 2 for binary: malignant vs. benign) |

### 3.3 Quantitative Comparison: Convergence Behavior

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation of Convergence Rate Difference**

For a model trained from scratch on $N_T$ samples, the generalization error bound (PAC-Bayes style) is approximately:

$$
\epsilon_{scratch} \approx \mathcal{O}\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{C \cdot d}{N_T}}\right)
$$

where $d$ is the model complexity (number of parameters) and $C$ is a constant. For transfer learning with $N_S \gg N_T$ source samples and effective dimensionality reduction $\tilde{d} < d$:

$$
\epsilon_{TL} \approx \mathcal{O}\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{C \cdot \tilde{d}}{N_T + \alpha \cdot N_S}}\right)
$$

**Textual Explanation:** Because the source dataset contributes $\alpha \cdot N_S$ to effective sample size (where $0 < \alpha \leq 1$ is a transfer coefficient), the denominator grows, shrinking the error bound.

**Step 1 — Substitute realistic numbers.**

Assume $N_T = 1000$, $N_S = 1{,}400{,}000$, $d = 25 \times 10^{6}$ (ResNet-50), $\tilde{d} = 5 \times 10^{6}$, and $C = 1$:

$$
\epsilon_{scratch} \propto \sqrt{\frac{25 \times 10^{6}}{1000}} = \sqrt{25000} \approx 158.1
$$

$$
\epsilon_{TL} \propto \sqrt{\frac{5 \times 10^{6}}{1000 + 1.0 \times 1{,}400{,}000}} = \sqrt{\frac{5 \times 10^{6}}{1{,}401{,}000}} \approx \sqrt{3.57} \approx 1.89
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the ratio.**

$$
\frac{\epsilon_{TL}}{\epsilon_{scratch}} \approx \frac{1.89}{158.1} \approx 0.012
$$

**Textual Interpretation:** The transfer-learned model has roughly **1.2% of the generalization error** of the from-scratch model — a **~83× improvement** in error magnitude, justifying why transfer learning is standard practice in medical AI pipelines.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Transfer Learning Pipeline for Medical Imaging

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Domain ImageNet 1.4M Images 1000 Classes] --> B[Pre-trained CNN Backbone ResNet50 VGG16 InceptionV3]
    B --> C[Learned Weights theta pretrained]
    C --> D{Transfer Strategy Selection}
    D -->|Strategy 1 Feature Extraction| E1[Freeze Backbone Layers]
    D -->|Strategy 2 Fine Tuning| E2[Unfreeze Top Layers with Low LR]
    E1 --> F1[Train New Classifier Head on Medical Data]
    E2 --> F2[Update All Unfrozen Layers with eta 1e-4]
    F1 --> G[Medical Image Dataset Chest Xray CT MRI Histopathology]
    F2 --> G
    G --> H[Validation and Hyperparameter Tuning]
    H --> I[Final Deployed Medical AI Model]
    I --> J[Clinical Use Diagnosis Triage Screening]
```

### 4.2 Feature Extraction vs. Fine-Tuning — Side-by-Side Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FE["FEATURE EXTRACTION APPROACH"]
        FE1[Input Xray] --> FE2[Conv1 Frozen]
        FE2 --> FE3[Conv2 Frozen]
        FE3 --> FE4[Conv3 Frozen]
        FE4 --> FE5[Conv4 Frozen]
        FE5 --> FE6[Conv5 Frozen]
        FE6 --> FE7[New FC Head Trainable]
        FE7 --> FE8[Output Class Prediction]
    end
    subgraph FT["FINE TUNING APPROACH"]
        FT1[Input Xray] --> FT2[Conv1 Frozen]
        FT2 --> FT3[Conv2 Frozen]
        FT3 --> FT4[Conv3 Frozen]
        FT4 --> FT5[Conv4 Trainable Low LR]
        FT5 --> FT6[Conv5 Trainable Low LR]
        FT6 --> FT7[New FC Head Trainable High LR]
        FT7 --> FT8[Output Class Prediction]
    end
```

### 4.3 Domain Adaptation in Cross-Modality Medical Imaging

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DA1[Source Domain D_S CT Scan Images] --> DA2[Source Pretrained Model CT backbone]
    DA2 --> DA3{Feature Alignment Module}
    DA3 --> DA4[Adversarial Domain Discriminator]
    DA3 --> DA5[Target Domain D_T MRI Scan Images]
    DA5 --> DA6[Shared Encoder Backbone]
    DA6 --> DA7[Disease Classifier]
    DA4 --> DA8[Adversarial Loss L adv]
    DA7 --> DA9[Classification Loss L cls]
    DA8 --> DA10[Total Loss L total equals L cls minus lambda L adv]
    DA9 --> DA10
    DA10 --> DA11[Adapted MRI Diagnostic Model]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Transfer Learning Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Stage 1 Data Preparation] --> S2[Stage 2 Model Selection]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3 Weight Initialization from Pretrained]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4 Layer Freezing Strategy]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 5 Replace Classifier Head]
    S5 --> S6[Stage 6 Training with Differential Learning Rates]
    S6 --> S7[Stage 7 Validation on Medical Holdout Set]
    S7 --> S8{Performance Satisfactory}
    S8 -->|Yes| S9[Stage 8 Clinical Deployment]
    S8 -->|No| S10[Stage 9 Hyperparameter Tuning]
    S10 --> S4
    S9 --> S11[Continuous Monitoring and Retraining]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**
**Define transfer learning. List any four pre-trained CNN architectures commonly used for medical image analysis.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Transfer learning is a deep learning technique where a model trained on a large source dataset is reused as the starting point for a related target task, especially when the target dataset is small.

Four pre-trained architectures:
1. **VGG-16 / VGG-19** — simple sequential structure, good for feature extraction.
2. **ResNet-50 / ResNet-101** — uses residual connections to mitigate vanishing gradients.
3. **InceptionV3** — multi-scale feature extraction via parallel convolutions.
4. **DenseNet-121** — dense connectivity for feature reuse.
5. *(Bonus)* **EfficientNet** — compound scaling of depth, width, and resolution.

> **Marking Scheme:** 1 mark for definition + 0.5 mark each for four architectures.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**
**Differentiate between feature extraction and fine-tuning in transfer learning with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Feature Extraction | Fine-Tuning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Trainable Parameters** | Only the new classifier head | All (or selected upper) layers |
| **Learning Rate** | Higher for new head | Very low (e.g., $1 \times 10^{-4}$) for backbone |
| **Use Case** | Very small medical datasets | Moderately sized medical datasets |
| **Risk of Overfitting** | Low | Higher if $\eta$ is too large |
| **Example** | Detecting pneumonia with 300 X-rays | Tumor classification with 5000 MRI slices |

> **Marking Scheme:** 1 mark for the comparison table + 1 mark for one valid difference + 1 mark for example.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Students often confuse **transfer learning** with **multi-task learning** — they are different. Transfer learning uses a pre-trained model as a starting point; multi-task learning trains several tasks jointly.
> 2. Always specify the **source domain**, **source task**, **target domain**, and **target task** in definitions.
> 3. Do NOT write the same answer for both feature extraction and fine-tuning — examiners will award **0 marks** for repetition.
> 4. Diagrams carry **1–2 marks**; never skip them in 7-mark sub-questions.
> 5. Spell out the **mathematical formulation**; do not skip the source and target domain notation.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**(a) Explain the mathematical formulation of transfer learning. Define the source domain, target domain, source task, and target task with a medical imaging example. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1.5 Marks) — Define domain and task triplets:**

A domain is defined as $\mathcal{D} = \{\mathcal{X}, P(X)\}$, where $\mathcal{X}$ is the feature space and $P(X)$ is the marginal probability distribution over the feature space. A task is $\mathcal{T} = \{\mathcal{Y}, f(\cdot)\}$, where $\mathcal{Y}$ is the label space and $f(\cdot)$ is the predictive function.

**Step 2 (1.5 Marks) — Specify source and target:**

For our medical example:
- **Source Domain $\mathcal{D}_S$:** ImageNet — feature space $\mathcal{X}_S$ of natural RGB images, $P(X_S)$ the natural-image distribution, with $N_S = 1{,}400{,}000$ samples.
- **Source Task $\mathcal{T}_S$:** 1000-class object classification, $f_S(\cdot)$ learned by ResNet-50.
- **Target Domain $\mathcal{D}_T$:** ChestX-ray14 dataset — feature space $\mathcal{X}_T$ of grayscale X-ray images, $P(X_T)$ the X-ray distribution, with $N_T = 112{,}120$ images.
- **Target Task $\mathcal{T}_T$:** 14-class pathology classification (e.g., pneumonia, effusion, nodule).

**Step 3 (2 Marks) — State the transfer learning condition:**

Transfer learning is applicable when $\mathcal{D}_S \neq \mathcal{D}_T$ (natural vs. medical images) AND/OR $\mathcal{T}_S \neq \mathcal{T}_T$ (1000 ImageNet classes vs. 14 pathology classes). Both conditions hold in our case.

**Step 4 (2 Marks) — Optimization formulation:**

The objective is to learn the target function $f_T(\cdot)$ by leveraging source knowledge:

$$
\theta_T^* = \arg\min_\theta \mathcal{L}_T(\theta) \quad \text{with} \quad \theta^{(0)} = \theta_S^*
$$

$$
\mathcal{L}_T(\theta) = \frac{1}{N_T} \sum_{i=1}^{N_T} \ell(f_\theta(x_i^T), y_i^T)
$$

**Conclusion:** This approach achieves higher accuracy with less compute and fewer labeled medical samples.

---

**(b) Discuss the architecture of a pre-trained ResNet-50 model adapted for lung nodule classification. Explain layer freezing, learning rate scheduling, and the choice of optimizer. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 Marks) — ResNet-50 base architecture:**

ResNet-50 contains 49 convolutional layers plus 1 fully-connected layer, organized into **5 stages**. Each stage has convolutional blocks with **identity skip connections** that enable training of very deep networks without vanishing gradients. The final output is a 1000-class softmax for ImageNet.

**Step 2 (2 Marks) — Adaptation to medical task:**

- **Step 2a:** Remove the original FC layer (1000 outputs) and replace with a new FC layer having $C = 2$ outputs (benign vs. malignant nodule), initialized randomly.
- **Step 2b:** Freeze **Stage 1, Stage 2, and Stage 3** (early and middle layers) to preserve generic edge and texture detectors.
- **Step 2c:** Unfreeze **Stage 4 and Stage 5** for fine-tuning on lung CT patches.

**Step 3 (1.5 Marks) — Learning rate scheduling:**

Use **differential learning rates**:
- Frozen layers: $\eta = 0$ (no updates).
- Stage 4: $\eta = 1 \times 10^{-5}$.
- Stage 5: $\eta = 5 \times 10^{-5}$.
- New FC head: $\eta = 1 \times 10^{-3}$.

Apply a **cosine annealing scheduler** that decays $\eta$ gradually to zero over 30 epochs.

**Step 4 (1.5 Marks) — Optimizer and loss:**

- **Optimizer:** Adam (adaptive moment estimation) with $\beta_1 = 0.9$, $\beta_2 = 0.999$, and weight decay $\lambda = 1 \times 10^{-4}$ for L2 regularization.
- **Loss:** Binary cross-entropy $\mathcal{L}_{BCE} = -[y \log(\hat{y}) + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})]$.
- **Augmentations:** Random rotation (±15°), horizontal flips, intensity jittering, and mixup ($\alpha = 0.2$) to combat overfitting on small datasets.

**Conclusion:** The transfer-learned ResNet-50 typically achieves **AUC > 0.95** on LIDC-IDRI lung nodule dataset, compared to **AUC ≈ 0.82** when trained from scratch with the same data.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2

**(a) Compare and contrast feature extraction and fine-tuning approaches in transfer learning. Use neat diagrams to support your answer. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 Marks) — Concept definitions:**

- **Feature Extraction:** The pre-trained CNN is used as a fixed feature extractor. The convolutional layers' weights are kept frozen, and only a new classifier (e.g., logistic regression, small MLP, or SVM) is trained on the extracted feature vectors.
- **Fine-Tuning:** All (or selected upper) layers of the pre-trained network are unfrozen and their weights are updated using backpropagation with a small learning rate.

**Step 2 (2 Marks) — Architectural diagram (textual representation):**

- **Feature Extraction path:** Input → [Frozen Conv1–Conv5] → Flatten → [Trainable FC] → Output.
- **Fine-Tuning path:** Input → [Frozen Conv1–Conv3] → [Trainable Conv4–Conv5] → Flatten → [Trainable FC] → Output.

**Step 3 (1.5 Marks) — Tabular comparison:**

| Parameter | Feature Extraction | Fine-Tuning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Trainable parameters | $\approx 10^4$ to $10^5$ | $\approx 10^6$ to $10^7$ |
| Training time per epoch | $< 1$ minute | $5$ to $15$ minutes |
| Data requirement | $< 500$ samples | $> 1000$ samples |
| Risk of catastrophic forgetting | None | High if $\eta$ too large |
| GPU memory | Low (**2–4 GB**) | Moderate (**8–12 GB**) |

**Step 4 (1.5 Marks) — When to use which:**

- Use **feature extraction** when the medical dataset is **very small** or when the source domain (ImageNet) is **semantically far** from the medical domain (e.g., histopathology patches differ greatly from natural photos).
- Use **fine-tuning** when there is **enough medical data** (typically $>1000$ labeled images per class) to support weight updates without overfitting.

**Conclusion:** A common hybrid strategy is to start with feature extraction for a few epochs, then unfreeze the top convolutional block and fine-tune with a low learning rate — this is the **progressive unfreezing** approach.

---

**(b) Explain domain adaptation in transfer learning for cross-modality medical imaging. Discuss the Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) approach with mathematical formulation. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1.5 Marks) — Define domain adaptation:**

Domain adaptation is a sub-field of transfer learning that handles the case where the source domain $\mathcal{D}_S$ (e.g., **CT scans**) and target domain $\mathcal{D}_T$ (e.g., **MRI scans**) have **different feature distributions** ($P(X_S) \neq P(X_T)$) but share the same task (e.g., tumor segmentation).

**Step 2 (2 Marks) — The distribution mismatch problem:**

Naïvely applying a model trained on CT to MRI images leads to **performance degradation** because:
- Intensity ranges differ (Hounsfield Units vs. MRI signal intensities).
- Contrast mechanisms differ (X-ray attenuation vs. magnetic relaxation).
- Texture statistics differ.

**Step 3 (2 Marks) — Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) formulation:**

MMD is a kernel-based statistical test that measures the distance between two probability distributions in a Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Space (RKHS):

$$
\text{MMD}^2(\mathcal{D}_S, \mathcal{D}_T) = \left\Vert \frac{1}{N_S}\sum_{i=1}^{N_S} \phi(x_i^S) - \frac{1}{N_T}\sum_{j=1}^{N_T} \phi(x_j^T) \right\Vert_{\mathcal{H}}^2
$$

where $\phi(\cdot)$ is the feature map induced by a kernel $k(\cdot,\cdot)$, commonly the **Gaussian kernel**:

$$
k(x, y) = \exp\left(-\frac{\Vert x - y \Vert^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
$$

**Step 4 (1.5 Marks) — Combined loss for domain adaptation:**

The total loss combines classification loss on labeled source data AND MMD alignment between source and target features:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{total} = \mathcal{L}_{cls}(\theta) + \lambda \cdot \text{MMD}^2(\mathcal{D}_S, \mathcal{D}_T)
$$

where $\lambda \geq 0$ is a trade-off hyperparameter (typical value: $\lambda = 0.1$ to $1.0$). Minimizing this loss aligns the feature distributions while preserving class discriminability.

**Conclusion:** MMD-based domain adaptation enables cross-modality tumor segmentation models that perform consistently on both CT and MRI, even with minimal labeled target data, by leveraging large amounts of labeled source data.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read 5 minutes before the exam):**

- ✅ **Definition:** Transfer learning reuses a model pre-trained on a large source domain to solve a related target task.
- ✅ **Two pre-requisites:** Either $\mathcal{D}_S \neq \mathcal{D}_T$ OR $\mathcal{T}_S \neq \mathcal{T}_T$ (or both).
- ✅ **Two main strategies:** **Feature extraction** (freeze backbone, train new head) and **Fine-tuning** (unfreeze top layers, train with very small learning rate).
- ✅ **Why medical imaging needs it:** Annotated medical data is **scarce and expensive**; pre-trained ImageNet models provide universal features.
- ✅ **Standard pre-trained backbones:** VGG-16, ResNet-50, InceptionV3, DenseNet-121, EfficientNet-B0.
- ✅ **Mathematical notation:** $\mathcal{D}_S = \{\mathcal{X}_S, P(X_S)\}$ and $\mathcal{T}_S = \{\mathcal{Y}_S, f_S(\cdot)\}$; same for $\mathcal{D}_T$ and $\mathcal{T}_T$.
- ✅ **Fine-tuning rule:** $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}_T(\theta_t)$, with $\eta \approx 1 \times 10^{-4}$ (very small).
- ✅ **Frozen layers:** Always freeze **early layers** (edges, textures) — they transfer universally.
- ✅ **Reinitialize the classifier head:** Output dimension must match the medical task (e.g., 2 for binary, $C$ for multi-class).
- ✅ **Domain adaptation:** Used when source and target modalities differ (CT vs. MRI); MMD measures distribution distance.
- ✅ **Common mistake to avoid:** Using the same learning rate for backbone and new head — always use **differential learning rates**.
- ✅ **Exam tip:** Always draw a **block diagram** of the transfer learning pipeline in 7-mark answers; it earns **1–2 extra marks**.
- ✅ **Loss function:** Cross-Entropy $\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_c \log(\hat{y}_c)$ for classification problems.
- ✅ **Optimizer:** Adam with weight decay ($1 \times 10^{-4}$) is the default for fine-tuning medical CNNs.
- ✅ **Data augmentation:** Always use rotation, flipping, and intensity jittering to combat the small medical dataset size.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Prediction and Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT862 - AI FOR MEDICAL IMAGE ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Prediction and Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **AI for Medical Image Analysis**, **Prediction** and **Classification** are two closely related but distinct supervised learning paradigms used to derive clinically meaningful decisions from imaging data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification** is the task of assigning a discrete categorical label $y \in \{1, 2, \ldots, K\}$ to an input medical image $\mathbf{x}$ (e.g., tumor vs. non-tumor, benign vs. malignant, type of lesion).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Prediction** (often referring to regression or risk-probability estimation) is the task of estimating a continuous-valued output $\hat{y} \in \mathbb{R}$ or a probability score $P(y \vert \mathbf{x})$ associated with a medical image (e.g., survival time in months, tumor volume, probability of malignancy).

Formally, both tasks seek to learn a mapping function:

$$f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$$

where $\mathcal{X}$ is the input image feature space and $\mathcal{Y}$ is the output label/probability space. The mapping is approximated by a parametric model $f_{\theta}(\mathbf{x})$, with parameters $\theta$ optimized by minimizing a loss function $\mathcal{L}(\hat{y}, y)$.

| Term | Output Type | Example in Medical Imaging |
|---|---|---|
| Binary Classification | $y \in \{0, 1\}$ | Cancer (1) / No Cancer (0) |
| Multi-class Classification | $y \in \{1, 2, \ldots, K\}$ | Glioma, Meningioma, Pituitary, None |
| Regression (Prediction) | $y \in \mathbb{R}$ | Estimated Glomerular Filtration Rate (eGFR) |
| Probability Estimation | $P(y \vert \mathbf{x}) \in [0,1]$ | Malignancy risk score |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **radiologist** standing in front of a CT scan film:

- **Classification** is like placing the film into one of **three labeled drawers**: "Normal," "Suspicious," or "Malignant." You decide *which category* it belongs to — a discrete, hard decision.

- **Prediction** is like writing a **percentage likelihood** (e.g., "78% chance of malignancy") or estimating a **continuous measurement** (e.g., "lesion volume ≈ 3.2 cm³") on a report. You are estimating a *numeric quantity* — a soft, graded estimate.

> [!NOTE]
> A single model can often do both. A logistic regression outputs a probability (prediction) and then applies a threshold (typically **0.5**) to make a class decision (classification). This duality is central to medical AI pipelines.

**Key Standard Metrics used throughout this module:**
- **Sensitivity (Recall)** = $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ — proportion of actual positives correctly identified
- **Specificity** = $\frac{TN}{TN+FP}$ — proportion of actual negatives correctly identified
- **Accuracy** = $\frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ — overall correct predictions
- **Dice Coefficient** = $\frac{2 \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$ — overlap measure for segmentation-based prediction
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Prediction and Classification Tasks

### 2.1.1 Classification Tasks in Medical Imaging

Classification in medical imaging operates at three hierarchical levels:

1. **Image-Level Classification** — The whole image is assigned one label.
   - *Example:* Chest X-ray → Pneumonia / No Pneumonia

2. **Region/Object-Level Classification** — A localized region (often output of a detector) is classified.
   - *Example:* Detected lung nodule → Benign / Malignant

3. **Pixel/Voxel-Level Classification (Semantic Segmentation)** — Every pixel/voxel is assigned a class label.
   - *Example:* Brain MRI voxel → White Matter / Gray Matter / CSF / Tumor

### 2.1.2 Prediction Tasks in Medical Imaging

Prediction tasks produce continuous or probabilistic outputs:

1. **Regression** — Predicts continuous clinical values.
   - *Example:* Cardiac MRI → Left Ventricular Ejection Fraction (LVEF) %

2. **Survival Prediction** — Estimates time-to-event data.
   - *Example:* CT scan → 5-year survival probability for oncology patients

3. **Risk Scoring** — Outputs probability of disease onset or progression.
   - *Example:* Retinal fundus image → Cardiovascular risk score

## 2.2 Core Classifier Families Used in Medical Imaging

### 2.2.1 Classical (Hand-crafted Feature) Classifiers

- **k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN):** Classifies a sample by majority vote of its $k$ closest training samples in feature space, using distance metric $d(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$.
- **Support Vector Machine (SVM):** Finds the maximum-margin hyperplane separating classes in (possibly kernel-transformed) feature space.
- **Random Forest (RF):** Ensemble of decision trees trained on bootstrapped samples with random feature subsets; final class is by majority vote.
- **Logistic Regression (LR):** Linear model with sigmoid activation for binary outcomes; outputs calibrated probabilities.

### 2.2.2 Deep Learning Classifiers

- **Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs):** ResNet, DenseNet, EfficientNet, VGG — hierarchical feature extractors followed by fully connected classifier head.
- **Vision Transformers (ViT):** Patch-based self-attention mechanism for global context modeling.
- **Hybrid CNN-Transformer Models:** Combine local feature extraction with global attention.

### 2.2.3 Why "Why" and "How" — Engineering Rationale

- **Classical methods** require hand-crafted features (e.g., HOG, LBP, GLCM texture features) but offer interpretability and work on small datasets.
- **Deep learning** automatically learns hierarchical features but requires large labeled datasets and significant compute.
- **Why this matters in medicine:** Regulatory bodies (FDA, CE) demand *interpretability*. Hybrid pipelines (deep features + classical classifier) often balance performance and explainability.

## 2.3 Decision Boundaries and Probability Estimation

A classifier can be viewed as learning a **decision function** $g(\mathbf{x})$ and an associated **decision boundary** $g(\mathbf{x}) = 0$.

For a **binary classifier**, the decision rule is:

$$\hat{y} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } P(y=1 \vert \mathbf{x}) \geq \tau \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

where $\tau$ is the **classification threshold** (default **0.5**, but tuned for medical sensitivity/specificity trade-offs).

The probability is typically modeled via the **sigmoid (logistic) function**:

$$P(y=1 \vert \mathbf{x}) = \sigma(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b)}}$$

For **multi-class** problems, the **softmax function** generalizes sigmoid:

$$P(y=k \vert \mathbf{x}) = \frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}$$

where $z_k$ is the logit (raw score) for class $k$.

## 2.4 Loss Functions for Prediction and Classification

### 2.4.1 Cross-Entropy Loss (Classification)

For binary classification:

$$\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y_i \log(\hat{y}_i) + (1 - y_i) \log(1 - \hat{y}_i) \right]$$

For multi-class:

$$\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} y_{i,k} \log(\hat{y}_{i,k})$$

### 2.4.2 Focal Loss (Imbalanced Medical Data)

Used when class imbalance is severe (e.g., tumor vs. non-tumor pixels):

$$\mathcal{L}_{FL} = -\alpha_t (1 - \hat{y}_t)^{\gamma} \log(\hat{y}_t)$$

where $\gamma \geq 0$ is the focusing parameter and $\alpha_t$ is a balancing weight.

### 2.4.3 Mean Squared Error (Regression / Continuous Prediction)

$$\mathcal{L}_{MSE} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$$

### 2.4.4 Mean Absolute Error (Robust Regression)

$$\mathcal{L}_{MAE} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is essential for KTU 2024 Scheme examination preparation.**

| Concept | Formula | Use Case in Medical Imaging |
|---|---|---|
| Sigmoid | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | Binary classification probability |
| Softmax | $P(k) = \frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ | Multi-class classification |
| Cross-Entropy | $-\sum y \log(\hat{y})$ | Classification loss |
| Focal Loss | $-\alpha(1-\hat{y})^{\gamma}\log(\hat{y})$ | Imbalanced tumor classification |
| MSE | $\frac{1}{N}\sum(y-\hat{y})^2$ | Continuous prediction (e.g., volume) |
| Sensitivity | $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Recall for cancer detection |
| Specificity | $\frac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | True negative rate |
| Precision (PPV) | $\frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | Positive predictive value |
| F1-Score | $\frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P+R}$ | Harmonic mean of P and R |
| AUC-ROC | $\int_0^1 TPR(FPR^{-1}(t))\,dt$ | Threshold-independent classifier quality |
| Dice Coefficient | $\frac{2 \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$ | Segmentation overlap |
| IoU (Jaccard) | $\frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | Localization accuracy |
| MAE | $\frac{1}{N}\sum \vert y - \hat{y} \vert$ | Robust regression error |
| R² Score | $1 - \frac{\sum(y-\hat{y})^2}{\sum(y-\bar{y})^2$ | Explained variance for regression |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In clinical AI deployment:
- **Screening tools** (e.g., diabetic retinopathy) emphasize **high sensitivity** to avoid missing cases.
- **Diagnostic confirmation tools** emphasize **high specificity** to avoid false alarms.
- **Prognostic tools** use **C-index** (concordance index) for survival prediction.
- The **ROC curve** plots TPR vs FPR as threshold $\tau$ varies — **AUC** summarizes overall classifier discrimination.
- The **Precision-Recall curve** is preferred for heavily imbalanced datasets (e.g., rare disease screening).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Logistic Regression Decision Function (Binary Classification)

We derive the linear classifier used in classical medical image classification (e.g., lesion vs. non-lesion using hand-crafted features).

### Step 1: Linear Score

The model computes a linear combination of input features:

$$z = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = \sum_{j=1}^{d} w_j x_j + b$$

where $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is the feature vector (e.g., intensity, texture, shape descriptors), $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is the weight vector, and $b \in \mathbb{R}$ is the bias.

### Step 2: Apply Sigmoid to Get Probability

We map the unbounded score $z$ to a probability in $[0, 1]$:

$$P(y=1 \vert \mathbf{x}; \mathbf{w}, b) = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

This is the **logistic function**, motivated by the log-odds being linear in $\mathbf{x}$:

$$\log\left(\frac{P(y=1 \vert \mathbf{x})}{1 - P(y=1 \vert \mathbf{x})}\right) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b$$

### Step 3: Decision Rule

The class label is decided by thresholding the probability:

$$\hat{y} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } P(y=1 \vert \mathbf{x}) \geq \tau \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

By default, $\tau = 0.5$, but in medical imaging $\tau$ is tuned to optimize sensitivity/specificity trade-off.

### Step 4: Likelihood of Dataset

Assuming samples are i.i.d., the likelihood is:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(y_i \vert \mathbf{x}_i; \mathbf{w}, b) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} \hat{y}_i^{y_i} (1 - \hat{y}_i)^{1-y_i}$$

### Step 5: Log-Likelihood (for numerical stability)

$$\ell(\mathbf{w}, b) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y_i \log(\hat{y}_i) + (1 - y_i) \log(1 - \hat{y}_i) \right]$$

### Step 6: Negative Log-Likelihood = Cross-Entropy Loss

$$\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\frac{1}{N} \ell(\mathbf{w}, b) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y_i \log(\hat{y}_i) + (1 - y_i) \log(1 - \hat{y}_i) \right]$$

### Step 7: Gradient Descent Update

Compute gradient with respect to weights:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{CE}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (\hat{y}_i - y_i) \mathbf{x}_i$$

The parameter update rule (with learning rate $\eta$):

$$\mathbf{w} \leftarrow \mathbf{w} - \eta \cdot \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{CE}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = \mathbf{w} - \eta \cdot \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (\hat{y}_i - y_i) \mathbf{x}_i$$

This iterative process converges to the maximum-likelihood estimate of $\mathbf{w}$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Softmax for Multi-Class Classification (e.g., 4-Class Brain Tumor Classification)

Consider classifying brain MRI into: *Glioma, Meningioma, Pituitary, No Tumor*.

### Step 1: Compute Logits

For each class $k \in \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$, compute the logit (raw score):

$$z_k = \mathbf{w}_k^T \mathbf{x} + b_k$$

### Step 2: Apply Softmax to Get Class Probabilities

$$P(y = k \vert \mathbf{x}) = \frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_{j=1}^{4} e^{z_j}}$$

### Step 3: Verify Probabilities Sum to Unity

$$\sum_{k=1}^{4} P(y=k \vert \mathbf{x}) = \frac{\sum_{k=1}^{4} e^{z_k}}{\sum_{j=1}^{4} e^{z_j}} = 1$$

This is a key property of softmax used to interpret outputs as a valid probability distribution.

### Step 4: Cross-Entropy Loss for Multi-Class

With one-hot encoded labels $y_k \in \{0, 1\}$ where $\sum_k y_k = 1$:

$$\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\sum_{k=1}^{4} y_k \log(\hat{y}_k)$$

For a single sample with true class $c$ (so $y_c = 1$, others = 0), this simplifies to:

$$\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\log(\hat{y}_c) = -\log\left(\frac{e^{z_c}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}\right)$$

### Step 5: Gradient with Respect to Logit $z_k$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{CE}}{\partial z_k} = \hat{y}_k - y_k$$

This elegant form is what makes softmax + cross-entropy the standard output layer for multi-class classification.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing a Classifier

| Scenario | Recommended Classifier | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Small dataset ($N < 1000$), tabular features | SVM, Random Forest, Logistic Regression | Avoids overfitting; interpretable |
| Large image dataset ($N > 10{,}000$) | CNN (ResNet, EfficientNet) | Learns hierarchical features |
| Global context critical (e.g., whole-slide pathology) | Vision Transformer (ViT) or Hybrid | Self-attention captures long-range dependencies |
| Multi-class with mutually exclusive classes | Softmax classifier (single-label) | Probabilities sum to 1 |
| Multi-label (e.g., multiple co-existing diseases) | Sigmoid per class (independent) | Each class probability independent |
| Heavy class imbalance | Focal loss + class weighting | Reduces easy-negative dominance |
| Need for calibration (clinical deployment) | Logistic Regression, calibrated CNN | Outputs true probabilities |
| Survival / time-to-event | Cox Proportional Hazards, DeepSurv | Handles censored data |
| Continuous biomarker prediction | Regression CNN with MSE/MAE | Direct continuous output |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Medical Image Classification Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Medical Image Input CT MRI X-ray] --> B[Preprocessing Resize Normalize Denoise]
    B --> C[Feature Extraction CNN ViT Hand-crafted]
    C --> D[Feature Vector x in R^d]
    D --> E{Classifier Head}
    E -->|Softmax| F[Class Probabilities P y given x]
    E -->|Sigmoid| G[Independent Class Scores]
    E -->|Linear| H[Continuous Prediction y_hat]
    F --> I[Threshold tau and Argmax]
    G --> I
    H --> J[Clinical Decision]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Report Generation]
```

## 4.2 Prediction vs. Classification Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Medical Image] --> B[Model f_theta]
    B --> C{Output Type}
    C -->|Discrete Label| D[Classification]
    C -->|Continuous Value| E[Prediction Regression]
    C -->|Probability| F[Risk Scoring]
    D --> D1[Argmax over K classes]
    D --> D2[Apply threshold tau]
    E --> E1[Direct numeric output]
    F --> F1[Sigmoid or Softmax]
    D1 --> G[Clinical Action]
    D2 --> G
    E1 --> G
    F1 --> G
```

## 4.3 Confusion Matrix and Metric Computation Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Predictions and Ground Truth] --> B[Construct Confusion Matrix]
    B --> C[Compute TP FN FP TN]
    C --> D[Calculate Metrics]
    D --> D1[Sensitivity TP over TP plus FN]
    D --> D2[Specificity TN over TN plus FP]
    D --> D3[Precision TP over TP plus FP]
    D --> D4[F1 Score harmonic mean]
    D --> D5[AUC ROC integration]
    D --> D6[Dice for segmentation]
    D1 --> E[Clinical Performance Report]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    D6 --> E
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a CNN Classifier for Medical Imaging

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Medical Image 224x224x3] --> B[Conv Block 1 Conv BN ReLU Pool]
    B --> C[Conv Block 2 Conv BN ReLU Pool]
    C --> D[Conv Block 3 Conv BN ReLU Pool]
    D --> E[Conv Block 4 Conv BN ReLU Pool]
    E --> F[Global Average Pooling]
    F --> G[Flattened Feature Vector 512d]
    G --> H[Fully Connected Layer 256 units ReLU]
    H --> I[Dropout 0.5]
    I --> J[Fully Connected Layer K units]
    J --> K[Softmax or Sigmoid Output]
    K --> L[Loss Computation Cross Entropy or Focal]
    L --> M[Backprop and Weight Update]
    M --> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The *classifier head* (the fully connected layers after feature extraction) is the component that converts a generic feature extractor into a prediction/classification model. In transfer learning, the backbone is often frozen (pre-trained on ImageNet or RadImageNet) and only the head is retrained on the medical dataset — a common and compute-efficient strategy in KTU-level projects.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between classification and prediction in medical image analysis with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Classification | Prediction |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Discrete class label | Continuous value or probability |
| Loss function | Cross-entropy | MSE / MAE |
| Example | Classifying chest X-ray as Normal/Pneumonia (binary) | Predicting left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) % from cardiac MRI (regression) |

Classification assigns a *category*; prediction estimates a *quantity*. Both are supervised learning tasks but differ in output type and evaluation metric.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the role of the softmax function in multi-class medical image classification?**

**Model Answer:**

The softmax function converts raw class scores (logits) $z_k$ into a normalized probability distribution over $K$ classes:

$$P(y=k \vert \mathbf{x}) = \frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}$$

It ensures outputs are in $[0, 1]$ and sum to 1, enabling probabilistic interpretation. In multi-class medical imaging (e.g., 4-class brain tumor classification), softmax provides class probabilities that can be thresholded or used with cross-entropy loss for training.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a CNN-based medical image classifier. Discuss the role of convolutional layers, pooling layers, and the fully connected classifier head. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the confusion matrix and derive the formulas for sensitivity, specificity, precision, F1-score, and AUC-ROC. Explain why AUC-ROC is preferred over raw accuracy in imbalanced medical datasets. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

A **CNN-based medical image classifier** has three main components:

1. **Convolutional Layers** — Apply learnable filters to extract spatial features such as edges, textures, and patterns. Each filter slides over the input image, performing convolution to produce a feature map.

   $$\mathbf{F}_{i,j,k} = \sum_{m,n} \mathbf{W}_{m,n,k} \cdot \mathbf{X}_{i+m, j+n} + b_k$$

2. **Pooling Layers** — Downsample feature maps to reduce dimensionality and provide translation invariance. Max-pooling selects the maximum value in each window:

   $$\mathbf{P}_{i,j,k} = \max_{m,n \in \text{window}} \mathbf{F}_{i+m, j+n, k}$$

3. **Fully Connected Classifier Head** — Flattened feature maps are passed to dense layers that combine features into class scores. The final layer uses softmax (multi-class) or sigmoid (binary) to produce probabilities.

4. **Training** — Cross-entropy loss is minimized via backpropagation and gradient descent.

5. **Example for medical imaging** — ResNet-50 applied to chest X-ray classification (Pneumonia / Normal / COVID-19).

#### Model Solution for (b):

The **confusion matrix** is a 2×2 table summarizing prediction outcomes:

|                | Actual Positive | Actual Negative |
|---|---|---|
| Predicted Positive | TP (True Positive) | FP (False Positive) |
| Predicted Negative | FN (False Negative) | TN (True Negative) |

**Derived metrics:**

- **Sensitivity (Recall / True Positive Rate):** $\text{Sens} = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$
- **Specificity (True Negative Rate):** $\text{Spec} = \frac{TN}{TN+FP}$
- **Precision (Positive Predictive Value):** $\text{PPV} = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$
- **F1-Score:** $F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P+R}$
- **AUC-ROC:** Area under the Receiver Operating Characteristic curve, which plots TPR vs FPR across all classification thresholds $\tau \in [0, 1]$:

  $$\text{AUC} = \int_0^1 \text{TPR}(\text{FPR}^{-1}(t))\, dt$$

**Why AUC-ROC is preferred over accuracy in imbalanced medical data:**

In a cancer screening dataset where only 1% of patients have cancer, a naive model predicting "no cancer" for all patients achieves 99% accuracy but is clinically useless (0% sensitivity). AUC-ROC evaluates the model's ability to **rank** positive cases higher than negative ones across all thresholds, independent of class prevalence. A perfect classifier has AUC = 1.0; random guessing has AUC = 0.5. Thus, AUC-ROC gives a threshold-independent, prevalence-independent assessment of discriminative power — critical for medical applications.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the end-to-end pipeline of a medical image classification system. Discuss the role of preprocessing, data augmentation, and transfer learning. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the Focal Loss function and derive its gradient. Why is it preferred over standard cross-entropy for medical image classification with severe class imbalance? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**End-to-End Medical Image Classification Pipeline:**

```
Raw Medical Image → Preprocessing → Augmentation → Train/Val/Test Split
       → CNN Backbone (Feature Extractor) → Classifier Head → Loss → Backprop → Trained Model
       → Inference on New Image → Probability → Threshold → Clinical Report
```

**Key stages:**

1. **Preprocessing** — Resize images to a standard dimension (e.g., $224 \times 224$), normalize pixel intensities to $[0,1]$ or using ImageNet statistics $\mu = [0.485, 0.456, 0.406]$, $\sigma = [0.229, 0.224, 0.225]$, apply bias correction (e.g., N4ITK for MRI), and skull-stripping where applicable.

2. **Data Augmentation** — Artificially expand training data via random rotations, flips, crops, intensity shifts, elastic deformations, and mixup. This combats overfitting in small medical datasets.

3. **Transfer Learning** — Initialize the CNN backbone with weights pre-trained on a large dataset (ImageNet or RadImageNet). Freeze early layers (generic features) and fine-tune later layers (task-specific). This drastically reduces labeled data requirements and training time.

4. **Classifier Head & Training** — Add a new fully connected head for the medical task; train with cross-entropy/focal loss using Adam optimizer.

5. **Evaluation & Deployment** — Validate on held-out test data, compute clinical metrics (sensitivity, specificity, AUC), and deploy with calibration checks.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Focal Loss Derivation:**

Standard cross-entropy for binary classification:

$$\mathcal{L}_{CE}(p_t) = -\log(p_t)$$

where $p_t = \hat{y}$ if $y = 1$, else $p_t = 1 - \hat{y}$.

The **Focal Loss** introduces a modulating factor $(1 - p_t)^{\gamma}$ to down-weight easy examples and focus training on hard negatives:

$$\mathcal{L}_{FL}(p_t) = -\alpha_t (1 - p_t)^{\gamma} \log(p_t)$$

where:
- $\gamma \geq 0$ is the **focusing parameter** (typical value: $\gamma = 2$)
- $\alpha_t \in [0, 1]$ is a **class balancing weight** to handle imbalance

**Gradient derivation** (with respect to model output $z$ where $p_t = \sigma(z)$):

Let $p = \sigma(z)$, so $dp/dz = p(1-p)$. We compute:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{FL}}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{FL}}{\partial p} \cdot \frac{\partial p}{\partial z}$$

First, $\frac{\partial p}{\partial z} = p(1-p)$.

Next, treat $p_t = p$ for class $y=1$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{FL}}{\partial p} = -\alpha_t \left[ \gamma (1-p)^{\gamma-1}(-1) \log(p) + (1-p)^{\gamma} \cdot \frac{1}{p} \right]$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{FL}}{\partial p} = \alpha_t (1-p)^{\gamma-1} \left[ \gamma \log(p) - \frac{(1-p)}{p} \right]$$

Multiplying:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{FL}}{\partial z} = \alpha_t (1-p)^{\gamma-1} \left[ \gamma \log(p) - \frac{(1-p)}{p} \right] \cdot p(1-p)$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{FL}}{\partial z} = \alpha_t (1-p)^{\gamma} \left[ \gamma p \log(p) - (1-p) \right]$$

When $p \to 1$ (easy/well-classified example), $(1-p)^{\gamma} \to 0$, gradient vanishes — this is the **focusing** behavior. When $p \to 0$ (hard example), the gradient remains large.

**Why Focal Loss is preferred for imbalanced medical data:**

In tumor detection, normal tissue pixels vastly outnumber tumor pixels (e.g., 1000:1 ratio). Standard cross-entropy is dominated by easy-negative samples, leading to a model that predicts "no tumor" everywhere. Focal Loss:
- Down-weights well-classified easy negatives via $(1-p_t)^{\gamma}$
- Preserves gradient signal from rare hard positives
- Combats the class imbalance problem without aggressive undersampling

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing classification with segmentation** — Image-level classification outputs ONE label per image; segmentation outputs a label PER PIXEL. Do not interchange these in answers.
> 2. **Forgetting threshold $\tau$ in binary classification** — Mention that the default 0.5 is rarely optimal in medicine; threshold tuning is part of the clinical deployment pipeline.
> 3. **Reporting only accuracy** — In imbalanced medical datasets, accuracy is misleading. Always pair it with sensitivity, specificity, and AUC.
> 4. **Skipping the softmax derivation** — When asked to "explain softmax," KTU examiners expect both the formula AND a verification that probabilities sum to 1.
> 5. **Confusing Focal Loss with class-weighted CE** — They are different: Focal Loss focuses on *hard examples* dynamically, while class weighting is a *static* rescaling.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Prediction and Classification**

- ☐ **Classification** = discrete label output (e.g., tumor/normal); **Prediction** = continuous value or probability output (e.g., survival time, risk score).
- ☐ Three hierarchical levels: image-level, region-level, pixel-level (segmentation).
- ☐ **Sigmoid** is used for **binary** classification; **Softmax** for **multi-class** single-label.
- ☐ Sigmoid: $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$; Softmax: $P(k) = \frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$.
- ☐ **Cross-Entropy** is the standard loss for classification: $\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\sum y \log(\hat{y})$.
- ☐ **Focal Loss** adds $(1-p_t)^{\gamma}$ factor to combat class imbalance in medical data.
- ☐ **MSE** for regression: $\mathcal{L}_{MSE} = \frac{1}{N}\sum(y-\hat{y})^2$.
- ☐ Always report **sensitivity, specificity, AUC-ROC** in medical classification — not just accuracy.
- ☐ **Dice Coefficient** = $\frac{2 \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$ for segmentation overlap; **IoU** = $\frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$.
- ☐ **Transfer learning** from ImageNet/RadImageNet is the standard practical strategy for small medical datasets.
- ☐ **Threshold tuning** is critical: medical screening favors low threshold (high sensitivity); confirmatory diagnosis favors high threshold (high specificity).
- ☐ **Calibration matters** in clinical AI — predicted probabilities must reflect true likelihoods (use Platt scaling or isotonic regression if needed).
- ☐ KTU exam tip: Always define *what* you are predicting, *which metric* you are using, and *why* that metric suits the clinical problem.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Experimental Data and Setup
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT862 - AI FOR MEDICAL IMAGE ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Experimental Data and Setup

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Experimental Data and Setup** in the context of AI for Medical Image Analysis refers to the **systematic, reproducible, and statistically valid configuration** of datasets, preprocessing pipelines, hardware infrastructure, software environments, and evaluation protocols required to rigorously train, validate, and benchmark deep learning models on medical imaging data.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)** for PEBRT862, this topic encompasses the *empirical scaffolding* upon which any credible medical AI model is built. It is not merely "loading images" — it is the principled engineering of an experiment such that reported metrics (Dice, Sensitivity, Specificity, AUC, F1) are **defensible, reproducible, and clinically meaningful**.

The four pillars of experimental setup in medical AI are:

| Pillar | Definition | KTU-Standard Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Provenance** | Origin, modality, ethics clearance of dataset | IRB Approval Number |
| **Partitioning Strategy** | How subjects are split into folds | Patient-Level Split Ratio |
| **Preprocessing Pipeline** | Normalization, resampling, augmentation | Intensity Range (e.g., HU window) |
| **Compute Substrate** | Hardware and software stack | GPU VRAM (e.g., **≥11 GB**) |

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Clinical Trial of Algorithms"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Plain English):**
> Imagine you are testing a *new drug* on patients. Before you can claim the drug works, you must:
> 1. **Describe the patients** (age, disease stage, demographics) — this is your **dataset description**.
> 2. **Split them** into a *treatment group* and a *placebo group* without mixing family members — this is **patient-level partitioning**.
> 3. **Standardize the dosage** the same way for everyone — this is **preprocessing & normalization**.
> 4. **Run the trial on standardized equipment** in a certified lab — this is your **hardware/software setup**.
> 5. **Measure outcomes** using pre-agreed clinical scales — these are your **evaluation metrics**.

In medical AI, if any of these five steps is sloppy, the entire experiment collapses. A model that scores **Dice = 0.92** on a leakage-corrupted test set is a *fictional model* — just as a drug tested on patients who were secretly also in the control group is fictional medicine.

---

### 1.3 Why This Topic is a *High-Yield* Module for KTU

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 examiners specifically test this topic because:
> - It is the **#1 source of paper retractions** in medical AI (data leakage, wrong splits).
> - It directly maps to **Course Outcome CO3**: *Design and execute experimental pipelines for medical image analysis tasks.*
> - It bridges *theoretical deep learning* with *clinical deployment reality*.

**Key constants and standard defaults** you must memorize:

- Standard split ratio: **70 / 15 / 15** (Train / Validation / Test)
- Recommended k-fold value: **k = 5** (or 10 for small datasets)
- Image resampling for CNNs: **1 mm × 1 mm × 1 mm** isotropic voxel spacing
- HU window for CT lung nodules: **[-1000, 400] Hounsfield Units**
- Random seed convention: **42** (industry standard for reproducibility)
- Minimum GPU memory for 3D medical models: **≥ 11 GB** (NVIDIA RTX 2080 Ti baseline)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decomposition of the Experimental Setup (Step-by-Step Logic)

The experimental setup is decomposed into **six sequential, interdependent stages**. Each stage must be documented in any KTU-style report.

#### Stage 1 — Dataset Characterization
- **What**: Report the *N* (number of samples), *C* (number of classes), modality (CT, MRI, X-ray, histopathology), and the *data source* (e.g., LIDC-IDRI, BraTS, CheXpert).
- **Why**: Reviewers and examiners must know *what population* the model was trained on. A model trained on 2D chest X-rays cannot be evaluated on 3D brain MRIs.
- **How**: Produce a **class distribution histogram**. Compute the **imbalance ratio**:

$$IR = \frac{\max_{c} \, N_c}{\min_{c} \, N_c}$$

where $N_c$ is the number of samples in class $c$. An $IR > 10$ is considered **severe imbalance** and mandates techniques like weighted loss or focal loss.

#### Stage 2 — Subject-Level (Patient-Level) Partitioning
- **What**: Splitting data such that **all images from a single patient fall into exactly one fold**.
- **Why**: This is the *single most important rule* in medical AI. If patient A's CT scan appears in training and their follow-up scan appears in testing, the model has seen *anatomically similar* data — this is **data leakage**, and Dice scores become inflated by **5–15 %** falsely.
- **How**: Use a *group-aware* splitter. Each unique `patient_id` is assigned to one of {Train, Val, Test} using a deterministic hash or stratified shuffle.

$$H(p) = \text{hash}(p) \mod k$$

where $p$ is the patient identifier and $k$ is the number of folds. This guarantees reproducibility without manual bookkeeping.

#### Stage 3 — Preprocessing Pipeline
- **What**: A *fixed, deterministic* sequence of transformations applied identically to training and inference samples.
- **Why**: Medical images have wildly different intensity scales (e.g., CT Hounsfield Units range from **-1000 to +3000**; MRI is arbitrary). Neural networks converge faster and generalize better on normalized inputs.
- **How**:
  - **Resampling** to a common voxel spacing (e.g., **1 mm × 1 mm × 1 mm**) using trilinear or nearest-neighbor interpolation.
  - **Intensity clipping** to a clinically relevant window.
  - **Z-score normalization** (per-image or per-dataset):

$$x_{\text{norm}} = \frac{x - \mu_{\text{dataset}}}{\sigma_{\text{dataset}}}$$

  - **Spatial resizing/cropping** to a fixed tensor size (e.g., $128 \times 128 \times 64$ for 3D).

#### Stage 4 — Data Augmentation Strategy
- **What**: On-the-fly transformations that *expand* the effective training set.
- **Why**: Medical datasets are *small* (often **<1000** patients). Augmentation combats overfitting.
- **How**: Apply *only label-preserving* geometric and photometric transforms:

  | Augmentation | Parameter Range | Label-Safe? |
  |---|---|---|
  | Random Rotation | $[-15^\circ, +15^\circ]$ | ✓ |
  | Random Flip (axial) | Horizontal & Vertical | ✓ |
  | Random Gamma Correction | $\gamma \in [0.7, 1.5]$ | ✓ |
  | Random Gaussian Noise | $\sigma \in [0, 0.1]$ | ✓ |
  | Vertical Flip (coronal brain) | — | ✗ (asymmetric anatomy) |

#### Stage 5 — Compute and Software Substrate
- **What**: The hardware (GPU model, VRAM, CPU RAM) and software (PyTorch/TensorFlow version, CUDA version, cuDNN, MONAI) stack.
- **Why**: For *reproducibility*, exact versions must be cited. A 0.3 % accuracy drop can occur between cuDNN 8.2 and 8.6 due to non-deterministic algorithms in convolutions.
- **How**: Set `torch.backends.cudnn.deterministic = True` and `torch.backends.cudnn.benchmark = False`. Log all versions in a `requirements.txt` or `environment.yml`.

#### Stage 6 — Evaluation Protocol
- **What**: Pre-defined metrics computed on a *held-out, never-seen* test set.
- **Why**: The test set is touched **exactly once** at the end of the project. Any iteration based on test performance is *cheating* — it is called **test-set leakage**.
- **How**: Use a frozen evaluation script that consumes predictions and ground-truth labels, computing Dice, Hausdorff Distance, Sensitivity, Specificity, AUC, and F1.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table contains the must-know formulas for "Experimental Data and Setup".** Master these for any 14-mark question on Module 3.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Range | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Imbalance Ratio | $IR = \dfrac{\max N_c}{\min N_c}$ | $\mathbb{R}_{\geq 1}$ | Detect severe class imbalance |
| 2 | Z-Score Normalization | $x' = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | dimensionless | MRI/CT intensity scaling |
| 3 | Min-Max Normalization | $x' = \dfrac{x - x_{\min}}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Bounded image tensors |
| 4 | HU Windowing | $x' = \text{clip}(x, a, b)$ | $[a, b]$ HU | CT preprocessing |
| 5 | Dice Coefficient (Loss) | $\text{Dice} = \dfrac{2 \vert P \cap G \vert}{\vert P \vert + \vert G \vert}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Segmentation metric |
| 6 | Patient-Level Split (Hash) | $H(p) = \text{hash}(p) \mod k$ | integer $\in [0, k-1]$ | Leakage-free fold assignment |
| 7 | Sensitivity (Recall) | $\text{Se} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Clinical detection rate |
| 8 | Specificity | $\text{Sp} = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | False-alarm control |
| 9 | F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Imbalanced classification |
| 10 | Sample Size Estimate | $n = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot p(1-p)}{e^2}$ | samples | Power analysis for dataset design |
| 11 | GPU Memory (3D volume) | $M \approx 4 \cdot B \cdot H \cdot W \cdot D \cdot k$ | bytes | VRAM planning |
| 12 | Cross-Validation Mean | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} x_i$ | metric units | Aggregate fold scores |

> **Notation legend:** $P$ = Prediction, $G$ = Ground Truth, $B$ = batch size, $H, W, D$ = height, width, depth, $k$ = constant multiplier (gradients, optimizer state ≈ **8–12×** activations for Adam).

---

### 2.3 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters Beyond the Exam Hall

In real-world medical AI engineering (e.g., deploying a tumor detector in a hospital PACS system), the experimental setup determines:

1. **Regulatory approval** (FDA, CE-Mark) — agencies audit your data splits and patient-level separation.
2. **Generalization** — a model trained on one scanner (Siemens) often fails on another (GE) due to distribution shift, which the *setup* must address.
3. **Reproducibility crisis** — a 2020 *Nature* survey found **>70 %** of medical AI researchers had failed to reproduce another's results, primarily due to opaque data splits.

Thus, mastering "Experimental Data and Setup" is not academic box-ticking — it is the **engineering ethics** of medical AI.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Computing Required Sample Size for a Medical AI Study

A common KTU question is: *"Given a desired sensitivity of 90 % with a 5 % margin of error at 95 % confidence, how many patients are required in the test set?"*

This comes from classical **binomial proportion confidence interval theory** (Wilson-style derivation).

**Step 1 — State the assumptions.**

We model the number of correctly classified patients as a Binomial random variable:

$$X \sim \text{Binomial}(n, p)$$

where $n$ is the test-set size and $p$ is the true sensitivity. The sample proportion is:

$$\hat{p} = \frac{X}{n}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Central Limit Theorem.**

For large $n$, the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}$ is approximately normal:

$$\hat{p} \sim \mathcal{N}\left(p, \, \frac{p(1-p)}{n}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Construct the 95 % confidence interval.**

The two-sided 95 % CI is:

$$p \in \left[ \hat{p} \pm Z_{0.975} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}} \right]$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the margin of error $e$.**

The margin of error is the half-width:

$$e = Z_{0.975} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}}$$

**Step 5 — Invert to solve for $n$.**

$$e^2 = Z_{0.975}^2 \cdot \frac{p(1-p)}{n}$$

Rearranging:

$$n = \frac{Z_{0.975}^2 \cdot p(1-p)}{e^2}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute KTU-standard values.**

For $p = 0.90$, $e = 0.05$, $Z_{0.975} = 1.96$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= \frac{(1.96)^2 \cdot (0.90)(1 - 0.90)}{(0.05)^2} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{3.8416 \cdot 0.09}{0.0025} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{0.345744}{0.0025} \\[6pt]
&= 138.2976
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Round up to nearest integer (conservative estimate).**

$$n = \lceil 138.2976 \rceil = 139 \text{ patients}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** To claim a sensitivity of **90 % ± 5 %** at 95 % confidence in your medical AI test set, you need at least **139 patients** in the test fold. This is why large public datasets like BraTS (~2,000 cases) are valued — they permit statistically meaningful claims.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Three Partitioning Strategies

This matrix is a favorite KTU 14-mark question pattern ("Compare and contrast train/val/test, k-fold, and leave-one-center-out").

| Criterion | Hold-Out (70/15/15) | Stratified k-Fold (k=5) | Leave-One-Center-Out (LOCO) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Computation Cost** | 1× training run | 5× training runs | *N* centers runs (expensive) |
| **Statistical Power** | Low (small test) | Medium (each fold ≈ 20 % test) | High (true external validation) |
| **Variance of Estimate** | High | Low (averaged over folds) | Medium (center-dependent) |
| **Leakage Risk** | Medium | Low (if patient-stratified) | **Zero** (different institution) |
| **Clinical Realism** | Low | Medium | **High** (multi-site deployment) |
| **KTU-Recommended When** | Dataset > 10,000 | 1,000 < *N* < 10,000 | *N* < 1,000 or multi-center data |
| **Random Seed Sensitive?** | Yes | Yes | No (deterministic by center) |

**Derivation of the LOCO variance:**

For LOCO with $C$ centers, each test set is one center, so the metric estimate for center $c$ is $\mu_c$. The reported final metric is:

$$\bar{\mu} = \frac{1}{C}\sum_{c=1}^{C} \mu_c$$

and its standard error is:

$$SE(\bar{\mu}) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{C}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1}{C-1}\sum_{c=1}^{C}(\mu_c - \bar{\mu})^2}$$

> This is the classical **between-center variance** — it quantifies how much the model's performance fluctuates *across hospitals*. A high $SE(\bar{\mu})$ means the model is *not generalizable* across institutions.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: GPU Memory Budget for a 3D U-Net

KTU 2024 often tests whether students can size a 3D experiment correctly.

**Given:**
- Input volume: $H \times W \times D = 128 \times 128 \times 64$ voxels
- Patch-based training: each patch is $64 \times 64 \times 32$
- Batch size $B = 2$
- 32-bit float precision (4 bytes per value)
- Optimizer: Adam (stores 2 momentum buffers + 1 master weight = **3×** the model size)
- Plus gradients = **1×** weights
- Plus activations (for backprop) ≈ **4–8×** weights for U-Net

**Step 1 — Memory for activations of one patch:**

$$M_{\text{act}} \approx 4 \cdot B \cdot H_p \cdot W_p \cdot D_p = 4 \cdot 2 \cdot 64 \cdot 64 \cdot 32 = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ bytes} = 1 \text{ MB}$$

**Step 2 — Add optimizer + gradient memory (assume 4× activation for safety):**

$$M_{\text{total}} = 1 \text{ MB} \times 12 = 12 \text{ MB per patch}$$

**Step 3 — For a full 3D U-Net with $\sim 30$ M parameters:**

$$M_{\text{model}} = 30 \times 10^6 \times 4 \times 4 = 480 \text{ MB}$$

**Step 4 — Total VRAM required:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{VRAM}} &\approx 480 \text{ MB (model+optim)} + 12 \text{ MB (small batch)} \\
&\approx 500 \text{ MB (far below modern GPU limits)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> However, for *full-volume* 3D inputs ($128^3$ with batch 2), activation memory can balloon to **6–10 GB**, justifying the **≥11 GB** GPU baseline cited in §1.3.

---

### 3.4 Verification: Patient-Level Splitting Proof of No Leakage

**Claim:** The deterministic hash split $H(p) = \text{hash}(p) \mod k$ guarantees that no patient appears in two folds.

**Proof by contradiction:**

Assume patient $p$ is assigned to fold $i$ and fold $j$ with $i \neq j$. Then:

$$\text{hash}(p) \mod k = i \quad \text{and} \quad \text{hash}(p) \mod k = j$$

But $\text{hash}(p)$ is a single integer, and $x \mod k$ is **single-valued**. Therefore $i = j$, contradicting the assumption. ∎

> This is the elegant mathematical guarantee behind the KTU-recommended partitioning method.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Experimental Pipeline (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Medical Images] --> B[Dataset Characterization]
    B --> C[Ethics and Anonymization Check]
    C --> D[Patient-Level Hash Splitting]
    D --> E[Train Fold 70 percent]
    D --> F[Validation Fold 15 percent]
    D --> G[Test Fold 15 percent]
    E --> H[Preprocessing Resample HU Window Normalize]
    F --> I[Preprocessing Resample HU Window Normalize]
    G --> J[Preprocessing Resample HU Window Normalize]
    H --> K[On-the-fly Data Augmentation]
    K --> L[Model Training GPU Loop]
    I --> M[Validation Evaluation per Epoch]
    M --> N{Is Metric Improving}
    N -- Yes --> L
    N -- No --> O[Save Best Checkpoint]
    O --> P[Test Set Evaluation Frozen Weights]
    P --> Q[Compute Dice, Hausdorff, AUC]
    Q --> R[Final Reported Metrics with 95 percent CI]
```

**Figure 4.1 — Full KTU-compliant experimental pipeline for a medical image segmentation task.**

---

### 4.2 Compute Substrate Stack (Layered Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Application Layer: PyTorch Lightning and MONAI]
    L2[Deep Learning Framework: PyTorch 2.1 plus cuDNN 8.9]
    L3[GPU Driver and CUDA Toolkit 12.1]
    L4[Hardware: NVIDIA A100 40GB or RTX 3090 24GB]
    L5[Host System: 64GB RAM, NVMe SSD, Ubuntu 22.04]

    L5 --> L4
    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
```

**Figure 4.2 — Vertical stack showing the software-hardware substrate for medical AI experiments. Each layer's version must be logged for reproducibility.**

---

### 4.3 Decision Tree for Choosing a Partitioning Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Dataset size N} -->|N greater than 10000| A1[Use Hold-Out 70 15 15]
    Q1 -->|1000 less than N less than 10000| A2[Use Stratified 5-Fold]
    Q1 -->|N less than 1000| Q2{Multi-center data available}
    Q2 -->|Yes| A3[Use Leave-One-Center-Out LOCO]
    Q2 -->|No| A4[Use 5-Fold with patient-level hash split]
```

**Figure 4.3 — KTU-recommended decision flow for selecting a data partitioning strategy based on dataset size and structure.**

---

### 4.4 Conceptual Map: How Each Experimental Element Maps to Course Outcomes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CO3[CO3: Design Experimental Pipelines] --> P1[Data Provenance]
    CO3 --> P2[Partitioning]
    CO3 --> P3[Preprocessing]
    CO3 --> P4[Evaluation]
    P1 --> T1[IRB and Anonymization]
    P2 --> T2[Patient-Level Hash]
    P3 --> T3[Resample and Normalize]
    P4 --> T4[Dice and 95 percent CI]
```

**Figure 4.4 — Mapping of experimental setup elements to the KTU 2024 Course Outcome CO3 and the corresponding reporting tasks T1–T4.**
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define "patient-level data splitting" and explain why it is critical in medical image analysis experiments.**

**Model Answer:**

Patient-level data splitting is a partitioning strategy in which **all images belonging to a single patient are assigned exclusively to one of the training, validation, or test sets**. It is critical because medical images from the same patient are *anatomically correlated* — multiple slices of one lung CT share the same nodules, vasculature, and tissue characteristics. If slices from one patient leak into both training and testing, the model effectively "memorizes" patient-specific features and reports **artificially inflated performance** (Dice scores can be inflated by **5–15 %**). Patient-level splitting prevents this **data leakage** and ensures reported metrics reflect *true generalization to new patients*. (3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for leakage mechanism, 1 mark for numerical impact.)

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the Hounsfield Unit (HU) window, and what window would you use for preprocessing lung CT images for nodule detection?**

**Model Answer:**

The Hounsfield Unit (HU) window is a **predefined intensity range** used to clip raw CT voxel values before feeding them to a neural network, eliminating irrelevant tissues and noise. For lung CT nodule detection, the standard window is **[-1000, +400] HU** (sometimes called the "lung window"). This range captures the air-filled lung parenchyma and the soft-tissue density of nodules while excluding high-density bone. (3 marks: 1 mark for HU definition, 1 mark for lung window values, 1 mark for rationale.)

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **six stages of a KTU-compliant experimental setup** for a medical image segmentation task. Include the role of random seeds, version logging, and ethics clearance.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A research team trains a brain tumor segmentation model on the **BraTS 2021 dataset (1,251 patients)**. They report a test Dice of **0.91** using a 70/15/15 hold-out split. Compute the **imbalance ratio** if the dataset has 700 patients with gliomas, 400 with meningiomas, and 151 with pituitary tumors. Is the imbalance severe? Justify and recommend a mitigation technique.

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks**

The six stages are:

**1. Dataset Characterization (1 mark):** Identify modality (MRI, CT, X-ray), total sample count $N$, number of classes $C$, and source (BraTS, LIDC, internal hospital data). Document patient demographics and acquisition protocols.

**2. Ethics and Anonymization (1 mark):** Obtain Institutional Review Board (IRB) approval. Strip DICOM metadata (patient name, ID, date of birth). Use anonymized IDs like `case_001`, `case_002`.

**3. Patient-Level Partitioning (1 mark):** Use the deterministic hash split $H(p) = \text{hash}(p) \mod k$ to assign each patient to exactly one of Train/Val/Test. Set a fixed random seed, conventionally **42**, for any random shuffle.

**4. Preprocessing Pipeline (1 mark):** Resample all volumes to a common voxel spacing (**1 mm × 1 mm × 1 mm**), clip intensities to the relevant HU window, apply Z-score normalization, and crop/pad to a fixed tensor size.

**5. Augmentation and Training (1 mark):** Apply on-the-fly augmentations (rotation, flip, gamma correction, Gaussian noise). Use a fixed seed for the augmentation generator. Log the **exact PyTorch, CUDA, and cuDNN versions**. Set `cudnn.deterministic = True`.

**6. Evaluation Protocol (2 marks):** Freeze the test set. Compute Dice, Hausdorff Distance, Sensitivity, Specificity, and AUC. Report **95 % confidence intervals** using the formula from §3.1. Use bootstrapping ($n = 1000$ resamples) for non-parametric CIs.

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Identify class counts.**

$N_{\text{glioma}} = 700$, $N_{\text{meningioma}} = 400$, $N_{\text{pituitary}} = 151$

**Step 2 — Compute Imbalance Ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\max N_c &= 700 \\
\min N_c &= 151 \\
IR &= \frac{700}{151} = 4.635
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret severity (2 marks).**

Since $IR \approx 4.64$, which is **well below the severe-imbalance threshold of 10**, the dataset is *moderately* imbalanced. The pituitary class is the minority. Standard training will likely under-detect pituitary tumors.

**Step 4 — Recommend mitigation (2 marks).**

Use **weighted cross-entropy loss** with class weights:

$$w_c = \frac{N}{C \cdot N_c}$$

For pituitary: $w_{\text{pituitary}} = \frac{1251}{3 \cdot 151} = 2.76$. This upweights pituitary errors. Alternatively, apply **focal loss** with $\gamma = 2$ to focus learning on hard, minority-class examples.

**Step 5 — Re-stratify the 70/15/15 split (1 mark).**

Ensure each fold maintains the original class proportions using **stratified splitting** (e.g., `StratifiedShuffleSplit` in scikit-learn). For the test set of ~188 patients: expect ~105 glioma, ~60 meningioma, ~23 pituitary samples.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **Z-score normalization** and **min-max normalization** with formulas. For which medical imaging modalities is each preferred, and why?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A team trains a polyp detection model with the following test results across 5-fold cross-validation on a colonoscopy dataset of 500 patients: **[0.88, 0.91, 0.85, 0.89, 0.87]**. Compute the **mean F1**, **standard deviation**, and **95 % confidence interval** (assume normal distribution, $Z_{0.975} = 1.96$). Is the model stable across folds?

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks**

**Z-Score Normalization (3 marks):**

$$x' = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$$

where $\mu$ and $\sigma$ are the dataset-level mean and standard deviation. This produces a **zero-mean, unit-variance** distribution. **Preferred for MRI**, because MRI intensities are *arbitrary units* with no physical meaning — they depend on the scanner's magnetic field strength and pulse sequence. Z-scoring makes the data scale-invariant across scanners.

**Min-Max Normalization (2 marks):**

$$x' = \frac{x - x_{\min}}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}$$

This scales values into $[0, 1]$. **Preferred for CT** when a fixed HU window has already been applied (e.g., [-1000, +400]), because the bounds are *physically meaningful* and we want to preserve the *relative contrast* within that clinical window. Z-scoring CT after HU-clipping would distort the lung-nodule-to-parenchyma contrast.

**Comparison table (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Z-Score | Min-Max |
|---|---|---|
| Output range | $(-\infty, +\infty)$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| Sensitivity to outliers | Lower (uses mean/std) | Higher (uses min/max) |
| Best for | MRI, multi-scanner data | CT (post-HU-window) |
| KTU memory hook | "Z for MRI" | "M for CT" |

---

##### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Compute the mean (1 mark).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x} &= \frac{0.88 + 0.91 + 0.85 + 0.89 + 0.87}{5} \\
&= \frac{4.40}{5} = 0.880
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute sample standard deviation (2 marks).**

Deviations from mean: $[+0.00, +0.03, -0.03, +0.01, -0.01]$

$$
\begin{aligned}
s^2 &= \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1} = \frac{(0.00)^2 + (0.03)^2 + (-0.03)^2 + (0.01)^2 + (-0.01)^2}{4} \\
&= \frac{0 + 0.0009 + 0.0009 + 0.0001 + 0.0001}{4} \\
&= \frac{0.0020}{4} = 0.0005
\end{aligned}
$$

$$s = \sqrt{0.0005} \approx 0.0224$$

**Step 3 — Compute 95 % CI (2 marks).**

Standard error of the mean:

$$SE = \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{0.0224}{\sqrt{5}} = \frac{0.0224}{2.236} \approx 0.0100$$

Margin of error:

$$e = Z_{0.975} \times SE = 1.96 \times 0.0100 = 0.0196$$

CI:

$$\bar{x} \pm e = 0.880 \pm 0.020 = [0.860, \, 0.900]$$

**Step 4 — Stability analysis (2 marks).**

Since the standard deviation is only **0.0224** and the range across folds is just **0.06** (0.85 to 0.91), the model is **highly stable across folds**. The 95 % CI is tight (≈ ±2 %), indicating consistent generalization. This is a strong, reproducible model.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing "patient-level" with "slice-level" splits** — *Maximum mark loss*. If a student splits at the image-slice level instead of the patient level, examiners will deduct **2–3 marks** immediately. Always mention *patient_id* explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting to log random seeds** — Examiners look for `seed = 42` or equivalent. Omitting it loses 1 mark.
