> 3. **Reporting only point estimates (e.g., Dice = 0.91) without CIs** — A point estimate alone is *not* statistically defensible. Always compute the **95 % confidence interval**.
> 4. **Wrong HU windows** — Using the brain window (0–80 HU) for lung images is a clinical error costing 1 mark. Memorize: **Lung = [-1000, +400]**, **Bone = [200, 3000]**, **Brain = [0, 80]**, **Abdomen = [-150, +250]**.
> 5. **Using `train_test_split` without the `groups` parameter** — In scikit-learn, you *must* pass `groups=patient_ids` to prevent leakage. Forgetting this is a 1-mark deduction.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Experimental Data and Setup"**

- [ ] **Patient-level splitting** is *non-negotiable*. Use `groups=patient_ids` or `H(p) = hash(p) mod k`.
- [ ] **Standard split ratios**: 70/15/15 for large datasets, **5-fold CV** for medium, **LOCO** for multi-center.
- [ ] **Hounsfield windows to memorize**:
  - Lung: `[-1000, +400]`
  - Bone: `[+200, +3000]`
  - Brain: `[0, +80]`
  - Abdomen: `[-150, +250]`
- [ ] **Z-score for MRI**, **Min-Max (post-HU window) for CT** — the KTU memory hook.
- [ ] **Isotropic resampling** to **1 mm × 1 mm × 1 mm** is the gold standard.
- [ ] **Sample size formula** for sensitivity claims: $n = \dfrac{Z^2 \cdot p(1-p)}{e^2}$. For 90 % ± 5 %, $n \geq 139$.
- [ ] **Reproducibility triad**: random seed (**42**), version-pinned `requirements.txt`, `cudnn.deterministic = True`.
- [ ] **Test set is sacred** — touch it only once, at the very end.
- [ ] **Report 95 % CIs**, never just point estimates.
- [ ] **Imbalance Ratio threshold**: $IR > 10$ is *severe* — use focal loss or weighted CE.
- [ ] **GPU memory for 3D U-Net**: budget **≥11 GB** VRAM as a safe default.
- [ ] **MONAI** is the KTU-recommended framework for medical imaging pipelines.
- [ ] **Class activation map / Grad-CAM** is *not* a substitute for proper experimental setup — both are required.
- [ ] **Ethics**: Always cite IRB approval number and confirm DICOM anonymization.

**One-line exam mnemonic:** *"Split by patient, not by picture — or your Dice is a lie."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Evaluation Metrics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT862 - AI FOR MEDICAL IMAGE ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Performance Evaluation Metrics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Performance Evaluation Metrics** in medical image analysis are **quantitative, statistically robust measures** used to assess the agreement, accuracy, and clinical reliability of predictions generated by artificial intelligence (AI) and deep learning models against expert-annotated *Ground Truth* (gold-standard) labels. In the KTU 2024 Scheme, these metrics form the empirical backbone of **Module 3**, bridging algorithmic output with diagnostic validity — the cornerstone of any Computer-Aided Diagnosis (CAD) system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PEBRT862):** Performance metrics are not merely "accuracy numbers." They are **clinical risk indicators**. A metric like *Recall* directly relates to *missed tumors* (false negatives), while *Precision* relates to *unnecessary biopsies* (false positives). Choosing the correct metric is a **clinical decision**, not just a mathematical one.

The four foundational cardinality counts derived from a binary classification scenario are:

- **True Positive (TP)**: Model correctly predicts the *presence* of pathology.
- **True Negative (TN)**: Model correctly predicts the *absence* of pathology.
- **False Positive (FP)** — *Type I Error*: Model incorrectly predicts *presence* of pathology (false alarm).
- **False Negative (FN)** — *Type II Error*: Model fails to detect *actual* pathology (missed diagnosis).

> [!NOTE]
> **Ground Truth vs. Prediction:** Every metric is computed by comparing **Predicted Labels** $\hat{Y}$ from the AI model against **Ground Truth Labels** $Y$ provided by expert radiologists/pathologists.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Tumor Detective

Imagine a radiologist (our AI model) screening **1000 patients** for lung cancer.

- **100 patients actually have cancer** (Ground Truth positives).
- **900 patients are healthy** (Ground Truth negatives).

The radiologist's report is a binary classification: *Cancer* or *No Cancer*.

- When the radiologist says *"Cancer"* and the patient **truly has cancer** → **TP** (a life saved).
- When the radiologist says *"Cancer"* but the patient is **healthy** → **FP** (unnecessary panic and biopsy).
- When the radiologist says *"No Cancer"* and the patient is **healthy** → **TN** (a correctly reassured patient).
- When the radiologist says *"No Cancer"* but the patient **truly has cancer** → **FN** (a life lost — the worst possible error).

These four numbers, when arranged in a 2×2 grid, form the **Confusion Matrix** — the *source of truth* from which *all* medical AI performance metrics are derived.

## 1.3 The Confusion Matrix — Visual Schema

|  | **Predicted Positive** $\hat{Y}=1$ | **Predicted Negative** $\hat{Y}=0$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** $Y=1$ | **TP** | **FN** |
| **Actual Negative** $Y=0$ | **FP** | **TN** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Row totals** = actual class frequencies. **Column totals** = predicted class frequencies. The **diagonal** = correct predictions. In an ideal classifier, all mass lies on the diagonal.

## 1.4 The Metric Hierarchy — A Bird's-Eye View

Medical image analysis demands metrics at **three granularities**:

1. **Pixel/Region-Level Metrics** → for *segmentation* tasks (e.g., tumor delineation). Examples: **Dice**, **IoU**, **Hausdorff Distance**.
2. **Image/Case-Level Metrics** → for *classification* tasks (e.g., cancer/no-cancer). Examples: **Accuracy**, **Sensitivity**, **Specificity**, **F1**.
3. **Threshold-Independent Metrics** → for evaluating models across all decision thresholds. Examples: **AUC-ROC**, **AUPRC**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why "Accuracy" Fails in Medical Imaging

The most common pitfall students make is reporting **Accuracy** as the primary metric for medical tasks. Consider a dataset of **10,000 chest X-rays**, where only **50** show pneumonia (1% prevalence).

A trivial model that predicts *"No Pneumonia"* for **every** patient achieves:

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{9950}{10000} = 99.5\%$$

This is a **useless model** — it catches zero sick patients. Hence, medical AI requires *class-imbalance-aware* metrics.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of Metric Families

### Family 1: Classification (Threshold-Dependent) Metrics

Given a binary confusion matrix, the core metrics decompose as follows:

**Step 1 — Accuracy (Overall Correctness):**

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$

- **Range:** $[0, 1]$
- **Use case:** Only when classes are roughly balanced.
- **Failure mode:** Misleading under high class imbalance.

**Step 2 — Sensitivity / Recall / True Positive Rate (TPR):**

$$\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

- **Range:** $[0, 1]$
- **Clinical meaning:** *"Of all sick patients, how many did we catch?"*
- **Critical in:** Cancer screening — missing a case is catastrophic.

**Step 3 — Specificity / True Negative Rate (TNR):**

$$\text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP}$$

- **Range:** $[0, 1]$
- **Clinical meaning:** *"Of all healthy patients, how many were correctly reassured?"*
- **Critical in:** Population screening where false alarms burden the system.

**Step 4 — Precision / Positive Predictive Value (PPV):**

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$

- **Range:** $[0, 1]$
- **Clinical meaning:** *"Of all positive predictions, how many are truly sick?"*
- **Critical in:** Confirmatory diagnostics where false positives lead to invasive procedures.

**Step 5 — F1-Score (Harmonic Mean of Precision and Recall):**

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}} = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$$

- **Range:** $[0, 1]$
- **Use case:** Single-number summary for imbalanced binary classification.
- **Why harmonic mean?** Penalizes extreme imbalance between Precision and Recall.

**Step 6 — F$_{\beta}$-Score (Weighted F-Measure):**

$$F_{\beta} = (1 + \beta^2) \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{(\beta^2 \cdot \text{Precision}) + \text{Recall}}$$

- $\beta > 1$ → Recall is weighted more heavily (clinical screening).
- $\beta < 1$ → Precision is weighted more heavily (confirmatory tests).
- $F_1$ is the special case where $\beta = 1$.

**Step 7 — Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC):**

$$MCC = \frac{(TP \cdot TN) - (FP \cdot FN)}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$$

- **Range:** $[-1, 1]$, where $1$ = perfect, $0$ = random, $-1$ = total disagreement.
- **Strength:** Considered the most informative single metric for binary classification under imbalance.
- **Property:** Uses *all four* confusion matrix cells symmetrically.

**Step 8 — Cohen's Kappa (Agreement Beyond Chance):**

$$\kappa = \frac{p_o - p_e}{1 - p_e}$$

where $p_o$ = observed accuracy and $p_e$ = expected agreement by chance.

- **Range:** $(-\infty, 1]$; values $\geq 0.81$ indicate almost perfect agreement.
- **Use case:** Adjusting for agreement that would occur by random chance.

### Family 2: Segmentation (Region/Pixel Overlap) Metrics

**Step 9 — Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC) / Sørensen–Dice Index:**

$$DSC = \frac{2 \cdot \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert} = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$$

- **Range:** $[0, 1]$
- **Use case:** Overlap between predicted segmentation mask $A$ and ground truth mask $B$.

**Step 10 — Jaccard Index / Intersection over Union (IoU):**

$$IoU = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP + FN}$$

- **Range:** $[0, 1]$
- **Relationship to Dice:** $DSC = \frac{2 \cdot IoU}{1 + IoU}$, and $IoU = \frac{DSC}{2 - DSC}$.
- Dice is more lenient; IoU penalizes both FP and FN more strictly.

**Step 11 — Hausdorff Distance (Boundary Error):**

$$H(A, B) = \max \left\{ \sup_{a \in A} \inf_{b \in B} d(a,b), \sup_{b \in B} \inf_{a \in A} d(a,b) \right\}$$

- **Units:** millimeters (or pixels).
- **Use case:** Quantifies the *worst-case* boundary deviation — critical in radiotherapy planning where geometric accuracy is paramount.

**Step 12 — Average Symmetric Surface Distance (ASSD):**

$$ASSD = \frac{1}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert} \left( \sum_{a \in A} \min_{b \in B} d(a,b) + \sum_{b \in B} \min_{a \in A} d(a,b) \right)$$

- More robust to outliers than Hausdorff distance.

### Family 3: Threshold-Independent Metrics

**Step 13 — ROC Curve and AUC:**

The **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC)** curve plots:

$$\text{TPR (Sensitivity)} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \text{FPR} = 1 - \text{Specificity}$$

across all possible classification thresholds.

The **Area Under the Curve (AUC-ROC)** summarizes the curve in a single scalar:

$$AUC = \int_0^1 TPR(FPR) \, d(FPR)$$

- **Range:** $[0, 1]$; $0.5$ = random, $1.0$ = perfect.
- **Probabilistic interpretation:** $AUC$ = probability that the model ranks a random positive case higher than a random negative case.

**Step 14 — Precision-Recall Curve and Average Precision (AP):**

The **PR curve** plots **Precision** vs. **Recall** across thresholds. The **Average Precision (AP)** is the area under this curve.

$$AP = \sum_n (R_n - R_{n-1}) \cdot P_n$$

- **Superior to AUC-ROC** in highly imbalanced medical datasets because PR curves are not skewed by the large TN count.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Metric** | **Formula** | **Range** | **Best Use Case** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | $\frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | $[0,1]$ | Balanced datasets |
| Sensitivity / Recall | $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | $[0,1]$ | Cancer screening |
| Specificity | $\frac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | $[0,1]$ | Confirmatory tests |
| Precision / PPV | $\frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | $[0,1]$ | Confirmatory diagnostics |
| F1-Score | $\frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$ | $[0,1]$ | Imbalanced classification |
| F$_{\beta}$-Score | $(1+\beta^2)\frac{P \cdot R}{\beta^2 P + R}$ | $[0,1]$ | Weighted precision-recall trade-off |
| MCC | $\frac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$ | $[-1,1]$ | Imbalanced single-metric |
| Cohen's Kappa | $\frac{p_o - p_e}{1 - p_e}$ | $(-\infty, 1]$ | Agreement beyond chance |
| Dice (DSC) | $\frac{2 \cdot \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$ | $[0,1]$ | Segmentation overlap |
| IoU / Jaccard | $\frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | $[0,1]$ | Object detection/segmentation |
| Hausdorff Distance | $\max(\sup \inf d, \sup \inf d)$ | $[0, \infty)$ | Boundary error in mm |
| AUC-ROC | $\int TPR \, d(FPR)$ | $[0,1]$ | Threshold-independent ranking |
| AP / AUPRC | $\sum (R_n - R_{n-1}) P_n$ | $[0,1]$ | Imbalanced threshold-independent |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In deployed medical AI systems, these metrics drive **regulatory approval**. The U.S. FDA and EU MDR require:

- **Sensitivity ≥ 90%** with acceptable Specificity for screening AI (e.g., diabetic retinopathy, mammography).
- **Dice ≥ 0.85** for tumor segmentation in radiotherapy planning.
- **AUC ≥ 0.95** for diagnostic support systems.

A misreported metric can delay approval, cause clinical harm, or trigger product recalls. Thus, mastering these metrics is a **legal, ethical, and engineering** competency.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Confusion Matrix to F1-Score

The F1-score is the **harmonic mean** of Precision ($P$) and Recall ($R$). We derive it from first principles.

**Step 1:** Define Precision and Recall from the binary confusion matrix:

$$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}, \quad R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

**Step 2:** Recall the definition of the harmonic mean of two positive scalars $a$ and $b$:

$$HM(a, b) = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{a} + \frac{1}{b}} = \frac{2ab}{a+b}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the harmonic mean definition to $P$ and $R$:

$$F_1 = HM(P, R) = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the expressions for $P$ and $R$:

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot \frac{TP}{TP+FP} \cdot \frac{TP}{TP+FN}}{\frac{TP}{TP+FP} + \frac{TP}{TP+FN}}$$

**Step 5:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $(TP+FP)(TP+FN)$:

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP^2 \cdot (TP+FP)^{-1} \cdot (TP+FN)^{-1} \cdot (TP+FP)(TP+FN)}{TP(TP+FN) + TP(TP+FP)} \cdot \frac{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)}{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)}$$

This simplifies to:

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP^2}{TP(TP+FN) + TP(TP+FP)}$$

**Step 6:** Factor $TP$ in the denominator:

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP^2}{TP \cdot [(TP+FN) + (TP+FP)]} = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$$

**Conclusion:**

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN} \quad \text{(Q.E.D.)}$$

This form is preferred in medical AI papers because it requires **only the confusion matrix counts**, not separate computation of $P$ and $R$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Relationship Between Dice and IoU

**Step 1:** Define both metrics on the same set $A$ (prediction) and $B$ (ground truth):

$$DSC = \frac{2 \cdot \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}, \quad IoU = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$$

**Step 2:** Use the set identity:

$$\vert A \cup B \vert = \vert A \vert + \vert B \vert - \vert A \cap B \vert$$

**Step 3:** Let $x = \vert A \cap B \vert$, $y = \vert A \setminus B \vert$, $z = \vert B \setminus A \vert$. Then:

$$\vert A \vert = x + y, \quad \vert B \vert = x + z, \quad \vert A \cup B \vert = x + y + z$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the formulas:

$$DSC = \frac{2x}{(x+y) + (x+z)} = \frac{2x}{2x + y + z}$$

$$IoU = \frac{x}{x + y + z}$$

**Step 5:** Let $IoU = J$ for brevity. Solve for $DSC$ in terms of $J$:

$$DSC = \frac{2 \cdot \frac{x}{x+y+z}}{1 + \frac{x}{x+y+z}} \cdot \frac{x+y+z}{x+y+z} = \frac{2 \cdot IoU}{1 + IoU}$$

**Step 6:** Invert for IoU in terms of DSC:

$$IoU = \frac{DSC}{2 - DSC}$$

**Numerical example:** If $DSC = 0.8$, then $IoU = \frac{0.8}{2 - 0.8} = \frac{0.8}{1.2} \approx 0.667$.

**Key insight:** IoU is *always* $\leq$ Dice. Dice is more forgiving — a model with $DSC = 0.5$ has only $IoU \approx 0.33$.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Full Metric Computation

**Scenario:** A deep learning model for brain tumor detection was tested on **200 MRI scans**. The results:

|  | **Predicted Tumor** | **Predicted Healthy** |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Tumor** | $TP = 45$ | $FN = 5$ |
| **Actual Healthy** | $FP = 10$ | $TN = 140$ |

**Step 1 — Total positives (actual):** $TP + FN = 50$
**Step 2 — Total negatives (actual):** $FP + TN = 150$

**Step 3 — Accuracy:**

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{45 + 140}{200} = \frac{185}{200} = 0.925$$

**Step 4 — Sensitivity (Recall):**

$$\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{45}{45 + 5} = \frac{45}{50} = 0.90$$

**Step 5 — Specificity:**

$$\text{Specificity} = \frac{140}{140 + 10} = \frac{140}{150} \approx 0.933$$

**Step 6 — Precision:**

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{45}{45 + 10} = \frac{45}{55} \approx 0.818$$

**Step 7 — F1-Score:**

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot 0.818 \cdot 0.90}{0.818 + 0.90} = \frac{1.4724}{1.718} \approx 0.857$$

**Step 8 — MCC:**

$$MCC = \frac{(45 \cdot 140) - (10 \cdot 5)}{\sqrt{(45+10)(45+5)(140+10)(140+5)}} = \frac{6300 - 50}{\sqrt{55 \cdot 50 \cdot 150 \cdot 145}} = \frac{6250}{\sqrt{59,812,500}} \approx \frac{6250}{7733.85} \approx 0.808$$

**Step 9 — Clinical interpretation:**

- **Sensitivity = 90%** → The model catches 9 out of 10 actual tumors. Acceptable but not perfect for screening.
- **Specificity = 93.3%** → The model rarely alarms on healthy patients.
- **F1 = 0.857** → Good balance. A $F_{\beta=2}$ score would emphasize the missed tumors more.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Which Metric to Report?

| **Clinical Task** | **Primary Metric** | **Secondary Metric** | **Reason** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cancer screening | **Sensitivity** | AUC-ROC | Missing a case is fatal |
| Confirmatory biopsy | **Precision (PPV)** | Specificity | Avoid unnecessary invasive procedures |
| Tumor segmentation | **Dice Coefficient** | Hausdorff Distance | Geometric overlap + boundary fidelity |
| Multi-class diagnosis (e.g., 5 skin lesion types) | **Macro-F1** | Per-class Sensitivity | Class imbalance is severe |
| Radiotherapy planning | **Hausdorff Distance (95th pct)** | Dice | Geometric accuracy in mm matters most |
| Population screening (rare disease) | **AUPRC** | Sensitivity @ fixed FPR | PR curves handle imbalance better |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** When asked *"Which metric is best?"* the correct answer is **"It depends on the clinical context and the cost asymmetry between FP and FN."** Reporting only Accuracy in an imbalanced medical problem is the most common reason for mark deduction in Module 3.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Metric Computation Pipeline

The following functional architecture flow maps how raw model outputs cascade into clinically meaningful metrics.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Raw Model Output Y-hat] --> nodeB[Apply Threshold tau]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Binary Prediction Map]
    nodeD[Ground Truth Labels Y] --> nodeE[Reference Map]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Confusion Matrix Generator]
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG[TP FP TN FN Counts]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Threshold-Dependent Metrics]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Threshold-Independent Metrics]
    nodeH --> nodeH1[Accuracy]
    nodeH --> nodeH2[Sensitivity]
    nodeH --> nodeH3[Specificity]
    nodeH --> nodeH4[Precision]
    nodeH --> nodeH5[F1 Score]
    nodeH --> nodeH6[MCC]
    nodeI --> nodeI1[ROC Curve Sweep]
    nodeI --> nodeI2[PR Curve Sweep]
    nodeI1 --> nodeI3[AUC ROC]
    nodeI2 --> nodeI4[Average Precision]
    nodeH1 --> nodeJ[Clinical Report]
    nodeH2 --> nodeJ
    nodeH3 --> nodeJ
    nodeH4 --> nodeJ
    nodeH5 --> nodeJ
    nodeH6 --> nodeJ
    nodeI3 --> nodeJ
    nodeI4 --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK[FDA CE Regulatory Submission]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Metric Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[Task Type] --> nodeQ1{Classification or Segmentation}
    nodeQ1 -->|Classification| nodeQ2{Balanced Classes}
    nodeQ1 -->|Segmentation| nodeSeg[Region Overlap Metrics]
    nodeSeg --> nodeSegA[Dice Coefficient]
    nodeSeg --> nodeSegB[IoU Jaccard]
    nodeSeg --> nodeSegC[Hausdorff Distance]
    nodeSeg --> nodeSegD[ASSD]
    nodeQ2 -->|Yes| nodeAcc[Report Accuracy]
    nodeQ2 -->|No| nodeQ3{Clinical Cost Asymmetry}
    nodeQ3 -->|FN more costly| nodeSens[Sensitivity F-beta beta greater than 1 AUPRC]
    nodeQ3 -->|FP more costly| nodeSpec[Specificity Precision]
    nodeQ3 -->|Symmetric| nodeMCC2[MCC F1]
    nodeAcc --> nodeFinal[Multi-metric report]
    nodeSens --> nodeFinal
    nodeSpec --> nodeFinal
    nodeMCC2 --> nodeFinal
    nodeSegA --> nodeFinal
    nodeSegB --> nodeFinal
    nodeSegC --> nodeFinal
    nodeSegD --> nodeFinal
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Multi-Class Extension (One-vs-Rest)

For an $N$-class medical task (e.g., classifying chest X-rays into *Normal*, *Pneumonia*, *COVID-19*, *Tuberculosis*), each metric is computed per-class and then aggregated.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeM[Multi Class Predictions] --> nodeLoop[For each class c in 1 to N]
    nodeLoop --> nodeOvR[One vs Rest Binarization]
    nodeOvR --> nodeCM[Confusion Matrix for class c]
    nodeCM --> nodeMet[Compute TP FP TN FN for c]
    nodeMet --> nodeAgg{Aggregation Strategy}
    nodeAgg -->|Macro| nodeMac[Average across classes equally]
    nodeAgg -->|Micro| nodeMic[Pool all CM cells globally]
    nodeAgg -->|Weighted| nodeW[Weighted by class support]
    nodeMac --> nodeOut[Final Multi Class Metric]
    nodeMic --> nodeOut
    nodeW --> nodeOut
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Macro vs. Micro matters in medical AI:** *Macro-F1* weights rare diseases equally (clinically fair), while *Micro-F1* is dominated by frequent classes (statistically convenient but can mask poor rare-disease performance).

## 4.4 Schematic: ROC vs. PR Curve Behavior Under Class Imbalance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeImp[Imbalance Level] --> nodeLow[Low Imbalance 1 to 1]
    nodeImp --> nodeHigh[High Imbalance 1 to 100]
    nodeLow --> nodeROC1[ROC and PR both informative]
    nodeHigh --> nodeROC2[ROC may look optimistic due to large TN]
    nodeHigh --> nodePRC2[PR curve sharply reflects model weakness]
    nodeROC1 --> nodeRec1[Report both AUC and AP]
    nodeROC2 --> nodeRec2[Report AUC with caution]
    nodePRC2 --> nodeRec3[Prefer AUPRC]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Model)]**

> Define the **Sensitivity** and **Specificity** metrics in the context of medical image classification. Why is **Accuracy** often a misleading metric in medical imaging datasets?

**Model Answer:**

Sensitivity (also called Recall or True Positive Rate) is defined as the proportion of actual positive cases correctly identified by the model:

$$\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

It quantifies the model's ability to detect *actual* pathology — clinically critical for screening tasks where missing a case (FN) is dangerous.

Specificity (True Negative Rate) is the proportion of actual negative cases correctly identified:

$$\text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP}$$

It measures the model's ability to correctly rule out disease, important in confirmatory settings where false alarms (FP) burden the system.

**Accuracy** is misleading under class imbalance because it is dominated by the majority class. In a dataset with 99% healthy patients, a trivial "always predict healthy" model achieves 99% accuracy while detecting zero sick patients. Sensitivity and Specificity are *class-conditional* metrics and remain informative regardless of prevalence. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model)]**

> Differentiate between the **Dice Coefficient** and the **Jaccard Index (IoU)** for segmentation evaluation. Derive the mathematical relationship between them.

**Model Answer:**

The **Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC)** measures the overlap between predicted mask $A$ and ground truth mask $B$:

$$DSC = \frac{2 \cdot \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$$

The **Jaccard Index (IoU)** measures the intersection over the union:

$$IoU = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$$

**Derivation:** Let $x = \vert A \cap B \vert$, $y = \vert A \setminus B \vert$, $z = \vert B \setminus A \vert$. Then $DSC = \frac{2x}{2x+y+z}$ and $IoU = \frac{x}{x+y+z}$. Hence:

$$DSC = \frac{2 \cdot IoU}{1 + IoU}, \quad IoU = \frac{DSC}{2 - DSC}$$

Dice is more lenient (higher value for the same overlap), while IoU penalizes both FP and FN more strictly. Both are reported in segmentation literature, but **Dice is preferred in medical imaging** due to its sensitivity to small structures. **(3 marks)**

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Model)]**

> **(a)** Construct the **Confusion Matrix** for a binary tumor classification model tested on 250 histopathology images, where: 60 are True Positives, 5 are False Negatives, 15 are False Positives, and 170 are True Negatives. From this matrix, compute the **Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, Precision,** and **F1-Score**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss why the **F1-Score** is preferred over plain **Accuracy** in highly imbalanced medical datasets. Also, explain the role of the **F$_{\beta}$ parameter** in tuning model behavior for screening vs. confirmatory diagnostics. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Confusion Matrix Construction and Metric Computation (7 marks):**

| | **Predicted Positive** | **Predicted Negative** | **Row Total** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | $TP = 60$ | $FN = 5$ | $65$ |
| **Actual Negative** | $FP = 15$ | $TN = 170$ | $185$ |
| **Column Total** | $75$ | $175$ | $N = 250$ |

**Step 1 — Accuracy:**

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{N} = \frac{60 + 170}{250} = \frac{230}{250} = 0.92$$

**Step 2 — Sensitivity:**

$$\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{60}{60 + 5} = \frac{60}{65} \approx 0.923$$

**Step 3 — Specificity:**

$$\text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{170}{170 + 15} = \frac{170}{185} \approx 0.919$$

**Step 4 — Precision:**

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{60}{60 + 15} = \frac{60}{75} = 0.80$$

**Step 5 — F1-Score:**

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN} = \frac{2 \cdot 60}{2 \cdot 60 + 15 + 5} = \frac{120}{140} \approx 0.857$$

**(b) F1 vs. Accuracy and Role of F$_{\beta}$ (7 marks):**

**Why F1 beats Accuracy under imbalance:**

Consider a dataset of 1000 CT scans with 20 positive (cancer) cases. A trivial "always-negative" model achieves:

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{980}{1000} = 98\%$$

— yet it detects *zero* cancer cases. Accuracy is dominated by the majority (TN) class and hides total model failure. F1, in contrast, uses only $TP$, $FP$, $FN$ (ignoring $TN$), so it is sensitive to both minority-class performance and prediction quality.

**Role of F$_{\beta}$ parameter:**

$$F_{\beta} = (1+\beta^2) \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{\beta^2 P + R}$$

- $\beta = 1$ → equal weight to Precision and Recall (balanced F1).
- $\beta > 1$ (e.g., $\beta = 2$) → **Recall is weighted more heavily**. Used in *screening* (e.g., mammography) where missing cancer is worse than over-calling.
- $\beta < 1$ (e.g., $\beta = 0.5$) → **Precision is weighted more heavily**. Used in *confirmatory diagnostics* (e.g., surgical biopsy decision) where false positives trigger invasive procedures.

**Clinical summary:** F1 (and its weighted variants) are *clinically tunable* — the choice of $\beta$ encodes the cost asymmetry between missed cases and false alarms, which is the central clinical question in any medical AI deployment. **(7 marks complete)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model)]**

> **(a)** Explain the **ROC Curve** and **AUC-ROC** metric. Describe how the AUC value is computed and interpret its probabilistic meaning. Why might **AUC-ROC** be optimistic on highly imbalanced medical datasets, and what alternative is preferred? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Define the **Hausdorff Distance** and the **Average Symmetric Surface Distance (ASSD)**. Explain their use in evaluating **radiotherapy planning** segmentation models, and state one limitation of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) ROC Curve, AUC, and Imbalance Limitations (7 marks):**

The **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve** is constructed by sweeping the classification threshold $\tau$ from $0$ to $1$ and plotting:

$$\text{TPR (Sensitivity)} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \text{FPR} = 1 - \text{Specificity}$$

at each threshold. The diagonal $y = x$ represents random guessing. A perfect classifier's curve passes through the top-left corner $(0, 1)$.

**AUC computation** uses the trapezoidal rule:

$$AUC = \int_0^1 TPR(FPR) \, d(FPR) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{(FPR_{i+1} - FPR_i)(TPR_{i+1} + TPR_i)}{2}$$

**Probabilistic interpretation:** $AUC = P(\text{score}(x^+) > \text{score}(x^-))$, the probability that the model ranks a randomly chosen positive case higher than a randomly chosen negative case. Range: $[0.5, 1]$ for useful classifiers.

**Why AUC-ROC is optimistic on imbalanced data:** Since FPR = $\frac{FP}{FP + TN}$, when $TN$ is very large (typical in medical datasets), FPR stays small even when $FP$ is non-negligible. Thus, a poor model can still exhibit a high AUC-ROC because the curve is anchored in the TN-dominated low-FPR region.

**Preferred alternative:** The **Precision-Recall curve** and its scalar summary **Average Precision (AP / AUPRC)** focus on the *positive class*, ignoring TN entirely. AUPRC is far more sensitive to model degradation under severe class imbalance and is the recommended metric for rare-disease screening AI. **(7 marks complete)**

**(b) Hausdorff Distance and ASSD in Radiotherapy (7 marks):**

**Hausdorff Distance** measures the worst-case boundary discrepancy between predicted segmentation $A$ and ground truth $B$:

$$H(A, B) = \max \left\{ \sup_{a \in A} \inf_{b \in B} d(a, b), \sup_{b \in B} \inf_{a \in A} d(a, b) \right\}$$

The first term is the *forward* Hausdorff distance (every point in $A$ to its nearest neighbor in $B$), and the second is the *backward* distance. Units are typically millimeters.

**Use in radiotherapy planning:** Organs-at-risk (OARs) and tumor volumes must be delineated with sub-millimeter precision because dose calculations depend on geometry. Hausdorff distance flags any *single* boundary point that is dangerously mispredicted, which is critical for avoiding radiation-induced damage to healthy tissue.

**Limitation of Hausdorff:** A single outlier point can dominate the metric, making it sensitive to noise and annotation errors. The **95th percentile Hausdorff distance** $H_{95}$ is often used as a more robust alternative.

**Average Symmetric Surface Distance (ASSD):**

$$ASSD = \frac{1}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert} \left( \sum_{a \in A} \min_{b \in B} d(a, b) + \sum_{b \in B} \min_{a \in A} d(a, b) \right)$$

ASSD averages the boundary distances in both directions, providing a *typical* (rather than worst-case) error measure.

**Use in radiotherapy:** ASSD captures the average geometric fidelity, complementing Hausdorff. Reporting both gives a complete picture: ASSD reflects overall accuracy; Hausdorff flags worst-case outliers.

**Limitation of ASSD:** A small average error can mask large localized errors, which Hausdorff would catch. Hence both metrics are typically reported together in MICCAI-style challenges and clinical reports. **(7 marks complete)**

---

## 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Module 3 — Performance Evaluation Metrics:**
>
> 1. **Reporting Accuracy as the only metric for imbalanced data** — examiners explicitly test whether students know Accuracy is misleading. Always pair Accuracy with Sensitivity/Specificity/F1. *Lose up to 2 marks.*
>
> 2. **Confusing Precision with Recall** — Precision answers *"of those predicted positive, how many are correct?"*; Recall answers *"of those actually positive, how many did we catch?"* Mixing them up is a *definitional* error. *Lose 1-2 marks.*
>
> 3. **Forgetting units for Hausdorff Distance** — must be in *millimeters* (clinical) or *pixels* (algorithmic), never unitless. *Lose 1 mark.*
>
> 4. **Computing F1 from the arithmetic mean** — F1 is the *harmonic* mean, not the arithmetic mean. Using $(P+R)/2$ is incorrect. *Lose 1-2 marks.*
>
> 5. **Ignoring multi-class aggregation strategy** — when reporting multi-class F1, students must state *Macro, Micro, or Weighted*. Reporting a bare F1 without aggregation method is incomplete. *Lose 1 mark.*
>
> 6. **Not relating metrics to clinical cost** — when justifying metric choice, always tie back to FP vs. FN cost asymmetry (e.g., *missing a tumor is more harmful than a false alarm*). Pure mathematical answers without clinical context lose marks. *Lose 1-2 marks.*

---

## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Performance Evaluation Metrics**

- [x] **Confusion Matrix** is the foundation: $TP$, $FP$, $TN$, $FN$ — all metrics derive from it.
- [x] **Accuracy** = $\frac{TP+TN}{N}$. Useful only on balanced datasets. **Misleading under class imbalance.**
- [x] **Sensitivity (Recall / TPR)** = $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$. The most clinically critical metric for *screening*.
- [x] **Specificity (TNR)** = $\frac{TN}{TN+FP}$. Critical for *confirmatory* diagnostics.
- [x] **Precision (PPV)** = $\frac{TP}{TP+FP}$. Answers *"how trustworthy is a positive prediction?"*
- [x] **F1-Score** = $\frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$ = harmonic mean of P and R. Single best metric for imbalanced binary classification.
- [x] **F$_{\beta}$** tunes the Precision-Recall trade-off: $\beta > 1$ emphasizes Recall (screening), $\beta < 1$ emphasizes Precision (confirmation).
- [x] **MCC** uses all four cells symmetrically, range $[-1, 1]$. The most informative single metric for binary imbalance.
- [x] **Cohen's Kappa** corrects accuracy for chance agreement. $\kappa \geq 0.81$ = almost perfect.
- [x] **Dice Coefficient** = $\frac{2 \cdot \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$. Preferred for medical segmentation.
- [x] **IoU (Jaccard)** = $\frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$. Relationship: $DSC = \frac{2 \cdot IoU}{1 + IoU}$.
- [x] **Hausdorff Distance** = worst-case boundary error in mm. Critical for radiotherapy planning.
- [x] **ASSD** = average boundary error. Reports typical performance; Hausdorff reports worst-case.
- [x] **AUC-ROC** = probability of correct ranking. Sweeps all thresholds.
- [x] **AUPRC** = preferred over AUC-ROC for imbalanced data, because it ignores the dominant TN count.
- [x] **Multi-class metrics** require explicit aggregation: *Macro* (equal class weight), *Micro* (global pool), *Weighted* (by class frequency). *Macro* is preferred in medical AI for fair rare-disease evaluation.
- [x] **Clinical rule of thumb**: Pair Sensitivity with Specificity for screening; pair Dice with Hausdorff for segmentation; pair AUPRC with Sensitivity@fixed-FPR for rare diseases.
- [x] **Never report a metric without stating the clinical context and the cost asymmetry** that justifies its choice.

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **PEBRT862 — AI for Medical Image Analysis**, the term **Experimental Results** refers to the rigorously tabulated, statistically validated, and visually documented outcomes produced when a trained deep-learning model (e.g., a U-Net, ResNet, or Vision Transformer) is evaluated on an **unseen, sequestered test partition** of a medical imaging dataset. These results constitute the empirical evidence that justifies the scientific claims of the research and typically comprise three orthogonal pillars:

1. **Quantitative Evaluation** — numerical scores computed using standardized metrics (Dice, IoU, Sensitivity, Specificity, Hausdorff Distance, AUC, etc.).
2. **Qualitative Visualization** — visual side-by-side comparison of ground-truth annotations, model predictions, and error maps (false-positive/false-negative overlays).
3. **Comparative & Ablative Analysis** — benchmarking against state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods and isolating the contribution of each architectural component through ablation studies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-aligned):**
> *Experimental Results* in medical image analysis is the systematic, reproducible, and metric-driven reporting framework that quantifies a model's segmentation, classification, or detection performance on clinically relevant test data using clinically interpretable, gold-standard benchmarked measures.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a **radiologist's diagnostic report**. A single sentence ("there is a tumor") is not enough — the report must include:
- **Measurements** (e.g., tumor diameter = 2.4 cm) — analogous to the **Dice coefficient**.
- **Confidence levels** (e.g., 95% CI) — analogous to **statistical significance testing (p-value)**.
- **Comparison with prior cases** (e.g., "consistent with glioma") — analogous to **comparison with SOTA methods**.
- **Justification for tools used** (e.g., "MRI with contrast") — analogous to **ablation studies** (justifying each module's presence).

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Diagnostic Report" Analogy:**
> A medical-imaging model without rigorous experimental results is like a radiologist handing you a diagnosis with no scan images, no measurements, and no comparison — it cannot be trusted, replicated, or clinically deployed.

---

## 1.3 The Three Mandatory Constituents of an Experimental Results Section

| Constituent | Purpose | KTU Examiner Priority |
|-------------|---------|------------------------|
| **Quantitative Metrics** | Numerical proof of performance | ⭐⭐⭐ (Very High) |
| **Comparison with SOTA** | Contextual positioning of the work | ⭐⭐ (High) |
| **Ablation Study** | Justification of design choices | ⭐⭐ (High) |

> [!TIP]
> KTU valuation panels explicitly look for **justification of every claim with a metric, a comparison, and a p-value where applicable**. A paper that reports only accuracy is marked down — Dice/IoU for segmentation, AUC for classification, and Hausdorff for boundary fidelity are non-negotiable in the 2024 scheme.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an Experimental Results Block (Structured Logic)

A publication-grade experimental-results block follows this causal chain:

1. **Dataset Declaration** → Specify the dataset (e.g., BraTS 2021, LIDC-IDRI, ISIC 2018, NIH ChestX-ray14), the number of patients/scans, the train/val/test split ratio, and the **inter-rater agreement (Dice between annotators)** to establish an upper performance ceiling.
2. **Pre-processing Specification** → Document normalization (z-score, min-max), resampling (e.g., to 1 mm³ isotropic voxels), and augmentation pipelines. This makes results reproducible.
3. **Implementation Details** → Hardware (GPU type, VRAM), framework (PyTorch / MONAI / nnU-Net), optimizer (Adam, SGD), learning rate, scheduler, epochs, batch size, and loss function (e.g., Dice Loss + Cross-Entropy).
4. **Cross-Validation Strategy** → k-fold (typically 5-fold) cross-validation is the gold standard for small medical datasets to avoid patient-level data leakage.
5. **Metric Computation** → Apply the appropriate metric family (overlap-based, distance-based, or classification-based).
6. **Statistical Testing** → Apply paired t-test, Wilcoxon signed-rank, or DeLong's test to confirm that improvements over baselines are **statistically significant (p < 0.05)**.
7. **Reporting** → Tabulate mean ± standard deviation across folds, present qualitative figures, and discuss failure cases.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all high-yield formulas** for Module-3 problems in the KTU 2024 scheme. Memorize the units and ranges — they are common viva/ESE traps.

### A. Overlap-Based Metrics (Segmentation)

| Metric | Mathematical Formula | Range | Ideal Value | Clinical Meaning |
|--------|----------------------|-------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Dice / F1** | $DSC = \dfrac{2\,\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$ | $[0,1]$ | $1$ | Volume overlap (twice intersection over sum) |
| **Jaccard / IoU** | $IoU = \dfrac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | $[0,1]$ | $1$ | Intersection-over-Union |
| **Volume Similarity (VS)** | $VS = 1 - \dfrac{\vert \vert A \vert - \vert B \vert \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$ | $[0,1]$ | $1$ | Absolute volume difference normalized |

### B. Distance-Based Metrics (Boundary Fidelity)

| Metric | Mathematical Formula | Unit | Ideal Value | Use Case |
|--------|----------------------|------|-------------|----------|
| **Hausdorff Distance (HD)** | $HD(A,B) = \max\!\left(\max_{a\in A}\min_{b\in B} d(a,b),\; \max_{b\in B}\min_{a\in A} d(b,a)\right)$ | mm / voxels | $0$ | Worst-case boundary error |
| **95th Percentile HD (HD95)** | 95th percentile of $d(a,b)$ for $a\in A$ | mm / voxels | $0$ | Robust to outliers |
| **Average Symmetric Surface Distance (ASSD)** | $\dfrac{1}{\vert S_A \vert + \vert S_B \vert}\!\left(\sum_{a\in S_A} d(a, S_B) + \sum_{b\in S_B} d(b, S_A)\right)$ | mm | $0$ | Mean boundary error |

### C. Classification / Detection Metrics

| Metric | Formula | Notes |
|--------|---------|-------|
| **Sensitivity (Recall / TPR)** | $Sens = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Lesion-detection rate |
| **Specificity (TNR)** | $Spec = \dfrac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | Healthy-tissue accuracy |
| **Precision (PPV)** | $Prec = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | Reliability of positive call |
| **Accuracy** | $Acc = \dfrac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | Misleading on imbalanced data |
| **F1-Score** | $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot Prec \cdot Sens}{Prec + Sens}$ | Harmonic mean of P & R |
| **MCC (Matthews)** | $MCC = \dfrac{TP\cdot TN - FP\cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$ | Robust to imbalance |
| **AUC-ROC** | $\int_0^1 TPR(FPR)\,d(FPR)$ | Threshold-independent |
| **AUC-PR** | $\int_0^1 Prec(Rec)\,d(Rec)$ | Preferred for imbalanced data |

> [!NOTE]
> **Set Notation Convention:** In the formulas above, $A$ denotes the predicted mask (or set of positive voxels) and $B$ denotes the ground-truth mask. $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes the cardinality (number of voxels/elements). The notation $d(a,b)$ denotes the Euclidean distance between points $a$ and $b$.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These metrics are not academic abstractions — they directly drive **FDA / CE-mark regulatory decisions** for AI-based medical devices (e.g., the FDA's GMLP — Good Machine Learning Practice):

- **Dice ≥ 0.85** is a common clinical-acceptance threshold for brain-tumor segmentation in neurosurgical planning.
- **Sensitivity ≥ 0.95** is the de-facto benchmark for **lesion detection in mammography** (missing a cancer is costlier than a false alarm).
- **Hausdorff Distance (HD95) < 5 mm** is required for **radiotherapy target-volume delineation**, where a 5 mm error can place a high-dose beam outside the tumor.
- **AUC-ROC ≥ 0.90** is the standard for **chest X-ray triage algorithms** (e.g., pneumonia, COVID-19).

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION_3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Dice–IoU Algebraic Relationship

This is the **most-asked derivation** in KTU ESE viva and Part-B questions. Knowing it cold earns you 4–5 easy marks.

**Given:**

$$
IoU = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}
$$

**Step 1 — Apply the inclusion–exclusion principle to the denominator:**

$$
\vert A \cup B \vert = \vert A \vert + \vert B \vert - \vert A \cap B \vert
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute back into the IoU expression:**

$$
IoU = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert - \vert A \cap B \vert}
$$

**Step 3 — Introduce the Dice numerator. Note that $DSC = \dfrac{2\,\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$, so let us denote $\vert A \cap B \vert = I$, $\vert A \vert = a$, $\vert B \vert = b$:**

$$
DSC = \frac{2I}{a+b}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $I$ in terms of $DSC$:**

$$
I = \frac{DSC\,(a+b)}{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $I$ into the IoU expression. We need $a+b$ in the denominator too. Recall that:**

$$
IoU = \frac{I}{a+b-I}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $I = \frac{DSC\,(a+b)}{2}$ into both numerator and denominator:**

$$
IoU = \frac{\frac{DSC\,(a+b)}{2}}{(a+b) - \frac{DSC\,(a+b)}{2}}
$$

**Step 7 — Factor out $(a+b)$ from numerator and denominator:**

$$
IoU = \frac{DSC/2}{1 - DSC/2}
$$

**Step 8 — Multiply numerator and denominator by 2:**

$$
IoU = \frac{DSC}{2 - DSC}
$$

**Final result — the celebrated Dice–IoU identity:**

$$
\boxed{\,IoU = \frac{DSC}{2 - DSC} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad DSC = \frac{2\,IoU}{1 + IoU}\,}
$$

**Sanity check (boundary values):**

- If $DSC = 1$ (perfect overlap) → $IoU = 1/(2-1) = 1$ ✓
- If $DSC = 0$ (no overlap) → $IoU = 0/(2-0) = 0$ ✓
- If $DSC = 0.5$ → $IoU = 0.5/1.5 \approx 0.333$ ✓ (Dice is always ≥ IoU)

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Full KTU 14-Mark Style)

A brain-tumor segmentation model produces the following confusion-matrix counts on the test set:

$$
TP = 850,\quad FP = 50,\quad FN = 75,\quad TN = 9025
$$

**Total positive ground-truth voxels:** $\vert B \vert = TP + FN = 850 + 75 = 925$

**Total predicted positive voxels:** $\vert A \vert = TP + FP = 850 + 50 = 900$

**True intersection:** $\vert A \cap B \vert = TP = 850$

**Step 1 — Compute Dice Similarity Coefficient:**

$$
DSC = \frac{2 \times 850}{900 + 925} = \frac{1700}{1825} \approx 0.9315
$$

**Step 2 — Compute IoU (Jaccard) directly:**

$$
IoU = \frac{850}{900 + 925 - 850} = \frac{850}{975} \approx 0.8718
$$

**Step 3 — Verify the Dice–IoU identity:**

$$
IoU_{\text{predicted}} = \frac{0.9315}{2 - 0.9315} = \frac{0.9315}{1.0685} \approx 0.8718 \;\;\checkmark
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Sensitivity (Recall):**

$$
Sens = \frac{TP}{TP+FN} = \frac{850}{850+75} = \frac{850}{925} \approx 0.9189
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Specificity:**

$$
Spec = \frac{TN}{TN+FP} = \frac{9025}{9025+50} = \frac{9025}{9075} \approx 0.9945
$$

**Step 6 — Compute Precision (PPV):**

$$
Prec = \frac{TP}{TP+FP} = \frac{850}{900} \approx 0.9444
$$

**Step 7 — Compute F1-Score (equivalent to Dice for binary tasks):**

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \times 0.9444 \times 0.9189}{0.9444 + 0.9189} = \frac{1.7358}{1.8633} \approx 0.9315 \;\;\checkmark
$$

**Step 8 — Compute Overall Accuracy:**

$$
Acc = \frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN} = \frac{850+9025}{850+9025+50+75} = \frac{9875}{10000} = 0.9875
$$

**Step 9 — Compute Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC):**

$$
MCC = \frac{(850)(9025) - (50)(75)}{\sqrt{(900)(925)(9075)(9100)}}
$$

**Expand the numerator:**

$$
Num = 7{,}671{,}250 - 3{,}750 = 7{,}667{,}500
$$

**Expand the denominator term-by-term:**

$$
900 \times 925 = 832{,}500
$$

$$
9075 \times 9100 = 82{,}582{,}500
$$

$$
Product = 832{,}500 \times 82{,}582{,}500 = 6.8760 \times 10^{13}
$$

$$
\sqrt{Product} \approx 8.2922 \times 10^{6}
$$

**Final MCC:**

$$
MCC = \frac{7{,}667{,}500}{8{,}292{,}200} \approx 0.9247
$$

**Step 10 — Clinical Interpretation (mandatory for full marks):**

> The model achieves a Dice of 0.9315 and IoU of 0.8718, indicating strong volumetric overlap with the ground-truth tumor mask. A sensitivity of 0.9189 means the model correctly identifies ~92% of the true tumor voxels (low false-negative rate — critical in oncology). The very high specificity of 0.9945 confirms negligible false alarms on healthy tissue. The MCC of 0.9247 confirms robust performance even under the heavy class imbalance (only 9.25% positive voxels).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Hausdorff Distance

**Given** two boundary point sets $A$ and $B$, the **directed Hausdorff distance** from $A$ to $B$ is:

$$
h(A,B) = \max_{a \in A} \; \min_{b \in B} \; d(a,b)
$$

**The (undirected) Hausdorff distance** is the maximum of the two directed distances:

$$
HD(A,B) = \max\bigl( h(A,B),\; h(B,A) \bigr)
$$

**Step-by-step explanation for students:**

1. For every point $a$ in set $A$, find its **nearest** point $b$ in set $B$ → store the minimum distance.
2. Repeat for every $a$ → you get a distance profile.
3. Take the **maximum** of this profile → $h(A,B)$.
4. Repeat the whole process in the reverse direction to get $h(B,A)$.
5. The Hausdorff distance is the **worst-case** of the two directions — i.e., "how far is the most badly-matched point?"

> [!NOTE]
> **Why HD95?** The standard HD is famously sensitive to a single noisy outlier point. The **95th-percentile Hausdorff Distance (HD95)** takes the 95th percentile of the distance profile instead of the maximum, making it the de-facto standard in MICCAI challenges.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The Complete Experimental Results Pipeline

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Trained Deep Learning Model] --> B[Load Sequestered Test Set]
    B --> C[Inference No Grad]
    C --> D[Generate Predicted Masks or Class Scores]
    D --> E[Pixel wise Comparison vs Ground Truth]
    E --> F[Construct Confusion Matrix TP FP FN TN]
    F --> G1[Compute Dice Coefficient]
    F --> G2[Compute IoU Jaccard]
    F --> G3[Compute Sensitivity]
    F --> G4[Compute Specificity]
    F --> G5[Compute Hausdorff HD95]
    G1 --> H[Aggregate Mean Std over k Folds]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    G5 --> H
    H --> I[Paired t Test or Wilcoxon p lt 0.05]
    I --> J[Benchmark against SOTA Methods]
    J --> K[Ablation Study Component wise]
    K --> L[Qualitative Figures Overlay Visualizations]
    L --> M[Final Result Table and Discussion]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The pipeline moves left-to-right: the trained model makes predictions on held-out data, these are compared voxel-by-voxel against expert annotations to build a confusion matrix, the matrix is fed into five parallel metric calculators, the per-fold scores are aggregated with mean ± std, a statistical test confirms significance, the model is benchmarked against published SOTA, ablations justify each component, and qualitative figures close the loop with clinician-friendly visualizations.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Metric Selection Decision Block

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Task Type] --> B{Segmentation or Classification}
    B -- Segmentation --> C{Overlap or Boundary}
    C -- Overlap --> D1[Dice Coefficient]
    C -- Overlap --> D2[IoU Jaccard]
    C -- Boundary --> E1[Hausdorff HD95]
    C -- Boundary --> E2[ASSD Average Surface Distance]
    B -- Classification --> F{Balanced or Imbalanced}
    F -- Balanced --> G1[Accuracy]
    F -- Imbalanced --> G2[AUC ROC and AUC PR]
    F -- Imbalanced --> G3[Matthews MCC]
    F -- Clinical Safety --> G4[Sensitivity Recall Mandatory High]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision rule of thumb for KTU viva:** *Overlap-based* metrics (Dice/IoU) measure **volumetric agreement**; *boundary-based* metrics (HD95/ASSD) measure **contour fidelity**; for classification, *never* trust accuracy alone on imbalanced data — pair it with AUC-ROC and sensitivity.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Confusion Matrix Reference (Block Layout)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Predicted Positive] --> B[True Positive TP]
    A --> C[False Positive FP Type I Error]
    D[Predicted Negative] --> E[False Negative FN Type II Error]
    D --> F[True Negative TN]
    B --- G[Ground Truth Positive]
    F --- H[Ground Truth Negative]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical mnemonic:** A *false negative* (missed cancer) is called a **Type II error** and is usually costlier than a false positive — this is why sensitivity is weighted more heavily than specificity in oncology screening.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]`
**Define the Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC) used in medical image segmentation. State its range and the condition under which it equals the F1-score.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC)** is a statistical overlap metric that quantifies the similarity between a predicted segmentation mask $A$ and a ground-truth mask $B$:

$$
DSC = \frac{2\,\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}
$$

**Range:** $0 \leq DSC \leq 1$, where $0$ indicates no overlap and $1$ indicates perfect overlap.

**Equality with F1:** The DSC **is mathematically identical to the F1-score** when applied to a binary confusion matrix. This is because $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot Prec \cdot Sens}{Prec + Sens} = \dfrac{2\,TP}{2\,TP + FP + FN}$, and since $\vert A \cap B \vert = TP$, $\vert A \vert = TP+FP$, $\vert B \vert = TP+FN$, the two expressions reduce to the same formula. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]`
**List any three distance-based metrics used to evaluate medical image segmentation and briefly explain why they are clinically important.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Three distance-based metrics are:

1. **Hausdorff Distance (HD):** Measures the maximum distance between any point on the predicted boundary and its nearest point on the ground-truth boundary. **Clinical importance:** Captures the *worst-case* boundary error — critical for surgical planning where one misaligned voxel can damage healthy tissue.

2. **95th-Percentile Hausdorff Distance (HD95):** The 95th percentile of the distance profile, robust to outlier points. **Clinical importance:** The de-facto standard in MICCAI challenges; preferred over HD because a single noisy outlier does not inflate the score.

3. **Average Symmetric Surface Distance (ASSD):** The mean of all directed nearest-point distances between the two surfaces. **Clinical importance:** Reflects the *average* boundary deviation, giving a balanced view of contour quality across the entire lesion.

**(3 marks)**

---

## PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) Derive the algebraic relationship between the Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC) and the Intersection-over-Union (IoU) metric. Show that DSC is always greater than or equal to IoU. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

Start with the definitions:

$$
DSC = \frac{2\,\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}, \qquad IoU = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}
$$

Apply the inclusion–exclusion identity $\vert A \cup B \vert = \vert A \vert + \vert B \vert - \vert A \cap B \vert$ to the IoU denominator. Let $I = \vert A \cap B \vert$, $a = \vert A \vert$, $b = \vert B \vert$:

$$
IoU = \frac{I}{a+b-I}
$$

From the Dice formula, solve for $I$:

$$
I = \frac{DSC\,(a+b)}{2}
$$

Substitute into the IoU expression:

$$
IoU = \frac{\dfrac{DSC\,(a+b)}{2}}{(a+b) - \dfrac{DSC\,(a+b)}{2}} = \frac{DSC/2}{1 - DSC/2} = \frac{DSC}{2-DSC}
$$

Inverting to express DSC in terms of IoU:

$$
DSC = \frac{2\,IoU}{1+IoU}
$$

**Proof that DSC ≥ IoU:**

Consider the function $f(DSC) = DSC - IoU = DSC - \dfrac{DSC}{2-DSC}$. Compute the difference:

$$
DSC - \frac{DSC}{2-DSC} = \frac{DSC(2-DSC) - DSC}{2-DSC} = \frac{DSC - DSC^2}{2-DSC} = \frac{DSC(1-DSC)}{2-DSC}
$$

Since $0 \leq DSC \leq 1$, the numerator is non-negative, and the denominator is positive. Therefore $DSC - IoU \geq 0$, proving $DSC \geq IoU$. Equality holds only at $DSC = 0$ (no overlap) or $DSC = 1$ (perfect overlap). **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) A binary segmentation model is evaluated on a test set. The voxel-wise counts are: TP = 1200, FP = 100, FN = 80, TN = 98620. Compute the Dice coefficient, IoU, sensitivity, specificity, precision, and F1-score. Provide a clinical interpretation of the sensitivity and specificity values. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

Total positive ground truth: $TP + FN = 1200 + 80 = 1280$

Total predicted positive: $TP + FP = 1200 + 100 = 1300$

Total voxels: $1200 + 100 + 80 + 98620 = 100000$

**Dice Coefficient:**

$$
DSC = \frac{2 \times 1200}{1300 + 1280} = \frac{2400}{2580} \approx 0.9302
$$

**IoU (Jaccard):**

$$
IoU = \frac{1200}{1300 + 1280 - 1200} = \frac{1200}{1380} \approx 0.8696
$$

**Sensitivity (Recall):**

$$
Sens = \frac{1200}{1280} = 0.9375
$$

**Specificity:**

$$
Spec = \frac{98620}{98620 + 100} = \frac{98620}{98720} \approx 0.9990
$$

**Precision (PPV):**

$$
Prec = \frac{1200}{1300} \approx 0.9231
$$

**F1-Score:**

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \times 0.9231 \times 0.9375}{0.9231 + 0.9375} = \frac{1.7308}{1.8606} \approx 0.9302 \;\;\checkmark
$$

(Note: $F_1 = DSC$ as expected.)

**Clinical Interpretation:**

The sensitivity of **0.9375 (93.75%)** means the model correctly identifies 93.75% of the true lesion voxels — i.e., only ~6% of the diseased tissue is missed. In oncology, this is acceptable but should ideally exceed 0.95 for screening applications. The specificity of **0.999 (99.9%)** is excellent, indicating the model rarely flags healthy tissue as diseased (low false-alarm rate). The Dice of 0.93 confirms strong volumetric overlap, and the IoU of 0.87 (which is lower than Dice, as theoretically proven) shows that boundary precision still has room for improvement. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) Explain the role of ablation studies in validating an AI model for medical image analysis. Describe a typical ablation table and justify why ablations are mandatory for KTU-level research reporting. (7 marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Definition:** An *ablation study* is a controlled experiment in which individual components of a deep-learning architecture (e.g., skip connections, attention modules, specific loss functions, data-augmentation steps) are systematically removed or replaced, and the resulting change in performance metrics is measured.

**Purpose (3 roles):**

1. **Causal Justification** — It proves that each component *causally contributes* to the final performance, rather than being a redundant or cosmetic add-on.
2. **Component Ranking** — It ranks components by their marginal contribution (e.g., does the attention block add more Dice than the residual connection?).
3. **Hyperparameter Sensitivity** — It exposes which design choices are robust and which are fragile.

**Typical Ablation Table Structure:**

| Configuration | Dice | IoU | HD95 | Sensitivity |
|---------------|------|-----|------|-------------|
| Baseline U-Net | 0.852 | 0.781 | 7.2 | 0.871 |
| + Residual blocks | 0.879 | 0.812 | 6.1 | 0.901 |
| + Attention gate | 0.902 | 0.841 | 5.4 | 0.919 |
| + Dice loss | 0.918 | 0.862 | 4.8 | 0.928 |
| + Heavy augmentation | 0.930 | 0.870 | 4.2 | 0.938 |
| **Full model** | **0.941** | **0.886** | **3.9** | **0.945** |

**Why mandatory for KTU reporting:**

- Shows the examiner that the student understands *why* the architecture is designed the way it is — not just *what* it is.
- Provides quantitative evidence of the marginal value of each component.
- Demonstrates scientific rigor expected at the B.Tech project level (and aligns with conference/journal standards at MICCAI, CVPR, IEEE TMI). **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Compare and contrast the ROC-AUC and PR-AUC curves for medical image classification. Under what data-distribution condition is PR-AUC strictly preferred over ROC-AUC? (7 marks)**

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Definitions:**

- **ROC-AUC** plots the True Positive Rate (TPR = Sensitivity) on the y-axis against the False Positive Rate (FPR = 1 − Specificity) on the x-axis, sweeping the decision threshold from 0 to 1. AUC is the area under this curve.

$$
AUC_{ROC} = \int_0^1 TPR(FPR)\, d(FPR)
$$

- **PR-AUC** plots Precision (PPV) on the y-axis against Recall (Sensitivity) on the x-axis, again sweeping the threshold. AUC is the area under this curve.

$$
AUC_{PR} = \int_0^1 Precision(Recall)\, d(Recall)
$$

**Comparison Table (write in answer):**

| Property | ROC-AUC | PR-AUC |
|----------|---------|--------|
| Y-axis | Sensitivity (TPR) | Precision |
| X-axis | 1 − Specificity (FPR) | Recall (TPR) |
| Sensitivity to class imbalance | **Insensitive** (a large number of easy TN can mask poor performance) | **Highly sensitive** (TN excluded, only TP/FP/FN matter) |
| Baseline (random) value | 0.5 | Equal to the positive-class fraction $\pi$ |
| Computational cost | Same | Same |
| Preferred when | Data is roughly balanced | Data is heavily imbalanced (e.g., 1% positives) |

**When is PR-AUC strictly preferred:**

When the dataset is **heavily imbalanced** (positive class fraction $\pi \ll 0.5$, e.g., 1–5%), as is typical in medical imaging (rare tumors, incidental nodules, COVID-19 screening). In such cases, ROC-AUC can be artificially inflated by a large pool of true negatives — the FPR denominator $FP + TN$ becomes dominated by $TN$, making the curve look optimistic even if precision is poor. PR-AUC excludes TN entirely from its axis, so a high PR-AUC *guarantees* that the model's positive predictions are reliable.

**Numerical illustration (optional):** Suppose 100 positives and 10000 negatives. A model that flags 200 negatives as positive gives $FPR = 200/10000 = 0.02$ (very small, good-looking ROC), but $Precision = 80/280 \approx 0.286$ (very bad-looking PR). This is the classic imbalance trap. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
>
> 1. **Reporting only accuracy** on an imbalanced medical dataset is the #1 reason for losing 3–4 marks. Always pair accuracy with sensitivity, specificity, and (preferably) AUC.
> 2. **Forgetting to specify the dataset split (train/val/test) and the cross-validation strategy.** Examiners will deduct 2 marks if results are reported without a clear "on a held-out 20% test set, 5-fold CV, mean ± std" preamble.
> 3. **Mixing up TP+FP vs TP+FN** in the Dice formula. Dice uses *both* predicted and ground-truth positives, not just one.
> 4. **Reporting Dice without IoU** (or vice-versa) — KTU 2024 expects you to report *both* and show awareness of the Dice–IoU identity.
> 5. **Ignoring statistical significance.** A 0.5% Dice improvement may not be significant — without a paired t-test or Wilcoxon p-value, the examiner will not award full marks.
> 6. **Forgetting the confusion matrix in derivations.** Many students compute Sensitivity correctly but write the formula as $TP/(TP+FP)$ (which is Precision). Read the question twice.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and keep it beside your exam desk the night before the ESE.**

- **Experimental Results** = **quantitative metrics + qualitative visualizations + SOTA comparison + ablation study**.
- **Dice** uses $\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert$ in the denominator; **IoU** uses $\vert A \cup B \vert$ → **Dice ≥ IoU always**.
- **DSC = F1** for binary segmentation; both equal $\dfrac{2\,TP}{2\,TP + FP + FN}$.
- **Boundary metrics** (HD, HD95, ASSD) measure *contour* quality; **overlap metrics** (Dice, IoU) measure *volume* agreement.
- **HD95** is preferred over **HD** because HD is sensitive to a single outlier.
- **Sensitivity** = recall = TPR; **Specificity** = TNR; **1 − Specificity** = FPR.
- **ROC-AUC** is insensitive to class imbalance; **PR-AUC** is the **clinically preferred** metric for rare-disease screening.
- **Accuracy is misleading on imbalanced data** — pair it with sensitivity, specificity, F1, MCC, or AUC.
- **Statistical significance** is non-negotiable: report mean ± std across k folds and a paired t-test / Wilcoxon p-value.
- **Ablation table** must show incremental component-wise gains (Baseline → +Residual → +Attention → +Loss → +Augmentation → Full).
- **Clinical thresholds to memorize:** Dice ≥ 0.85 for tumor segmentation; Sensitivity ≥ 0.95 for screening; HD95 < 5 mm for radiotherapy.
- **Always declare** dataset, split, preprocessing, hardware, framework, optimizer, learning rate, batch size, epochs, and loss function in the implementation details.
- **Five-fold cross-validation** is the gold standard for small medical datasets to avoid patient-level data leakage.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Breast Cancer Detection from Mammograms Using AI
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT862 - AI FOR MEDICAL IMAGE ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Breast Cancer Detection from Mammograms Using AI

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Mammography** is a specialized low-dose X-ray imaging modality used for the early detection and diagnosis of breast cancer. It captures high-resolution grayscale images of the internal breast tissue, allowing visualization of micro-calcifications, masses, architectural distortions, and asymmetries that are otherwise imperceptible through physical examination.

**AI-based Mammogram Analysis** refers to the application of machine learning (ML) and deep learning (DL) algorithms — primarily **Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs)** — to automatically detect, localize, and classify suspicious breast lesions from mammographic images. The goal is to assist radiologists in the early diagnosis of breast cancer, reduce inter-observer variability, and improve the overall accuracy and efficiency of screening programs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 3):**
> AI for mammogram analysis focuses on the end-to-end pipeline covering **data acquisition → preprocessing → lesion detection → classification (benign/malignant) → BI-RADS scoring**, with emphasis on CNN architectures, transfer learning, and clinical performance evaluation.

## 1.2 The BI-RADS Standard (Foundational Concept)

The **Breast Imaging Reporting and Data System (BI-RADS)** is a standardized assessment scale developed by the **American College of Radiology (ACR)**. It provides a uniform vocabulary for reporting mammogram findings and is the clinical ground truth used to train and validate AI models.

| BI-RADS Category | Clinical Meaning | Malignancy Risk |
|------------------|------------------|------------------|
| 0 | Incomplete – Need additional imaging | N/A |
| 1 | Negative | 0% |
| 2 | Benign findings | 0% |
| 3 | Probably benign | ≤ 2% |
| 4 | Suspicious abnormality | 2% – 95% |
| 5 | Highly suggestive of malignancy | ≥ 95% |
| 6 | Known biopsy-proven malignancy | 100% |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy — "The Radiologist's Second Pair of Eyes"**
> Imagine a senior radiologist who has examined over **1,00,000 mammograms** in her career. She can spot tiny, faint patterns that a junior doctor might miss. An AI model is essentially a mathematical "digital twin" of that experienced radiologist — it learns the subtle visual signatures of cancerous tissue by studying thousands of annotated examples. However, instead of getting tired, distracted, or biased, the AI processes each image with identical focus, flagging suspicious regions in **seconds**. The radiologist and AI together form a *human-in-the-loop* diagnostic system that is more accurate than either alone.

## 1.4 Key Public Benchmark Datasets

- **DDSM (Digital Database for Screening Mammography):** ~2,620 studies, widely used legacy dataset.
- **CBIS-DDSM:** Curated, improved version of DDSM with standardized ROI annotations.
- **MIAS (Mammographic Image Analysis Society):** 322 digitised films from the UK.
- **INbreast:** 115 cases with precise mass and calcification contours.
- **BCDR (Breast Cancer Digital Repository):** Multicentric European dataset with clinical metadata.
- **OPTIMAM Mammography Image Database (OMI-DB):** > 170,000 mammograms from UK NHS.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Pipeline — The "Why" and "How"

The AI-based mammogram analysis pipeline follows a structured sequence:

1. **Image Acquisition** — Full-Field Digital Mammography (FFDM) or digitized film.
2. **Preprocessing** — *Why:* Mammograms suffer from noise, pectoral muscle interference, and low contrast. *How:* Apply median/Gaussian filtering, pectoral muscle removal, intensity normalization, and contrast enhancement (CLAHE).
3. **Region of Interest (ROI) Extraction** — *Why:* Reduces search space for the model. *How:* Use sliding windows, U-Net segmentation, or whole-image input.
4. **Data Augmentation** — *Why:* Medical datasets are small and class-imbalanced. *How:* Apply rotations, flips, zoom, intensity jittering, and elastic deformations.
5. **Model Training (CNN Backbone)** — *Why:* CNNs automatically learn hierarchical features (edges → textures → lesion patterns). *How:* Use architectures like **ResNet-50, VGG-16, Inception-v3, DenseNet, EfficientNet**.
6. **Classification / Detection** — Output: *benign* vs *malignant* + lesion bounding box.
7. **Post-processing & BI-RADS Mapping** — Translate AI confidence into clinical BI-RADS categories.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates the **mathematical backbone** required to solve KTU exam problems on this topic. Memorise the metric definitions and CNN loss function structure.

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|
| True Positive (TP) | Correctly identified malignant lesion | Integer count |
| True Negative (TN) | Correctly identified normal/benign case | Integer count |
| False Positive (FP) | Normal tissue flagged as malignant (Type I error) | Integer count |
| False Negative (FN) | Malignant lesion missed (Type II error) | Integer count |
| Sensitivity (Recall) | $\text{Sens} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Unitless, range $[0,1]$ |
| Specificity | $\text{Spec} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | Unitless, range $[0,1]$ |
| Precision (PPV) | $\text{PPV} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Unitless, range $[0,1]$ |
| F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$ | Unitless, range $[0,1]$ |
| Accuracy | $\text{Acc} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Unitless, range $[0,1]$ |
| AUC-ROC | Area under $TPR$ vs $FPR$ curve | Unitless, range $[0,1]$ |
| Binary Cross-Entropy Loss | $L_{BCE} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y_i \log(\hat{y}_i) + (1-y_i) \log(1-\hat{y}_i) \right]$ | N = batch size |
| Dice Coefficient | $\text{Dice} = \frac{2 \cdot \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$ | Segmentation metric |
| Intersection over Union (IoU) | $\text{IoU} = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | Object detection |
| Focal Loss (for imbalance) | $L_{FL} = -\alpha_t (1-p_t)^{\gamma} \log(p_t)$ | $\gamma \in [0,5]$, $\alpha \in [0,1]$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Substitution Rule for Markdown Tables:** Whenever a formula contains the absolute value or set cardinality symbol, write it as `\vert ... \vert` instead of the vertical bar `|` to prevent breaking the markdown table parser.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Population-level screening** (e.g., Sweden's MASAI trial) showed AI-assisted screening **reduced radiologist workload by ~44%** with no loss in cancer detection rate.
- **Computer-Aided Detection (CAD)** is now embedded in nearly all modern mammography machines (e.g., Hologic's GenAI, Siemens' AI-Rad Companion).
- **Multimodal fusion** of mammography with **ultrasound, MRI, and clinical risk factors** (age, family history, BRCA status) improves predictive accuracy.
- **Edge deployment** on GPU-accelerated PACS workstations enables **sub-second inference** during live clinical workflow.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Confusion Matrix to Clinical Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivation shows how raw classifier outputs (TP, TN, FP, FN) reduce to clinically interpretable metrics. Every step is expanded explicitly for KTU exam reproducibility.

### Step 1 — Define the Confusion Matrix Elements

Given a binary classifier that outputs *malignant* ($y=1$) or *benign* ($y=0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
TP &= \text{Count of malignant cases correctly predicted as malignant} \\
TN &= \text{Count of benign cases correctly predicted as benign} \\
FP &= \text{Count of benign cases incorrectly predicted as malignant} \\
FN &= \text{Count of malignant cases incorrectly predicted as benign}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Derive Sensitivity (True Positive Rate)

Sensitivity measures the proportion of *actual* malignancies that the model successfully *recalls*. Clinically, this is the most important metric because **missing a cancer (FN) is life-threatening**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sensitivity} &= \frac{\text{Correctly identified malignancies}}{\text{Total actual malignancies}} \\
&= \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Derive Specificity (True Negative Rate)

Specificity measures the proportion of *actual* benign cases that the model correctly spares from false alarms.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Specificity} &= \frac{\text{Correctly identified benign cases}}{\text{Total actual benign cases}} \\
&= \frac{TN}{TN + FP}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Derive the F1-Score

F1 is the **harmonic mean** of Precision and Recall. We use harmonic mean (not arithmetic) because it heavily penalises models that are strong in only one metric — a property critical for medical imaging where both false alarms and missed cancers are costly.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}} \\
&= 2 \cdot \frac{\left(\frac{TP}{TP+FP}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{TP}{TP+FN}\right)}{\frac{TP}{TP+FP} + \frac{TP}{TP+FN}} \\
&= \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Problem:** A CNN mammogram classifier is tested on **500** patients. The confusion matrix obtained is: **TP = 180, FN = 20, FP = 30, TN = 270**. Compute Sensitivity, Specificity, Precision, Accuracy, and F1-Score.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total cases} &= TP + TN + FP + FN = 180 + 270 + 30 + 20 = 500 \quad \text{(✓ consistency check)} \\
\text{Sensitivity} &= \frac{180}{180 + 20} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90 \\
\text{Specificity} &= \frac{270}{270 + 30} = \frac{270}{300} = 0.90 \\
\text{Precision} &= \frac{180}{180 + 30} = \frac{180}{210} \approx 0.857 \\
\text{Accuracy} &= \frac{180 + 270}{500} = \frac{450}{500} = 0.90 \\
F_1 &= \frac{2 \cdot 180}{2 \cdot 180 + 30 + 20} = \frac{360}{410} \approx 0.878
\end{aligned}
$$

> The model achieves **90% sensitivity** — clinically excellent for cancer screening where the target is $\geq 85\%$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Why Focal Loss Solves Class Imbalance

In mammography datasets, **benign cases vastly outnumber malignant ones** (often a 10:1 ratio). Standard Cross-Entropy gets overwhelmed by easy negatives.

The **Focal Loss** modifies Cross-Entropy by adding a modulating factor $(1-p_t)^{\gamma}$ that down-weights confident (easy) examples:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{CE} &= -\log(p_t) \\
L_{FL} &= -(1-p_t)^{\gamma} \log(p_t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Effect of $\gamma$:**
- When the model is **confident and correct** ($p_t \to 1$), the factor $(1-p_t)^{\gamma} \to 0$, suppressing the loss.
- When the model is **wrong** ($p_t$ small), the factor $\approx 1$, preserving the gradient signal.

**Therefore, training focuses learning capacity on the rare, hard malignant cases** — exactly what mammography datasets need.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Classical CAD vs Deep Learning CAD

| Parameter | Classical CAD (Hand-crafted) | Deep Learning CAD (CNN) |
|---|---|---|
| Feature Design | Manual (texture, edges, morphology) | Automatic hierarchical features |
| Sensitivity Range | 70% – 85% | 85% – 95% |
| False Positives per Image | 1.0 – 2.5 | 0.3 – 0.8 |
| Training Data Required | Thousands (with hand-labelled features) | Tens of thousands (raw images) |
| Interpretability | High (explicit rules) | Low (black-box, needs Grad-CAM) |
| Adaptability to New Datasets | Poor | Excellent (transfer learning) |
| Computational Cost at Inference | Low | Moderate (GPU) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End AI Mammography Pipeline (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Mammogram FFDM Image] --> B[Preprocessing Module]
    B --> B1[Pectoral Muscle Removal]
    B --> B2[CLAHE Contrast Enhancement]
    B --> B3[Intensity Normalization]
    B1 --> C[Augmentation Layer]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> C1[Rotation and Flip]
    C --> C2[Elastic Deformation]
    C --> C3[Intensity Jitter]
    C1 --> D[CNN Backbone ResNet50 or EfficientNet]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E[Feature Map Extraction]
    E --> F[Global Average Pooling]
    F --> G[Fully Connected Layer 128 units]
    G --> H[Softmax Output Layer]
    H --> H1[Class Benign]
    H --> H2[Class Malignant]
    H1 --> I[BI-RADS Mapping Module]
    H2 --> I
    I --> J[Clinical Report Generation]
    J --> K[Radiologist Review and Sign Off]
```

## 4.2 CNN Architecture for Lesion Classification (Detailed Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT_BLOCK
        IN1[Mammogram Image 224x224x1]
    end
    subgraph CONV_BLOCK_1
        CB1A[Conv2D 3x3 ReLU]
        CB1B[Conv2D 3x3 ReLU]
        CB1P[MaxPool 2x2]
    end
    subgraph CONV_BLOCK_2
        CB2A[Conv2D 3x3 ReLU]
        CB2B[Conv2D 3x3 ReLU]
        CB2P[MaxPool 2x2]
    end
    subgraph CONV_BLOCK_3
        CB3A[Conv2D 3x3 ReLU]
        CB3B[Conv2D 3x3 ReLU]
        CB3C[Conv2D 3x3 ReLU]
        CB3P[MaxPool 2x2]
    end
    subgraph DENSE_BLOCK
        DENS1[Fully Connected 256 ReLU]
        DROP1[Dropout 0.5]
        DENS2[Fully Connected 2 Softmax]
    end
    IN1 --> CB1A --> CB1B --> CB1P --> CB2A --> CB2B --> CB2P --> CB3A --> CB3B --> CB3C --> CB3P --> DENS1 --> DROP1 --> DENS2
    DENS2 --> OUT1[Probability Benign]
    DENS2 --> OUT2[Probability Malignant]
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow for BI-RADS Mapping (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start AI Inference] --> Q1{Model Probability of Malignancy p}
    Q1 -->|p less than 0.05| R1[Map to BI-RADS 1 or 2]
    Q1 -->|0.05 to 0.30| R2[Map to BI-RADS 3 Probably Benign]
    Q1 -->|0.30 to 0.70| R3[Map to BI-RADS 4 Suspicious Recommend Biopsy]
    Q1 -->|p greater than 0.70| R4[Map to BI-RADS 5 Highly Suggestive of Malignancy]
    R1 --> END1[Send to Radiologist Dashboard]
    R2 --> END1
    R3 --> END1
    R4 --> END1
```

## 4.4 Multi-View Fusion Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CC[CC View Craniocaudal] --> CN1[CNN Branch 1]
    MLO[MLO View Mediolateral Oblique] --> CN2[CNN Branch 2]
    CN1 --> FC1[Feature Vector 512D]
    CN2 --> FC2[Feature Vector 512D]
    FC1 --> CONC[Concatenation Layer 1024D]
    FC2 --> CONC
    CONC --> FCF[Fusion Dense Layer 256]
    FCF --> SOFT[Softmax Classifier]
    SOFT --> FINAL[Final Benign or Malignant Decision]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap
