## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Define the term **BI-RADS** and explain the clinical significance of categories **3, 4, and 5**.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> BI-RADS (Breast Imaging Reporting and Data System) is a standardized risk-stratification scale developed by the American College of Radiology to ensure uniform reporting of mammogram findings. Category 3 indicates a *probably benign* finding with malignancy risk $\leq 2\%$, requiring short-interval follow-up (6 months). Category 4 indicates a *suspicious* abnormality with malignancy risk 2%–95%, warranting biopsy for definitive diagnosis. Category 5 indicates findings *highly suggestive of malignancy* with risk $\geq 95\%$, requiring immediate clinical action. *(1 mark – definition, 1 mark – categories 3 & 4, 1 mark – category 5 clinical action).*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** List any **three public benchmark datasets** used for training AI models on mammograms and state **one limitation** of each.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **DDSM** – Limitation: low spatial resolution, scanned film artefacts. *(1 mark)*
> 2. **MIAS** – Limitation: small size (322 images) leads to overfitting. *(1 mark)*
> 3. **INbreast** – Limitation: limited ethnic diversity and small sample size. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
>
> ### **Question A (14 Marks)**
>
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **end-to-end pipeline** of an AI-based mammogram analysis system. Clearly explain the role of **data augmentation** and **transfer learning** in handling limited medical data. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** An AI mammogram classifier was tested on **1,000 patients**. The confusion matrix is: **TP = 250, FN = 50, FP = 100, TN = 600**. Compute the **Sensitivity, Specificity, Precision, Accuracy, and F1-Score**. Comment on whether the model is clinically acceptable for cancer screening. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Pipeline + Augmentation + Transfer Learning (7 marks)**

The pipeline consists of the following sequential stages:

1. **Image Acquisition** – Digital mammograms (FFDM) are obtained from screening centres. *(1 mark)*
2. **Preprocessing** – Includes pectoral muscle removal, contrast enhancement using CLAHE, and intensity normalization to a standard range. *(1 mark)*
3. **Data Augmentation** – Since malignant cases are rare, the dataset is artificially expanded using **rotations (90°/180°/270°), horizontal/vertical flips, zoom, shearing, and elastic deformations**. This increases data diversity and reduces overfitting. *(2 marks)*
4. **Transfer Learning** – A CNN pre-trained on **ImageNet** (e.g., ResNet-50, Inception-v3) is fine-tuned on mammograms. The early layers (edges, textures) transfer universally, while later layers adapt to lesion-specific patterns. This overcomes the limitation of small medical datasets. *(2 marks)*
5. **Classification + BI-RADS Mapping + Clinical Reporting.** *(1 mark)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

Given: TP = 250, FN = 50, FP = 100, TN = 600. Total = 1,000.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sensitivity} &= \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{250}{250 + 50} = \frac{250}{300} = 0.8333 \\
\text{Specificity} &= \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{600}{600 + 100} = \frac{600}{700} = 0.8571 \\
\text{Precision} &= \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{250}{250 + 100} = \frac{250}{350} = 0.7143 \\
\text{Accuracy} &= \frac{TP + TN}{1000} = \frac{250 + 600}{1000} = 0.85 \\
F_1 &= \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN} = \frac{500}{500 + 100 + 50} = \frac{500}{650} = 0.7692
\end{aligned}
$$

**Clinical Acceptability Comment (1 mark):** Sensitivity = 83.3% is **borderline acceptable** (screening target $\geq 85\%$). To improve recall, **threshold lowering** or **focal loss retraining** is recommended.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part A:**
> Students often write "data augmentation adds new data" — this is **wrong**. Augmentation *transforms* existing images, it does not create new clinical information. Write it as *"augmentation expands the effective training distribution via label-preserving transformations."* Likewise, transfer learning is **not** "using a bigger dataset" — it is *reusing learned low-level features* from a source domain. Lose 1–2 marks here if phrased loosely.

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **role of CNNs** in breast cancer detection. Compare **classical hand-crafted CAD** with **deep learning CAD** across at least **four parameters**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the **Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE) loss** from maximum likelihood estimation for a binary mammogram classifier. Show why BCE is preferred over Mean Squared Error (MSE) for classification. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — CNN Role + Classical vs Deep Learning CAD (7 marks)**

**Role of CNNs (3 marks):**
- CNNs automatically learn **hierarchical spatial features** from mammograms: low-level (edges, gradients) in early layers, mid-level (textures, micro-calcification clusters) in middle layers, and high-level (lesion morphology) in deep layers.
- **Transfer learning** with ImageNet-pretrained backbones dramatically reduces the need for large annotated mammogram datasets.
- **End-to-end learning** eliminates manual feature engineering, enabling generalization across imaging devices and patient populations.

**Comparison Table (4 marks — 1 mark per correct comparison point):**

| Parameter | Classical CAD | Deep Learning CAD |
|---|---|---|
| Feature Extraction | Hand-crafted (GLCM, LBP, edges) | Automatic via convolutional filters |
| Sensitivity | 70% – 85% | 85% – 95% |
| False Positives per Image | 1.0 – 2.5 | 0.3 – 0.8 |
| Generalization to New Centers | Poor | Strong (with fine-tuning) |

**Part (b) — Derivation of BCE Loss (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Bernoulli likelihood for binary classification:** *(1 mark)*

$$
P(y \vert x) = \hat{y}^{\,y} \cdot (1 - \hat{y})^{\,(1-y)}
$$

**Step 2 — Log-likelihood for N samples:** *(1 mark)*

$$
\log P(Y \vert X) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y_i \log(\hat{y}_i) + (1-y_i) \log(1-\hat{y}_i) \right]
$$

**Step 3 — Maximize log-likelihood ↔ Minimize negative log-likelihood:** *(1 mark)*

$$
L_{NLL} = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y_i \log(\hat{y}_i) + (1-y_i) \log(1-\hat{y}_i) \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Normalize by N to obtain mean loss:** *(1 mark)*

$$
L_{BCE} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y_i \log(\hat{y}_i) + (1-y_i) \log(1-\hat{y}_i) \right]
$$

**Step 5 — Why BCE > MSE for classification (3 marks):**
- BCE is the **direct negative log-likelihood** of the Bernoulli distribution → gradient descent performs *maximum likelihood estimation*.
- MSE assumes Gaussian noise — inappropriate for categorical outputs.
- BCE loss gradients are **stronger when predictions are wrong**, accelerating convergence. MSE gradients saturate when $\hat{y} \to 0$ or $\hat{y} \to 1$ (sigmoid saturation), causing **vanishing gradients**.
- For class-imbalanced mammogram data, BCE also pairs naturally with **focal loss** extensions, whereas MSE does not.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B:**
> A common error in the BCE derivation is to *assume* the loss form rather than *derive* it. You **must** start from the Bernoulli likelihood and use the $\log(\cdot)$ transformation explicitly. Also, do not confuse "BCE uses logs, MSE uses squares" as a complete justification — KTU examiners expect the **gradient saturation / vanishing gradient** argument for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3**

- **Mammography** is a low-dose X-ray modality; the gold standard for early breast cancer screening.
- **BI-RADS** is the clinical ground-truth scale — know categories **1, 3, 4, 5** and their malignancy risk ranges.
- **Key datasets:** DDSM, CBIS-DDSM, MIAS, INbreast, OMI-DB — know sizes and limitations.
- **Preprocessing steps:** pectoral muscle removal, CLAHE, normalization, augmentation.
- **CNN backbones** used: VGG-16, ResNet-50, Inception-v3, DenseNet, EfficientNet.
- **Transfer learning** is essential due to small medical datasets.
- **Loss functions:** BCE for classification, **Focal Loss** for class imbalance, **Dice Loss** for segmentation.
- **Critical metrics:** Sensitivity (recall), Specificity, Precision, F1-Score, **AUC-ROC** (threshold-independent).
- **Screening clinical target:** Sensitivity $\geq 85\%$, Specificity $\geq 90\%$, FP rate $\leq 0.5$ per image.
- **Multi-view fusion** (CC + MLO views) improves accuracy by 3% – 7% over single-view models.
- **Explainability tools:** Grad-CAM, Saliency Maps, LIME — required for clinical trust and regulatory approval.
- **Key challenges:** class imbalance, inter-centre variability, label noise, regulatory approval (FDA / CE marking).
- **Recent breakthrough:** Google's **Mirai** model (2020) demonstrated **AUC = 0.84** for 5-year future cancer risk prediction.
- **Threshold tuning rule:** Lower decision threshold $\to$ Higher sensitivity, Lower specificity — used in screening contexts.
- **Dice Coefficient** is the standard metric for lesion **segmentation**; **IoU (Jaccard)** is the standard for **object detection** bounding boxes.
- **Always write** absolute value and cardinality as `\vert ... \vert` in markdown tables to avoid parser breakage.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Breast Tumor Detection in Ultrasound Images Using AI
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT862 - AI FOR MEDICAL IMAGE ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Breast Tumor Detection in Ultrasound Images Using AI

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3: Breast Tumor Detection in Ultrasound Images Using AI

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Breast Tumor Detection in Ultrasound Images Using AI** is a computer-aided diagnosis (CAD) framework that applies deep learning and classical machine learning algorithms to B-mode breast ultrasound (BUS) images in order to automatically localize, segment, and classify breast lesions as **benign** (non-cancerous) or **malignant** (cancerous). The system replaces subjective radiologist interpretation with quantitative, reproducible, and high-throughput diagnostic intelligence based on features such as shape, margin, orientation, echo pattern, and posterior acoustic behavior.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology:** The official term used in the syllabus is *Computer-Aided Detection/Diagnosis (CADe/CADx)*. For breast ultrasound, the system is generally referred to as **BUS-CAD** (Breast Ultrasound Computer-Aided Diagnosis).

The pipeline is built on a backbone of **Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs)**, often implemented as **U-Net, ResNet, DenseNet, or VGG-16** architectures, pretrained on **ImageNet** and fine-tuned using curated datasets such as **BUSI (Breast Ultrasound Images Dataset)** and **UDIAT**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a senior radiologist who has personally examined **50,000 breast ultrasound scans** over a 20-year career. This doctor can, in seconds, look at a new scan and say, *"That round, well-circumscribed dark spot with smooth edges is a fibroadenoma — most likely benign."* Now imagine we can compress all of that experience into a mathematical model.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Intuition:** An AI breast tumor detector works exactly like that veteran radiologist. During training, it is shown tens of thousands of ultrasound images *with* the correct diagnosis label. It learns to recognize visual patterns (texture, edges, shape, brightness gradients) that statistically correlate with malignancy. When a *new, unseen* scan is given, it produces a probability score — for example, **0.87** — meaning *“87% chance this is malignant.”*

The key difference: the AI does not "see" the tumor the way humans do. It operates on **pixel intensity values** (a grayscale matrix where each pixel is a number from 0–255) and detects statistically recurring mathematical patterns that humans cannot consciously compute.

### 1.3 Standard Clinical Metrics Used in BUS-CAD

The clinical performance of any breast tumor AI system is quantified using four primary metrics (all bolded below because they are *standard KTU terminology*):

- **Sensitivity (Recall / True Positive Rate):** Probability that a malignant tumor is correctly flagged. Clinically non-negotiable — must be high.
- **Specificity (True Negative Rate):** Probability that a benign case is correctly identified.
- **Accuracy:** Overall proportion of correct predictions.
- **AUC-ROC (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve):** Threshold-independent measure of class separability; the gold-standard single-number summary.

> [!TIP]
> **Why these four?** In oncology, a *false negative* (missing cancer) is far more dangerous than a *false positive* (unnecessary biopsy). Hence KTU examiners frequently emphasize **Sensitivity** in numerical problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Pipeline — Step-by-Step Logic

A KTU-style answer describing a BUS-CAD system should always follow this five-stage pipeline. Memorize the order — it is the most repeated question in this module.

**Stage 1 — Image Acquisition & Preprocessing**
- Raw BUS images contain **speckle noise** (a granular interference pattern inherent to ultrasound physics).
- Preprocessing steps (in order):
  1. **Speckle Noise Reduction** using filters: Median, Gaussian, Anisotropic Diffusion, or Non-Local Means (NLM).
  2. **Contrast Enhancement** using **CLAHE (Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization)** to make tumor boundaries visible.
  3. **Image Resizing** to a fixed dimension (typically 224×224 or 256×256 pixels) to match CNN input layers.
  4. **Normalization** — pixel values scaled to [0, 1] by dividing by 255.

**Stage 2 — Region of Interest (ROI) Extraction / Segmentation**
- Either a radiologist manually crops the ROI, OR
- An automatic segmentation network (commonly **U-Net**) generates a binary mask isolating the lesion from healthy tissue.

**Stage 3 — Feature Extraction**
- **Classical approach:** Hand-crafted features using GLCM (Gray Level Co-occurrence Matrix), LBP (Local Binary Pattern), HOG (Histogram of Oriented Gradients).
- **Deep Learning approach:** A CNN backbone (e.g., ResNet-50) automatically learns hierarchical features — low-level (edges, blobs) to high-level (tumor morphology).

**Stage 4 — Classification**
- The extracted feature vector is fed into a classifier:
  - Classical ML: **SVM, Random Forest, K-Nearest Neighbors, Logistic Regression**.
  - Deep Learning: Fully Connected (Dense) layers with **Softmax** activation producing $P(\text{benign})$ and $P(\text{malignant})$.

**Stage 5 — Post-Processing & BI-RADS Mapping**
- The model output probability is mapped to a **BI-RADS category** (Breast Imaging Reporting and Data System):
  - BI-RADS 2: Benign
  - BI-RADS 3: Probably Benign
  - BI-RADS 4: Suspicious
  - BI-RADS 5: Highly Suggestive of Malignancy
  - BI-RADS 6: Known Biopsy-Proven Malignancy

> [!NOTE]
> **"Why U-Net for segmentation?"** U-Net's encoder–decoder architecture with **skip connections** preserves fine spatial detail — critical for tracing irregular malignant margins.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Why It Matters |
|------|----------------|
| Speckle filtering | Speckle mimics microcalcifications, increasing false positives |
| CLAHE | Tumors in BUS are often *hypoechoic* (darker than surroundings) with subtle margins — contrast makes these visible |
| Resizing to 224×224 | Matches ImageNet-pretrained network input expectations |
| Normalization | Stabilizes gradient descent during training |
| Data augmentation | BUS datasets are small (~700 images); rotation, flipping, zooming artificially expand training set |
| Transfer learning | Avoids training from scratch; leverages low-level visual features already learned from 1.2M natural images |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory memorization for KTU 2024 ESE. Every equation below has appeared (or is highly likely to appear) in the Part B 14-mark question.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | Sensitivity (Recall / TPR) | $Sensitivity = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $TP$ = True Positives, $FN$ = False Negatives |
| 2 | Specificity (TNR) | $Specificity = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | $TN$ = True Negatives, $FP$ = False Positives |
| 3 | Accuracy | $Accuracy = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Total correct predictions / Total samples |
| 4 | Precision (PPV) | $Precision = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Positive Predictive Value |
| 5 | F1-Score | $F1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{Precision \cdot Recall}{Precision + Recall}$ | Harmonic mean of precision and recall |
| 6 | Dice Coefficient (Segmentation) | $Dice = \dfrac{2 \cdot \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$ | $A$ = predicted mask, $B$ = ground truth |
| 7 | IoU (Jaccard Index) | $IoU = \dfrac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | Intersection over Union |
| 8 | Binary Cross-Entropy Loss | $L_{BCE} = -\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left[y_i \log(\hat{y}_i) + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{y}_i)\right]$ | $y_i$ = true label, $\hat{y}_i$ = predicted probability |
| 9 | Dice Loss | $L_{Dice} = 1 - \dfrac{2\sum p_i g_i + \epsilon}{\sum p_i + \sum g_i + \epsilon}$ | $p_i$ = prediction, $g_i$ = ground truth, $\epsilon$ = smoothing |
| 10 | Softmax Activation | $\sigma(z_j) = \dfrac{e^{z_j}}{\sum_{k=1}^{K} e^{z_k}}$ | Converts logits to probabilities |
| 11 | 2D Convolution Output Size | $W_{out} = \dfrac{W_{in} - F + 2P}{S} + 1$ | $W$ = width, $F$ = filter, $P$ = padding, $S$ = stride |
| 12 | Receptive Field Growth | $RF_{l+1} = RF_l + (F_l - 1) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{l} S_i$ | Layer-by-layer growth of RF |
| 13 | AUC-ROC Definition | $AUC = \int_{0}^{1} TPR(FPR^{-1}(t)) \, dt$ | Integral of ROC curve |
| 14 | CLAHE Clip Limit | $T_{clip} = c \cdot \dfrac{N_x N_y}{L_{gray}}$ | $c$ = clip factor, $L_{gray}$ = number of gray levels |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** These same equations are used in industrial defect detection, satellite image analysis, and any medical imaging modality (CT, MRI, X-ray). Mastering them gives transferrable skills for any CAD application.

## 2.4 Ultrasound-Specific Imaging Artifacts to Know

| Artifact | Description | Effect on AI |
|----------|-------------|--------------|
| Speckle Noise | Granular interference pattern from wave scattering | Mimics texture of malignant tissue |
| Acoustic Shadowing | Dark region behind a strongly attenuating mass | Can cause under-segmentation |
| Posterior Enhancement | Bright region behind fluid-filled cysts | Can cause over-segmentation |
| Edge Shadowing | Thin dark line at curved interfaces | Confuses margin detection |
| Reverberation | Parallel echo lines from strong reflectors | Adds false texture features |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly call speckle "Gaussian noise." Speckle is **multiplicative noise**, modeled as $I_{observed} = I_{true} \cdot n_{speckle} + n_{additive}$. This distinction matters when selecting filters (NLM > Gaussian for BUS).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Confusion Matrix to All Clinical Metrics

**Problem Setup (typical KTU-style):** A BUS-CAD system is tested on a hospital dataset of 1000 breast ultrasound cases. Ground truth confirms 400 malignant and 600 benign cases. The AI system reports the following outcomes:

| | Malignant (Actual) | Benign (Actual) |
|---|---|---|
| **Predicted Malignant** | 360 | 80 |
| **Predicted Benign** | 40 | 520 |

Compute: Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, Precision, F1-Score.

**Step 1 — Extract confusion matrix entries.**

From the table, we read directly:

$$
TP = 360, \quad FN = 40, \quad FP = 80, \quad TN = 520
$$

**Step 2 — Verify the totals.**

$$
TP + FN = 360 + 40 = 400 \quad \text{(matches actual malignant count)} \\
FP + TN = 80 + 520 = 600 \quad \text{(matches actual benign count)} \\
TP + FP + FN + TN = 360 + 80 + 40 + 520 = 1000 \quad \text{(matches total)}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Sensitivity.**

Sensitivity is the proportion of *actual malignant* cases that are correctly identified as malignant.

$$
Sensitivity = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{360}{360 + 40} = \frac{360}{400} = 0.90
$$

So **Sensitivity = 90%**.

**Step 4 — Compute Specificity.**

Specificity is the proportion of *actual benign* cases that are correctly identified as benign.

$$
Specificity = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{520}{520 + 80} = \frac{520}{600} \approx 0.8667
$$

So **Specificity ≈ 86.67%**.

**Step 5 — Compute Accuracy.**

$$
Accuracy = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{360 + 520}{1000} = \frac{880}{1000} = 0.88
$$

So **Accuracy = 88%**.

**Step 6 — Compute Precision (PPV).**

Precision answers: *"Of all patients the AI flagged as malignant, how many actually had cancer?"*

$$
Precision = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{360}{360 + 80} = \frac{360}{440} \approx 0.8182
$$

So **Precision ≈ 81.82%**.

**Step 7 — Compute F1-Score.**

F1 is the harmonic mean of Precision and Sensitivity, designed to penalize extreme imbalance between them.

$$
F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{Precision \cdot Sensitivity}{Precision + Sensitivity}
$$

$$
F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8182 \times 0.90}{0.8182 + 0.90} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.7364}{1.7182}
$$

$$
F1 = 2 \cdot 0.4286 \approx 0.8571
$$

So **F1-Score ≈ 85.71%**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Dice Coefficient from First Principles

**Why Dice?** When segmenting a tumor, we need a metric that measures the *overlap* between predicted mask $A$ and ground-truth mask $B$.

**Step 1 — Define the Dice Coefficient.**

$$
Dice(A, B) = \frac{2 \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}
$$

The factor of 2 makes the score range from 0 (no overlap) to 1 (perfect overlap).

**Step 2 — Convert to pixel-count form.**

Let $|A|$ = number of pixels predicted as tumor, $|B|$ = number of pixels actually tumor, $|A \cap B|$ = pixels correctly predicted as tumor. Then:

$$
Dice = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}
$$

where $FP$ = pixels wrongly marked as tumor and $FN$ = missed tumor pixels.

**Step 3 — Numerical Example.**

Suppose:
- $TP = 8000$ pixels (correctly identified tumor)
- $FP = 1000$ pixels (healthy tissue wrongly marked as tumor)
- $FN = 500$ pixels (missed tumor)

$$
Dice = \frac{2 \times 8000}{2 \times 8000 + 1000 + 500} = \frac{16000}{17500} \approx 0.9143
$$

So **Dice Score ≈ 91.43%**, indicating a strong segmentation model.

**Step 4 — Relationship to IoU.**

$$
Dice = \frac{2 \cdot IoU}{1 + IoU} \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad IoU = \frac{Dice}{2 - Dice}
$$

This means **Dice ≥ IoU** for the same prediction, and Dice is the more commonly reported metric in medical image segmentation papers.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: CNN Output Dimension for the First Convolutional Layer

**Problem:** A BUS image of size 224×224×1 (grayscale) is passed through a Conv2D layer with 32 filters, each of size 3×3, stride $S=1$, and zero padding $P=1$. Compute the output feature map dimensions and the number of trainable parameters.

**Step 1 — Compute Output Width.**

$$
W_{out} = \frac{W_{in} - F + 2P}{S} + 1 = \frac{224 - 3 + 2(1)}{1} + 1 = \frac{223}{1} + 1 = 224
$$

So the output is **224×224×32**.

**Step 2 — Compute Trainable Parameters per Filter.**

Each filter has $F \times F \times C_{in} = 3 \times 3 \times 1 = 9$ weights, plus 1 bias.

$$
Params_{per\_filter} = 9 + 1 = 10
$$

**Step 3 — Total Parameters for the Layer.**

$$
Params_{total} = N_{filters} \times Params_{per\_filter} = 32 \times 10 = 320
$$

So the first Conv2D layer has only **320 trainable parameters** — remarkably few, which is why deep networks can be trained even on small BUS datasets via transfer learning.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Benign vs Malignant Ultrasound Features

This is the **single most important table** for the KTU 14-mark question on tumor characteristics. Memorize it.

| Feature | Benign Tumor | Malignant Tumor |
|---------|-------------|-----------------|
| **Shape** | Oval, round, well-defined | Irregular, stellate, spiculated |
| **Margin** | Smooth, circumscribed | Indistinct, microlobulated, angular |
| **Orientation** | Parallel to skin ("wider-than-tall") | Not parallel ("taller-than-wide") |
| **Echo Pattern** | Hypoechoic, homogeneous | Markedly hypoechoic, heterogeneous |
| **Posterior Features** | Enhancement (cysts) or none | Acoustic shadowing |
| **Calcifications** | Macrocalcifications (coarse) | Microcalcifications (clustered) |
| **Boundary** | Abrupt interface | Echogenic halo |
| **BI-RADS Score** | 2 or 3 | 4, 5, or 6 |

> [!NOTE]
> **"Taller-than-wide"** is a key BI-RADS descriptor — it means the tumor's AP dimension exceeds its transverse dimension. This is one of the **strongest single predictors of malignancy** in BUS.

## 3.5 Classical vs Deep Learning Comparison

| Aspect | Classical ML (SVM, RF) | Deep Learning (CNN, U-Net) |
|--------|------------------------|----------------------------|
| Feature Engineering | Manual (GLCM, LBP, HOG) | Automatic, learned end-to-end |
| Dataset Size Required | Small (hundreds) | Large (thousands) |
| Computation | Low (CPU) | High (GPU strongly recommended) |
| Interpretability | High | Low (black box) |
| Accuracy on BUS | 80–88% | 92–98% |
| Best Use Case | Resource-limited clinics | Research hospitals with GPU servers |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End BUS-CAD Pipeline (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw B-mode Breast Ultrasound Image] --> B[Preprocessing Module]
    B --> B1[Speckle Noise Reduction - NLM Filter]
    B1 --> B2[CLAHE Contrast Enhancement]
    B2 --> B3[Resize to 224x224]
    B3 --> B4[Pixel Normalization 0 to 1]
    B4 --> C{Choose Analysis Path}
    C -->|Segmentation| D[U-Net Architecture]
    C -->|Classification| E[CNN Backbone - ResNet50]
    D --> D1[Encoder Block - Down Sampling]
    D1 --> D2[Bottleneck Layer]
    D2 --> D3[Decoder Block - Up Sampling]
    D3 --> D4[Skip Connections for Detail Preservation]
    D4 --> F1[Tumor Binary Mask Output]
    E --> E1[Conv Block 1 - Low Level Edges]
    E1 --> E2[Conv Block 2 - Textures and Patterns]
    E2 --> E3[Conv Block 3 - Tumor Morphology]
    E3 --> E4[Global Average Pooling]
    E4 --> E5[Fully Connected Dense Layer]
    E5 --> E6[Softmax Output - Benign or Malignant]
    E6 --> F2[Probability Score and BI-RADS Mapping]
    F1 --> G[Post Processing and Report Generation]
    F2 --> G
    G --> H[Final Diagnostic Report for Radiologist]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The flowchart traces the lifecycle of a single ultrasound image from raw acquisition to final diagnostic report. The diamond-shaped decision node `C` shows the fork between *segmentation* (where is the tumor?) and *classification* (what is the tumor?).

## 4.2 U-Net Architecture Detail (Functional Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    In[Input 224x224x1] --> E1[Encoder 1: 64 filters]
    E1 --> MP1[MaxPool 2x2]
    MP1 --> E2[Encoder 2: 128 filters]
    E2 --> MP2[MaxPool 2x2]
    MP2 --> E3[Encoder 3: 256 filters]
    E3 --> MP3[MaxPool 2x2]
    MP3 --> Bottleneck[Bottleneck: 512 filters]
    Bottleneck --> UP1[Up-Conv 2x2]
    UP1 --> D1[Decoder 1: 256 filters]
    E3 -.Skip Connection.-> D1
    D1 --> UP2[Up-Conv 2x2]
    UP2 --> D2[Decoder 2: 128 filters]
    E2 -.Skip Connection.-> D2
    D2 --> UP3[Up-Conv 2x2]
    UP3 --> D3[Decoder 3: 64 filters]
    E1 -.Skip Connection.-> D3
    D3 --> Out[Output Mask 224x224x1]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Skip connections** (the dotted arrows) copy feature maps from encoder to decoder at matching resolutions. This is the defining innovation of U-Net (Ronneberger et al., 2015) and is why it excels at pixel-perfect medical image segmentation.

## 4.3 Confusion Matrix Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CM[Confusion Matrix for BUS Classifier]
        direction LR
        A1[" "] -->|" "| B1[Predicted Benign]
        A1 -->|" "| B2[Predicted Malignant]
        C1[Actual Benign] --> D1["TN = 520"]
        C1 --> D2["FP = 80"]
        C2[Actual Malignant] --> E1["FN = 40"]
        C2 --> E2["TP = 360"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> The four cells (TN, FP, FN, TP) form the foundation of every classification metric in BUS-CAD. KTU examiners expect students to **draw and label** this matrix in numerical problems.

## 4.4 Training Loop Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Training Image Batch] --> B[Forward Pass Through CNN]
    B --> C[Prediction - Softmax Probabilities]
    C --> D[Compute Loss - BCE or Dice Loss]
    D --> E[Backpropagation - Compute Gradients]
    E --> F[Optimizer - Adam or SGD - Update Weights]
    F --> G{Validation Accuracy Improved}
    G -->|Yes| H[Save Best Model Checkpoint]
    G -->|No| I[Continue Training]
    H --> I
    I --> J{All Epochs Complete}
    J -->|No| A
    J -->|Yes| K[Final Trained Model for Deployment]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]
**Define BI-RADS in the context of breast ultrasound imaging. Mention any four categories.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **BI-RADS (Breast Imaging Reporting and Data System)** is a standardized classification scheme developed by the American College of Radiology (ACR) to provide a uniform vocabulary for reporting breast imaging findings and to stratify cancer risk.

The four main categories are:
1. **BI-RADS 2:** Benign finding (e.g., simple cyst) — 0% risk of malignancy.
2. **BI-RADS 3:** Probably benign — short-interval follow-up recommended (≤2% risk).
3. **BI-RADS 4:** Suspicious abnormality — biopsy should be considered (2–95% risk).
4. **BI-RADS 5:** Highly suggestive of malignancy — appropriate action should be taken (≥95% risk).

> [!TIP]
> **Why 3-marker answer?** BI-RADS definition + 4 categories (each = 0.5) + clinical utility of standardization = perfect 3-mark structure.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]
**List and briefly explain any three artifacts that appear in breast ultrasound images.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Speckle Noise:** A granular multiplicative interference pattern caused by the scattering of ultrasound waves from sub-wavelength tissue structures. It reduces image contrast and can mimic microcalcifications, increasing false positives in CAD.

2. **Acoustic Shadowing:** A dark (hypoechoic) region appearing *behind* a strongly attenuating structure such as a malignant tumor. It can cause under-segmentation of the tumor in deep regions.

3. **Posterior Acoustic Enhancement:** A bright (hyperechoic) region appearing *behind* a fluid-filled or low-attenuation structure such as a simple cyst. It can cause over-segmentation, leading the algorithm to include healthy tissue in the predicted mask.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's expectation:** Each artifact should have a *one-line definition* and a *one-line effect on AI* — 1 mark per artifact.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Module 3]

**(a)** Describe the complete end-to-end pipeline for AI-based breast tumor detection in ultrasound images. Explain each preprocessing step with its purpose. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a U-Net used for tumor segmentation. Discuss why skip connections are critical for this task. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

The end-to-end AI-based breast tumor detection pipeline in ultrasound images consists of the following five sequential stages:

**Stage 1 — Image Acquisition (1 mark)**
B-mode ultrasound images of the breast are acquired using a linear-array transducer (typically 7–15 MHz). These are 2D grayscale images stored as 2D matrices of pixel intensity values.

**Stage 2 — Preprocessing (3 marks)** — *most marks are awarded here*

- **(i) Speckle Noise Reduction:** Speckle is a *multiplicative* noise inherent to ultrasound. A **Non-Local Means (NLM)** filter or **Anisotropic Diffusion** filter is applied. Purpose: remove granular texture that could be confused with microcalcifications.
- **(ii) Contrast Enhancement using CLAHE:** Standard histogram equalization over-amplifies noise. CLAHE limits the contrast amplification using a clip limit, computed as $T_{clip} = c \cdot \frac{N_x N_y}{L_{gray}}$. Purpose: enhance the visibility of hypoechoic tumor regions.
- **(iii) Image Resizing:** The image is resized to a fixed dimension (224×224 or 256×256). Purpose: match the input layer size of the CNN.
- **(iv) Normalization:** Pixel values are scaled to [0, 1] by dividing by 255. Purpose: stabilize gradient descent and accelerate convergence.

**Stage 3 — ROI / Segmentation (1 mark)**
Either a radiologist manually crops the tumor ROI, or a U-Net automatically segments the lesion into a binary mask.

**Stage 4 — Feature Extraction & Classification (1 mark)**
A pretrained CNN (e.g., ResNet-50) extracts hierarchical features from the ROI. A fully connected layer with Softmax activation produces $P(\text{benign})$ and $P(\text{malignant})$.

**Stage 5 — Post-Processing (1 mark)**
The probability output is mapped to a BI-RADS category, and the result is integrated into a structured diagnostic report for the radiologist.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**U-Net Architecture (3 marks for diagram, 4 marks for explanation)**

The U-Net consists of two symmetric paths:

- **Contracting Path (Encoder):** A series of convolution + ReLU + max-pooling blocks. Each block halves the spatial resolution and doubles the number of feature channels (64 → 128 → 256 → 512). It captures *context* — the global "what" of the image.

- **Expansive Path (Decoder):** A symmetric series of up-convolutions + concatenation + convolution blocks. Each block doubles the spatial resolution and halves the channels. It enables *precise localization* — the exact pixel boundaries of the tumor.

- **Bottleneck:** The deepest layer at the bottom of the U, with 1024 feature channels. It encodes the most abstract, compressed representation of the input.

**Why Skip Connections Are Critical (4 marks):**

1. **Spatial Detail Recovery:** Max-pooling in the encoder irreversibly discards fine spatial detail. Skip connections directly copy high-resolution feature maps from encoder to decoder, allowing the network to recover pixel-accurate tumor boundaries.

2. **Gradient Flow:** Skip connections provide a shortcut path for gradients during backpropagation, mitigating the vanishing gradient problem in deep networks.

3. **Multi-Scale Feature Fusion:** The encoder captures both low-level (edges, gradients) and high-level (tumor morphology) features. Skip connections fuse these at the decoder, enabling segmentation at multiple scales.

4. **Limited Data Training:** U-Net can be trained effectively on small medical datasets (a few hundred images) because the skip connections provide a strong prior for spatial localization.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently **forget to mention the bottleneck layer** when describing U-Net. Always include the four components: **Encoder, Bottleneck, Decoder, Skip Connections**. Skipping the bottleneck costs 1 mark.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Module 3 - Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Differentiate between classical machine learning and deep learning approaches for breast tumor classification. Compare their accuracy, data requirements, and interpretability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A BUS-CAD system is tested on 800 breast ultrasound cases. The confusion matrix is as follows: TP = 320, FP = 60, FN = 80, TN = 340. Compute: (i) Accuracy, (ii) Sensitivity, (iii) Specificity, (iv) Precision, (v) F1-Score, and (vi) Dice Coefficient. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

| Parameter | Classical ML (SVM, RF, KNN) | Deep Learning (CNN, U-Net) |
|-----------|------------------------------|----------------------------|
| **Feature Engineering** | Manual — GLCM, LBP, HOG, shape descriptors | Automatic, hierarchical, learned end-to-end |
| **Dataset Size Needed** | Small (200–1000 images) | Large (5000+ images or use transfer learning) |
| **Hardware** | CPU is sufficient | GPU strongly recommended (NVIDIA CUDA) |
| **Training Time** | Minutes | Hours to days |
| **Accuracy on BUS** | 80–88% | 92–98% |
| **Interpretability** | High (can inspect extracted features) | Low (black box, needs Grad-CAM for explanation) |
| **Robustness to Noise** | Moderate (manual features may be noise-sensitive) | High (CNNs learn noise-invariant features) |
| **Clinical Trust** | Higher (explainable) | Lower (requires XAI techniques) |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Classical ML is preferable in resource-constrained clinics with small datasets and a need for explainability. Deep learning is preferable in well-equipped hospitals prioritizing diagnostic accuracy. Modern BUS-CAD research favors **hybrid models** that combine hand-crafted radiomics features with deep CNN embeddings for the best of both worlds.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $TP = 320$, $FP = 60$, $FN = 80$, $TN = 340$, Total = 800.

**(i) Accuracy (1 mark):**

$$
Accuracy = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{320 + 340}{800} = \frac{660}{800} = 0.825
$$

**Accuracy = 82.5%**

**(ii) Sensitivity (1.5 marks):**

$$
Sensitivity = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{320}{320 + 80} = \frac{320}{400} = 0.80
$$

**Sensitivity = 80%**

**(iii) Specificity (1.5 marks):**

$$
Specificity = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{340}{340 + 60} = \frac{340}{400} = 0.85
$$

**Specificity = 85%**

**(iv) Precision (1 mark):**

$$
Precision = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{320}{320 + 60} = \frac{320}{380} \approx 0.8421
$$

**Precision ≈ 84.21%**

**(v) F1-Score (1 mark):**

$$
F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{Precision \cdot Recall}{Precision + Recall} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8421 \times 0.80}{0.8421 + 0.80}
$$

$$
F1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.6737}{1.6421} = 2 \cdot 0.4103 \approx 0.8206
$$

**F1-Score ≈ 82.06%**

**(vi) Dice Coefficient (1 mark):**

$$
Dice = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN} = \frac{2 \times 320}{2 \times 320 + 60 + 80} = \frac{640}{780} \approx 0.8205
$$

**Dice ≈ 82.05%**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Many students mistakenly put $FN$ in the denominator of the Specificity formula. Specificity is computed *only* on actual-negative cases. Memorize: **Sensitivity uses columns containing "actual positive" only; Specificity uses columns containing "actual negative" only.**
>
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** In Dice, students often write the formula as $\frac{TP}{TP+FP+FN}$ *without the factor of 2*. This silently reduces the score. Always include the leading "2·".

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — print this section and read it 5 times before the exam.**

- ✅ **BUS = B-mode Ultrasound**; the AI system is called **BUS-CAD**.
- ✅ The two main public datasets for this task are **BUSI** and **UDIAT**.
- ✅ The standard CNN backbones used are **ResNet, VGG, DenseNet, Inception**.
- ✅ The standard segmentation network is **U-Net**, defined by its **encoder–decoder with skip connections**.
- ✅ **Preprocessing pipeline order:** Speckle filter → CLAHE → Resize → Normalize.
- ✅ **Speckle is multiplicative noise**, not Gaussian — so use NLM or anisotropic diffusion, not a simple Gaussian filter.
- ✅ **CLAHE** stands for *Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization*; it prevents the over-amplification of noise in homogeneous regions.
- ✅ **BI-RADS** stratifies risk from 2 (benign) to 6 (biopsy-proven cancer).
- ✅ **"Taller-than-wide" orientation** is a strong predictor of malignancy.
- ✅ **Sensitivity is non-negotiable in oncology** — a missed cancer (FN) is far worse than a false alarm (FP).
- ✅ **Dice Loss** is preferred over Cross-Entropy for class-imbalanced tumor segmentation.
- ✅ **Transfer learning** is essential because BUS datasets are small (typically 700–2000 images).
- ✅ **AUC-ROC** is the gold-standard threshold-independent performance metric.
- ✅ The four pillars of the confusion matrix: **TP, TN, FP, FN** — and the direction of division in each metric.
- ✅ **IoU < Dice** for the same prediction; the relationship is $IoU = \frac{Dice}{2 - Dice}$.
- ✅ Always mention the **bottleneck layer** when describing U-Net.
- ✅ **Data augmentation** techniques used: rotation, flipping, zooming, brightness jittering, elastic deformation.
- ✅ **Grad-CAM** is the standard explainability technique for visualizing which image regions the CNN used to make its decision.

> [!TIP]
> **Final exam mantra:** *“Speckle filter first, then CLAHE, then CNN, then BI-RADS report.”* If you remember this pipeline order, you can answer 80% of descriptive questions on this module.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AI Based Skin Cancer Detection - Machine Learning Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT862 - AI FOR MEDICAL IMAGE ANALYSIS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** AI Based Skin Cancer Detection - Machine Learning Techniques # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **AI-Based Skin Cancer Detection** refers to the application of computational intelligence—specifically classical **Machine Learning (ML)** and pattern recognition algorithms—to automatically analyze dermoscopic images of skin lesions and classify them as **malignant** (e.g., melanoma, basal cell carcinoma) or **benign** (e.g., nevus, seborrheic keratosis). Under the KTU 2024 syllabus, this topic emphasizes the *traditional* ML pipeline: hand-crafted feature engineering followed by statistical classifiers, as opposed to end-to-end deep learning. Mathematically, the task is a binary (or multi-class) classification problem: $$ f: \mathbf{X} \rightarrow \mathbf{Y}, \quad \text{where } \mathbf{X} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{n}, \; \mathbf{Y} \in \{0, 1, \dots, K-1\} $$ Here, $\mathbf{X}$ is the extracted feature vector from a dermoscopic image and $\mathbf{Y}$ is the class label (e.g., 0 = benign, 1 = melanoma). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 focuses on **classical ML techniques** for skin cancer—covering the ABCD rule, geometric/texture features, and statistical classifiers (SVM, Random Forest, KNN, ANN). End-to-end CNNs are typically covered as a comparative baseline, not the primary focus. ## 1.2 The Clinical Motivation Skin cancer is the **most common** form of human cancer globally. **Melanoma**, though accounting for only ~1% of skin cancers, causes the **majority** of skin-cancer-related deaths. Early detection improves the 5-year survival rate from **~15% (late stage)** to over **~99% (early stage)**. This makes automated, AI-driven screening a high-impact application of medical image analysis. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Dermatologist's Checklist"** > Imagine a dermatologist examining a mole with a magnifying glass. Without realizing it, they subconsciously follow the **ABCD rule**: > - **A**symmetry – Is one half different from the other? > - **B**order – Are the edges ragged or smooth? > - **C**olor – Is the color uneven (multiple shades)? > - **D**iameter – Is it larger than 6 mm (a pencil eraser)? > > A classical ML system **quantifies** exactly these observations into numerical features (e.g., an asymmetry index = 0.27, color variance = 38.5) and feeds them into a classifier that has "learned" from thousands of previously labeled lesions. The ML model acts like a **mathematical twin of the expert's intuition**, but at machine speed and with perfect reproducibility. ## 1.4 Types of Skin Lesions Relevant to AI Classification | Class | Description | Clinical Risk | |---|---|---| | **Melanoma (MEL)** | Malignant melanocyte tumor | **High** — metastasizes rapidly | | **Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC)** | Malignant epithelial tumor | Moderate — slow-growing | | **Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC)** | Malignant keratinocyte tumor | Moderate | | **Actinic Keratosis (AK)** | Pre-cancerous lesion | Precursor | | **Benign Keratosis (BKL)** | Seborrheic / solar keratosis | **Low** (benign) | | **Dermatofibroma (DF)** | Benign fibrous nodule | **Low** | | **Melanocytic Nevi (NV)** | Common mole (benign) | **Low** | | **Vascular Lesion (VASC)** | Hemangioma, angioma | **Low** | ## 1.5 The Classical ML Pipeline (Five Mandatory Stages) 1. **Image Acquisition** — Dermoscopic / clinical image capture. 2. **Preprocessing** — Hair removal, illumination correction, color normalization. 3. **Segmentation** — Isolating the lesion from healthy skin. 4. **Feature Extraction** — Computing hand-crafted descriptors (shape, color, texture). 5. **Classification** — Mapping features to a diagnostic label using a trained model. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Classical ML Matters Even in 2025:** Classical ML models require **far less data** than deep CNNs, are **interpretable** (crucial for medical regulatory approval under FDA / CE Mark), and can run on low-resource edge devices. KTU examiners frequently test this pipeline because it demonstrates *engineering rigor* in feature design. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The ABCD Rule — Quantitative Foundation The **ABCD rule** of dermoscopy is the canonical feature-extraction framework formalized by **Nachbar et al. (1994)**. Each criterion yields a numerical score, and a **Total Dermatoscopic Score (TDS)** is computed: $$ \text{TDS} = A \times 1.3 + B \times 0.1 + C \times 0.5 + D \times 0.5 $$ ### 2.1.1 Asymmetry Index (A) The lesion is divided by **two perpendicular axes** (major and minor). Asymmetry is the non-overlapping fraction: $$ A = \frac{\Delta A_1 + \Delta A_2}{2 \cdot A_{\text{lesion}}} $$ where $\Delta A_1, \Delta A_2$ are the non-overlapping areas when the lesion is folded along the two principal axes, and $A_{\text{lesion}}$ is the total lesion area. $A \in [0, 1]$. ### 2.1.2 Border Score (B) The lesion is divided into **8 octants**. Each octant is examined for an abrupt cutoff of pigment: $$ B = \sum_{i=1}^{8} b_i, \quad b_i \in \{0, 1\} $$ So $B \in [0, 8]$. Each octant contributes 1 if the border transition is sharp. ### 2.1.3 Color Score (C) Six clinically relevant colors are checked: **white, red, light-brown, dark-brown, blue-gray, black**. Each present color adds 1: $$ C = \sum_{j=1}^{6} c_j, \quad c_j \in \{0, 1\} $$ So $C \in [0, 6]$. ### 2.1.4 Diameter (D) A simple geometric measurement: $$ D = \text{maximum Euclidean diameter of the lesion (in mm)} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Clinical Threshold:** A TDS **< 4.75** is considered **benign**; **4.75 – 5.45** is **suspicious**; **> 5.45** is highly suggestive of **malignancy**. ## 2.2 Color Feature Descriptors Once a lesion is segmented, the following color features are extracted in **RGB** and **HSV** (or CIE-Lab) color spaces: - **Mean Color Vector:** $$ \mu_c = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} I_c(x_i, y_i), \quad c \in \{R, G, B, H, S, V\} $$ - **Color Variance (per channel):** $$ \sigma_c^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( I_c(x_i, y_i) - \mu_c \right)^2 $$ - **Color Skewness (3rd moment):** $$ \gamma_c = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( \frac{I_c(x_i, y_i) - \mu_c}{\sigma_c} \right)^3 $$ ## 2.3 Texture Feature Descriptors ### 2.3.1 Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) The GLCM $P_{d,\theta}(i,j)$ counts how often pixel-pairs with intensities $i$ and $j$ co-occur at offset $d$ and angle $\theta$. From it, **Haralick features** are derived: $$ \text{Contrast} = \sum_{i,j} (i-j)^2 \, P_{d,\theta}(i,j) $$ $$ \text{Homogeneity} = \sum_{i,j} \frac{P_{d,\theta}(i,j)}{1 + (i-j)^2} $$ $$ \text{Energy} = \sum_{i,j} P_{d,\theta}(i,j)^2 $$ $$ \text{Entropy} = -\sum_{i,j} P_{d,\theta}(i,j) \log P_{d,\theta}(i,j) $$ $$ \text{Correlation} = \frac{\sum_{i,j} (i - \mu_i)(j - \mu_j) P_{d,\theta}(i,j)}{\sigma_i \sigma_j} $$ ### 2.3.2 Local Binary Pattern (LBP) For a pixel with intensity $I_c$ and $P$ neighbors on a circle of radius $R$: $$ \text{LBP}_{P,R} = \sum_{p=0}^{P-1} s(I_p - I_c) \cdot 2^p, \quad s(x) = \begin{cases} 1, & x \geq 0 \\ 0, & x < 0 \end{cases} $$ The **LBP histogram** (256 bins for $P=8$) is a powerful rotation-invariant texture descriptor. ## 2.4 Shape Feature Descriptors | Feature | Formula | Meaning | |---|---|---| | **Area** | $A = \sum_{(x,y) \in S} 1$ | Number of lesion pixels | | **Perimeter** | $P = \sum \sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2}$ | Boundary length | | **Circularity** | $C = \frac{4\pi A}{P^2}$ | 1.0 = perfect circle | | **Compactness** | $\kappa = \frac{P^2}{A}$ | Inverse of circularity | | **Eccentricity** | $e = \sqrt{1 - \frac{b^2}{a^2}}$ | $a$ = major axis, $b$ = minor axis | | **Solidity** | $S = \frac{A}{A_{\text{convex hull}}}$ | Convexity measure | ## 2.5 Classical Classifiers (KTU High-Yield) ### 2.5.1 Support Vector Machine (SVM) Finds the **maximum-margin hyperplane** separating classes. For non-linearly separable data, the **kernel trick** maps features into a higher-dimensional space: $$ K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^T \phi(\mathbf{x}_j) $$ Common kernels: **Linear**, **Polynomial**, **Radial Basis Function (RBF)**: $$ K_{\text{RBF}}(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp\left( -\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \Vert^2 \right), \quad \gamma > 0 $$ Decision function: $$ f(\mathbf{x}) = \text{sign}\left( \sum_{i=1}^{N_s} \alpha_i y_i K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) + b \right) $$ where $N_s$ is the number of **support vectors** (the data points lying on the margin). ### 2.5.2 Random Forest (RF) An **ensemble** of $T$ decision trees trained on **bootstrap samples** with **random feature subsets** at each split. Final prediction is by **majority vote** (classification) or **average** (regression): $$ \hat{y} = \text{mode}\left( \{ h_t(\mathbf{x}) \}_{t=1}^{T} \right) $$ ### 2.5.3 K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) Assigns label based on the **majority vote** of the $K$ closest training samples (using Euclidean, Manhattan, or Mahalanobis distance): $$ \hat{y} = \arg\max_{c} \sum_{i \in N_K(\mathbf{x})} \mathbb{1}(y_i = c) $$ ### 2.5.4 Artificial Neural Network (ANN) A layered network of neurons with weights $W$, biases $b$, and activation $\sigma$: $$ a^{(l)} = \sigma\left( W^{(l)} a^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)} \right), \quad \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}} \quad \text{(sigmoid)} $$ Trained via **backpropagation** minimizing cross-entropy loss: $$ \mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{c=1}^{K} y_{ic} \log \hat{y}_{ic} $$ ## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL KTU FORMULA TABLE — Memorize These!** | Concept | Formula / Definition | Typical Value / Range | |---|---|---| | **ABCD Total Score** | $\text{TDS} = 1.3A + 0.1B + 0.5C + 0.5D$ | Benign < 4.75; Malignant > 5.45 | | **Asymmetry** | $A = (\Delta A_1 + \Delta A_2) / 2A_{\text{lesion}}$ | $0 \leq A \leq 1$ | | **Border Score** | $B = \sum_{i=1}^{8} b_i$ | $0 \leq B \leq 8$ | | **Color Score** | $C = \sum_{j=1}^{6} c_j$ (6 colors) | $0 \leq C \leq 6$ | | **Circularity** | $C = 4\pi A / P^2$ | 1.0 (circle) to 0 (irregular) | | **Eccentricity** | $e = \sqrt{1 - b^2/a^2}$ | 0 (circle) to 1 (line) | | **GLCM Contrast** | $\sum_{i,j}(i-j)^2 P(i,j)$ | Higher → more heterogeneous | | **GLCM Homogeneity** | $\sum_{i,j} P(i,j) / (1+(i-j)^2)$ | Higher → smoother texture | | **GLCM Energy** | $\sum_{i,j} P(i,j)^2$ | Higher → more uniform | | **LBP operator** | $\sum_{p=0}^{P-1} s(I_p - I_c) 2^p$ | $P=8$ typical | | **SVM RBF kernel** | $K(x_i,x_j) = \exp(-\gamma \Vert x_i - x_j \Vert^2)$ | $\gamma > 0$ | | **Cross-Entropy Loss** | $-\frac{1}{N}\sum_i \sum_c y_{ic} \log \hat{y}_{ic}$ | Lower is better | | **Accuracy** | $\frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | $0 \leq \text{Acc} \leq 1$ | | **Sensitivity (Recall)** | $\frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Also called **True Positive Rate** | | **Specificity** | $\frac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | Also called **True Negative Rate** | | **Precision** | $\frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Positive Predictive Value | | **F1-Score** | $\frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of P and R | | **Dice Coefficient** | $\frac{2 \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$ | $0 \leq \text{Dice} \leq 1$ | | **IoU (Jaccard)** | $\frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | $0 \leq \text{IoU} \leq 1$ | ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility Classical ML for skin cancer detection has produced **FDA-cleared** systems (e.g., the **Moleanalyzer Pro**). It enables **triage in teledermatology**—flagging suspicious lesions for expert review in regions with a shortage of dermatologists. The same ABCD-inspired feature pipeline is used in smartphone-based dermatoscopes for **self-screening**. > [!NOTE] > **Industry Connection:** A 2024 survey in *The Lancet Digital Health* found that ML-augmented screening reduces unnecessary biopsies by **~30%** while maintaining > 95% sensitivity. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Computing the ABCD Total Dermatoscopic Score (TDS) **Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A dermoscopic image of a skin lesion is analyzed. The measurements are: - Asymmetry area overlap ratios: $\Delta A_1 = 220$ pixels, $\Delta A_2 = 180$ pixels, total lesion area $A_{\text{lesion}} = 1000$ pixels. - Border octant analysis: 6 out of 8 octants show abrupt pigment cutoff. - Color count: 5 of 6 clinical colors (white, red, light-brown, dark-brown, black) are present; blue-gray is absent. - Maximum diameter: $D = 7.2$ mm. **Compute the Total Dermatoscopic Score (TDS) and classify the lesion.** ### Step 1 — Compute Asymmetry Score (A) $$ A = \frac{\Delta A_1 + \Delta A_2}{2 \cdot A_{\text{lesion}}} = \frac{220 + 180}{2 \times 1000} $$ $$ A = \frac{400}{2000} = 0.20 $$ > **Reasoning:** Asymmetry ranges from 0 (perfectly symmetric) to 1 (maximally asymmetric). Our value of 0.20 indicates mild asymmetry. ### Step 2 — Determine Border Score (B) Six octants out of 8 show abrupt cutoff: $$ B = \sum_{i=1}^{8} b_i = 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 0 + 0 = 6 $$ > **Reasoning:** Only the octants with sharp pigment transitions contribute. A score of 6/8 indicates a significantly irregular border. ### Step 3 — Determine Color Score (C) Five of six clinical colors are detected: $$ C = \sum_{j=1}^{6} c_j = 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 0 = 5 $$ > **Reasoning:** Multiple distinct colors (especially if blue-gray or white are present) raise suspicion of melanoma. Our lesion shows color variegation. ### Step 4 — Determine Diameter (D) $$ D = 7.2 \text{ mm} $$ > **Reasoning:** Diameter > 6 mm is a clinical warning sign. ### Step 5 — Compute TDS $$ \text{TDS} = 1.3 \cdot A + 0.1 \cdot B + 0.5 \cdot C + 0.5 \cdot D $$ $$ \text{TDS} = 1.3 \times 0.20 + 0.1 \times 6 + 0.5 \times 5 + 0.5 \times 7.2 $$ $$ \text{TDS} = 0.26 + 0.60 + 2.50 + 3.60 $$ $$ \boxed{\text{TDS} = 6.96} $$ ### Step 6 — Classification Since **TDS = 6.96 > 5.45**, the lesion is **highly suspicious for malignancy** and warrants biopsy. > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Note:** KTU problems often provide only 3 of the 4 components and ask you to compute the 4th. Always show all intermediate computations. ## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Confusion Matrix and Performance Metrics **Problem Statement:** A binary classifier for melanoma detection is tested on 200 dermoscopic images. The results yield: - True Positives (TP) = 80 (correctly identified melanomas) - False Positives (FP) = 10 (benign flagged as melanoma) - False Negatives (FN) = 5 (melanoma missed) - True Negatives (TN) = 105 (correctly identified benign) **Compute: Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, Precision, and F1-Score.** ### Step 1 — Verify the Total $$ \text{Total} = TP + FP + FN + TN = 80 + 10 + 5 + 105 = 200 \;\;\checkmark $$ ### Step 2 — Compute Accuracy $$ \text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{80 + 105}{200} = \frac{185}{200} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Accuracy} = 0.925 = 92.5\%} $$ ### Step 3 — Compute Sensitivity (Recall / TPR) $$ \text{Sensitivity} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{80}{80 + 5} = \frac{80}{85} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Sensitivity} = 0.9412 = 94.12\%} $$ ### Step 4 — Compute Specificity (TNR) $$ \text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{105}{105 + 10} = \frac{105}{115} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Specificity} = 0.9130 = 91.30\%} $$ ### Step 5 — Compute Precision (PPV) $$ \text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{80}{80 + 10} = \frac{80}{90} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Precision} = 0.8889 = 88.89\%} $$ ### Step 6 — Compute F1-Score $$ F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \times \text{Sensitivity}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Sensitivity}} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8889 \times 0.9412}{0.8889 + 0.9412} $$ $$ F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8365}{1.8301} = 2 \cdot 0.4571 $$ $$ \boxed{F_1 = 0.9142} $$ ### Step 7 — Interpretation > **Reasoning:** With **94% sensitivity**, the model misses only ~6% of melanomas — clinically acceptable (false negatives are far more dangerous than false positives in oncology). The **88.9% precision** means ~11% of flagged lesions will be false alarms, manageable via follow-up screening. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Classifier Selection for Skin Cancer | Classifier | Pros | Cons | Best Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | **SVM (RBF)** | Excellent on small datasets; high accuracy with ABCD features | Slow on large data; kernel choice is empirical | Mid-size datasets (≤ 10k images) | | **Random Forest** | Handles mixed features; built-in feature importance; robust | Less accurate on high-dim data | Multi-class (7-class ISIC challenge) | | **KNN** | Simple, no training phase | Slow inference; curse of dimensionality | Baseline benchmarking only | | **ANN (MLP)** | Learns non-linear combinations | Needs careful regularization; black-box | When feature engineering is partial | | **Logistic Regression** | Highly interpretable; outputs probabilities | Limited to linear boundaries | Clinical acceptance / FDA submission | ## 3.4 GLCM Computation Walk-through For a $4 \times 4$ lesion patch with quantized intensities $\{0, 1, 2, 3\}$, the GLCM $P_{d=1,\theta=0°}$ (horizontal neighbors) is built by counting co-occurrences. Suppose the normalized GLCM is: $$ P = \begin{bmatrix} 0.10 & 0.05 & 0.02 & 0.00 \\ 0.05 & 0.20 & 0.06 & 0.01 \\ 0.02 & 0.06 & 0.25 & 0.04 \\ 0.00 & 0.01 & 0.04 & 0.09 \end{bmatrix} $$ ### Contrast $$ \text{Contrast} = \sum_{i,j} (i-j)^2 P(i,j) = (0)^2(0.10) + (1)^2(0.05) + (2)^2(0.02) + (1)^2(0.05) + (0)^2(0.20) + \dots $$ Computing term by term: - Diagonal entries $(i=j)$ contribute 0. - Off-diagonal $(i,j) = (0,1), (1,0), (0,2), (2,0), \ldots$ contribute $(i-j)^2 \cdot P(i,j)$. $$ \text{Contrast} = 1 \cdot (0.05+0.05) + 4 \cdot (0.02+0.02) + 1 \cdot (0.06+0.06) + 4 \cdot (0.01+0.01) + 1 \cdot (0.04+0.04) + 9 \cdot 0 + \dots $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Contrast} \approx 0.62} $$ ### Homogeneity $$ \text{Homogeneity} = \sum_{i,j} \frac{P(i,j)}{1 + (i-j)^2} $$ $$ = \frac{0.10 + 0.05 + 0.02 + 0.05 + 0.20 + 0.06 + 0.02 + 0.06 + 0.25 + 0.04 + 0.01 + 0.04 + 0.09}{1 + (i-j)^2 \text{ per cell}} $$ The diagonal cells (where $i=j$) get weight 1, adjacent cells (e.g., $(0,1)$) get weight 1/2, cells two apart get weight 1/5, and so on. After summation: $$ \boxed{\text{Homogeneity} \approx 0.79} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > A **higher contrast** and **lower homogeneity** suggest a more chaotic, irregular texture—often characteristic of malignant lesions. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End Classical ML Pipeline for Skin Cancer Detection ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input Dermoscopic Image] --> B[Preprocessing] B --> B1[Hair Removal
Dull Razor Algorithm] B --> B2[Color Normalization
Gray-World Assumption] B --> B3[Illumination Correction
Histogram Equalization] B1 --> C[Lesion Segmentation] B2 --> C B3 --> C C --> C1[Otsu Thresholding] C --> C2[Region Growing] C --> C3[Active Contour
Snake Model] C1 --> D[Feature Extraction] C2 --> D C3 --> D D --> D1[ABCD Geometric
Features] D --> D2[Color Features
RGB / HSV Stats] D --> D3[Texture Features
GLCM and LBP] D1 --> E[Feature Vector] D2 --> E D3 --> E E --> F[Classifier Engine] F --> F1[Support Vector Machine] F --> F2[Random Forest] F --> F3[KNN] F --> F4[ANN / MLP] F1 --> G[Diagnosis] F2 --> G F3 --> G F4 --> G G --> G1[Benign Lesion] G --> G2[Malignant Melanoma] G --> G3[Suspicious
Follow-up Required] ``` ## 4.2 ABCD Rule Computation Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Segmented Lesion ROI] --> B[Compute Centroid
and Principal Axes] B --> C[Fold Along Axis 1] B --> D[Fold Along Axis 2] C --> E[Asymmetry Index A] D --> E A --> F[Divide into 8 Octants] F --> G[Detect Pigment Edges
per Octant] G --> H[Border Score B] A --> I[Color Histogram
in 6 Channels] I --> J[Color Score C] A --> K[Longest Euclidean
Distance] K --> L[Diameter D] E --> M[TDS Aggregation] H --> M J --> M L --> M M --> N{Threshold Check} N -->|TDS less than 4.75| O[Benign] N -->|TDS 4.75 to 5.45| P[Suspicious] N -->|TDS greater than 5.45| Q[Malignant] ``` ## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram: Classical ML vs Deep CNN ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CL[Classical ML Pipeline] A1[Raw Image] --> A2[Preprocessing] A2 --> A3[Manual Feature
Engineering] A3 --> A4[ABCD + GLCM + LBP] A4 --> A5[SVM / RF / KNN / ANN] A5 --> A6[Class Label] end subgraph DL[Deep CNN Pipeline] B1[Raw Image] --> B2[Convolutional
Feature Maps] B2 --> B3[Hierarchical
Feature Learning] B3 --> B4[Pooling and FC Layers] B4 --> B5[Softmax Classifier] B5 --> B6[Class Label] end CL -.Comparison.- DL ``` ## 4.4 Multi-Class Confusion Matrix Schematic (7-Class ISIC) ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Predicted Class] --> M1[MEL Melanoma] P --> M2[NV Melanocytic Nevi] P --> M3[BCC Basal Cell Carcinoma] P --> M4[AKIEC Actinic Keratosis] P --> M5[BKL Benign Keratosis] P --> M6[DF Dermatofibroma] P --> M7[VASC Vascular] A[Actual Class] --> N1[MEL] A --> N2[NV] A --> N3[BCC] A --> N4[AKIEC] A --> N5[BKL] A --> N6[DF] A --> N7[VASC] M1 --> X[7x7 Confusion Matrix] N1 --> X X --> Perf[Per-Class Precision
Recall and F1] ``` ## 4.5 ROC Curve Conceptual Schematic ```mermaid graph LR O[Origin 0,0] --> TPR[True Positive Rate
Sensitivity] TPR --> C[1,1 Ideal Classifier] O --> D[0.5,0.5 Random Guess] O -.Diagonal Reference.-> C D -.Random Baseline.-> C C --> AUC[Area Under Curve AUC] AUC --> V0[Excellent AUC 0.9 to 1.0] AUC --> V1[Good AUC 0.8 to 0.9] AUC --> V2[Fair AUC 0.7 to 0.8] AUC --> V3[Poor AUC 0.5 to 0.7] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Mermaid Diagrams:** Each block represents a processing stage. The "Reasoning" for the pipeline order is: **(1)** image quality must be normalized before analysis; **(2)** only the lesion ROI is diagnostically relevant; **(3)** features are the "language" classifiers understand; **(4)** classifiers output the final decision. --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Q: Define the ABCD rule of dermoscopy. List the four parameters and state the Total Dermatoscopic Score (TDS) threshold for melanoma suspicion.** **Model Answer:** The ABCD rule of dermoscopy is a quantitative scoring system used to standardize the clinical evaluation of pigmented skin lesions, introduced by Nachbar et al. (1994). The four parameters are: - **A — Asymmetry:** Measures the geometric asymmetry of the lesion along its two principal axes. Computed as the non-overlapping area ratio (range 0 to 1). - **B — Border:** Evaluates the sharpness of the lesion's edge. The lesion is divided into 8 octants; each octant with an abrupt pigment cutoff contributes 1 (range 0 to 8). - **C — Color:** Counts the presence of 6 clinically significant colors — white, red, light-brown, dark-brown, blue-gray, and black (range 0 to 6). - **D — Diameter:** The maximum Euclidean diameter of the lesion, measured in millimeters. The Total Dermatoscopic Score (TDS) is computed as: $$ \text{TDS} = 1.3A + 0.1B + 0.5C + 0.5D $$ A TDS **< 4.75** indicates a benign lesion, **4.75 – 5.45** is suspicious, and a TDS **> 5.45** is highly suggestive of melanoma. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q: Differentiate between Sensitivity, Specificity, and Accuracy in the context of a melanoma classifier. Which metric is most clinically critical and why?** **Model Answer:** Given a binary classifier with TP, FP, FN, TN: - **Sensitivity (Recall / True Positive Rate):** $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ — the proportion of actual melanomas correctly detected. - **Specificity (True Negative Rate):** $\frac{TN}{TN+FP}$ — the proportion of benign lesions correctly identified. - **Accuracy:** $\frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ — the overall proportion of correct classifications. **Sensitivity is the most clinically critical** metric in melanoma detection. The reason is asymmetric error cost: a **False Negative** (missed melanoma) can lead to cancer metastasis and death, while a False Positive (unnecessary biopsy of a benign lesion) has a far lower clinical cost. Therefore, melanoma classifiers are optimized for high sensitivity (often ≥ 95%), even at the expense of specificity. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Q: (a)** Describe in detail the classical Machine Learning pipeline for AI-based skin cancer detection. Explain each stage with appropriate algorithms. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Given a dermoscopic image with the following measurements: - $\Delta A_1 = 300$, $\Delta A_2 = 200$, $A_{\text{lesion}} = 1000$ - Border: 5 of 8 octants are abrupt - Color: 4 of 6 colors are present - Diameter: 6.5 mm Compute the ABCD scores, the TDS, and classify the lesion. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks The classical ML pipeline for skin cancer detection consists of **five mandatory stages**: **Stage 1 — Image Acquisition (1 mark):** Dermoscopic images are captured using a dermatoscope (magnification 10x–30x), often with standardized lighting. Public datasets such as **ISIC (International Skin Imaging Collaboration)**, **PH2**, and **HAM10000** are used for benchmarking. **Stage 2 — Preprocessing (2 marks):** - **Hair Removal:** The Dull Razor algorithm morphologically closes dark hair structures and replaces them with surrounding skin texture. - **Illumination Correction:** Gray-world color constancy assumes the average scene color is gray; per-channel scaling normalizes lighting. - **Color Space Conversion:** RGB is converted to HSV or CIE-Lab to decouple luminance from chrominance for stable color features. **Stage 3 — Segmentation (1 mark):** The lesion is isolated from healthy skin. Common techniques: - **Otsu's Thresholding** — automatic global threshold based on intra-class variance minimization. - **Region Growing** — seeds expand by intensity similarity. - **Active Contour (Snake)** — a deformable curve that locks onto lesion boundaries. **Stage 4 — Feature Extraction (2 marks):** - **Geometric (ABCD):** Asymmetry, Border, Color, Diameter scores. - **Texture:** GLCM (Haralick features: contrast, homogeneity, energy, entropy) and LBP histograms. - **Color Statistics:** Mean, variance, and skewness in RGB/HSV channels. **Stage 5 — Classification (1 mark):** The feature vector is passed to a trained classifier: - **SVM with RBF kernel** — best for small-to-medium datasets. - **Random Forest** — robust ensemble, provides feature importance. - **KNN** — simple distance-based baseline. - **ANN/MLP** — learns non-linear feature combinations. **Model Evaluation:** Performance is reported using **Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, Precision, F1-score**, and the **AUC-ROC curve**. --- #### Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Asymmetry Score (A) (2 marks):** $$ A = \frac{\Delta A_1 + \Delta A_2}{2 \cdot A_{\text{lesion}}} = \frac{300 + 200}{2 \times 1000} = \frac{500}{2000} = 0.25 $$ **Step 2 — Border Score (B) (1 mark):** $$ B = 5 \quad \text{(out of 8 octants)} $$ **Step 3 — Color Score (C) (1 mark):** $$ C = 4 \quad \text{(out of 6 clinical colors)} $$ **Step 4 — Diameter (D) (1 mark):** $$ D = 6.5 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 5 — Total Dermatoscopic Score (2 marks):** $$ \text{TDS} = 1.3 \times 0.25 + 0.1 \times 5 + 0.5 \times 4 + 0.5 \times 6.5 $$ $$ \text{TDS} = 0.325 + 0.50 + 2.00 + 3.25 $$ $$ \boxed{\text{TDS} = 6.075} $$ **Step 6 — Classification:** Since **6.075 > 5.45**, the lesion is classified as **highly suspicious for malignancy (Melanoma)** and biopsy is strongly recommended. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` — ALTERNATIVE **Q: (a)** Explain the Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier in detail. With a suitable diagram, describe the role of kernels (Linear, Polynomial, RBF) in handling non-linearly separable data. Discuss why SVM is preferred for small medical image datasets. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A melanoma classifier is tested on 250 dermoscopic images. The confusion matrix obtained is: | | Predicted Malignant | Predicted Benign | |---|---|---| | **Actual Malignant** | 90 (TP) | 8 (FN) | | **Actual Benign** | 12 (FP) | 140 (TN) | Calculate: Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, Precision, and F1-Score. Comment on the clinical suitability of this classifier. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks **SVM Fundamentals (3 marks):** Support Vector Machine is a **supervised binary classifier** that finds the **optimal hyperplane** maximizing the **margin** between two classes. The hyperplane is defined as: $$ \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0 $$ The decision function is: $$ f(\mathbf{x}) = \text{sign}(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b) $$ Training minimizes: $$ \min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i $$ subject to $y_i(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$, where $\xi_i \geq 0$ are **slack variables** allowing soft margin violations, and $C$ is the regularization parameter. **Kernels (2 marks):** When data is not linearly separable in the original feature space, the **kernel trick** implicitly maps inputs to a higher-dimensional space: - **Linear Kernel:** $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j$ - **Polynomial Kernel:** $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = (\gamma \mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j + r)^d$ - **RBF (Gaussian) Kernel:** $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp(-\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \Vert^2)$ The RBF kernel can project data into an **infinite-dimensional space**, enabling separation of complex, non-linear patterns (typical in ABCD feature space). **Why SVM for Medical Imaging (2 marks):** - **Effective on small datasets** (hundreds–thousands of images) where deep CNNs overfit. - **Strong theoretical guarantees** (max-margin principle → good generalization). - **Robust to high-dimensional feature vectors** (e.g., concatenated ABCD + GLCM + LBP). - **Interpretable support vectors** can be visualized for clinical trust. --- #### Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Verify Totals (1 mark):** $$ TP + FN + FP + TN = 90 + 8 + 12 + 140 = 250 \;\;\checkmark $$ **Step 2 — Accuracy (1 mark):** $$ \text{Accuracy} = \frac{90 + 140}{250} = \frac{230}{250} = 0.92 = 92.0\% $$ **Step 3 — Sensitivity (1 mark):** $$ \text{Sensitivity} = \frac{90}{90 + 8} = \frac{90}{98} = 0.9184 = 91.84\% $$ **Step 4 — Specificity (1 mark):** $$ \text{Specificity} = \frac{140}{140 + 12} = \frac{140}{152} = 0.9211 = 92.11\% $$ **Step 5 — Precision (1 mark):** $$ \text{Precision} = \frac{90}{90 + 12} = \frac{90}{102} = 0.8824 = 88.24\% $$ **Step 6 — F1-Score (1 mark):** $$ F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8824 \times 0.9184}{0.8824 + 0.9184} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8103}{1.8008} = 2 \times 0.4500 = 0.9000 $$ **Step 7 — Clinical Commentary (1 mark):** The classifier achieves **92% accuracy** and **91.84% sensitivity**. While sensitivity is good, the FDA-recommended threshold for melanoma screening is **≥ 95% sensitivity** to minimize missed cancers. The current model misses ~8% of melanomas (FN = 8), which is clinically **suboptimal**. Recommendation: increase the decision threshold (accept more false positives) to push sensitivity above 95%. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Read Carefully!** > > 1. **Forgetting the weights in the TDS formula** (1.3, 0.1, 0.5, 0.5) — Examiners allocate **2 marks** just for the correct formula. Students often write $A+B+C+D$ and lose full credit. > 2. **Confusing Sensitivity with Specificity** — In melanoma screening, **Sensitivity is the gold standard**; many students quote accuracy instead. Examiners explicitly check this. > 3. **Forgetting to normalize** the GLCM before computing Haralick features. The GLCM $P$ must satisfy $\sum_{i,j} P(i,j) = 1$. > 4. **Mixing up Precision and Recall** in the F1 formula. The F1 formula is $2PR/(P+R)$, not $(P+R)/2$. > 5. **Omitting the hair-removal preprocessing step** — KTU examiners often dock 1 mark for a pipeline that goes "image → features → classifier" without preprocessing. > 6. **Citing fabricated references** — NEVER invent author names. If a reference is required, use the standard "Nachbar et al. (1994) for ABCD rule" or "ISIC Challenge dataset." --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist** ### Core Concepts - ✅ Skin cancer types: **MEL, NV, BCC, AKIEC, BKL, DF, VASC** (7-class ISIC taxonomy). - ✅ Melanoma is rare (~1% of skin cancers) but causes the majority of deaths — early detection is critical. - ✅ Classical ML pipeline: **Acquisition → Preprocessing → Segmentation → Feature Extraction → Classification**. ### ABCD Rule Essentials - ✅ **A**symmetry: $A = (\Delta A_1 + \Delta A_2)/(2A_{\text{lesion}})$, range [0, 1]. - ✅ **B**order: 8 octants, range [0, 8], each octant with abrupt cutoff scores 1. - ✅ **C**olor: 6 colors, range [0, 6]. - ✅ **D**iameter: maximum Euclidean distance (mm), threshold 6 mm. - ✅ **TDS = 1.3A + 0.1B + 0.5C + 0.5D**; threshold **4.75 / 5.45**. - ✅ TDS < 4.75 → benign; 4.75–5.45 → suspicious; > 5.45 → malignant. ### Feature Descriptors - ✅ **Geometric:** Area, Perimeter, Circularity ($4\pi A/P^2$), Eccentricity, Solidity. - ✅ **Color:** Mean, Variance, Skewness in RGB/HSV. - ✅ **Texture:** GLCM Haralick (contrast, homogeneity, energy, entropy, correlation); LBP histogram. ### Classifiers - ✅ **SVM with RBF kernel** is the gold standard for classical skin cancer detection. - ✅ **Random Forest** provides feature importance; **KNN** is a baseline; **ANN/MLP** handles non-linearity. - ✅ Kernel trick: $K(x_i, x_j) = \phi(x_i)^T \phi(x_j)$; RBF = $\exp(-\gamma \Vert x_i - x_j \Vert^2)$. ### Performance Metrics - ✅ Accuracy = $(TP+TN)/\text{Total}$; Sensitivity = $TP/(TP+FN)$; Specificity = $TN/(TN+FP)$. - ✅ Precision = $TP/(TP+FP)$; F1 = $2PR/(P+R)$. - ✅ **Sensitivity is the clinically critical metric** in melanoma screening (target ≥ 95%). ### Datasets - ✅ **ISIC Archive** — largest public dermoscopic dataset (50,000+ images). - ✅ **HAM10000** — 10,015 images, 7 classes. - ✅ **PH2** — 200 images, manual annotation. ### Preprocessing - ✅ **Dull Razor** for hair removal. - ✅ **Gray-World** color constancy for illumination normalization. - ✅ Convert to **HSV or CIE-Lab** for stable color features. ### Segmentation - ✅ **Otsu's thresholding** — automatic global threshold. - ✅ **Active Contour (Snake)** — boundary deformation model. - ✅ **Region Growing** — seed-based expansion. > [!NOTE] > **Final Tip for KTU 2024:** When asked to "design a system," always include the **complete pipeline with all 5 stages**, justify classifier choice with **at least one dataset citation** (ISIC), and **end with a metrics-based evaluation** (sensitivity + specificity + F1). This structure scores full marks on 14-mark design questions. ---

topics.title: Brain Stroke Detection from Computed Tomography Images Using AI
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT862 - AI FOR MEDICAL IMAGE ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Brain Stroke Detection from Computed Tomography Images Using AI

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Brain Stroke Detection from Computed Tomography Images Using AI

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Brain stroke detection from Computed Tomography (CT) images using Artificial Intelligence** is the computational process of automatically identifying, localizing, and classifying regions of abnormal cerebral tissue (ischemic core, penumbra, or hemorrhage) in non-contrast or contrast-enhanced CT scans through the application of deep learning models — primarily Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and their encoder–decoder variants such as U-Net, V-Net, and 3D Residual Networks — trained on annotated medical image datasets to provide rapid, reproducible, and quantitative clinical decision support.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 4 Focus**
> A stroke is a *time-critical neurological emergency*. In the *"golden hour"* (≤ 4.5 hours from onset), every minute of delayed treatment causes the loss of approximately **1.9 million neurons**. AI-based CT analysis is engineered to reduce the door-to-needle time and assist radiologists in making faster, more accurate triage decisions.

### 1.2 Types of Stroke (Clinical Foundation)

| Stroke Type | Pathology | Appearance on Non-Contrast CT (NCCT) | Prevalence |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ischemic Stroke** | Blockage of a cerebral artery → loss of blood supply | Hypodense (dark) region; loss of grey-white matter differentiation | **~87 %** of all strokes |
| **Hemorrhagic Stroke** | Rupture of a blood vessel → bleeding in the brain | Hyperdense (bright) region; Hounsfield Units **+50 to +80 HU** | **~13 %** of all strokes |
| **Subarachnoid Hemorrhage (SAH)** | Bleeding into subarachnoid space | Hyperdense CSF, star-pattern around sulci | Subset of hemorrhagic |
| **Transient Ischemic Attack (TIA)** | Temporary occlusion (resolves in < 24 h) | Often normal on CT | Warning sign |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The X-Ray Detective and the Shadow Puzzle"**
> Imagine a detective (the radiologist) looking at a black-and-white photograph of a city's power grid (the brain CT slice). A power outage (stroke) shows up as a slightly darker patch, almost invisible to the untrained eye. The AI system is like giving the detective a **super-powered magnifying glass** that has been shown **millions of similar photographs** (training data) and has learned the exact *shade of grey* and *texture pattern* that indicates an outage. The AI scans every pixel in milliseconds, highlights the suspicious region, and says: *"I am 96 % confident there is an ischemic lesion here in the left MCA territory."* The detective verifies, and treatment begins within minutes instead of hours.

### 1.4 Hounsfield Unit (HU) — The Quantitative Backbone of CT
