The **Hounsfield Unit (HU)** is the standardized linear attenuation coefficient scale used in CT imaging. It is the single most important physical quantity that any AI stroke model must understand — either through explicit feature engineering or implicitly through learned convolutional filters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard HU Reference Values (must be memorized for KTU exams)**
> - **Air = −1000 HU**
> - **Fat = −100 HU**
> - **Water = 0 HU**
> - **Cerebrospinal Fluid (CSF) = 0 to +15 HU**
> - **White Matter = +20 to +30 HU**
> - **Grey Matter = +35 to +45 HU**
> - **Acute Blood (Hemorrhage) = +50 to +80 HU**
> - **Calcification = > +150 HU**
> - **Bone = +700 to +3000 HU**

### 1.5 Why AI for Stroke Detection? — Clinical Motivation

- **Speed**: AI inference on a single CT slice takes **< 200 ms** versus a radiologist's 5–15 min read time.
- **Reproducibility**: No inter-observer variability.
- **Availability**: 24/7 triage in primary stroke centres without on-site neurologists.
- **Quantification**: Automatic lesion volume (in mL) calculation — critical for thrombolysis eligibility (typically < 70 mL of ischemic core).
- **ASPECTS Scoring**: Automated **Alberta Stroke Program Early CT Score (ASPECTS)** estimation, a 10-point topographic score used to measure early ischemic changes in the MCA territory.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Pipeline — The 7-Stage Workflow

The complete AI pipeline for stroke detection from CT images follows a rigid sequence. Each stage is critical, and a defect in any one stage propagates to the final prediction.

### Stage 1 — Image Acquisition
- Non-Contrast CT (NCCT) is the **first-line** imaging modality for stroke (faster, cheaper, no contrast risk).
- Slice thickness: typically **0.5 mm to 5 mm** (commonly 5 mm in emergency settings).
- Multi-detector CT (MDCT) scanners produce volumetric data: $\sim$ 30–50 axial slices per brain.

### Stage 2 — Preprocessing
- **Hounsfield Unit Windowing**: Clip to a brain window of *[W:80, L:40]* or stroke window *[W:30, L:30]*.
- **Resampling**: Isotropic voxel resampling to (e.g.) 1 mm × 1 mm × 1 mm.
- **Skull Stripping**: Removal of non-brain tissues (skull, scalp) using algorithms such as BET (Brain Extraction Tool) or HD-BET.
- **Intensity Normalization**: Z-score normalization: $\hat{x} = (x - \mu)/\sigma$ per image.
- **Bias Field Correction**: N4ITK algorithm to remove low-frequency intensity inhomogeneity.

### Stage 3 — Data Annotation
- Ground-truth lesion masks are drawn by expert neuroradiologists.
- Multi-rater agreement (Dice ≥ 0.85) is preferred.

### Stage 4 — Data Augmentation (Mitigating Small Dataset Problem)
- Geometric: rotation (≤ 15°), flipping, affine transformation, elastic deformation.
- Intensity: gamma correction, Gaussian noise, brightness/contrast jitter.
- Advanced: MixUp, CutMix, GAN-based synthetic lesion generation.

### Stage 5 — Model Architecture
- Encoder–decoder architectures dominate: **U-Net**, **V-Net**, **nnU-Net**.
- 3D context is preferred for volumetric stroke segmentation.

### Stage 6 — Training
- Loss: Dice Loss / Focal Loss / Tversky Loss / Combined CE-Dice.
- Optimizer: Adam or AdamW, initial learning rate $10^{-4}$.
- Epochs: 100–500 with early stopping.

### Stage 7 — Post-processing & Clinical Reporting
- Connected component analysis to remove false positives (small volumes < 0.1 mL).
- Lesion volume computation in mL.
- ASPECTS scoring overlay on a standardized brain atlas.
- Confidence heatmap (saliency/Grad-CAM).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are KTU 2024 high-yield. Memorize the table — questions are asked directly from this section.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hounsfield Unit | $HU = 1000 \cdot \dfrac{\mu - \mu_{water}}{\mu_{water}}$ | $\mu$ = linear attenuation coefficient of tissue | Air = −1000, Water = 0 |
| 2 | Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC) | $DSC = \dfrac{2 \cdot \vert P \cap G \vert}{\vert P \vert + \vert G \vert}$ | $P$ = predicted mask, $G$ = ground truth | Range 0–1; 1 = perfect overlap |
| 3 | IoU (Jaccard Index) | $IoU = \dfrac{\vert P \cap G \vert}{\vert P \cup G \vert}$ | Same as above | Stricter than Dice |
| 4 | Dice Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{Dice} = 1 - DSC$ | Used in segmentation | Gradient-stable form adds $\epsilon$ |
| 5 | Sensitivity (Recall) | $Sn = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $TP$ = True Positive, $FN$ = False Negative | **Most critical in stroke** (miss = death) |
| 6 | Specificity | $Sp = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | $TN$, $FP$ | Penalizes over-segmentation |
| 7 | Precision (PPV) | $Pr = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | — | — |
| 8 | F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{Pr \cdot Sn}{Pr + Sn}$ | Harmonic mean | — |
| 9 | Voxel-to-mL Conversion | $V_{mL} = N_{vox} \cdot (s_x \cdot s_y \cdot s_z)$ | $N_{vox}$ = lesion voxel count, $s$ = voxel size in cm | $s$ in cm because 1 mL = 1 cm³ |
| 10 | Pixel Normalization | $\hat{x} = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | $\mu, \sigma$ from training set | Z-score normalization |
| 11 | Min-Max Windowing | $x' = \dfrac{x - x_{min}}{x_{max} - x_{min}}$ | $x_{min}, x_{max}$ = window bounds | Bounded to [0, 1] |
| 12 | Binary Cross-Entropy | $\mathcal{L}_{BCE} = -[y \log(\hat{y}) + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})]$ | $y$ = label, $\hat{y}$ = prediction | Pixel-wise classification |
| 13 | Focal Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{FL} = -\alpha(1-\hat{y})^\gamma \log(\hat{y})$ | $\alpha$ = class weight, $\gamma$ = focusing param (≥ 2) | Tackles class imbalance (lesion is small) |
| 14 | Tversky Index | $TI = \dfrac{TP}{TP + \alpha FN + \beta FP}$ | $\alpha, \beta$ weights | Balances FN vs FP |
| 15 | ASPECTS | Score = $10 - \sum_{regions} affected$ | 10 regions of MCA territory | ≤ 7 = poor outcome, ≤ 6 = ineligible for tPA |
| 16 | PSNR | $PSNR = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{MAX^2}{MSE}\right)$ | $MAX$ = max intensity, $MSE$ = mean squared error | dB unit; image quality |
| 17 | SSIM | $SSIM = \dfrac{(2\mu_x \mu_y + c_1)(2\sigma_{xy} + c_2)}{(\mu_x^2 + \mu_y^2 + c_1)(\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + c_2)}$ | Luminance, contrast, structure | Range [-1, 1] |
| 18 | Convolution Output Size | $O = \dfrac{I - K + 2P}{S} + 1$ | $I$ = input, $K$ = kernel, $P$ = padding, $S$ = stride | Used in U-Net design |
| 19 | Receptive Field | $RF_l = RF_{l-1} + (K_l - 1) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{l-1} S_i$ | $K_l$ = kernel, $S_i$ = stride | Determines lesion context |
| 20 | Lesion-to-Volume Threshold | tPA eligible if $V_{ischemic} < 70$ mL | From ECASS-III trial | Clinical decision rule |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility**
> - These metrics power FDA-cleared products like **Viz.ai LVO**, **RapidAI**, **Avicenna AI**, and **BrainNow Stroke**.
> - In real deployments, sensitivity ≥ 0.95 is mandatory (regulatory requirement) because a missed stroke is a life-threatening event.

## 2.3 Why Stroke Detection is Hard — The AI Challenge

1. **Class Imbalance**: A stroke lesion may occupy < 1 % of voxels in a CT slice. The model is overwhelmed by the "background" class.
2. **Subtle Intensity Changes**: Early ischemic signs on NCCT cause only a **2–8 HU** drop in attenuation — barely visible to the human eye.
3. **Inter-patient Anatomical Variability**: Ventricle size, atrophy, and skull shape vary widely.
4. **Limited Annotated Data**: Public datasets contain < 1000 patients (e.g., **ATLAS, ISLES 2018**).
5. **Multi-center Domain Shift**: Scanners from Siemens, GE, Philips have different noise characteristics.

## 2.4 Choice of Architecture — Why U-Net Dominates

The **U-Net** (Ronneberger et al., 2015) is the de-facto architecture for medical image segmentation because of:
- **Symmetric encoder–decoder** with skip connections preserving fine spatial detail.
- Works with **few annotated samples** (as few as 30–50 images).
- Outputs a **pixel-wise probability map** — directly interpretable as a lesion heatmap.

> The skip connections in U-Net carry high-resolution features from the encoder directly to the decoder, allowing the network to combine *what* (semantic) with *where* (localization) — essential for detecting small, diffuse stroke lesions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Hounsfield Unit Scale

The Hounsfield Unit scale is a **linear transform** of the X-ray linear attenuation coefficient $\mu$ of a tissue, anchored to the attenuation of distilled water at standard temperature and pressure.

### Step 1 — Define the Linear Attenuation Coefficient
A CT scanner measures $\mu(x, y, z)$, the linear attenuation coefficient at each voxel, which quantifies the fraction of X-ray photons absorbed or scattered per unit path length.

### Step 2 — Establish the Reference
The reference is pure water: $\mu_{water}$.

### Step 3 — Construct the Linear Map
The HU value is defined such that:
- Water maps to $HU = 0$
- Air maps to $HU = -1000$

The linear map satisfying both anchors is:

$$
HU(x, y, z) = 1000 \cdot \frac{\mu(x, y, z) - \mu_{water}}{\mu_{water}}
$$

### Step 4 — Verify Boundary Conditions

Substituting $\mu = \mu_{water}$ (water):

$$
HU = 1000 \cdot \frac{\mu_{water} - \mu_{water}}{\mu_{water}} = 1000 \cdot 0 = 0 \;\; \checkmark
$$

Substituting $\mu \approx 0$ (air, X-rays barely attenuated):

$$
HU = 1000 \cdot \frac{0 - \mu_{water}}{\mu_{water}} = -1000 \;\; \checkmark
$$

### Step 5 — Verify for Acute Blood
A hemorrhage has $\mu_{blood} \approx 1.05 \cdot \mu_{water}$ (about 5 % denser than water).

$$
HU_{blood} = 1000 \cdot \frac{1.05 \mu_{water} - \mu_{water}}{\mu_{water}} = 1000 \cdot 0.05 = +50 \;\; HU
$$

This matches the clinical expectation of **+50 to +80 HU** for acute intraparenchymal hemorrhage.

### Step 6 — Why this matters for AI
The HU value at each voxel is the **raw input feature** to the CNN. Windowing operations (e.g., clipping to [0, 80] HU for hemorrhage detection) are linear transformations of HU that improve the signal-to-noise ratio for the model.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Dice Loss Gradient (Stabilized Form)

The Dice Similarity Coefficient is the most common evaluation metric in stroke segmentation. The Dice Loss is its complement, used for training:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{Dice} = 1 - \frac{2 \sum_{i} p_i g_i}{\sum_{i} p_i + \sum_{i} g_i}
$$

where $p_i \in [0, 1]$ is the predicted probability and $g_i \in \{0, 1\}$ is the ground-truth label at voxel $i$.

### Step 1 — Add Numerical Stability Constant
In practice, we add $\epsilon$ (e.g., $10^{-6}$) to the denominator to prevent division-by-zero when the prediction and ground truth are both empty (rare but possible in healthy slices):

$$
\mathcal{L}_{Dice} = 1 - \frac{2 \sum_{i} p_i g_i + \epsilon}{\sum_{i} p_i + \sum_{i} g_i + \epsilon}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Gradient with Respect to $p_i$
Let $P = \sum_i p_i g_i$ (intersection), $Q = \sum_i p_i$ (predicted sum), $G = \sum_i g_i$ (ground truth sum). The numerator of the DSC is $2P$, denominator is $Q + G$.

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{Dice}}{\partial p_i} = \frac{\partial}{\partial p_i}\left(1 - \frac{2P}{Q + G}\right) = -\frac{2 g_i (Q + G) - 2P \cdot 1}{(Q + G)^2}
$$

$$
= -\frac{2 g_i (Q + G) - 2P}{(Q + G)^2} = -\frac{2 \big(g_i(Q + G) - P\big)}{(Q + G)^2}
$$

### Step 3 — Interpret the Gradient
The gradient is **largest when the prediction $p_i$ disagrees with the ground truth $g_i$** and the lesion is small (small $Q + G$). This is the exact opposite of cross-entropy loss, which gives tiny gradients on confident correct predictions of the dominant background class — and is exactly why Dice Loss is preferred for stroke segmentation despite its instability.

### Step 4 — Combined Loss (Best Practice)
In practice, a **weighted combination** is used:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{total} = \alpha \cdot \mathcal{L}_{BCE} + (1 - \alpha) \cdot \mathcal{L}_{Dice}, \quad \alpha = 0.5
$$

This combines the stable gradient of BCE with the imbalance-handling of Dice.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choice of Loss for Stroke Detection

| Loss Function | Strength | Weakness | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **BCE** | Stable gradient, pixel-wise | Ignores class imbalance | Initial experimentation |
| **Weighted BCE** | Handles imbalance | Hyperparameter sensitive | Quick baselines |
| **Dice Loss** | Directly optimizes the evaluation metric | Gradient instability on small lesions | Stroke segmentation |
| **Focal Loss** | Focuses on hard examples | Two extra hyperparameters ($\alpha, \gamma$) | Tiny lesion detection (< 1 % voxels) |
| **Tversky Loss** | Tunable FN vs FP trade-off | Requires domain knowledge | Clinical sensitivity tuning |
| **Combo CE + Dice** | Combines benefits | Slightly slower convergence | **Industry default** |
| **Lovász-Hinge** | Optimizes IoU directly | Complex implementation | Final fine-tuning |
| **Hausdorff Loss** | Captures boundary errors | Computationally expensive | Boundary-critical structures |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Voxel-to-mL Conversion

**Problem:** A U-Net predicts a connected lesion mask in slice 24 of a brain CT. The mask has 12,500 voxels. The CT volume has voxel spacing of 0.45 mm × 0.45 mm × 5.0 mm. Compute the lesion volume in mL.

### Step 1 — Compute Voxel Volume in cm³
Spacing in cm: $0.045 \times 0.045 \times 0.5$ cm.

$$
V_{voxel} = 0.045 \times 0.045 \times 0.5 = 0.0010125 \;\; \text{cm}^3
$$

### Step 2 — Multiply by Number of Voxels

$$
V_{lesion} = 12{,}500 \times 0.0010125 = 12.65625 \;\; \text{cm}^3
$$

### Step 3 — Convert cm³ to mL
By definition, $1 \; \text{cm}^3 \equiv 1 \; \text{mL}$ (the density of soft tissue is ~1 g/mL).

$$
\boxed{V_{lesion} \approx 12.66 \; \text{mL}}
$$

### Step 4 — Clinical Interpretation
A 12.66 mL ischemic core is **well within** the threshold of < 70 mL for intravenous thrombolysis (tPA) eligibility. The patient is therefore a candidate for emergency reperfusion therapy.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — U-Net Output Size Calculation

**Problem:** Input CT slice is $256 \times 256$ pixels. The encoder has 4 down-sampling blocks, each with $3 \times 3$ convolution, stride 1, padding 1, and a $2 \times 2$ max-pool with stride 2. Compute the spatial size at the bottleneck.

### Step 1 — Initial Size
$$
S_0 = 256
$$

### Step 2 — After Each Max-Pool
After each block: $S_{i+1} = S_i / 2$.

$$
S_1 = 128, \quad S_2 = 64, \quad S_3 = 32, \quad S_4 = 16
$$

### Step 3 — Note on Convolution
The $3 \times 3$ conv with padding 1 and stride 1 **preserves** spatial size, so only max-pooling halves the resolution. The bottleneck feature map is therefore $16 \times 16$.

### Step 4 — Decoder Path
The decoder applies 4 up-sampling blocks (transposed conv or upsample + conv) to restore $16 \rightarrow 32 \rightarrow 64 \rightarrow 128 \rightarrow 256$. Skip connections concatenate the corresponding encoder feature maps at each level, recovering high-resolution details lost in pooling.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End AI Pipeline for Brain Stroke Detection

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the full operational architecture from raw CT acquisition to clinical reporting. Node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes to comply with Mermaid parser safety.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Patient Arrives in ER] --> A2[Non-Contrast CT Scan Acquisition]
    A2 --> A3[DICOM Volume Reconstruction]
    A3 --> A4[Skull Stripping BET Algorithm]
    A4 --> A5[HU Windowing W80 L40]
    A5 --> A6[Z-Score Intensity Normalization]
    A6 --> A7[Resampling 1mm Isotropic]
    A7 --> A8[Data Augmentation Pipeline]
    A8 --> A9[3D U-Net Encoder]
    A9 --> A10[Bottleneck 16x16x16 Features]
    A10 --> A11[3D U-Net Decoder]
    A11 --> A12[Skip Connections from Encoder]
    A12 --> A13[Voxel-wise Probability Map]
    A13 --> A14[Threshold 0.5 Binary Mask]
    A14 --> A15[Connected Component Filter]
    A15 --> A16[Lesion Volume mL Calculation]
    A16 --> A17[ASPECTS Score Computation]
    A17 --> A18[Grad-CAM Saliency Overlay]
    A18 --> A19[DICOM-SR Report to PACS]
    A19 --> A20[Radiologist Verification]
    A20 --> A21[Clinical Decision tPA Yes or No]

    B1[Annotated Training Dataset] --> A8
    B2[Loss Function Dice plus BCE] -.trains.-> A9
    B3[Adam Optimizer] -.updates weights.-> A9
```

## 4.2 U-Net Architecture Detail (Stroke Segmentation)

The diagram below shows the symmetric encoder–decoder structure with skip connections — the workhorse of stroke segmentation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L0[Input 256x256x1 CT Slice] --> L1[Conv Block 64 ch]
    L1 --> L2[MaxPool to 128x128]
    L2 --> L3[Conv Block 128 ch]
    L3 --> L4[MaxPool to 64x64]
    L4 --> L5[Conv Block 256 ch]
    L5 --> L6[MaxPool to 32x32]
    L6 --> L7[Conv Block 512 ch]
    L7 --> L8[MaxPool to 16x16]
    L8 --> L9[Bottleneck Conv 1024 ch]
    L9 --> L10[UpConv to 32x32]
    L10 --> L11[Concat with L7]
    L11 --> L12[Conv Block 512 ch]
    L12 --> L13[UpConv to 64x64]
    L13 --> L14[Concat with L5]
    L14 --> L15[Conv Block 256 ch]
    L15 --> L16[UpConv to 128x128]
    L16 --> L17[Concat with L3]
    L17 --> L18[Conv Block 128 ch]
    L18 --> L19[UpConv to 256x256]
    L19 --> L20[Concat with L1]
    L20 --> L21[Conv Block 64 ch]
    L21 --> L22[1x1 Conv Sigmoid Output]
    L22 --> L23[Lesion Probability Map]
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow for Hemorrhagic vs Ischemic Stroke Triage

This block diagram captures the algorithmic decision tree used in clinical AI triage systems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[CT Image Input] --> D2[AI Segmentation Model]
    D2 --> D3{Lesion Detected?}
    D3 -- No --> D4[Normal No Acute Findings]
    D3 -- Yes --> D5{Mean HU of Lesion}
    D5 -- Greater than 50 --> D6[Hemorrhagic Stroke]
    D5 -- Less than 30 --> D7[Ischemic Stroke]
    D5 -- 30 to 50 --> D8[Indeterminate MRI Follow-up]
    D6 --> D9[Reverse Anticoagulation]
    D6 --> D10[Neurosurgery Consult]
    D7 --> D11[Lesion Volume Calculation]
    D11 --> D12{Volume less than 70 mL}
    D12 -- Yes --> D13[IV tPA Eligible]
    D12 -- No --> D14[Mechanical Thrombectomy Candidate]
    D13 --> D15[Stroke Unit Admission]
    D14 --> D15
    D8 --> D16[Schedule MRI within 24 hours]
```

## 4.4 Multi-Modal AI Integration Schema

Modern stroke AI systems rarely use NCCT alone. This block diagram shows how CT, CT-Angiography, CT-Perfusion, and clinical metadata are fused.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[NCCT Volume] --> M3[Modality 1 CNN Branch]
    M2[CTA Volume] --> M4[Modality 2 CNN Branch]
    M5[CTP Perfusion Maps] --> M6[Modality 3 CNN Branch]
    M7[Clinical Metadata Age BP NIHSS] --> M8[MLP Branch]
    M3 --> M9[Feature Concatenation Layer]
    M4 --> M9
    M6 --> M9
    M8 --> M9
    M9 --> M10[Fusion Classifier]
    M10 --> M11[Output Lesion Type Volume ASPECTS]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP Set A]`

**Define Hounsfield Unit (HU). Mention the HU range for (i) acute blood and (ii) normal white matter.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Hounsfield Unit (HU)** is a standardized quantitative scale for describing radiodensity in CT imaging, defined as:

$$
HU = 1000 \cdot \frac{\mu - \mu_{water}}{\mu_{water}}
$$

where $\mu$ is the linear attenuation coefficient of the tissue and $\mu_{water}$ is that of distilled water (reference). The scale is anchored such that water = 0 HU and air = −1000 HU.

(i) **Acute intraparenchymal blood: +50 to +80 HU** (the increased protein and hemoglobin content of fresh blood makes it denser than brain parenchyma).

(ii) **Normal white matter: +20 to +30 HU** (myelinated lipid-rich tissue is slightly less dense than grey matter).

**Mark Split:** Definition + formula = 2 marks; Two HU values correctly stated = 1 mark. ✔

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP Set B]`

**What is the Dice Similarity Coefficient? Why is it preferred over pixel accuracy for stroke lesion segmentation?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC)** is a statistical overlap metric between the predicted segmentation mask $P$ and the ground truth mask $G$, defined as:

$$
DSC = \frac{2 \cdot \vert P \cap G \vert}{\vert P \vert + \vert G \vert}
$$

DSC ranges from 0 (no overlap) to 1 (perfect overlap).

**Why DSC is preferred over pixel accuracy:**

A typical stroke lesion occupies less than **1 % of the total brain volume**. If a naive model predicted "no stroke" for every voxel, pixel accuracy would be > 99 %, but the model would be clinically useless (zero sensitivity). DSC is **insensitive to the dominant background class** and directly measures the overlap of the small, clinically critical lesion region. Hence, DSC is the standard evaluation metric for stroke (and most medical image) segmentation.

**Mark Split:** Formula + definition = 2 marks; Reason (class imbalance argument) = 1 mark. ✔

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]`

**A) (a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the complete AI pipeline for automatic brain stroke detection from non-contrast CT images. Describe each preprocessing step and its clinical motivation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the U-Net architecture in detail. Explain the role of skip connections and justify why U-Net is the de-facto standard for stroke lesion segmentation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

The complete AI pipeline consists of the following sequential stages:

**1. Image Acquisition (1 mark):** Non-Contrast CT (NCCT) is acquired using a multi-detector CT scanner. Typical parameters: 120 kVp, 200–400 mA, slice thickness 0.5–5 mm, producing a 3D volume of $\sim$30–50 axial slices covering the entire brain in < 10 seconds.

**2. Preprocessing (3 marks):**

| Step | Technique | Clinical/Technical Motivation |
|---|---|---|
| Skulling Stripping | BET / HD-BET algorithm | Removes skull and scalp — they are bright and would create false positives |
| HU Windowing | Clip to [W:80, L:40] or [W:30, L:30] | Enhances the contrast of subtle ischemic regions; reduces dynamic range for CNN |
| Resampling | Linear interpolation to 1 mm isotropic | Standardizes spatial resolution across scanners from different vendors |
| Intensity Normalization | Z-score: $\hat{x} = (x - \mu)/\sigma$ | Stabilizes training; removes inter-scanner intensity drift |
| Bias Field Correction | N4ITK algorithm | Removes low-frequency intensity inhomogeneity caused by coil/sensor variations |

**3. Data Augmentation (1 mark):** Random rotation, flipping, elastic deformation, and gamma correction are applied to expand the effective dataset size by 5–10×, mitigating overfitting on the typically small medical dataset.

**4. Model Training (1 mark):** A 3D U-Net is trained with a combined Dice + BCE loss using the Adam optimizer. The model learns to output a voxel-wise probability map of lesion presence.

**5. Post-processing & Reporting (1 mark):** Thresholding (≥ 0.5) produces a binary mask, connected-component analysis removes tiny spurious regions, and the lesion volume is computed in mL. ASPECTS score is automatically calculated. The final structured (DICOM-SR) report is pushed to the hospital PACS for radiologist sign-off.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistake #1**
> Students often confuse the **brain window (W:80, L:40)** with the **stroke window (W:30, L:30)**. The stroke window has a *narrower* width to amplify subtle hypodensity. Writing "W:400, L:80" (a bone window) will cost 1 mark.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**1. U-Net Architecture (3 marks):**
The U-Net, proposed by Ronneberger et al. (2015), is a symmetric encoder–decoder fully convolutional network. It has three components:

- **Contracting Path (Encoder):** Repeated application of two $3 \times 3$ convolutions (each followed by ReLU) and a $2 \times 2$ max-pool with stride 2. At each downsampling, the number of feature channels doubles (64 → 128 → 256 → 512 → 1024).
- **Bottleneck:** The deepest feature map at minimum spatial resolution captures the most abstract, semantic information.
- **Expanding Path (Decoder):** Each step applies a $2 \times 2$ transposed convolution (up-conv) to halve the channel count and double the spatial resolution, followed by concatenation with the corresponding encoder feature map, and two $3 \times 3$ convolutions.
- **Final Layer:** A $1 \times 1$ convolution maps the 64-channel feature map to the desired number of output classes (1 for binary stroke mask), followed by a sigmoid activation.

**2. Role of Skip Connections (2 marks):**
The encoder loses spatial precision due to repeated max-pooling. Skip connections **concatenate** the high-resolution features from each encoder level directly to the corresponding decoder level. This allows the decoder to combine:
- **Semantic context** (from deep bottleneck features — *what* is the lesion)
- **Spatial precision** (from shallow encoder features — *where* exactly is the lesion)

This is critical for stroke because lesions are **small, irregular, and have blurred boundaries** — exactly the case where naive up-sampling fails.

**3. Why U-Net is the Standard for Stroke (2 marks):**
- **Data efficiency:** Works with as few as 30–50 annotated slices.
- **Pixel-wise output:** Directly produces a lesion probability map — interpretable as a heatmap.
- **End-to-end trainable:** No separate proposal generation or post-processing needed for inference.
- **Proven clinical success:** Powers nearly all state-of-the-art stroke segmentation pipelines, including the winning solutions of the **ISLES 2018 challenge**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistake #2**
> Students often claim skip connections "add" features. They actually **concatenate** them (channel-wise), and the subsequent convolution learns to combine them. Writing "skip connections add the feature maps" is technically wrong and costs 0.5–1 mark. Also, distinguish U-Net from FCN: U-Net has a *symmetric* structure, FCN does not.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]`

**B) (a)** Differentiate between ischemic and hemorrhagic stroke. Explain how their appearance differs on non-contrast CT and how AI models can automatically distinguish between them. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define the Dice Loss and Focal Loss functions. For a stroke lesion occupying 0.5 % of total brain voxels, derive why standard cross-entropy loss is inadequate and explain how Dice Loss overcomes this limitation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

**1. Pathophysiology (2 marks):**
- **Ischemic stroke** is caused by an occlusion of a cerebral artery (commonly by a thrombus or embolus), leading to reduced blood flow, oxygen deprivation, and eventual neuronal death. It accounts for **~87 %** of all strokes.
- **Hemorrhagic stroke** is caused by the rupture of a blood vessel within the brain parenchyma (intracerebral hemorrhage) or in the subarachnoid space, leading to blood extravasation and increased intracranial pressure. It accounts for **~13 %** of all strokes but has a higher mortality rate.

**2. CT Appearance (3 marks):**
- **Ischemic stroke on NCCT:** Appears as a **hypodense (dark)** region due to cytotoxic edema (water influx). The HU drops by **2–8 HU** from normal brain tissue, making early detection challenging. Loss of grey-white matter differentiation, sulcal effacement, and obscuration of the lentiform nucleus are early signs.
- **Hemorrhagic stroke on NCCT:** Appears as a **hyperdense (bright)** region because fresh blood has a high protein/iron content. HU values are **+50 to +80** (compared to white matter at +20 to +30).

**3. AI Distinction (2 marks):**
- A **classification CNN** (e.g., ResNet, DenseNet) is trained on cropped lesion patches. It outputs a probability score: $P(\text{ischemic})$ and $P(\text{hemorrhagic})$.
- The implicit feature learned by the first conv layer is essentially an **intensity filter**: hemorrhage detectors respond to bright (high HU) regions, while ischemia detectors respond to dark (low HU) regions with preserved shape context.
- A **segmentation + threshold** hybrid is also common: segment the lesion first, then compute its mean HU. If mean HU > 50, classify as hemorrhage; if < 30, classify as ischemic; if in between, schedule MRI for confirmation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistake #3**
> Do NOT confuse **hypodense** (dark, ischemic) with **hyperdense** (bright, hemorrhagic). Mixing these up is a 1-mark loss. Also, do not state that ischemic stroke is "always invisible" on CT — early signs (loss of grey-white differentiation) ARE visible to a well-trained AI even within the first 6 hours.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

**1. Definitions (2 marks):**
- **Dice Loss** is the complement of the Dice Similarity Coefficient, defined as:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{Dice} = 1 - \frac{2 \sum_i p_i g_i + \epsilon}{\sum_i p_i + \sum_i g_i + \epsilon}
$$

- **Focal Loss**, introduced by Lin et al. (2017), is a dynamically scaled cross-entropy that down-weights easy examples and focuses training on hard negatives:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{FL} = -\alpha(1 - \hat{y})^\gamma \log(\hat{y})
$$

where $\alpha$ balances class frequencies and $\gamma \geq 2$ is the focusing parameter.

**2. Why BCE Fails on Small Lesions (3 marks):**
The total loss is the *sum* (or mean) of per-voxel losses. For a brain volume with $N = 256 \times 256 \times 30 = 1{,}966{,}080$ voxels, a stroke lesion of 0.5 % = **9,830 voxels** has:
- Background voxels: $N - 9{,}830 = 1{,}956{,}250$
- Lesion voxels: $9{,}830$

If the model correctly classifies all background voxels (an easy task — the background is large and homogeneous), the loss contribution from the background is approximately zero. The model's gradient is therefore **dominated by the 0.5 % lesion voxels** — but if any of those lesion voxels are misclassified, the gradient is also small in absolute terms because the loss is averaged. Empirically, the model converges to a trivial local minimum of "predict all background" and achieves > 99 % pixel accuracy with **0 % sensitivity** to the stroke. The clinical model is useless.

**3. How Dice Loss Solves This (2 marks):**
The Dice Loss is computed **globally over the entire volume**, not per-voxel. The numerator $2 \sum_i p_i g_i$ depends on the sum of products of predictions and ground truths across **all** voxels. The denominator includes all predicted positives $\sum_i p_i$ and all ground-truth positives $\sum_i g_i$. When the lesion is tiny, the denominator is small, making the ratio (and its gradient) **highly sensitive** to errors in the small lesion region. The model cannot "cheat" by predicting all background — doing so yields $DSC = 0$ and $\mathcal{L}_{Dice} = 1$, the maximum loss. Thus, Dice Loss intrinsically balances the gradient contributions and forces the model to learn the rare class.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistake #4**
> Do NOT claim Dice Loss "ignores" the background. It is sensitive to **both** over- and under-segmentation. The ratio is symmetric in $P$ and $G$. Also, in the derivation, the **gradient formula is what matters** — students who write only the loss formula without showing *why* the gradient is imbalanced for BCE lose 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Brain Stroke Detection from CT using AI**
>
> **Clinical Foundation**
> - Ischemic stroke = **87 %** of cases; hypodense on CT; **2–8 HU** drop.
> - Hemorrhagic stroke = **13 %** of cases; hyperdense on CT; **+50 to +80 HU**.
> - tPA eligible if ischemic core **< 70 mL** (ECASS-III rule).
> - Golden hour: **4.5 hours**; loss of **1.9 million neurons/minute**.
> - ASPECTS ≤ 6 → generally ineligible for IV thrombolysis.
>
> **Imaging Physics**
> - HU formula: $HU = 1000 \cdot (\mu - \mu_{water})/\mu_{water}$.
> - Water = 0 HU, Air = −1000 HU.
> - Brain window: W:80, L:40. Stroke window: W:30, L:30.
>
> **Preprocessing Pipeline**
> - Skull strip → HU window → resample → normalize → bias-correct → augment.
>
> **Loss Functions**
> - BCE → unstable on class-imbalanced stroke.
> - Dice Loss → globally balanced; standard for stroke.
> - Focal Loss → down-weights easy background.
> - Best practice: $\mathcal{L} = 0.5 \cdot \mathcal{L}_{BCE} + 0.5 \cdot \mathcal{L}_{Dice}$.
>
> **Architecture**
> - U-Net is the de-facto standard; symmetric encoder–decoder with **skip connections** (concatenation, not addition).
> - 3D > 2D for volumetric context.
> - nnU-Net auto-configures preprocessing, architecture, and training.
>
> **Evaluation Metrics**
> - **DSC** (primary), IoU, Sensitivity (recall), Specificity, F1.
> - Voxel-to-mL: $V_{mL} = N_{vox} \cdot s_x s_y s_z$ (spacing in cm).
> - **Sensitivity must be ≥ 0.95** in clinical deployment.
>
> **Datasets**
> - ISLES (Ischemic Stroke Lesion Segmentation) 2015, 2017, 2018.
> - ATLAS (Anatomical Tracings of Lesions After Stroke) v2.0.
> - CQ500, RSNA Intracranial Hemorrhage Detection.
>
> **Clinical Translation**
> - FDA-cleared systems: Viz.ai LVO, RapidAI, Avicenna AI, BrainNow.
> - "Time is brain" — every AI second saved translates to neurons saved.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**AI-based COVID-19 detection from Computed Tomography (CT) scans** is defined as a deep learning–driven computer-aided diagnosis (CAD) framework that automatically identifies pulmonary manifestations of SARS-CoV-2 infection by analyzing volumetric 3D chest CT data through a pipeline comprising image acquisition, lung parenchyma segmentation, slice-level feature extraction, and patient-level classification into COVID-19, non-COVID pneumonia, or normal categories.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this falls under **Supervised Representation Learning for Volumetric Medical Image Classification**, with the formal objective function being the minimization of a categorical cross-entropy loss over a labeled dataset:

$$ \mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{c=1}^{C} y_{i,c} \log(\hat{p}_{i,c}) $$

where $N$ denotes the number of training samples, $C$ the number of classes, $y_{i,c}$ the ground-truth one-hot label, and $\hat{p}_{i,c}$ the predicted softmax probability for class $c$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is mapped to **CO4 (Module 4)** — *Apply deep learning architectures to domain-specific volumetric and multi-modal medical image analysis problems.* The standard benchmark target is **Sensitivity (Recall) ≥ 90%** and **AUC ≥ 0.95** for clinically viable screening systems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**The Radiologist's Assistant Analogy:**

Imagine a senior radiologist who, over 20 years, has examined **over 50,000** chest CT scans and has developed an almost instinctive ability to spot subtle ground-glass opacities (GGOs) — the visual fingerprints of COVID-19 in the lung tissue. Now imagine training a fresh medical graduate to replicate that intuition in days instead of decades.

That fresh graduate is your **Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)**.

- The **CT scan** is the textbook the graduate reads.
- The **radiologist's labeled annotations** (COVID / non-COVID) are the answer key.
- The **deep network** is the graduate's brain — adjustable via backpropagation.
- The **GPU cluster** is the study lamp that allows the graduate to read 10,000 slices per minute.

When you train the model well, the network learns hierarchical visual features:
- **Low-level:** edges, texture gradients, intensity variations in Hounsfield Units.
- **Mid-level:** lobe boundaries, bronchovascular patterns, pleural surfaces.
- **High-level:** bilateral, peripheral, lower-lobe predominant ground-glass opacities — the *pathognomonic signature* of COVID-19 pneumonia.

## 1.3 Key Imaging Constants & Metrics

| Parameter | Value / Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Hounsfield Unit (HU) for normal lung** | **−700 to −600 HU** | Air-filled alveoli appear dark |
| **HU for ground-glass opacity (GGO)** | **−600 to −100 HU** | Partial alveolar filling |
| **HU for consolidation** | **−100 to +100 HU** | Complete alveolar filling |
| **Standard CT slice thickness** | **0.625 mm – 5 mm** | Thinner = higher diagnostic quality |
| **CT sensitivity (radiologist baseline)** | **60–80%** for early COVID | RT-PCR was the gold standard |
| **AI model target AUC** | **≥ 0.95** | FDA-grade performance benchmark |
| **Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC)** | **≥ 0.85** | Acceptable lung segmentation overlap |

> [!NOTE]
> **Hounsfield Units (HU)** are a dimensionless scale where **water = 0 HU** and **air = −1000 HU**. The HU scale is the *fundamental "language"* of CT imaging — every deep learning model must operate in or convert back to HU space for clinical interpretability.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The COVID-19 CT Analysis Pipeline — A Six-Stage Framework

The end-to-end AI pipeline for COVID-19 detection from CT scans can be decomposed into **six canonical stages**, each of which is independently examinable in KTU assessments.

### Stage 1 — Image Acquisition & DICOM Ingestion
- Raw data is stored in **DICOM (Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine)** format.
- Each volume contains **N_s ≈ 200–600 axial slices** with metadata: pixel spacing, slice thickness, patient orientation, kVp, mAs.
- A typical high-resolution chest CT volume = **512 × 512 × 300 voxels ≈ 75 MB**.

### Stage 2 — Pre-processing & HU Normalization
The raw CT intensities (stored as 12-bit integers) are converted to physical HU values:

$$ HU = 1000 \times \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}} - \mu_{\text{air}}} $$

where $\mu$ is the linear attenuation coefficient. Lung windowing clips the dynamic range to clinically relevant bounds:

$$ I_{\text{clipped}}(x,y) = \text{clip}(I(x,y),\ HU_{\min}, HU_{\max}) = \text{clip}(I(x,y),\ -1000,\ +400) $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept:** Models that operate on raw 16-bit DICOM integers (without HU conversion) generalize poorly across scanners. Always apply **HU normalization + lung windowing** before feeding the volume to a CNN.

### Stage 3 — Lung Parenchyma Segmentation
The 3D U-Net (Ronneberger et al., 2015) is the de facto standard. Its loss function is a weighted combination of **Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE)** and **Dice Loss**:

$$ \mathcal{L}_{\text{seg}} = \alpha \cdot \mathcal{L}_{BCE} + (1-\alpha) \cdot \mathcal{L}_{Dice} $$

$$ \mathcal{L}_{Dice} = 1 - \frac{2 \sum_{i} p_i g_i + \epsilon}{\sum_{i} p_i + \sum_{i} g_i + \epsilon} $$

where $p_i$ is the predicted probability, $g_i$ is the ground-truth binary mask, and $\epsilon = 10^{-6}$ is a smoothing constant to prevent division by zero.

### Stage 4 — Slice / Patch Sampling
Because feeding all 300 slices at once is memory-prohibitive, two sampling strategies dominate:
1. **Slice-wise (2D):** Treat each axial slice as an independent RGB-like 3-channel image → 2D ResNet/EfficientNet.
2. **Volume-wise (2.5D / 3D):** Stack $k$ consecutive slices as channels, or use a 3D CNN (e.g., 3D ResNet-18, I3D).

### Stage 5 — Feature Extraction & Classification
Backbone architectures commonly deployed:
- **ResNet-50/101/152** — residual learning mitigates vanishing gradients.
- **DenseNet-121/201** — feature reuse via dense connectivity.
- **EfficientNet-B0..B7** — compound scaling of depth, width, and resolution.
- **Vision Transformer (ViT)** — global self-attention for long-range lesion patterns.

The classification head typically uses **Global Average Pooling (GAP)** followed by a fully connected layer with softmax activation:

$$ \hat{p}_c = \frac{e^{z_c}}{\sum_{j=1}^{C} e^{z_j}}, \quad c \in \{0, 1, \dots, C-1\} $$

### Stage 6 — Patient-Level Aggregation
Slice-level predictions are aggregated to a patient-level decision via:
- **Max-pooling (most suspicious slice wins)**
- **Mean-pooling (average confidence)**
- **Top-k pooling (average of k most-confident slices)**

> [!TIP]
> **Empirical Finding (KTU Reference):** Max-pooling tends to give the highest sensitivity but lower specificity; mean-pooling gives the most balanced performance for imbalanced COVID datasets.

## 2.2 Why COVID-19 Is a "Hard" AI Problem

| Challenge | Description |
|---|---|
| **Class imbalance** | COVID-19 slices are far fewer than normal slices (ratio ≈ 1:20). |
| **Inter-class similarity** | COVID-19 GGOs resemble other viral pneumonias (influenza, H1N1). |
| **Intra-class variability** | GGOs evolve from focal → multifocal → bilateral over 2–3 weeks. |
| **Domain shift** | Models trained on one scanner/vendor fail on another. |
| **Limited annotations** | Expert radiologist masks are expensive (~30 min per volume). |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Equation | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Hounsfield Conversion** | $HU = 1000 \cdot \dfrac{\mu - \mu_{water}}{\mu_{water} - \mu_{air}}$ | Convert raw intensities to physical scale |
| 2 | **Lung Window Clip** | $I = \text{clip}(HU,\ -1000,\ +400)$ | Standardize dynamic range |
| 3 | **Softmax (Multi-class)** | $\hat{p}_c = \dfrac{e^{z_c}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ | Class probability normalization |
| 4 | **Categorical Cross-Entropy** | $\mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\sum_c y_c \log \hat{p}_c$ | Multi-class classification loss |
| 5 | **Binary Cross-Entropy** | $\mathcal{L}_{BCE} = -(y\log p + (1-y)\log(1-p))$ | Pixel-wise segmentation loss |
| 6 | **Dice Loss** | $\mathcal{L}_{Dice} = 1 - \dfrac{2\sum p_i g_i}{\sum p_i + \sum g_i}$ | Region-overlap segmentation loss |
| 7 | **Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC)** | $DSC = \dfrac{2\vert P \cap G \vert}{\vert P \vert + \vert G \vert}$ | Segmentation accuracy (target ≥ 0.85) |
| 8 | **Sensitivity (Recall)** | $Se = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | True positive rate (clinical priority) |
| 9 | **Specificity** | $Sp = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | True negative rate |
| 10 | **Precision (PPV)** | $PPV = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Positive predictive value |
| 11 | **F1-Score** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{PPV \cdot Se}{PPV + Se}$ | Harmonic mean of precision & recall |
| 12 | **AUC-ROC** | $AUC = \int_0^1 TPR(FPR^{-1}(t))\, dt$ | Threshold-independent ranking quality |
| 13 | **IoU (Jaccard Index)** | $IoU = \dfrac{\vert P \cap G \vert}{\vert P \cup G \vert}$ | Bounding-box / mask overlap |
| 14 | **Focal Loss (Imbalance)** | $\mathcal{L}_{FL} = -\alpha_t (1-p_t)^\gamma \log p_t$ | Down-weights easy negatives |
| 15 | **Residual Block** | $y = \mathcal{F}(x, \{W_i\}) + x$ | Skip connection (ResNet core) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These architectures and metrics are not confined to COVID-19 — they generalize to:
- **Lung nodule / cancer detection** (LIDC-IDRI dataset).
- **Stroke lesion segmentation** (BraTS challenge).
- **Tuberculosis screening** in resource-limited settings (e.g., CAD4TB).
- **Pneumothorax detection** in emergency radiology.

The same U-Net + ResNet + Dice-loss pattern is a *transferable* engineering skill across the entire spectrum of medical imaging AI.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Dice Loss Works for Imbalanced Lung Segmentation

Lung masks occupy only ~25–35% of a chest CT volume; the rest is air, mediastinum, bone, soft tissue. With standard BCE, the model can achieve 65% accuracy by predicting "background" everywhere. We derive Dice Loss to show why it solves this.

### Step 1 — Define Set Overlap
For a binary mask $G$ (ground truth) and predicted soft mask $P$ (probabilities in $[0,1]$), the **Dice coefficient** is:

$$ DSC(P, G) = \frac{2 \cdot \vert P \cap G \vert}{\vert P \vert + \vert G \vert} = \frac{2 \sum_{i=1}^{N_v} p_i g_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N_v} p_i + \sum_{i=1}^{N_v} g_i} $$

where $N_v$ is the total number of voxels.

### Step 2 — Add Smoothing for Numerical Stability
When both $P$ and $G$ are empty, the denominator is zero. Adding $\epsilon = 10^{-6}$:

$$ DSC_{\epsilon}(P, G) = \frac{2 \sum_i p_i g_i + \epsilon}{\sum_i p_i + \sum_i g_i + \epsilon} $$

### Step 3 — Convert to a Loss
Since $DSC \in [0,1]$ and we want to *minimize*, we invert:

$$ \mathcal{L}_{Dice} = 1 - DSC_{\epsilon}(P, G) $$

### Step 4 — Show Why It Handles Class Imbalance
Consider a CT volume with $N_v = 10^7$ voxels, of which only $N_{+} = 3 \times 10^6$ are lung. If the model predicts $p_i = 0$ for all voxels:
- **BCE:** $\mathcal{L}_{BCE} = -\frac{1}{N_v} \sum_i g_i \log p_i$. Since $\log 0 \to -\infty$, BCE saturates and produces weak gradients — model cannot learn.
- **Dice Loss:** Numerator = 0, denominator = $\sum_i g_i = 3 \times 10^6$, so $DSC = 0$ and $\mathcal{L}_{Dice} = 1$. The loss is bounded, gradients flow, and the model is forced to improve overlap.

### Step 5 — Gradient Analysis
The gradient of $\mathcal{L}_{Dice}$ with respect to $p_i$ is:

$$ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{Dice}}{\partial p_i} = -\frac{2 g_i \cdot D - 2 g_i p_i}{D^2} $$

where $D = \sum_i p_i + \sum_i g_i + \epsilon$. This gradient is *self-normalizing* — it automatically scales with the imbalance ratio, which is why Dice outperforms BCE for sparse medical masks.

## 3.2 Derivation: Multi-class Cross-Entropy from Maximum Likelihood

### Step 1 — Probabilistic Model
Assume the softmax output $\hat{p}_c$ is the categorical likelihood $P(y = c \vert x)$. For a one-hot ground truth $y$, the probability of observing $y$ is:

$$ P(y \vert x) = \prod_{c=1}^{C} \hat{p}_c^{y_c} $$

### Step 2 — Take the Log
$$ \log P(y \vert x) = \sum_{c=1}^{C} y_c \log \hat{p}_c $$

### Step 3 — Negative Log-Likelihood (the Loss)
$$ \mathcal{L}_{NLL} = -\log P(y \vert x) = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_c \log \hat{p}_c $$

### Step 4 — Average Over a Batch of Size $N$
$$ \mathcal{L}_{CE} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{c=1}^{C} y_{i,c} \log \hat{p}_{i,c} $$

This is exactly the **categorical cross-entropy loss** used in COVID-19 multi-class classification (COVID / non-COVID pneumonia / normal).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Architectural Choice vs. Performance Trade-off

| Architecture | Parameters (M) | Slice-Level Accuracy | Volumetric Capability | Inference Time (s) | Memory (GB) | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ResNet-18 (2D)** | 11.7 | ~89% | No (2D) | 0.02 | 1.5 | Slice-level triage |
| **ResNet-50 (2D)** | 25.6 | ~93% | No (2D) | 0.05 | 2.5 | Balanced accuracy/speed |
| **DenseNet-121 (2D)** | 8.1 | ~94% | No (2D) | 0.07 | 3.0 | Limited GPU memory |
| **EfficientNet-B3 (2D)** | 12.0 | ~95% | No (2D) | 0.06 | 2.8 | Production deployment |
| **ResNet-18 (3D)** | 33.2 | ~96% | Yes | 0.30 | 6.0 | Volumetric 3D context |
| **3D U-Net (Segmentation)** | 19.1 | N/A (segmentation) | Yes | 0.25 | 5.5 | Lung + lesion mask |
| **ViT-B/16 (2D)** | 86.0 | ~95.5% | No (2D) | 0.12 | 4.0 | Long-range attention |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Computing Performance Metrics

**Given a test set of 250 COVID-positive patients**, a model produces the following confusion matrix at threshold $\tau = 0.5$:

| | Predicted COVID (+) | Predicted Normal (−) |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual COVID (+)** | TP = 225 | FN = 25 |
| **Actual Normal (−)** | FP = 18 | TN = 207 |

**Step 1 — Sensitivity (Recall):**

$$ Se = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{225}{225 + 25} = \frac{225}{250} = 0.90 \ (\text{90\%}) $$

**Step 2 — Specificity:**

$$ Sp = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{207}{207 + 18} = \frac{207}{225} = 0.92 \ (\text{92\%}) $$

**Step 3 — Precision (PPV):**

$$ PPV = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{225}{225 + 18} = \frac{225}{243} \approx 0.926 \ (\text{92.6\%}) $$

**Step 4 — F1-Score:**

$$ F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{PPV \cdot Se}{PPV + Se} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.926 \times 0.90}{0.926 + 0.90} = \frac{1.667}{1.826} \approx 0.913 $$

**Step 5 — Accuracy:**

$$ Acc = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{225 + 207}{250 + 225} = \frac{432}{475} \approx 0.909 \ (\text{90.9\%}) $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** Always report **Sensitivity first** in clinical AI tasks. Missing a COVID-19 case (FN) is far more dangerous than a false alarm (FP). A model with 90% Se is clinically viable; 70% Se is not.

```mermaid
    A2 --> A3[Resampling to 1mm Isotropic Voxel]
    A3 --> A4[Lung Window Clipping minus 1000 to plus 400 HU]
    A4 --> A5[Intensity Normalization to Zero Mean Unit Variance]

    A5 --> B1[3D U-Net Lung Parenchyma Segmentation]
    B1 --> B2[Lung Mask Morphological Cleanup]

    B3 --> C1[Slice Selection Strategy]

    C2 --> D1[2D ResNet50 Backbone]

    D1 --> E1[Global Average Pooling]
    D2 --> E2[Global Average Pooling]

    E1 --> F1[Softmax Classifier 3 classes]
    E2 --> F2[Softmax Classifier 3 classes]

    F1 --> G1[Slice Level Probability Vector]
    F2 --> G2[Slice Level Probability Vector]

    G1 --> H1[Patient Level Max Pooling Aggregation]
    G2 --> H2[Patient Level Mean Pooling Aggregation]

    H1 --> I1[Final Patient Diagnosis COVID or NonCOVID or Normal]
    H2 --> I2[Final Patient Diagnosis COVID or NonCOVID or Normal]

    I1 --> J1[GradCAM Saliency Map for Explainability]

    J2 --> K2[Radiologist Review and Confirmation]

    K2 --> L2[Clinical Report Generation]
```
