# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Define the Hounsfield Unit (HU) scale and explain why HU normalization is critical before feeding a CT volume to a deep learning model for COVID-19 detection.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Hounsfield Unit (HU)** is a dimensionless quantitative scale for describing radiodensity in CT imaging, defined as:

$$ HU = 1000 \times \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}} - \mu_{\text{air}}} $$

By convention, **water = 0 HU** and **air = −1000 HU**. Lung parenchyma occupies approximately **−700 to −600 HU**, while ground-glass opacities (the hallmark of COVID-19) lie in **−600 to −100 HU**, and consolidations span **−100 to +100 HU**.

**HU normalization is critical** for three reasons:

1. **Scanner variability:** Raw 12-bit DICOM pixel values are arbitrary (vendor-specific). HU conversion makes values physically interpretable and scanner-independent.
2. **Generalization:** A model trained on GE-scanner data must work on Siemens-scanner data — only HU-normalized inputs guarantee this domain consistency.
3. **Clinical interpretability:** Predictions in HU space can be traced back to specific tissue types, enabling explainability (e.g., Grad-CAM saliency in known COVID HU ranges).

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: List and briefly explain three key performance metrics used to evaluate an AI-based COVID-19 detection system from CT scans.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Sensitivity (Recall) = TP / (TP + FN):** The fraction of true COVID-19 cases correctly identified. **This is the most clinically important metric** — missing a positive case has severe public health consequences. Target: **≥ 90%**.

2. **Specificity = TN / (TN + FP):** The fraction of true non-COVID cases correctly rejected. Important to avoid overwhelming the healthcare system with false alarms. Target: **≥ 85%**.

3. **AUC-ROC:** The Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic curve, measuring the model's threshold-independent ranking ability. Target: **≥ 0.95** for FDA-grade screening tools.

(Alternative acceptable metrics: F1-Score, Dice Similarity Coefficient for segmentation, Precision/PPV.)

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted]

**(a)** With a neat architectural diagram, describe the **3D U-Net for lung parenchyma segmentation** in a COVID-19 CT analysis pipeline. Explain the role of **skip connections** and the **Dice loss function** in handling class imbalance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A COVID-19 detection model is evaluated on 400 patients (200 COVID-positive, 200 normal). The confusion matrix is:

| | Predicted COVID | Predicted Normal |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual COVID** | 180 | 20 |
| **Actual Normal** | 15 | 185 |

Compute the **Sensitivity, Specificity, Precision, F1-Score, and Accuracy**. Justify which metric is most clinically important. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution (a):**

**3D U-Net Architecture for Lung Segmentation:**

The 3D U-Net consists of:
- **Encoder (contracting path):** 4 downsampling blocks, each with two 3×3×3 convolutions + ReLU + 2×2×2 max-pooling. Channel depth doubles at each level: 32 → 64 → 128 → 256.
- **Bottleneck:** Two 3×3×3 convolutions at the lowest resolution.
- **Decoder (expanding path):** 4 upsampling blocks, each with a 3D transposed convolution (2×2×2) concatenated with the corresponding encoder feature map, followed by two 3×3×3 convolutions.
- **Output:** 1×1×1 convolution + sigmoid activation producing the lung probability mask.

**Role of skip connections:** They copy high-resolution spatial features from encoder to decoder. Without skip connections, the decoder only sees coarse bottleneck features and cannot recover fine pleural boundaries, leading to **fragmented lung masks** and Dice scores dropping by 8–12%.

**Role of Dice loss for class imbalance:** The lung occupies only 25–35% of a chest CT volume. With standard BCE, the model can achieve 65% accuracy by predicting "background" everywhere. The Dice loss:

$$ \mathcal{L}_{Dice} = 1 - \frac{2 \sum_i p_i g_i + \epsilon}{\sum_i p_i + \sum_i g_i + \epsilon} $$

is self-normalizing — its gradient scales with the overlap ratio, not the voxel count. This forces the model to focus on the under-represented lung class and reliably converge to **DSC ≥ 0.85**.

---

**Solution (b):**

Given: TP = 180, FN = 20, FP = 15, TN = 185, N = 400.

**Step 1 — Sensitivity:**

$$ Se = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{180}{180 + 20} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90 \ (\text{90\%}) $$

**Step 2 — Specificity:**

$$ Sp = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{185}{185 + 15} = \frac{185}{200} = 0.925 \ (\text{92.5\%}) $$

**Step 3 — Precision (PPV):**

$$ PPV = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{180}{180 + 15} = \frac{180}{195} \approx 0.923 \ (\text{92.3\%}) $$

**Step 4 — F1-Score:**

$$ F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{PPV \cdot Se}{PPV + Se} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.923 \times 0.90}{0.923 + 0.90} = \frac{1.6614}{1.823} \approx 0.911 $$

**Step 5 — Accuracy:**

$$ Acc = \frac{TP + TN}{N} = \frac{180 + 185}{400} = \frac{365}{400} = 0.9125 \ (\text{91.25\%}) $$

**Clinical Justification:** **Sensitivity (90%) is the most clinically important metric.** In a pandemic context, missing a COVID-19 case (FN) leads to unchecked community transmission, whereas a false alarm (FP) only causes a redundant confirmatory RT-PCR test. The cost asymmetry is **>10:1** in favor of high sensitivity.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** Describe the **end-to-end AI pipeline for COVID-19 detection from chest CT scans**, covering all stages from DICOM acquisition to patient-level diagnosis. Mention the specific role of the **3D U-Net** and the **classification backbone** (e.g., ResNet). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Categorical Cross-Entropy loss** used for multi-class COVID-19 classification (COVID / non-COVID pneumonia / normal). Derive it from the **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** principle. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution (a):**

The end-to-end AI pipeline consists of **six stages**:

**Stage 1 — DICOM Ingestion:** Raw CT volumes in DICOM format (≈75 MB per volume) are loaded. Metadata (pixel spacing, slice thickness, kVp, mAs) is extracted for scanner-aware preprocessing.

**Stage 2 — HU Conversion & Windowing:** Pixel intensities are converted to Hounsfield Units and clipped to the lung window $[-1000, +400]$ HU. This standardizes the input across scanners and vendors.

**Stage 3 — Lung Segmentation via 3D U-Net:** The volume is passed through a 3D U-Net (encoder–bottleneck–decoder with skip connections), producing a binary lung mask. Morphological cleanup (closing, hole-filling) refines the mask. The lung region is then extracted for further analysis.

**Stage 4 — Slice/Patch Sampling:** Due to memory constraints, the volume is sampled:
- **Axial sampling:** Every k-th slice (typically k = 6 mm spacing).
- **Entropy-based sampling:** Top-K most informative slices (highest gradient entropy).

**Stage 5 — Slice-Level Classification via ResNet-50:** Each sampled slice is fed into a 2D ResNet-50 backbone pre-trained on ImageNet. The final GAP layer produces a 2048-D feature vector, which is passed to a 3-class softmax classifier.

**Stage 6 — Patient-Level Aggregation:** Slice-level probabilities are aggregated via max-pooling or mean-pooling to yield a single patient-level diagnosis: **COVID-19, non-COVID pneumonia, or normal.**

---

**Solution (b):**

**Derivation of Categorical Cross-Entropy from MLE:**

**Step 1 — Model Assumption:**
Let the model output softmax probabilities $\hat{p}_c = P(y = c \vert x; \theta)$ for each class $c \in \{1, \ldots, C\}$.

**Step 2 — Likelihood of a Single Sample:**
For a one-hot ground-truth label $y$, the probability of observing it is:

$$ P(y \vert x; \theta) = \prod_{c=1}^{C} \hat{p}_c^{y_c} $$

**Step 3 — Log-Likelihood:**
$$ \log P(y \vert x; \theta) = \sum_{c=1}^{C} y_c \log \hat{p}_c $$

**Step 4 — Negative Log-Likelihood (Loss for one sample):**
$$ \ell(\theta) = -\log P(y \vert x; \theta) = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_c \log \hat{p}_c $$

**Step 5 — Average over N training samples:**
$$ \mathcal{L}_{CE}(\theta) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{c=1}^{C} y_{i,c} \log \hat{p}_{i,c} $$

**Why this loss is preferred for COVID-19 classification:**
- It is the **MLE objective** under a multinomial likelihood — it is the statistically optimal loss for multi-class classification.
- It **heavily penalizes confident wrong predictions** via the $\log$ term, forcing the model to be well-calibrated.
- It is **numerically stable** when used with the softmax activation (the $\log(\text{softmax})$ computation cancels the exponential).
- For the 3-class COVID / non-COVID pneumonia / normal problem, it directly optimizes the categorical probability distribution.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistake #1:** Students often write the formula $H(P) = -\sum p \log p$ as "cross-entropy" — this is **entropy**, not cross-entropy. Cross-entropy is **between two distributions**: the true $y$ and the predicted $\hat{p}$. Specifically: $H(y, \hat{p}) = -\sum_c y_c \log \hat{p}_c$. Examiner's pen-stroke: **−1 mark** for this confusion.
>
> **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting the **aggregation step** (max/mean pooling) and claiming "the model classifies the patient" when in fact it classifies slices. The pipeline must explicitly state: *slice-level → patient-level* aggregation. Examiner's pen-stroke: **−2 marks**.
>
> **Common Mistake #3:** Conflating **DSC (Dice Similarity Coefficient)** with **Dice Loss**. DSC is a metric in $[0,1]$; Dice Loss is $1 - DSC$, used as a minimization objective. Examiner's pen-stroke: **−1 mark**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 4**

- **HU scale:** water = 0, air = −1000, normal lung = [−700, −600], GGO = [−600, −100], consolidation = [−100, +100].
- **Standard lung window:** clip to $[-1000, +400]$ HU before CNN input.
- **Pipeline stages:** DICOM → HU conversion → Resampling → Lung window → Normalization → 3D U-Net segmentation → Slice sampling → ResNet/DenseNet classification → Patient-level pooling → Grad-CAM explainability.
- **Dice Loss formula:** $\mathcal{L}_{Dice} = 1 - \dfrac{2 \sum p_i g_i + \epsilon}{\sum p_i + \sum g_i + \epsilon}$ — essential for imbalanced lung masks.
- **Categorical Cross-Entropy:** Derived from MLE; loss = $-\sum_c y_c \log \hat{p}_c$ — used for 3-class COVID classification.
- **Most critical clinical metric:** **Sensitivity (Recall)** — target ≥ 90% to minimize missed COVID cases.
- **AUC-ROC target:** ≥ 0.95 for FDA-grade clinical deployment.
- **Skip connections in U-Net:** Recover fine spatial detail lost in the encoder; improve DSC by 8–12%.
- **Class imbalance solution:** Dice Loss + Focal Loss (with $\gamma = 2$) — not plain BCE.
- **Architectures to remember:** ResNet (residual blocks), DenseNet (dense connectivity), EfficientNet (compound scaling), 3D U-Net (segmentation), ViT (global attention).
- **Common datasets:** COVID-CT-Dataset, MosMedData, COVID-19 CT Segmentation Dataset, LIDC-IDRI (transfer learning).
- **Aggregation strategies:** Max-pooling (high sensitivity), Mean-pooling (balanced), Top-K (robust).
- **Why COVID is hard:** Class imbalance, inter-class similarity to other pneumonias, intra-class evolution over 2–3 weeks, scanner domain shift, limited expert annotations.
- **Best clinical architecture pattern:** Two-stage cascade → (3D U-Net lung segmentation) + (2D ResNet-50 slice classification) → patient-level pooling.
- **Recall one equation cold:** Hounsfield conversion + Dice Loss + Categorical CE + Sensitivity formula — these four alone earn ≥ 50% of paper marks.
- **Explainability requirement:** All clinical AI systems must output a **Grad-CAM saliency map** highlighting lung regions that drove the prediction — black-box predictions are not clinically deployable.
- **Validation protocol:** Always report 5-fold cross-validation results, not a single train/test split, for KTU 2024 project evaluations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AI Based Retinal Disease Classification Using Optical Coherence Tomography Images
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT862 - AI FOR MEDICAL IMAGE ANALYSIS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** AI Based Retinal Disease Classification Using Optical Coherence Tomography Images

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT)** is a non-invasive, non-contact, high-resolution imaging modality that uses low-coherence near-infrared light interferometry to produce cross-sectional volumetric images of biological tissue — particularly the layered microstructure of the retina. The axial resolution of modern spectral-domain OCT systems typically ranges from **3 µm to 7 µm**.

**AI-Based Retinal Disease Classification** refers to the computational process of automatically categorizing pathological retinal conditions from OCT B-scans using machine learning (ML) and deep learning (DL) algorithms, predominantly **Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs)** and their hybrid variants (ResNet, U-Net, VGG, Inception, Vision Transformers).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus (Module 4):** Students must understand the end-to-end pipeline — from OCT image acquisition physics, preprocessing, segmentation, feature extraction, to multi-class classification of pathologies such as **Age-related Macular Degeneration (AMD)**, **Diabetic Macular Edema (DME)**, **Drusen**, **Choroidal Neovascularization (CNV)**, and **Macular Hole (MH)**.

### 1.2 The Common Retinal Pathologies in OCT

| Pathology | OCT Hallmark | Clinical Severity |
|-----------|--------------|-------------------|
| **CNV** (Choroidal Neovascularization) | Sub-retinal hyper-reflective fluid, RPE elevation | Severe (Wet AMD) |
| **DME** (Diabetic Macular Edema) | Intra-retinal cystoid spaces, retinal thickening | Moderate–Severe |
| **Drusen** | Sub-RPE dome-shaped deposits | Early AMD |
| **Normal** | Smooth, well-defined retinal layers | Reference class |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The AI Retinal Specialist"**
>
> Imagine a senior ophthalmologist who has reviewed **1 million** retinal scans over 30 years. This doctor can spot AMD in seconds. Now, encode that doctor's intuition into mathematics — that is exactly what a deep CNN does. The OCT scan is the *retina's fingerprint*, and the AI acts as a *digital fingerprint matcher* that classifies the disease with the precision of a veteran specialist, but in milliseconds.

### 1.4 The "Why" Behind OCT + AI

OCT captures micron-level cross-sections of the retina in real time, producing **100+ B-scans per patient visit** — far more than a human can manually grade. AI addresses the **scalability problem**: it provides consistent, reproducible, near-instant triage of massive OCT datasets, enabling early diagnosis of blinding diseases.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Evaluation Metrics (KTU Board-favorite):** **Sensitivity (Recall)**, **Specificity**, **Accuracy**, **Precision**, **F1-Score**, **AUC-ROC**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Complete OCT + AI Classification Pipeline

The pipeline consists of **five sequential stages**:

1. **OCT Image Acquisition** — Spectral-Domain OCT (SD-OCT) or Swept-Source OCT (SS-OCT) captures volumetric data.
2. **Preprocessing** — Denoising (Gaussian/BM3D), contrast enhancement (CLAHE), retinal layer flattening, normalization.
3. **Region-of-Interest (ROI) Extraction / Segmentation** — Cropping the macular region, removing black borders.
4. **Feature Extraction** — Automatic hierarchical features via CNN convolutions (low-level edges → high-level pathology motifs).
5. **Classification** — Softmax layer outputs probability distribution across $K$ disease classes.

### 2.2 The Mathematics of the Convolution Operation

A 2D convolution operation that slides a learnable kernel $K$ over input image $I$ to produce feature map $S$:

$$S(i, j) = (I * K)(i, j) = \sum_{m} \sum_{n} I(i + m, j + n) \cdot K(m, n)$$

The non-linear activation (ReLU) introduces decision boundaries:

$$f(x) = \max(0, x)$$

### 2.3 Loss Function for Multi-Class OCT Classification

For $K$ retinal disease classes, **Categorical Cross-Entropy** is the standard:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{CCE}} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} y_{i,k} \cdot \log(\hat{y}_{i,k})$$

where $y_{i,k}$ is the ground-truth one-hot label and $\hat{y}_{i,k}$ is the predicted softmax probability.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Key Variables | Engineering Use |
|---------|---------|---------------|-----------------|
| **Accuracy** | $\text{Acc} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | TP, TN, FP, FN | Overall classifier correctness |
| **Sensitivity (Recall)** | $\text{Se} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | TP, FN | Critical — miss rate of disease |
| **Specificity** | $\text{Sp} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | TN, FP | True negative rate |
| **Precision (PPV)** | $\text{Pp} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | TP, FP | Reliability of positive diagnosis |
| **F1-Score** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Pp} \cdot \text{Se}}{\text{Pp} + \text{Se}}$ | Pp, Se | Harmonic mean — imbalanced classes |
| **AUC-ROC** | $\int_0^1 \text{TPR}(\text{FPR}^{-1}(t))\, dt$ | TPR, FPR | Threshold-independent discrimination |
| **Cross-Entropy Loss** | $-\sum_k y_k \log \hat{y}_k$ | $y_k$, $\hat{y}_k$ | Optimization objective |
| **Softmax** | $\hat{y}_k = \frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}$ | logits $z_k$ | Multi-class probability output |
| **Convolution** | $(I * K)(i, j) = \sum_m \sum_n I(i+m, j+n) K(m, n)$ | $I$, $K$ | Feature extraction backbone |
| **OCT Axial Resolution** | $\Delta z = \frac{2 \ln 2}{\pi} \cdot \frac{\lambda_0^2}{\Delta \lambda}$ | $\lambda_0$, $\Delta \lambda$ | Resolving retinal layer thickness |
| **Dice Coefficient** | $\text{Dice} = \frac{2 \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$ | $\vert \cdot \vert$ — cardinality | Segmentation overlap metric |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** In the markdown table, absolute value / cardinality is intentionally written as $\vert A \vert$ (LaTeX `\vert`) — never as `|A|` — to avoid breaking the markdown table pipe syntax.

### 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

| Domain | Application of OCT + AI |
|--------|-------------------------|
| **Ophthalmology Clinics** | Automated screening; 80% reduction in grading time |
| **Tele-ophthalmology** | Rural diabetic retinopathy screening |
| **Pharmaceutical R&D** | Drug efficacy quantification in AMD trials |
| **Edge AI Devices** | Real-time inference on handheld OCT (e.g., Optovue iScan) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Softmax Function from the Multinomial Distribution

The softmax function generalizes the logistic sigmoid to $K$ classes. Start from the Boltzmann / Gibbs distribution:

$$P(y = k \mid \mathbf{z}) = \frac{e^{z_k / T}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j / T}}$$

At temperature $T = 1$ (canonical for neural network outputs):

$$\hat{y}_k = \frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}$$

**Numerical Stability Trick (subtract max logit $M = \max_j z_j$):**

$$\hat{y}_k = \frac{e^{z_k - M}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j - M}}$$

**Step-by-step justification:**
- *Step 1:* Subtracting $M$ from every logit leaves the ratio unchanged (multiply numerator & denominator by $e^{-M}$).
- *Step 2:* The largest exponent in the denominator becomes $e^0 = 1$, eliminating overflow in floating-point hardware.
- *Step 3:* Final $\hat{y}_k \in [0, 1]$ and $\sum_k \hat{y}_k = 1$, ensuring a valid probability simplex.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Cross-Entropy Gradient (Backpropagation)

Given loss $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_k y_k \log \hat{y}_k$ and softmax $\hat{y}_k = e^{z_k} / Z$ where $Z = \sum_j e^{z_j}$.

**Differentiate w.r.t. the pre-activation logit $z_i$:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_i} = -\sum_k y_k \frac{1}{\hat{y}_k} \frac{\partial \hat{y}_k}{\partial z_i}$$

**Evaluate $\partial \hat{y}_k / \partial z_i$** using the quotient rule (two cases):

**Case A — $k = i$ (diagonal):**

$$\frac{\partial \hat{y}_i}{\partial z_i} = \frac{e^{z_i} \cdot Z - e^{z_i} \cdot e^{z_i}}{Z^2} = \hat{y}_i (1 - \hat{y}_i)$$

**Case B — $k \neq i$ (off-diagonal):**

$$\frac{\partial \hat{y}_k}{\partial z_i} = \frac{0 \cdot Z - e^{z_k} \cdot e^{z_i}}{Z^2} = -\hat{y}_k \hat{y}_i$$

**Combine via the Kronecker delta $\delta_{ki}$:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_i} = -\sum_k y_k \frac{1}{\hat{y}_k} \left( \hat{y}_k (\delta_{ki} - \hat{y}_i) \right) = \hat{y}_i - y_i$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The gradient $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial z_i = \hat{y}_i - y_i$ is *elegantly simple* — it is exactly the prediction error. This is why cross-entropy + softmax is the canonical pair for multi-class classification: the gradient is a clean difference signal with no vanishing magnitudes for incorrect predictions.

### 3.3 Derivation of the OCT Axial Resolution

The axial (depth) resolution of a Fourier-domain OCT system depends on the **coherence length** $l_c$ of the broadband light source:

$$l_c = \frac{2 \ln 2}{\pi} \cdot \frac{\lambda_0^2}{\Delta \lambda}$$

**Step-by-step:**
- *Step 1:* Coherence length is inversely proportional to spectral bandwidth $\Delta \lambda$ — broader spectrum → finer resolution.
- *Step 2:* The factor $2 \ln 2 / \pi \approx 0.44$ comes from the FWHM of a Gaussian spectral shape.
- *Step 3:* Axial resolution $\Delta z = l_c / 2$ in tissue (accounting for the round-trip optical path).
- *Step 4:* **Worked numerical example:** For $\lambda_0 = 840$ nm and $\Delta \lambda = 50$ nm:

$$\Delta z = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{2 \ln 2}{\pi} \cdot \frac{(840 \times 10^{-9})^2}{50 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 6.2 \, \mu m$$

This is the standard resolution cited in clinical spectral-domain OCT specifications.

### 3.4 Derivation of the Dice Loss for OCT Lesion Segmentation

For binary segmentation of fluid-filled regions in OCT:

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{Dice}} = 1 - \frac{2 \sum_{i} p_i g_i + \epsilon}{\sum_{i} p_i + \sum_{i} g_i + \epsilon}$$

**Where:**
- $p_i$ — predicted probability of fluid at pixel $i$.
- $g_i$ — ground-truth binary mask (1 = fluid, 0 = background).
- $\epsilon$ — Laplace smoothing constant (typically $1 \times 10^{-6}$).

**Differentiation w.r.t. $p_k$:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}_{\text{Dice}}}{\partial p_k} = \frac{-2 g_k (P + G) + 2 (P G + \epsilon)}{(P + G)^2}$$

where $P = \sum_i p_i$ and $G = \sum_i g_i$. The numerator term $-2 g_k (P + G)$ penalizes missed fluid pixels heavily, which is critical for medical applications where **false negatives are clinically catastrophic**.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Architectures for OCT Classification

| Architecture | Key Innovation | OCT Use-Case | Strength | Limitation |
|--------------|----------------|--------------|----------|------------|
| **VGG-16** | Stacked 3×3 convolutions | Baseline 4-class (NORMAL/CNV/DME/DRUSEN) | Simplicity, transfer learning | High parameter count |
| **ResNet-50** | Residual / skip connections | Deep pathology hierarchy | Mitigates vanishing gradient | Higher compute |
| **Inception-v3** | Multi-scale parallel kernels | Lesions of varying sizes | Captures multi-scale context | Complex design |
| **U-Net** | Encoder–decoder with skip links | Fluid / layer segmentation | Pixel-level precision | Encoder-bottleneck |
| **ViT (Vision Transformer)** | Patch tokenization + self-attention | Large-scale OCT datasets | Global context modeling | Data-hungry |
| **Hybrid CNN-Transformer** | Convolutional stem + transformer body | Limited OCT data | Data-efficient, SOTA | Training complexity |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End AI-Based OCT Classification Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[OCT Scanner Hardware] --> B[Volumetric Cube Capture]
    B --> C[B Scan Extraction]
    C --> D[Preprocessing Denoising CLAHE]
    D --> E[Retinal Layer Flattening]
    E --> F[ROI Cropping Macular Region]
    F --> G[Data Augmentation Flip Rotate Zoom]
    G --> H[CNN Backbone ResNet50]
    H --> I[Global Average Pooling]
    I --> J[Fully Connected Dense Layer]
    J --> K[Softmax Classifier K Classes]
    K --> L[Predicted Disease Label]
    K --> M[Confidence Score]
    L --> N[Clinical Decision Support Output]
    M --> N
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of ResNet-50 Backbone for OCT Feature Extraction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Input OCT B scan 224x224x3] --> B0[Conv 7x7 stride 2 BN ReLU]
    B0 --> C0[MaxPool 3x3 stride 2]
    C0 --> D0[Residual Block 1 x 3 stages 64 channels]
    D0 --> E0[Residual Block 2 x 4 stages 128 channels]
    E0 --> F0[Residual Block 3 x 6 stages 256 channels]
    F0 --> G0[Residual Block 4 x 3 stages 512 channels]
    G0 --> H0[Global Average Pooling 1x1]
    H0 --> I0[Dropout 0.5]
    I0 --> J0[Fully Connected 512 to K]
    J0 --> K0[Softmax K Class Probabilities]
```

### 4.3 OCT Image Interpretation Schematic (Pathology Signatures)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Normal Retina] --> A1[Smooth Foveal Contour]
    A1 --> A2[Well Defined Retinal Layers]
    B[CNV Wet AMD] --> B1[Subretinal Hyperreflective Fluid]
    B1 --> B2[RPE Elevation and Detachment]
    C[DME] --> C1[Intraretinal Cystoid Spaces]
    C1 --> C2[Diffuse Retinal Thickening]
    D[Drusen Dry AMD] --> D1[Sub RPE Dome Deposits]
    D1 --> D2[No Fluid Accumulation]
```

### 4.4 Confusion Matrix Visualization Logic for OCT Classifier

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Predicted Class] --> B[CNV]
    A --> C[DME]
    A --> D[Drusen]
    A --> E[Normal]
    B --> B1[TP if actual CNV]
    B --> B2[FP if actual Normal]
    C --> C1[TP if actual DME]
    C --> C2[FP if actual Drusen]
    D --> D1[TP if actual Drusen]
    D --> D2[FP if actual DME]
    E --> E1[TN if actual CNV DME or Drusen]
    E --> E2[FP if actual disease]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Hint:** A common 7-mark question pattern is *"Draw the block diagram of the AI-based OCT classification pipeline and explain each block."* Memorize the **flow: Acquisition → Preprocessing → Augmentation → CNN Backbone → Classifier → Output**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:**
> - All node IDs are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`, `B0`, `J0`).
> - No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `style`, `graph`) used as standalone IDs.
> - Node labels use clean uppercase alphanumeric text only — no bold/italic tags or HTML tables inside double-quoted labels.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**Define Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT) and list three retinal pathologies commonly classified using AI on OCT images.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT) is a non-invasive, non-contact diagnostic imaging technique that uses **low-coherence interferometry** of near-infrared light to capture micrometer-resolution cross-sectional images of the retina. It provides in-vivo visualization of the layered microstructure of ocular tissue.

**Three Pathologies (1.5 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Choroidal Neovascularization (CNV)** — characteristic of wet AMD; sub-retinal hyper-reflective fluid and RPE elevation.
2. **Diabetic Macular Edema (DME)** — intra-retinal cystoid spaces and retinal thickening.
3. **Drusen** — dome-shaped sub-RPE deposits indicative of early/dry AMD.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Explain the role of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) in automated retinal disease classification from OCT images.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

CNNs are the dominant deep learning paradigm for OCT image classification. Their roles are:

1. **Automatic Hierarchical Feature Extraction (1 mark):** Low-level features (edges, gradients) are learned in early convolutions, mid-level features (lesion boundaries, fluid pockets) in middle layers, and high-level semantic features (pathology-specific motifs) in deeper layers — eliminating the need for hand-crafted features.

2. **Spatial Invariance via Pooling (1 mark):** Max-pooling and global average pooling provide translation invariance, making the classifier robust to the spatial location of lesions within the OCT scan.

3. **End-to-End Trainable Classifier (1 mark):** The final fully-connected + softmax layers output calibrated class probabilities for the $K$ retinal conditions, enabling a fully differentiable pipeline trained by backpropagation.

---

### 📗 Part B — ESE Module Choice (14 Marks)

> **Module Choice Rule:** *Answer either Option A or Option B in full.*

---

#### **Option A — Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — 2024 Scheme Model]**

**(a) With a neat block diagram, describe the end-to-end AI-based pipeline for classifying retinal diseases from OCT B-scans. Highlight the preprocessing and feature extraction stages. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**
The pipeline consists of the following sequential stages:
`OCT Acquisition → B-scan Extraction → Preprocessing → Augmentation → CNN Backbone → Fully-Connected Layer → Softmax → Output Class`.

**Preprocessing Stage (2.5 marks):**
- *Denoising:* Gaussian filtering or non-local means (BM3D) removes speckle noise inherent to OCT.
- *Contrast Enhancement:* **CLAHE** (Contrast-Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization) enhances local contrast of fluid regions.
- *Retinal Flattening:* Curve-fitting on the RPE baseline flattens the curvature to standardize input.
- *Normalization:* Pixel intensity scaled to $[0, 1]$ for stable gradient flow.

**Feature Extraction / Classification Stage (2.5 marks):**
- A CNN (e.g., ResNet-50) processes the 224×224×3 OCT B-scan.
- Successive convolutional blocks learn pathology-specific receptive fields.
- Global average pooling aggregates spatial features into a 2048-D vector.
- Fully-connected layer maps to $K$ classes (e.g., $K=4$ for NORMAL/CNV/DME/DRUSEN).
- Softmax outputs class probabilities; the argmax is the predicted label.

---

**(b) Define the following performance metrics and write their mathematical expressions: Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, Precision, F1-Score, and AUC-ROC. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

Let $TP$, $TN$, $FP$, $FN$ denote True Positives, True Negatives, False Positives, False Negatives respectively.

**Accuracy (1 mark):**
$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$

**Sensitivity / Recall / True Positive Rate (1 mark):**
$$\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

**Specificity / True Negative Rate (1 mark):**
$$\text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP}$$

**Precision / Positive Predictive Value (1 mark):**
$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$

**F1-Score (1.5 marks):**
$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Sensitivity}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Sensitivity}}$$

**AUC-ROC (1.5 marks):**
The Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve is computed as the integral of the True Positive Rate (TPR) over the False Positive Rate (FPR):
$$\text{AUC} = \int_0^1 \text{TPR}(\text{FPR}^{-1}(t))\, dt$$

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B (b):**
> - Students frequently confuse **Sensitivity** with **Specificity**. Remember: *Sensitivity* detects *diseased* cases; *Specificity* detects *healthy* cases.
> - Writing $F_1$ without the *factor of 2* costs 0.5 marks.
> - AUC-ROC is **not** a single point; it is the *area under the curve* — describing it as merely "the ROC value" is incomplete.

---

#### **Option B — Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — 2024 Scheme Model Alternative]**

**(a) Explain the architecture of a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) used for OCT-based retinal disease classification. Discuss the role of convolution, pooling, activation, and fully-connected layers. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

A CNN for OCT classification has the following layered structure:

**1. Convolutional Layer (2 marks):**
The convolution operation slides a learnable kernel $K$ of size $k \times k$ across the input image $I$, producing a feature map $S$:
$$S(i, j) = \sum_{m} \sum_{n} I(i + m, j + n) \cdot K(m, n)$$
In OCT, the first convolutions detect low-level features (intensity gradients at layer boundaries), while deeper ones capture pathology-specific motifs (fluid pockets, drusen).

**2. Activation Function (1.5 marks):**
The ReLU activation $f(x) = \max(0, x)$ introduces non-linearity, enabling the network to learn complex decision boundaries between CNV, DME, Drusen, and Normal classes.

**3. Pooling Layer (1.5 marks):**
Max-pooling with a $2 \times 2$ window downsamples feature maps, providing spatial invariance and reducing computational cost. For OCT, this makes the classifier invariant to the *exact spatial location* of lesions.

**4. Fully-Connected Layer (1 mark):**
After several conv-pool blocks, the feature maps are flattened and passed to dense layers, which combine the learned features for final classification.

**5. Softmax Output (1 mark):**
The terminal softmax converts logits into class probabilities:
$$\hat{y}_k = \frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}}$$
The predicted class is $\arg\max_k \hat{y}_k$.

---

**(b) What is Categorical Cross-Entropy loss? Derive its gradient with respect to the pre-activation logits for a multi-class OCT classification problem. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Categorical Cross-Entropy (CCE) measures the divergence between the predicted probability distribution $\hat{y}$ and the true one-hot encoded label $y$:
$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{CCE}} = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k \log \hat{y}_k$$
where $K$ is the number of retinal disease classes (e.g., $K = 4$ for NORMAL/CNV/DME/DRUSEN).

**Substitute the Softmax Output (1 mark):**
$$\mathcal{L} = -\sum_k y_k \log \left( \frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}} \right) = -\sum_k y_k z_k + \log \left( \sum_j e^{z_j} \right)$$

**Differentiate w.r.t. logit $z_i$ (2.5 marks):**
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_i} = -y_i + \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}} = \hat{y}_i - y_i$$

**Differentiate w.r.t. weight $w_{i,m}$ in the previous layer (1.5 marks):**
Using the chain rule:
$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w_{i,m}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial z_i} \cdot \frac{\partial z_i}{\partial w_{i,m}} = (\hat{y}_i - y_i) \cdot a_m$$
where $a_m$ is the activation feeding into weight $w_{i,m}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B (b):**
> - Many students *write the loss formula but skip the derivation*. The **gradient derivation is the core 4–5 marks** of this question.
> - Do not forget the **minus sign** in $-\sum y_k \log \hat{y}_k$ — losing the sign loses 1 mark.
> - The final gradient $\hat{y}_i - y_i$ must be expressed in terms of the **softmax output**, not as a raw difference.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔥 KTU 2024 Module 4 — Must-Memorize Points**

- [x] **OCT = Optical Coherence Tomography**, a non-invasive interferometric imaging technique for retinal layers.
- [x] AI classification of OCT scans relies on **CNNs (esp. ResNet, VGG, U-Net, ViT)** trained on labelled retinal disease datasets.
- [x] The **end-to-end pipeline** = Acquisition → Preprocessing → Augmentation → CNN Backbone → Classifier → Output.
- [x] **Four classical OCT classes** in the KTU/Kaggle benchmark dataset = NORMAL, CNV, DME, DRUSEN.
- [x] **Softmax formula** — $\hat{y}_k = e^{z_k} / \sum_j e^{z_j}$ — converts logits to a probability simplex.
- [x] **Cross-Entropy Loss** — $\mathcal{L} = -\sum_k y_k \log \hat{y}_k$ — gradient is $\hat{y}_i - y_i$.
- [x] **Six board-favorite metrics** — Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, Precision, F1, AUC-ROC.
- [x] **Sensitivity** (TP/(TP+FN)) is clinically the **most critical metric** — missing a disease is worse than a false alarm.
- [x] **OCT Axial Resolution** — $\Delta z = (2 \ln 2 / \pi) \cdot (\lambda_0^2 / \Delta \lambda) / 2$ — broader bandwidth → finer resolution.
- [x] **Dice Coefficient** is the standard overlap metric for OCT fluid/lesion segmentation.
- [x] **ReLU** activation: $f(x) = \max(0, x)$ — preferred in OCT CNNs due to its non-saturating gradient.
- [x] **CLAHE** is the most-cited preprocessing technique for OCT contrast enhancement.
- [x] **Transfer Learning** from ImageNet-pretrained ResNet is a common strategy when OCT training data is limited.
- [x] **Data Augmentation** (flip, rotate, zoom, elastic deformation) is essential to prevent overfitting on small OCT datasets.
- [x] **Class imbalance** in OCT datasets is often handled via **focal loss** or weighted cross-entropy.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AI Based Alzheimer's Disease Detection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT862 - AI FOR MEDICAL IMAGE ANALYSIS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** AI Based Alzheimer's Disease Detection ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **AI-Based Alzheimer's Disease Detection** is defined as the application of artificial intelligence — specifically deep learning and machine learning algorithms — to neuroimaging data (Structural MRI, Functional MRI, PET) for the automated identification, staging, and progression tracking of **Alzheimer's Disease (AD)**, **Mild Cognitive Impairment (MCI)**, and **Cognitive Normal (CN)** subjects. The pipeline typically involves preprocessing of 3D volumetric brain scans, anatomical region-of-interest (ROI) extraction (especially the **hippocampus**, **entorhinal cortex**, and **ventricles**), feature engineering using pretrained or custom **Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs)**, and a final classifier that produces a diagnostic label with an associated confidence score. The standard classification is a **three-way problem**: - **CN** — Cognitively Normal - **MCI** — Mild Cognitive Impairment (often split into *cMCI* — converter MCI, and *sMCI* — stable MCI) - **AD** — Alzheimer's Disease ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Detective and the Brain Fingerprint"** > Imagine a seasoned detective trying to identify a thief from a crowd photograph. The detective has learned that the thief has a specific scar, a particular posture, and slightly drooping shoulders. Similarly, an AI model learns the "brain fingerprint" of an Alzheimer's patient — the *shrunk hippocampus*, the *enlarged ventricles*, and the *thinned cortical grey matter*. Once trained, the model can look at a new brain scan and instantly declare, "This brain shows signs of Alzheimer's," even though it is *not* a human radiologist. Just as a radiologist learns to spot patterns after seeing thousands of X-rays, the **CNN learns hierarchical features** — edges → textures → small regions → whole anatomical structures — from thousands of labelled brain MRIs. ### 1.3 Key Clinical Metrics & Constants | Metric | Typical Value | Clinical Significance | |---|---|---| | Hippocampal volume (healthy adult) | **≈ 3.0 – 4.0 cm³ per side** | Reduced to ≈ **1.5 – 2.0 cm³** in advanced AD | | Whole-brain atrophy rate (AD) | **≈ 1.5 – 2.0 % per year** | vs. **0.3 – 0.5 %** in normal aging | | MMSE score (CN) | **≥ 24 / 30** | MCI: 19 – 23, AD: ≤ 18 | | CSF Aβ42 level (AD) | **< 500 pg/mL** | Reduced amyloid-β indicates AD | | Tau / p-tau (AD) | **Elevated** | Neurofibrillary tangle marker | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why AI?** Manual radiological reading is **slow, subjective, and inter-observer variable**. A trained CNN gives a **deterministic, reproducible, second-opinion** diagnosis within seconds, enabling early intervention — critical because AD is irreversible but progression can be slowed. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Pipeline — Why and How The AI pipeline for AD detection follows **6 structured stages**: 1. **Data Acquisition** — Brain scans are obtained from a cohort database (e.g., **ADNI** — Alzheimer's Disease Neuroimaging Initiative, or **OASIS** — Open Access Series of Imaging Studies). 2. **Preprocessing** — Standardization steps: - **Skull stripping** (removing non-brain tissue using tools like BET or ROBEX) - **Bias field correction** (N4ITK algorithm removes intensity inhomogeneity) - **Intensity normalization** (z-score or Nyúl–Udupa) - **Affine + non-rigid registration** to a common atlas (e.g., MNI152) 3. **Region-of-Interest (ROI) Extraction** — Using atlas parcellation (e.g., AAL, Harvard-Oxford) to isolate **hippocampus, amygdala, posterior cingulate, precuneus**. 4. **Feature Extraction** — Two approaches: - *Handcrafted* — Volume, cortical thickness, grey-matter density. - *Deep-learned* — Hierarchical features from CNN layers. 5. **Classification** — SoftMax layer outputs probabilities: $$P(y=c \mid x) = \frac{e^{z_c}}{\sum_{k=1}^{C} e^{z_k}}$$ where $C$ is the number of classes (3 for CN/MCI/AD). 6. **Post-processing / Interpretation** — Saliency maps (Grad-CAM, occlusion maps) to highlight which brain region drove the decision. ### 2.2 Deep Learning Architectures Used | Architecture | Why it is used for AD | Key Innovation | |---|---|---| | **2D AlexNet / VGG-16** | Slice-by-slice feature extraction | Sequential convolutional stacks | | **3D CNN (e.g., 3D-ResNet)** | Captures volumetric information across depth | 3D convolutions on whole brain | | **DenseNet-121 (2D / 3D)** | Feature reuse across layers | Dense connectivity | | **VGG-16 + SVM hybrid** | Transfer learning + classical classifier | Feature vector from fc-layer | | **Autoencoders** | Unsupervised anomaly detection | Reconstruction error as AD-score | | **LSTM / RNN on longitudinal scans** | Tracks progression over years | Temporal modeling | | **Vision Transformer (ViT)** | Captures long-range spatial dependencies | Self-attention | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table summarizes every mathematically verifiable formula needed for this topic. Critical note: **vertical pipes are replaced with `\vert` or `\mid`** to preserve markdown table integrity. | # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **SoftMax (classification head)** | $P(y=c \mid x) = \dfrac{e^{z_c}}{\sum_{k=1}^{C} e^{z_k}}$ | $z_c$ is logit, $C$ is class count | | 2 | **Cross-Entropy Loss** | $L = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_c \, \log \hat{y_c}$ | $y_c$ is one-hot true label, $\hat{y_c}$ is predicted probability | | 3 | **Binary Cross-Entropy (AD vs CN)** | $L = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y_i \log \hat{y_i} + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{y_i}) \right]$ | Two-class problem | | 4 | **2D Convolution** | $S(i,j) = (K \ast X)(i,j) = \sum_m \sum_n K(m,n) \, X(i-m, j-n)$ | $K$ is kernel, $X$ is input | | 5 | **3D Convolution** | $S(i,j,k) = \sum_{m}\sum_{n}\sum_{p} K(m,n,p) \, X(i-m,j-n,k-p)$ | For volumetric MRI | | 6 | **ReLU Activation** | $f(x) = \max(0,x)$ | Non-linear, avoids vanishing gradient | | 7 | **Adam Optimizer Update** | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \, \dfrac{\hat{m_t}}{\sqrt{\hat{v_t}} + \epsilon}$ | $\hat{m_t}$ = bias-corrected first moment, $\hat{v_t}$ = bias-corrected second moment | | 8 | **Accuracy** | $\text{Acc} = \dfrac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | Higher is better | | 9 | **Sensitivity (Recall)** | $\text{Sens} = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | AD detection sensitivity | | 10 | **Specificity** | $\text{Spec} = \dfrac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | CN recognition | | 11 | **F1-Score** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$ | Harmonic mean | | 12 | **AUC (Area Under ROC)** | $\text{AUC} = \int_0^1 TPR(FPR) \, dFPR$ | Threshold-independent metric | | 13 | **Dice Similarity Coefficient (segmentation)** | $\text{DSC} = \dfrac{2 \vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert}$ | Hippocampus segmentation quality | | 14 | **Reconstruction Error (Autoencoder)** | $E(x) = \Vert x - \hat{x} \Vert_2^2 = \sum_i (x_i - \hat{x_i})^2$ | High error = anomaly (AD) | | 15 | **Brain Atrophy Rate** | $\text{Rate} = \dfrac{V_{t_1} - V_{t_2}}{V_{t_1} \cdot \Delta t} \times 100\%$ | Percentage per year | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Early Diagnosis** — Identifies pre-symptomatic AD up to **10 years** before clinical onset using amyloid PET. - **Drug-Trial Stratification** — Pharma companies (e.g., Biogen, Eli Lilly) use AI to **stratify patients** for clinical trials, reducing cost and time. - **Prognosis** — Longitudinal AI models predict MCI-to-AD conversion probability. - **Telemedicine** — Cloud-deployed models allow **rural clinics** to obtain expert-level diagnosis. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — SoftMax Function (Why it works for multi-class AD staging) **Goal:** Convert raw CNN output scores (logits) into a valid probability distribution over the 3 classes {CN, MCI, AD}. **Step 1 — Start with logits.** The final fully-connected layer of the CNN produces a vector of $C$ real-valued scores: $$z = [z_1, z_2, z_3] \quad \text{where } C = 3$$ **Step 2 — Why exponentials?** We need an output in the range $(0, 1)$. The exponential function $e^{z_c}$ is always positive and amplifies differences — larger $z$ becomes disproportionately larger after exponentiation. Also, exponentials are **monotonically increasing**, so ordering is preserved. **Step 3 — Why divide by the sum?** Without normalization, the three exponentials are unbounded. Dividing by their total makes the outputs sum to 1, satisfying the axioms of probability. **Step 4 — Combine:** $$P(y=c \mid x) = \frac{e^{z_c}}{\sum_{k=1}^{C} e^{z_k}}$$ **Step 5 — Numerical example.** Suppose a CNN produces logits for an MRI scan: $$z = [2.0, 1.0, 0.1]$$ Then: $$\begin{aligned} e^{z_1} &= e^{2.0} \approx 7.389 \\ e^{z_2} &= e^{1.0} \approx 2.718 \\ e^{z_3} &= e^{0.1} \approx 1.105 \\ \text{Sum} &= 7.389 + 2.718 + 1.105 = 11.212 \end{aligned}$$ Therefore: $$P(\text{CN} \mid x) = 7.389 / 11.212 \approx 0.659 \quad (\text{65.9 %})$$ $$P(\text{MCI} \mid x) = 2.718 / 11.212 \approx 0.242 \quad (\text{24.2 %})$$ $$P(\text{AD} \mid x) = 1.105 / 11.212 \approx 0.099 \quad (\text{9.9 \%})$$ **Final predicted class:** CN (highest probability). This patient most likely does not have AD. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cross-Entropy Loss (How the model learns to be correct) **Goal:** Quantify the error between predicted probability $\hat{y}$ and true label $y$, then minimize it during training. **Step 1 — True label one-hot vector.** For a patient known to have AD in class $c = 3$: $$y = [0, 0, 1]$$ **Step 2 — Predicted probability** (from SoftMax above for some other scan): $$\hat{y} = [0.10, 0.30, 0.60]$$ **Step 3 — Apply the cross-entropy formula:** $$L = -\sum_{c=1}^{C} y_c \log \hat{y_c}$$ Only the term with $y_c = 1$ survives: $$L = -(1 \cdot \log 0.60) = -(-0.5108) = 0.5108$$ **Step 4 — Interpretation.** The model predicted AD with **60 %** confidence when the truth is AD. Loss = **0.51 nats** — moderately small, indicating the model is somewhat confident and correct. If the model had predicted $\hat{y} = [0.95, 0.04, 0.01]$ for the same true label, then: $$L = -\log(0.01) = 4.605$$ This is a **large** loss — the model is heavily penalized for being wrong. **Step 5 — Gradient descent minimizes L.** Through backpropagation, the cross-entropy gradient: $$\frac{\partial L}{\partial z_c} = \hat{y_c} - y_c$$ is used to update the CNN weights so that future predictions approach the true distribution. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Dice Coefficient for Hippocampus Segmentation **Goal:** Quantify how well the AI-segmented hippocampus overlaps with the ground-truth manual segmentation by a radiologist. **Step 1 — Definition:** $$\text{DSC} = \frac{2 \mid A \cap B \mid}{\mid A \mid + \mid B \mid}$$ where $A$ = AI segmentation mask, $B$ = ground-truth mask. **Step 2 — Numerical example.** Suppose: - AI masks **1000** voxels of the hippocampus. - Ground truth masks **900** voxels. - The two masks overlap in **800** voxels. Then: $$\begin{aligned} \mid A \cap B \mid &= 800 \\ \mid A \mid &= 1000 \\ \mid B \mid &= 900 \\ \text{DSC} &= \frac{2 \times 800}{1000 + 900} = \frac{1600}{1900} \approx 0.8421 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Interpretation.** A Dice of **0.84** indicates **excellent** agreement (typical acceptable threshold ≥ 0.80). State-of-the-art hippocampus segmentation models (e.g., HippoDeep, FastSurfer) reach DSC ≈ 0.88 – 0.90. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Architecture vs Performance (Typical ADNI Results) | Method | Input | Task | Accuracy | AUC | Dataset | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **VoxCNN (3D)** | Whole sMRI | CN vs AD | 92.0 % | 0.95 | ADNI | | **VGG-16 + SVM** | 2D slice (axial) | CN vs AD | 90.5 % | 0.93 | ADNI | | **3D-ResNet-18** | Whole sMRI | CN vs MCI vs AD | 86.4 % | 0.91 | ADNI | | **DenseNet (transfer)** | PET + MRI | CN vs AD | 94.2 % | 0.97 | ADNI | | **Autoencoder + SVM** | sMRI | Anomaly score | 88.0 % | 0.89 | OASIS | | **LSTM (longitudinal)** | 4 time-point MRI | MCI→AD conversion | 83.5 % | 0.86 | ADNI | | **ViT (Vision Transformer)** | 2D slice | CN vs MCI vs AD | 89.7 % | 0.94 | ADNI | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 End-to-End AI-AD Detection Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Patient Brain Scan Acquisition
MRI / PET / fMRI] --> B[Preprocessing Module
Skull Stripping Bias Correction
Intensity Normalization] B --> C[Atlas Registration
MNI152 Template] C --> D[Region of Interest Extraction
Hippocampus Entorhinal Cortex
Ventricles Posterior Cingulate] D --> E1[Handcrafted Features
Volume Cortical Thickness
Grey Matter Density] D --> E2[Deep Feature Extractor
3D CNN VGG16 ResNet50
DenseNet Vision Transformer] E1 --> F[Feature Vector Concatenation] E2 --> F F --> G[Classification Head
SoftMax Layer] G --> H1[Class CN Probable] G --> H2[Class MCI Probable] G --> H3[Class AD Probable] H1 --> I[Grad CAM Saliency Map] H2 --> I H3 --> I I --> J[Clinical Report Generation
Decision Support System] ``` ### 4.2 Three-Way Classification Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[3D Brain MRI Input] --> B[3D Convolutional Layer 1
ReLU + MaxPool] B --> C[3D Convolutional Layer 2
ReLU + MaxPool] C --> D[3D Convolutional Layer 3
ReLU + MaxPool] D --> E[Flatten Layer] E --> F[Fully Connected Layer 1
512 neurons Dropout 0.5] F --> G[Fully Connected Layer 2
3 neurons Softmax] G --> H1[Output CN] G --> H2[Output MCI] G --> H3[Output AD] ``` ### 4.3 AD Progression Timeline with AI Risk Stratification ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Stage 0 Cognitively Normal
MMSE 24 to 30] --> B[Stage 1 Subjective Memory Decline
MMSE 23 to 27] B --> C[Stage 2 Mild Cognitive Impairment
MMSE 19 to 23] C --> D[Stage 3 Moderate AD
MMSE 10 to 18] D --> E[Stage 4 Severe AD
MMSE less than 10] A2[AI Risk Score 0.05] -.-> A B2[AI Risk Score 0.25] -.-> B C2[AI Risk Score 0.55] -.-> C D2[AI Risk Score 0.80] -.-> D E2[AI Risk Score 0.95] -.-> E ``` ### 4.4 Hippocampus Segmentation Sub-Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Registered T1 MRI] --> B[Patch Extraction
64x64x64 voxel patches] B --> C[U Net Encoder Path
Conv Pool Down Sampling] C --> D[Bottleneck
512 feature maps] D --> E[U Net Decoder Path
Up Conv Skip Connections] E --> F[Final 1x1x1 Conv Layer
Sigmoid Activation] F --> G[Binary Hippocampus Mask] G --> H[Dice Coefficient Evaluation
Threshold 0.80] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why these block diagrams?** In KTU 2024 theory examinations, examiners often award marks for clearly drawn block diagrams showing **intermediate transformations and module responsibilities**. The above diagrams collectively cover the full pipeline, decision layer, clinical progression, and the segmentation sub-module. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]** **List the three main clinical stages used in AI-based classification of Alzheimer's disease, and state the typical MMSE score range for each.** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The three stages are: > 1. **Cognitively Normal (CN):** MMSE ≥ 24 / 30 (1 mark) > 2. **Mild Cognitive Impairment (MCI):** MMSE 19 – 23 / 30 (1 mark) > 3. **Alzheimer's Disease (AD):** MMSE ≤ 18 / 30 (1 mark) **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Name any two publicly available neuroimaging datasets commonly used for AI-based AD detection research.** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > 1. **ADNI** — Alzheimer's Disease Neuroimaging Initiative (provides longitudinal MRI, PET, CSF biomarkers, cognitive tests). (1.5 marks) > 2. **OASIS** — Open Access Series of Imaging Studies (provides cross-sectional and longitudinal T1-weighted MRI scans). (1.5 marks) --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, ESE Module 4]** **(a)** Explain with a block diagram the complete pipeline of an AI-based Alzheimer's disease detection system using structural MRI as input. Clearly state the role of each block. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 3D CNN outputs the following raw logits for a brain MRI scan of a patient: $$z = [1.2, 0.4, 2.5]$$ Compute the SoftMax probabilities for the three classes CN, MCI and AD. Which stage is the patient most likely in? **(7 marks)** --- > **Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]** > > The complete AI-based AD detection pipeline consists of the following sequential blocks: > > 1. **Data Acquisition Block (1 mark):** Brain MRI volumes (typically T1-weighted) are obtained from databases such as **ADNI** or **OASIS**, with associated clinical labels (CN, MCI, AD). > > 2. **Preprocessing Block (1 mark):** The raw scans undergo skull stripping (e.g., BET), bias-field correction (N4ITK), intensity normalization (z-score), and registration to a standard atlas (MNI152). > > 3. **ROI Extraction Block (1 mark):** Anatomical regions critical for AD — **hippocampus, entorhinal cortex, ventricles, posterior cingulate** — are isolated using atlas parcellation (AAL or Harvard-Oxford). > > 4. **Feature Extraction Block (1 mark):** Handcrafted features (volume, cortical thickness) and deep features (CNN feature maps) are extracted and concatenated into a feature vector. > > 5. **Classification Block (1 mark):** A fully-connected layer with SoftMax activation assigns probability to each of the three classes. > > 6. **Post-processing / Visualization Block (1 mark):** Grad-CAM or saliency maps highlight the brain regions responsible for the decision, aiding radiologist interpretation. > > 7. **Clinical Report Generation (1 mark):** The final probability vector and explainability maps are packaged into a decision-support report. > > *(Block diagram as shown in Section 4.1 should accompany the answer.)* > **Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]** > > **Step 1 — Write the SoftMax formula (1 mark):** > $$P(y=c \mid x) = \frac{e^{z_c}}{\sum_{k=1}^{C} e^{z_k}}$$ > > **Step 2 — Compute exponentials (2 marks):** > $$\begin{aligned} > e^{z_1} &= e^{1.2} \approx 3.320 \\ > e^{z_2} &= e^{0.4} \approx 1.492 \\ > e^{z_3} &= e^{2.5} \approx 12.182 > \end{aligned}$$ > > **Step 3 — Sum the exponentials (1 mark):** > $$\text{Sum} = 3.320 + 1.492 + 12.182 = 16.994$$ > > **Step 4 — Compute probabilities (2 marks):** > $$\begin{aligned} > P(\text{CN} \mid x) &= \frac{3.320}{16.994} \approx 0.195 \quad (19.5\%) \\ > P(\text{MCI} \mid x) &= \frac{1.492}{16.994} \approx 0.088 \quad (8.8\%) \\ > P(\text{AD} \mid x) &= \frac{12.182}{16.994} \approx 0.717 \quad (71.7\%) > \end{aligned}$$ > > **Step 5 — Conclusion (1 mark):** The patient is most likely in the **AD (Alzheimer's Disease)** stage, as the model assigns the highest probability (71.7 %) to that class. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, ESE Module 4 Alternative]** **(a)** Discuss the role of **3D Convolutional Neural Networks** in Alzheimer's disease detection. How are they superior to 2D slice-based CNNs? Mention at least two specific architectures. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **Dice Similarity Coefficient** used to evaluate hippocampus segmentation. An AI model predicted 1200 voxels as hippocampus, the ground truth marked 1000 voxels, and the overlap was 850 voxels. Compute the DSC. **(7 marks)** --- > **Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]** > > **Definition (1 mark):** A 3D CNN is a deep learning architecture that uses **three-dimensional kernels** (height × width × depth) to slide over volumetric brain MRI data, learning spatial features across all three anatomical axes simultaneously. > > **Why 3D CNNs for AD (3 marks):** > 1. **Volumetric Context Preservation** — A 2D CNN processes each axial slice independently, losing inter-slice anatomical continuity. A 3D CNN preserves the **3D structural relationships** between neighbouring slices, which is critical for detecting subtle hippocampal atrophy. > 2. **Better Feature Hierarchy** — Lower 3D layers capture voxel-level textures; mid layers capture regional shapes; deep layers capture whole-structure patterns (e.g., ventricular enlargement). > 3. **Reduced Preprocessing Burden** — The network itself learns optimal slice-wise fusion, eliminating the need for hand-crafted slice aggregation. > 4. **Higher Clinical Accuracy** — Published benchmarks (e.g., on ADNI) show 3D CNNs achieve **90 – 95 %** accuracy, surpassing 2D slice-based approaches (typically 85 – 90 %). > > **Specific Architectures (2 marks):** > 1. **3D ResNet (e.g., 3D-ResNet-18 / 50)** — Uses residual skip connections to enable training of very deep networks without vanishing gradients. > 2. **3D DenseNet** — Uses dense connectivity where every layer receives feature maps from all preceding layers, maximizing feature reuse. > 3. **3D U-Net** (bonus) — An encoder-decoder network widely used for **voxel-wise segmentation** of the hippocampus. > > **Limitations (1 mark):** Higher computational cost and memory consumption; requires GPU with large VRAM (≥ 12 GB). > **Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]** > > **Definition and Purpose (2 marks):** The **Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC)** measures the spatial overlap between the AI-predicted segmentation mask $A$ and the radiologist-marked ground-truth mask $B$. It is defined as: > $$\text{DSC} = \frac{2 \mid A \cap B \mid}{\mid A \mid + \mid B \mid}$$ > The value ranges from **0** (no overlap) to **1** (perfect overlap). A threshold of **≥ 0.80** is generally accepted as "good" segmentation quality in medical imaging. > > **Given Data (1 mark):** > - $\mid A \mid$ (AI mask size) = 1200 voxels > - $\mid B \mid$ (ground-truth size) = 1000 voxels > - $\mid A \cap B \mid$ (overlap) = 850 voxels > > **Step 1 — Compute the numerator (1 mark):** > $$2 \mid A \cap B \mid = 2 \times 850 = 1700$$ > > **Step 2 — Compute the denominator (1 mark):** > $$\mid A \mid + \mid B \mid = 1200 + 1000 = 2200$$ > > **Step 3 — Compute DSC (1 mark):** > $$\text{DSC} = \frac{1700}{2200} = 0.7727$$ > > **Step 4 — Interpretation (1 mark):** A DSC of **0.77** indicates **acceptable but not excellent** overlap. The model is slightly **over-segmenting** (predicting 1200 voxels vs. 1000 true) and is missing ≈ 150 ground-truth voxels. To meet the clinical threshold of 0.80, the model should be retrained with more diverse data or fine-tuned with a Dice-loss objective. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Forgetting the SoftMax denominator** — Students often write $P = e^{z_c}$ and skip the normalization. This will cost **1 – 2 marks** instantly. > 2. **Confusing sensitivity with specificity** — Sensitivity (TP / TP+FN) is for *AD detection*, specificity (TN / TN+FP) is for *CN recognition*. Mixing them up is a frequent deduction. > 3. **Not stating units or clinical context** — When listing a metric, always anchor it to AD (e.g., "DSC for hippocampus segmentation in ADNI"). Examiners reward clinical relevance. > 4. **Skipping the block diagram in 14-mark answers** — A 7-mark sub-question on a pipeline almost always expects a labelled diagram. Without it, expect a **2 – 3 mark penalty**. > 5. **Reporting SoftMax probabilities without summing to 1** — Always verify $\sum P_c = 1$; if not, recompute. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — AI-Based Alzheimer's Disease Detection** - **Core problem:** 3-class classification of {CN, MCI, AD} from brain MRI / PET. - **Key datasets:** **ADNI** (longitudinal, multimodal) and **OASIS** (cross-sectional MRI). - **Primary imaging modality:** **T1-weighted structural MRI** (others: PET for amyloid, fMRI for connectivity). - **Anatomical hallmarks of AD:** **Hippocampal atrophy**, **ventricular enlargement**, **cortical thinning**, **posterior cingulate hypometabolism**. - **Standard deep learning architectures:** **3D-CNN**, **3D-ResNet**, **3D-DenseNet**, **VGG-16 + SVM**, **U-Net (segmentation)**, **ViT**, **LSTM (longitudinal)**. - **SoftMax formula:** $P(y=c \mid x) = \dfrac{e^{z_c}}{\sum_{k=1}^{C} e^{z_k}}$ — must sum to 1. - **Cross-entropy loss:** $L = -\sum_{c} y_c \log \hat{y_c}$ — used to train the network. - **Dice coefficient:** $\text{DSC} = \dfrac{2 \mid A \cap B \mid}{\mid A \mid + \mid B \mid}$ — gold standard for segmentation evaluation; threshold ≥ 0.80. - **Clinical metrics:** **MMSE** (cognitive score), **CSF Aβ42** (amyloid marker), **Tau / p-Tau** (tangle marker), **hippocampal volume** (≈ 3 – 4 cm³ healthy). - **Evaluation metrics:** **Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, F1-Score, AUC** (threshold-independent). - **Explainability tools:** **Grad-CAM, saliency maps, occlusion analysis** — required for clinical acceptance. - **Preprocessing steps:** **Skull stripping → bias correction → normalization → atlas registration**. - **State-of-the-art accuracy:** **90 – 95 %** for CN vs AD on ADNI; **83 – 88 %** for CN vs MCI vs AD (3-class). - **Key clinical insight:** **AD atrophy rate ≈ 1.5 – 2 % / year** (vs. 0.3 – 0.5 % in normal aging) — exploited as a longitudinal AI feature. - **Pipeline order to memorize:** **Acquire → Preprocess → Register → ROI → Extract → Classify → Explain → Report**. - **Why AI over manual reading:** Speed (seconds), reproducibility, and ability to detect sub-millimetre changes invisible to the human eye.
